node.title: Day on the Farm
node.content: This book looks at a simple story about a day on a farm. This book follows Letters & Sounds Phase 1, which focuses on phonemic awareness through developing speaking and listening skills. This book is a pre-reader. Features of the Book - Phase 1, no text, only 1 letter sound per page is introduced. Purpose of the Book - vivid and playful illustrations depict some basic information about each page image. Day on the Farm can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and illustrations, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Have you been to a farm before? What kinds of animals live on farms?
node.esiss_activities: What kinds of animals live on a farm? Draw a picture of yourself with a farm animal of your choice.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.title: Where Do I Live?

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Where do animals in different parts of the world live? In the ocean, the desert, the jungle?
node.esiss_activities: Where do different kinds of animals live? Draw a picture of an animal of your choice in their natural habitat e.g. a lion in a safari, a fish in the sea.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: What do most people do at the beach? Build sandcastles, surf, swim and enjoy the sunshine.
node.esiss_activities: What do you like to take to the beach? Write or draw a list of items that you might take to the beach. E.g. a bucket and spade.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Who is in your family? Learn about the family in the story and compare and contrast to your own.
node.esiss_activities: Who is in your family? Write a story or draw a picture of your own family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: What do you need if you are going to jump in muddy puddles? Write a list of suitable clothing items, or draw pictures of the items you might need to stay dry.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: The first day at school can be exciting. What happens on the first day of school in this story?
node.esiss_activities: What is your school like? Can you draw a picture of your classroom or of your teacher?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: When you ride your bike up a hill, do you huff and puff? A young girl races on her bike. Does she win the race?
node.esiss_activities: How many wheels are there on a bike? Draw a picture of a typical bike and label the parts.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_blurb: The day in the life of a typical green frog. What does the frog eat? What else does he do?
node.esiss_activities: A frog is an amphibian. It can live on land and water. Draw a frog in its natural environment.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

Wheels that Move can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and photographs, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. node.esiss_activities: Go outside and find as many types of wheels as you can. Either draw pictures of them or take some photographs. Compare and contrast the wheels that you find.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Explore the fascinating world of bugs. Get a close up look at some creepy crawlies and find out why they are so fascinating.
node.esiss_activities: Get outside into your garden or local park. Take a magnifying glass and a notepad. Seek out some bugs and get up close. Take a look and draw some pictures of the bugs that you see, while you are out in the field.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of the baby animal that you love the most.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of any of the sea creatures in this story. Get a grown-up to help you research the animal, either in a library book or on the Internet.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: Draw a plan of your ideal vegetable garden. Where would you plant the carrots, the lettuces, the radishes etc?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: Find your own recipe to bake some bread and ask a grown-up to help you bake some of your own bread.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.title: Sports Day

node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of yourself taking part in a sports event. It can be any sport that you enjoy.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

Musical Instruments can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and photographs, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. node.esiss_blurb: Can you play a musical instrument? What kind of instrument would you like to learn to play? The drums, the flute or the guitar?
node.esiss_activities: Ask a grown-up to help you play some music. Research the instruments that interest you and find out more about what these instruments sound like.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.esiss_blurb: Pets are much loved by everyone. Which pet would you like?
node.esiss_activities: If you have a family pet, draw a picture of it. Or imagine the pet you would love to have and draw a picture of this animal.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: What can you find in outer space? Take a closer look and explore the world of space.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of planet Earth. Around Earth, draw the things that are up in space. For example, the Moon and the stars.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

No tricky words used in this story.

node.esiss_activities: Ask a grown-up to help you play some music and dance to it. Research the types of dance that interest you and find out more about the styles of music that go with these dances.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.title: Games Galore
node.content: This book looks at a simple story about playing different kinds of games. Games Galore can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and photographs, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: What is your most loved family game? Hopscotch, snakes and ladders or chess? Explore the world of traditional games.
node.esiss_activities: Get out some traditional games, like checkers or snakes and ladders, and have a game with a family member or a friend.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1440 ACELA1433

node.title: Goldilocks and the Three Bears
node.content: This book looks at a simple story about the retelling of Goldilocks and the Three Bears. This book follows Letters & Sounds Phase 1, which focuses on phonemic awareness through developing speaking and listening skills. This book is a pre-reader. Features of the Book - Phase 1, no text. Purpose of the Book - vivid and playful illustrations depict some basic information about each page image. Goldilocks and the Three Bears can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and illustrations, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Do you know the classic tale of Goldilocks and the Three Bears? What does Goldilocks do when she enters the Bears' house? Will she get away in time when the Bears return?
node.esiss_activities: Draw your own version of Goldilocks and the Three Bears. You decide what happens in the story. You might try to style the story in a cartoon style of drawing.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

node.title: Stuck in the Mud
node.content: This book looks at a simple story about a boy who takes his bike out on a rainy day and gets it stuck in the mud. Purpose of the Book - vivid and playful illustrations depict some basic information about each page image. Stuck in the Mud can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and illustrations, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: What should a young boy do on a rainy day? Take his bike out the park, or stay dry inside? What will happen when he rides into a mud puddle?
node.esiss_activities: Make a list of the items you might need to go outside in the rain, in order to stay dry. For example, an umbrella or a rain coat (rain jacket). You can write the items or draw pictures of them.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

node.title: Go to Bed!

No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: What is your bedtime routine each night? Read through the pages in this book and compare and contrast to your own bedtime routine. Does the boy get to bed on time?
node.esiss_activities: List each of the things in order that you need to do, to get ready for bed each night. You can write a list in words or in pictures.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

node.esiss_blurb: Do you know the classic tale of the Three Billy Goats Gruff? What does Troll do when the goats approach the bridge? Do the billy goats get to the other side?
node.esiss_activities: Draw your own version of the Three Billy Goats Gruff. You decide what happens in the story. You might try to style the story in a cartoon- style of drawing.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

Flying to the Moon can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and illustrations, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever dreamed of flying to the Moon? In this exciting story, an astronaut lands on the Moon. But, who does he encounter when he arrives?
node.esiss_activities: What is out there in space? Imagine you are the astronaut and draw a picture of what you see when you land on the Moon.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

The Grunch and the Children can be used to introduce and reinforce the following pre-reading skills and strategies: recognising the relationship between pages and illustrations, learning about the key features of a book and how to turn pages while reading a story, predicting the story line using the images from the book and responding to simple questions about the story's content. node.esiss_blurb: The Grunch guards the forest at night. When an angry bear approaches some campers, who will save the day?
node.esiss_activities: Make a list of the items you need to go camping. The list can be in words or in pictures. For example: a tent, tent pegs, sleeping bags etc.

node.esiss_activities: Ask a grown-up for help and take some photographs of different weather patterns on each of the different days of the week. Compare and contrast the days at th end.

node.esiss_activities: Next time you are in the car, take notice of the traffic lights and any traffic jams. Think about what it feels like to be stuck in traffic. Do you have any good ideas to help with passing the time while in a traffic jam?

node.esiss_blurb: What happens when we get lost? How do we find our way back home? Explore the concept of being lost or being alone in this book of vibrant and unusual images.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a map of where you live. Include your street, your house and any local parks or beaches.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

node.esiss_activities: Write a list (or draw a list) of the food that you can see in your fridge and kitchen cupboards.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1578 ACELA1433

node.title: Pat, Tap, Sat
node.content: This book looks at a simple story about some characters and objects who tap and pat. Features of the Book - Phase 2 letter sounds, consonants and vowels - s, a, t, p. Punctuation - fullstops. Purpose of the Book - vivid and playful illustrations depict and help the text explain some basic information about each page image. Pat, Tap, Sat can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness - making predictions. The book has been written using a strictly controlled vocabulary. Text uses only decodable regular words (according to the week by week progression of Letters and Sounds) along with 'tricky words'. No tricky words used in this story.
node.esiss_blurb: Pat, tap and sat. Can you work out who taps and pats in the pictures of this story?
node.esiss_activities: The boy in the story pats and taps his dog. Draw a picture of yourself with a dog and write the words 'pat' and 'tap' beneath the image.

The book has been written using a strictly controlled vocabulary. Text uses only decodable regular words (according to the week by week progression of Letters and Sounds) along with 'tricky words'. Tricky words featured in the text - the, to.

Purpose of the Book - vivid photographs depict and help the text explain some basic information about each page image. Go to the Zoo can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness - making predictions. The book has been written using a strictly controlled vocabulary. Text uses only decodable regular words (according to the week by week progression of Letters and Sounds) along with 'tricky words'. Tricky words featured in the text - the, go, to.
node.esiss_blurb: What do the kids see at the zoo? Have you been to the zoo before? Explore the world of zoo with this delightful collection of zoo images.
node.esiss_activities: Write a short story about going to the zoo. You can use the text in this story to help guide you.

Punctuation - fullstops, exclamation marks and question marks. Purpose of the Book - vivid photographs depict and help the text explain some basic information about each page image. Nature Conservation can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness - making predictions. This book also supports non-fiction strategies - gaining information from photographs, encountering curriculum science related terms and concepts. The book has been written using a strictly controlled vocabulary. Text uses only decodable regular words (according to the week by week progression of Letters and Sounds) along with key interest words that relate to this topic specifically.
node.esiss_blurb: Nature conservation is the act of protection of the environment and the living things within it. Understand the basic concepts around nature conservation, through reading this vibrant and informative life science title.
node.esiss_activities: The Life Science topic in this non-fiction title focuses on nature conservation. Re-read the text in this title and see if you can list some ways in which you can try to reduce your household resource consumption.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1474 ACELA1471 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669
node.trickywords: wildlife, pollutants, resources, conservation, environment, minerals

node.esiss_blurb: What is a habitat and why are they worth saving? Habitats are important to all living things, but many are under threat from human development. Understand the basic scientific concepts around habitats and the need for their preservation, through reading this vibrant and informative life science title.
node.esiss_activities: The Life Science topic in this non-fiction title focuses on habitats. Re-read the text in this title and see if you can list some ways in which humans can try to preserve habitats. Go outside and see if you can identify a micro-habitat in your garden.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1474 ACELA1471 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669

node.trickywords: reading, growled, stopped, opened, frowned, beamed, nodded, saved, called, looked, climbed, reading, trotted, meowed, shivering

node.title: The Mystery Robot
node.content: This book looks at a simple, funny and relatable story about a two kids, Pip and Ted, and their home robot. The robot is missing? What will happen next? Features of the Book - Phase 6 - Fluent. Punctuation - fullstops, speech marks and exclamation marks. Purpose of the Book - vivid and playful illustrations depict and help the text explain some basic information about each page image. The Mystery Robot can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness - making predictions. The book has been written using a strictly controlled vocabulary. Text uses only decodable regular words (according to the week by week progression of Letters and Sounds) along with 'tricky', interest words or words that feature suffixes.
node.esiss_blurb: Two kids, Pip and Ted, and their home robot. What could possibly go wrong? The robot goes missing and the house is in a big mess. What will happen next?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of your own dream home robot. List all the things that it can help you with around the house.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1474 ACELA1471 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669
node.trickywords: flying, shrugged, switched, grinned, helping, smashed, smeared, shredded, raced, floated, dived, coiled, bubbled

node.title: Toys
node.content: This book looks at the materials that toys can be made from. Features of the Book - High-frequency words - This, is, of Content words - toy, wood, plastic, wool, metal, glass, paper, cloth, blocks - Repeated phrases - This toy is made of ___. - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends: pl, gl, bl; long vowel sound: wood, wool - Punctuation - fullstops, exclamation mark Purpose of the Book Toys can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness - making predictions.

node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Using graphic sources, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Toys are made of all kinds of things. What are your toys made of?
node.esiss_activities: Look at your toys. What are they made of? Write some labels: • wood • metal • cloth • plastic Put each toy under the right label.

node.title: Up in Space
node.content: Scientists look at the moon and stars up in space. This simple text looks at things that are up in space, such as the moon, a comet, stars, and astronauts. (Big idea: Scientists study Earth and space.) Features of Up in Space • the topic (things that are up in space), which expands on the idea from pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support the text • initial consonant blends - "sp-", "st-" • exclamation mark • mixture of familiar (moon, stars) and less familiar (comet, astronauts) objects in space
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Do you know what you can find up in space? Look up. There is more there than you think!
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of planet Earth. Around Earth, draw the things that are up in space. Do you know any other things that are up in space? Add these things to your picture, too.

node.title: Bubbles Keep Me Clean
node.content: How do I take care of myself? I keep clean . In this book, a boy describes how he takes a bath. The simple text uses rhythm and rhyme to engage and support the reader. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping clean.) Features Of Bubbles Keep Me Clean ges 8 and 9 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support the text • body vocabulary - fingers, toes, ears, nose, chin, hair • digraph - "ch-" • initial blend - "cl-" • compound word - everywhere • singular and plural nouns - chin, hair, nose, fingers, toes, ears • repetition of "b" - rhyming words - toes/nose, hair/everywhere
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Having a bath is fun. Bubbles go everywhere, and they keep you clean!
node.esiss_activities: Draw three big bubbles. In each bubble, write something you do to keep yourself clean. Write a sentence under each bubble.

node.title: Too Big!
node.content: This story, told by a young boy, highlights a situation that children can easily relate to - growing too big for clothes and toys. He finds comfort in a hug from Mum. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using illustrations to understand text, Making connections with real-life experiences, and inferring. It provides opportunities to practise one-to-one word matching, directionality, recognising high-frequency words, and cross-checking using visual information. Look at Me is another Dragonflies emergent title with the theme of clothing. Text features • the high-frequency words - am, but, for, I, my • the repeated initial consonant "b" - big, bike, but • the initial digraphs -ch - chair• -sh - shirt, shoes • the initial consonant blend -sw - swing • the use of the adverb "too" • the exclamation mark for emphasis in the title and on page 8 • the repetitive sentence structure • the expressions on the characters' faces • the affirmative, reassuring ending • the cat featured in the illustrations
node.strategies: Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: This boy is too big for his bike. He is too big for his swing. His mother finds something that is just right.
node.esiss_activities: In this story, there are lots of "b" words. The words "big", "bike", and 'but" start with "b". Practise writing the letter "b". What other words do you know that start with this letter?
node.esiss_hfw: am, but, for, I, my
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: Going to the River
node.content: This narrative text features some African animals going to the river. The climax occurs when Lion appears and the other animals beat a hasty retreat! This book supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions, summarising the Determining main ideas, making connections (with the students' knowledge of African animals), and forming hypotheses. It provides opportunities to practise one-to-one word matching and to reinforce high-frequency words and knowledge of upper-case letters. The varying lengths of the animal names help to reinforce the concept of a word. Text features • the high-frequency words - going, is, the, to • the use of a variety of upper-case letters • the upper-case and lower-case forms of initial letters - Going, going; River, river • the -ing ending - going • the -un rime in run • the animal names • the repetitive sentence structure • the opportunities for prediction on page 7 • the change in structure on page 8 • the use of the exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the strong support for the text provided by the illustrations
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: All of the animals are going to the river to drink. What happens when Lion wants a drink? Watch out!
node.esiss_activities: Lion chases away all the animals. Imagine he goes back to the river and a crocodile swims up to him. Draw a picture of Lion. Write what he would say to the crocodile.
node.esiss_hfw: going, is, the, to

node.title: We Can Run and Play
node.content: How do I take care of myself? I run and play. This informational text is about children enjoying exercise. They can jump, hop, run, slide, climb, and swing. The lively photographs and brightly coloured illustrations engage the reader. The simple, repetitive text supports emergent readers. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by doing exercise.) Features of We Can Run and Play • the topic (doing exercise), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • combination of photographs and illustrations • background illustrations that convey motion • blends - "sw-", "sl-", "cl-", "pl-" • digraph - "th" • vowel sounds - "ee", "oo" • present tense verbs - run, jump, hop, slide, climb, swing, play
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: There are all kinds of ways to run and play. Try these ways to move and have fun!
node.esiss_activities: There are many ways to exercise. Make a list of the ways you know. Colour in the ones that you like to do. Ask your family what they like to do best.

node.title: This Fish
node.content: Fish come in all shapes and sizes. In this book, readers get to look at the eyes, fins, tails, and mouths of fish - little and big. Features of the Book High-frequency words - This, big, a Content words - eyes, fins, fish, mouth, tail Repeated phrases - This fish has ______ . Phonics and phonemic awareness - short i sound - fish, little, big, fins; CVCC word family for -ish Punctuation - full stop, exclamation mark Purpose of the Book This Fish can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - using texts as models for own writing - vocabulary and word study.
node.strategies: Making judgments|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Using texts as models, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Read about fish - their eyes, tails, fins, and mouths come in all shapes and sizes.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a fish with: • a big body • little eyes • a big fin • a little tail • a big mouth. Write labels for your picture.

node.title: Lunch Boxes
node.content: A group of children stack their coloured lunch boxes as high as they can. Inevitably, the stack falls! This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge, predicting using illustrations, and inferring. It provides opportunities for practising one-to-one word matching, directionality, and recognition of high-frequency words. The illustrations provide opportunities for extending the student's oral language. Text features • the high-frequency words - a, here, is, look, out • the repeated initial consonant -b - blue, box, boxes • the upper-case and lower-case forms of initial letters -B, L, b, l • the colour names • the high proportion of single syllable words • the change in Text structure on page 8 • the ending and the exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the visual sub-plot (the bird taking part in the game) Setting the scene Play the stacking game with the students' lunch boxes
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: In this book, the children build a tower from lunch boxes. What happens when a bird lands on top?
node.esiss_activities: What colours are the lunch boxes? Find some blocks that are the same colour and make a tower. How high can you make it? Count how many blocks you use.

node.title: We Like Fruit
node.content: How do I take care of myself? I eat good food. This simple text supports the view that fruit is good for you and shows the preparation of a fruit salad. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by eating good food.) Features of We Like Fruit • the topic (healthy eating), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • picture glossary • links between photographs and text • title page that supports the text on page 2 initial consonant blends - "fr-", "str-", "gr-" • compound words - strawberries, watermelon • plural nouns - apples, bananas, grapes, oranges, strawberries • medial sounds - "oo" in"food" and "good" (with different pronunciation) • Repetitive sentence structure with a change on page 11
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Do you like fruit? This boy loves all kinds of fruit. He helps his mother chop it up to make something special!
node.esiss_activities: Choose the piece of fruit you like. Draw a picture of the fruit. Add a label. Now draw a picture of the fruit cut in half.

node.title: I Can Help
node.content: People need to help each other. In this book, children explore ways of helping other people and each other. Featuresof I Can Help • the topic (helping one another), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • action photographs • initial consonant blends - "br", "fr" • words that begin and end with same letter - mum, dad • change in structure on page 8

node.esiss_blurb: Do you help around the house? Here are some good ways you can help your mum and dad, and your friends.
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book help others in many ways. Think about how you could help someone. Make a plan. What will you do?

node.title: Bubbles
node.content: This book describes the effect of the wind on bubbles blown by a child. Zany illustrations emphasise the humour in the story and encourage discussion. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge, making and confirming predictions, inferring, and evaluating. It provides opportunities for recognising high-frequency words and for practising directionality and one-to-one word matching. The repetition and close picture-text match provides support for new readers. Text features • the high-frequency words - my, on, the, they, to, up • the repeated initial consonant -b - birds, blew, bubbles• -d - dog, down • the upper-case and lower-case forms of t, b, p • the "o" sound in over • the use of repetition for effect • the irregular verb - blew • the use of onomatopoeia - pop! • the use of an exclamation mark for emphasis • the humorous ending • the unusual style of the illustrations
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When you blow bubbles, they float up, up, up. What happens when the bubbles come down again?
node.esiss_activities: Put some water into a bowl. Add some dishwashing liquid. Ask an adult to help you make a bubble blower from thin wire. Go outside and blow lots of bubbles!

node.title: The Picnic
node.content: This story describes how Mum and two children prepare for a picnic. A closer reading of the story reveals that the children and Mum have quite different ideas about the best kinds of food to pack! This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge, making and confirming predictions, using illustrations to understand text, Making connections to real-life experiences, and inferring. It provides opportunities for recognising high-frequency words and for practising directionality and one-to-one word matching. Text features • the high-frequency words - in, the, we • the use of the pronoun "we" to refer to the two children • the repetitive sentence structure • the plural nouns - apples, chips, cookies, picnics, sandwiches • the shift of focus from Mum to the children on alternate pages • the contrast between the food choices of Mum and the children • the use of an exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the visual sub-plot featuring the family cat and dog who appear on most pages • the quirky style of the illustrations
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Summarising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Visualising, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: This family loves picnics. They each put their favourite food into the picnic basket.
node.esiss_activities: Draw the food that the family took on the picnic. Write a label for each picture. Now draw what you would like to take on a picnic. Write some labels for your pictures.

node.title: The Puppy
node.content: A puppy plays around the house making a big mess. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - The, and, the, - Content words - Puppy, puppy, socks, blocks, soap, rope, mat, bat, cat, Stop, that, - Repeated phrase - The puppy and the, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - S (Stop), s (socks, soap, blocks), Th (The), th (the, that)• -at rime (mat, bat, cat, that), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation marks - Special Feature - Rhyme at the end of each line, Skills and Strategies - The Puppy can be used to introduce and reinforce: - responding to simple questions about the story content, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - writing using the conventions of capital letters and full stops - recognising rhyming words, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 89 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, scissors, glue, pencil, - water-based paint, painting shirts/aprons, wipeable tabletop, - paper divided into three sections, scissors, glue. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: This is a poem about a puppy that gets into all kinds of trouble. How do you stop a puppy being naughty?
node.esiss_activities: Some words in this book sound the same. They are called rhyming words. The words "socks" and "blocks" are rhyming words. Which words in the book rhyme with "cat"? Now write all the words you know that rhyme with "hand".

node.title: The Boat
node.content: In this story, a child constructs a cardboard boat and takes it to the beach to see if it will float. Features of the Book • The clear sequence of steps for the children to identify and follow. • Photographs to support the text. • Clear, large print. • The high-frequency word "The." • Initial "b" consonant - "beach," "box," and "boat." • Long and short vowel - "box" and "boat." • Labeled diagram in the back of the book. Purpose The Boat can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • responding to simple questions about the story's content • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • writing labels for a specific purpose • recording some simple directions.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making connections, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: What's better than making a boat? Taking it to the beach to sail it! If you follow the plan, you can make your own boat, too.
node.esiss_activities: The girl in the book has drawn a plan for her boat. Use the plan to build your own boat. Will your boat float in some water?

node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: Elena Makes Tortillas
node.content: Elena Makes Tortillas is a simple procedural book about making tortillas. Features of the Book • Two levels of text - body text and typical recipe language. • Consistent placement of text. • The high-frequency words - "Some," "it," and "to." • The exclamation points on page 12. • The use of illustrations and photographs. • The range of simple verbs - "press," "pat," "cook," and "roll." • The recipe on the inside back cover. Purpose Elena Makes Tortillas can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • practicing pointing one-to-one as each word is read • using knowledge of letters and sounds for • recognizing simple consonant-vowel-consonant words • encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding • recording the main points from the story.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Exploring text structure|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Elena is good at making tortillas. If you read this book, you'll know how to make tortillas, too. Just follow the recipe!
node.esiss_activities: Ask someone to help you make tortillas. Use the recipe at the back of this book. Follow the instructions as you cook.
node.esiss_hfw: some, it, to
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: A House for a Mouse
node.content: A House for a Mouse makes simple, factual statements about the equipment and materials used to make a mouse house. The illustration convey a second storyline. Features of the Book • One word change per page on pages 3 to 7. • The high-frequency words - "A" and "for." • The rhyme - "house" and "mouse." • Initial "b" consonant - "box," "bed," and "bowl." • The secondary story line in the illustrations. • The challenge of different lengths of text per page. Purpose A House for a Mouse can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • developing an awareness of the directionality of the print on each page • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • responding to simple questions about the story's content • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Determining author's purpose|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Visualising, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: What do you need to make a house for a mouse? This boy shows you how to make the perfect house for his little pet.
node.esiss_activities: Talk with someone at home about what you read in this book. How would you make your own house for a mouse? Write a list of the things you would use. Now, draw your mouse house. Draw a mouse, too.

node.title: Going Places
node.content: Creatures go by bus to a variety of interesting places. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - Going, I, am, going, to, the, - Content words - Places, shop, petrol station, park, movies, library, cafe, pool school, - Repeated phrase - I am going to the, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - P (Places), p (park, pool, petrol), s (shop, station, school), word ending -ing (going), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies - Going Places can be used to introduce and reinforce: - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - innovating on the story to create own versions of stories, plays, and songs, - predicting the storyline using illustrations from the book, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 87 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, scissors, glue, pencil, - paper for painting, paint, paintbrushes. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The people in this book are all riding on the big yellow bus. They are going to different places. Where is the child with the lunchbox going?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a map of your neighbourhood. Draw your street and show your house. Draw a friend's street and show their house. Write the names of the streets on the map. Use your map to show someone how to get from your house to your friend's house.

node.title: My Best Bear
node.content: A boy shows the reader his collection of bears, then snuggles into bed with his best bear. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of connecting with real-life experiences, using illustrations to understand text, forming and testing hypotheses, and inferring. It provides opportunities for practising one-to-one word matching, directionality, and recognising high-frequency words. The illustrations provide opportunities for developing students' oral language. Text features • the high-frequency words - but, is, my, this • the repeated initial consonant -b - bear, best, big, blue, but • the upper-case and lower-case forms of initial letters - bear, Bear, best, Best; my, My; this, This • the digraph -th - this • the high proportion of single-syllable words (which supports one-to-one matching) • the opposites - big and little, old and new • the adjectives used to describe the bears • the title as a support for reading the last page • the exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the child's evaluation of the bears suggested by the word "best" • the extra information provided in the illustrations
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: The boy in this book has lots of bears. Some are little. Some are big. Which is the best bear of all?
node.esiss_activities: What is your best toy? Draw a picture of it. Write a caption. This is my best _______.

node.title: Happy Birthday
node.content: A young girl is turning six, and everyone has a special way of saying "happy birthday". The joy and excitement of birthdays are captured in this cheerful text that the students will find easy to relate to. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge, making connections with real-life experiences, and using illustrations to understand text. The use of high-frequency words, repetition, and a close picture-text match are supports for the reader, but there are also subtle text changes that require the students to pay close attention to print. The content words on each page provide opportunities for the students to practise their decoding skills. Text features • the high-frequency words - a, it, my, on, said, with • the repeated initial letters - b, s, h • the digraphs -ph - phone• -th - with, birthday • the initial consonant blends - cl, fl , sm • the compound word - birthday • the repetitive Text structure • the use of speech bubbles • the names of the family members • the split picture on page 5 • the extra information provided in the illustrations (the girl's age on the cover, and page 4) • the warm family relationships portrayed in the illustrations
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Today is my birthday. Everyone says happy birthday to me. My dog says it with a lick. How does my class say it?
node.esiss_activities: Everyone says "Happy Birthday" in a different way. Write two sentences that describe how you would say happy birthday to Mum.
node.esiss_hfw: a, it, my, on, said, with
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: Crash!
node.content: Two children build a tower from furniture and toys and it crashes. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - The, go, up, - Content words - Crash, chairs, boxes, books, blocks, trucks, cars, bears, - Repeated phrase - The, go, up, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - g (go), b (boxes, books, blocks, bears), c (cars, crash)• plural "s" on content words, - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation marks - Special Feature - Large type used for emphasis on page 16 Skills and Strategies Crash! can be used to introduce and reinforce: - reading using expression to increase understanding, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - retelling the sequence of the story using own words, - writing labels for a specific purpose Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 87 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencil, markers, - collection of items suitable for building a tower, for example, corks, small blocks, bottle tops, - prepared picture cards, - old magazines, scissors, glue. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: We can build a big tower. We use chairs. We use boxes. How high can we go?
node.esiss_activities: Write answers to these questions. What did they put on their tower first? What did they put on their tower last? Why did the tower fall down?
node.esiss_hfw: the, go, up

node.title: A Mitt for Me
node.content: A boy travels from home to the store to buy a baseball mitt. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - A, for, Me, This, is, big, me, - Content words - Mitt, motorway, bridge, car park, mall, escalator, shop - Repeated phrase - This, is big, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - M (Mitt, Me), m (me, mall, mitt), b (bridge, big), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies A Mitt for Me can be used to introduce and reinforce: - predicting the story line using illustrations from the book, - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - innovating on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs., Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 93 cut and ready for use, - copies of BLM, scissors, glue, pencil, markers, - selection of large dress-up clothes, shoes, hats etc., - markers and large paper or pavement chalk, - paper cut in a T-shirt shape with star template markers. node.strategies: Making predictions|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Making predictions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: On his way to the mall the boy sees lots of big things. But what he finds at the mall is just the right size.
node.esiss_activities: Look around your house. How many big things can you see? Draw some of these things. Next to each thing, write: This ________ is big.

node.title: Wild Bear
node.content: Wild Bear takes a look at the activities in the day of a bear. Features of the Book • The repetitive text and supportive illustrations. • Each sentence begins with the same two words. • The capital letters for the name "Wild Bear." • The simple present tense of the verbs - "wakes" and "sleeps." • The three-word text that allows easy fingerpointing and identification of each word as a unit. Purpose Wild Bear can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: When Wild Bear wakes up, there are lots of things to do. What does he do when he's finished being so busy?
node.esiss_activities: What does Wild Bear do each day? Write a list of these things. Now make a list of what you do. What is the same? What is different?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: The Apple Star
node.content: This story surveys children's preferences for different coloured apples and dishes that can be made with apples. node.esiss_activities: Do you like apples? Ask each member of your family what kind of apple they like. Ask someone to cut an apple in half so you can look at what is inside. Now write a list of the things you can make with apples.

node.esiss_blurb: This girl has a new little sister. She has a little nose and little eyes. She has a little mouth, but she makes a big noise!

node.title: My Cat
node.content: A cat rests in different places around the house. node.title: In the Car
node.content: A family is very unhappy about getting into the car. However, they are cheered up when Mum buys everyone an ice cream. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - In, the, is, not, we, are, - Content words - Car, happy, Mum, Dad, Meg, Kate, Ben, Baby, Dino, - Repeated phrase - is not happy, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - D (Dad, Dino), - Punctuation - capital letters, full stops, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies - In the Car can be used to introduce and reinforce: - using knowledge of letters and sounds to recognise simple consonant-vowel-consonant words, - predicting the story line using illustrations from the book, - innovating on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs, - practising pointing one-to-one as each word is read, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 95 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencil, markers, - set of letter cards for each child - "n", "o", and "t", - substitute consonant cards - "c", "d", "g", "l", "p", "r", - coloured paper circles, large paper, glue, markers. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: The family is going for a trip in the car. Nobody is happy, not even the dog. They stop the car, and soon everyone is happy again.
node.esiss_activities: When you change the first letter of a word, you can make a new word. You can change "not" into "lot". What other "ot" words can you make? Change the first letter in "can" to make some new words.

node.content: Mum and the children are distracted from their picnic by farm animals. The birds take advantage of the situation and steal the picnic. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - The, Look, at, the, said, - Content words - Picnic, cow, sheep, goat, hen, horse, goose, turkey, picnic, - Repeated phrase - "Look at the," said Mum, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - h (hen, horse), g (goat, goose), - Punctuation - quotation marks, commas, full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark - Special Feature - Story line told in the illustrations, Skills and Strategies - The Picnic can be used to introduce and reinforce: - predicting the story line using illustrations from the book, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - innovating on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs, - identifying onset and rime within words, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 9six (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencil, markers, - rug, snack, - magnetic letters, - paper, markers. node.title: The Play
node.content: Children take different parts in a school play. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - The, We, were, the, in, a, - Content words - Play, trees, clouds, rats, hens, farmers, dogs, kids, play, - Repeated phrase - We, were, the, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - W (We), w (were); plural "s" on nouns, - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies - The Play can be used to introduce and reinforce: - monitoring reading and ensuring that it is making sense using meaning structural and visual information from the story, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - sharing own experiences related to the content of the story, - writing new words independently by hearing and recording the sounds in sequence, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 97 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, markers, pencil, - masks (these can be quickly made with a paper plate and string), - questions chart, - developmental art materials, - white glue, string, crayon. node.title: Here I Am
node.content: A family are ready to go out but before they leave they have to find one of the children. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - Here, I, Am, is, looking, for, me, here, am, - Content words - Mum, Dad, Adam, Jody, Kim, Sam, - Repeated phrase - is looking for me, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - M (Mum), m (me, Mum, Kim, Adam, Sam, am), D (Dad), d (Dad, Adam, Jody); word ending -ing (looking), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation marks - Special Feature - Same words used in different sentence structures on pages 14 and 1six, Skills and Strategies - Here I Am! can be used to introduce and reinforce: - encouraging children to read using expression to increase understanding, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - responding to simple questions about the story content, - writing using the conventions of capital letters and periods., Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 92 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, markers, pencil, - family photograph or picture, - word cards for "m" and "d" words, two large hoops, - magnetic letters, - old magazines, glue, scissors paper. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_activities: Everyone is looking for the girl. Where does Dad look? Where does Mum look? Write down three places that you would look.

node.title: My Friend

Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - My, I, with, is, my - Content words - Friend, read, Andy, write, draw, count, run, jump, sing, friend - Repeated phrase - I, with Andy Phonics and phonemic awareness - r (read, run), d Friend draw, Andy, read) Punctuation - full stops, capital letters Skills and Strategies My Friend can be used to introduce and reinforce: - developing an awareness of the directionality of the print on each page , - creating artwork or a piece of writing relating to the text, -writing using the conventions of letters and full stops, - sharing own experiences related to the content of the story Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 96 (cut and ready for use), copies of BLM, markers, glue, pencil, - paper, markers, pencil, - large paper, play dough. node.esiss_blurb: What do you do with your friends? This girl has a friend named Andrew. They do everything together.
node.esiss_activities: Andrew is a good friend. Who is a good friend for you?_x000D_ Think of four things you do with your friend._x000D_ Write each thing in a sentence like the one below._x000D_ I _______ with my friend.

node.title: Ruff and Me
node.content: A boy compares his facial features with his dog's. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - and, Me, Here, are, my, I, am, we, - Content words - Ruff, Ruff's, eyes, ears, teeth, - Repeated phrases - Here are my• Here are Ruff's, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - M (Me), m (my); plural "s" on nouns, - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, apostrophes, Skills and Strategies - Ruff and Me can be used to introduce and reinforce: - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - responding to simple questions about the story content, - creating artwork or a piece of writing related to the text, - writing labels for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 97 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencil, markers, - old magazines, scissors, glue, card, - markers and large paper or pavement chalk. node.esiss_blurb: I love my dog. His name is Ruff. We both have ears and eyes and a nose. But we don't look the same.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of yourself with your pet. If you don't have a pet, imagine you have a pet. Add labels for: • eyes • ears • teeth • nose.

node.title: Up the Tree
node.content: Cat gets stuck chasing a bird up a tree followed by the boy, girl, Dad, and Mum. They are all brought down by a firefighter but once they are down the bird and cat go up the tree again. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - Up, the, The, is, going, up, - Content words - Tree, bird, cat, boy, girl, Dad, Mum, firefighter, - Repeated phrase - The, is going up, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - g (going, girl); word ending -ing (going), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark - Special Features - Sequenced illustrations on page 15; Circular story structure, Skills and Strategies - Up the Tree can be used to introduce and reinforce: - retelling the sequence of the story using own words, - creating artwork or a piece of writing related to the text, - writing labels for a specific purpose, - recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 90 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencil, scissors, glue, - large piece of paper, paper strips, glue, stapler, - paint leaves for collage, glue, markers, scissors. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: The cat sees a bird and chases it up the tree. Everyone climbs the tree to save the cat. Who will save them all?
node.esiss_activities: Listen to the first sound of the words "Mum" and "Dad". What letter does "Mum" start with? What letter does "Dad" start with? What letter does your name start with?

node.title: We Get Squashed!

node.title: Let's Clean Up!
node.content: In this book, children are cleaning up at home and in their community. Clear photographs and a simple narrative reinforce the message that everybody can help to keep our environment clean. (Big idea: People need to keep planet Earth clean.) Features of Let's Clean Up • topic (keeping Earth clean), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support text • initial consonant blends - "cl-", "st-" • final consonant blend - "-lp" • digraph sounds - "ch" • compound word - everyone
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Taking care of planet Earth means helping to clean up from time to time. Read about the many places you can help to keep clean.
node.esiss_activities: With an adult, find a messy place in your home. Clean up the messy place together. Draw a picture that shows the messy place. Then draw a picture that shows the tidy place.

node.title: My Painting
node.content: A girl paints a picture of herself in her own community. node.content: A girl makes a sandcastle that is washed away when the tide comes in. node.title: Old and New
node.content: Two children assemble unwanted items for a garage sale and acquire some new friends. Features of the Book • Bold, easy to read text. • Repetitive and predictable sentence patterns. • The rhyming structure. • One sentence per page. • One word change per page with two on the last page. • The high-frequency words -"We" and "have." • The use of photographs. • The exclamation point on the last page. • The "ch" blend -"chairs." Purpose Old and New can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • pointing one-to-one as each word is read• • introducing and discussing new words • contained within the story• • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book• • writing labels for a specific purpose.

node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Determining author's purpose, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: These children are having a garage sale. Most things are old, but they find something new as well.
node.esiss_activities: Houses contain old things and new things. With a friend, play a game of I Spy. Choose the things that you can see inside. Which things are old? Which things are new?

node.title: Busy Bird
node.content: A busy mother bird builds her nest, hatches her eggs, and feeds her chicks. Features of the Book • Clear, evenly spaced, two-word text. • Consistent placement of text. • Playful illustrations to support text. • The high-frequency words -"Some" and "A." • The initial "b" consonant -"busy" and "bird." • The sequential nature of the visual story line. • The use of "s" to denote the plural form - "twigs," "eggs," "heads," and "chicks." • The use of the exclamation point on page 8. Purpose Busy Bird can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story• recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds• pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match• writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Forming opinions|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Forming opinions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: The bird in this book is very busy. She has a nest to build, some eggs to lay, and then what?
node.esiss_activities: This bird uses twigs and leaves to make a nest. If you were a bird, what would you use to make a nest? Draw a picture of the nest. Draw the baby birds inside the nest, too.
node.esiss_hfw: a, some
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Recognising Bias and Prejudice

node.title: I Can Read
node.content: I Can Read follows the experience of a small child as she enjoys reading to her family and friends. Features of the Book • The repetitive, predictable structure -"I can read to." • The change beginning on page 5 -the word "my." • The high-frequency words -"I," "to," and "my." • The hard "c" consonant -"can." • The change from "my" to "myself." • The passing of time shown by the girl's clothes. • The text in the illustrations. Purpose I Can Read can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context• recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds• hearing and recording the sounds in words sequentially• innovating on the story to create own versions of stories, plays, and songs.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The girl in this story loves to read. Who does she read to? Read the story and find out.
node.esiss_activities: This girl can read to all kinds of people. How many people, pets, or soft toys can you read to? Make a list of who will listen to you reading.
node.esiss_hfw: I, can, my

node.title: It's My Bread
node.content: While each new animal comes and claims ownership of the bread, the ants slowly and effectively carry the bread away, piece by piece. Features of the Book • The use of repetition. • The use of quotation marks. • The contraction "It's." • Supportive illustrations to predict unknown words. • Two-line text blocks requiring a return sweep. • The use of exclamation points. • The high-frequency words -"my," "said," and "the." Purpose It's My Bread can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • developing an awareness of the directionality of the print on each page • pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match • using knowledge of letters and sounds for • recognising simple consonant-vowel-consonant words illustrating a story to support the meaning of the text.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Summarising, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: A piece of bread is left on the ground. Is it the hen's bread? Is it the goat's bread? Who takes the bread while all the animals are arguing?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of each animal in the story. Make animal puppets by using glue to stick each animal onto a stick. Act out the play using your puppets.

node.title: My Chair
node.content: My Chair focuses on all of the things the main character can do in his wheelchair in the company of his friends. Features of the Book • Consistent placement of text. • The illustrations provide strong picture clues. • The range of simple verbs in a meaningful context. • The high-frequency words -"My" and "go." • The rhyme -"spin" and "win." • Clear, well-spaced text. • The ellipsis on page 7. Purpose My Chair can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: responding to simple questions about the story's content• recognising rhyming words• sharing ideas about the story with others in the group• writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.

node.title: Our Rocket
node.content: Reality meets fantasy as a group of children construct and "fly" a backyard rocket. Features of the Book •The word "Tuatara." •The repetitive text and close picture/text match. •Consistent position of text and illustrations. •Direct speech and quotation marks. •The sound of the letter "s" in "sat," "sun," and "said." •The high-frequency words -"and," "in" "said," and "the." •Well-known author. Purpose Old Tuatara can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context; encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding; predicting the story line using illustrations from the book; collecting information relating to the story to share with others.

node.content: A boy and his father prepare a habitat (called a "vivarium") for some snails. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - We, in, the, - Content words - Snails, soil, pebbles, rocks, leaves, grass, sticks, water, snails, - Repeated phrase - We put in the, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - p (put, pebbles), S (Snails), s (soil, sticks, grass, snails) W (We), w (water); plural "s" on content words, - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark - Special Feature - Procedural text, Skills and Strategies - Snails can be used to introduce and reinforce: - retelling the sequence of the story using own words, - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - writing labels for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 89 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, scissors, glue, markers, pencil, - snail, mirror, - play dough. node.title: Where Is Lunch?
node.content: Where Is Lunch? is a story of some little chimpanzees looking for their lunch, only to discover that the largest chimp is eating it. Features of the Book • The sequential numerals, supported by cumulative elements in the illustrations. • The repetitive elements of the main text. • One sentence covering two pages on pages 4 and 6. • The change from "Where is" to "There is " on the final page. • The large, bold text for the number of chimps. • Changing emphasis, shown in bold. • Consistent use of direct speech. • The question marks and exclamation point. • The high-frequency words - "is" and "my." Purpose Where Is Lunch? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: -monitoring the reading and ensuring it is making sense using meaning, structure, and visual information from the story• -encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding• -predicting the story line using illustrations from the book• -writing new words independently by hearing and recording the sounds in sequence.

node.esiss_activities: The chimpanzees all want their lunch. Write another ending to the story. Write about what happened to the lunch. Draw a picture.

node.title: My Family
node.content: A girl and her family clean their car together. Building Basic Skills -High-frequency words - My, This, is, my, and, me - Content words - Family, mum, dad, sister, brother, family - Repeated phrase - This is my Phonics and phonemic awareness - M (My), m (mom, me); word ending -er (sister, brother) Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies - My Family can be used to introduce and reinforce: - sharing own experiences related to the content of the story, - monitoring reading and ensuring that it is making sense using meaning, structural, and visual information from the story, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - collecting information related to the story and sharing it with others Materials - questions chart, copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 93 (cut and ready for use), copies of BLM, markers, pencil, - paper, - glue, crayons, stencils (To make stencils,write words in large letters on a piece of cardboard 3-4 inches tall. This will need to be prepared in time to dry and harden before the lesson. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.title: Thanksgiving
node.content: The animals each take something different for Thanksgiving dinner, but Turkey stays in bed! Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - will, the, in, - Content words - Thanksgiving, Bea,r honey, Rabbit, carrots, Rat, corn, Mouse, cheese, Squirrel, nuts, Skunk, pumpkin, Deer, apples, Turkey, bed, - Repeated phrase - will take the, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - R (Rabbit, Rat), c (carrots, corn), - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark - Special Feature - Background knowledge about Thanksgiving is necessary, Skills and Strategies - Thanksgiving can be used to introduce and reinforce: - sharing own experiences related to the content of the story, - rereading the text at difficulty and searching for meaning, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - writing a simple set of instructions, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 98 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - paper, pencils, markers, - play dough.
