node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Thanksgiving is a special celebration. Each animal brings something special to eat. What will Turkey bring?
node.esiss_activities: All the animals take something to the dinner. What would you take? Draw a picture of your food. Write the name of your food in the sentence below. I would take ____________ to the dinner.
node.esiss_hfw: will, the, in
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: What's Up?
node.content: What's Up? shows the air above us is bustling with living things and objects made by people. The book takes a close look at things that fly, and things that glide in the air around us. Features of the Book • The high-frequency words - "up," "A," "and," and "me." • The close picture/text match. • The repetition in the structure of the story. • Clear, well-placed text. • The contraction "What's." • The inital "b" consonant - "bird," "bat," and "bumble bee." • The "pl" blend - "plans." • The double vowels - "ee" and "oo." • The double consonants - "bubble" and "balloon." Purpose What's Up? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • responding to simple questions about the story's content • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • introducing and discussing new words contained within the story • collecting information related to the story to share with others.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Forming opinions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Forming opinions, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: If you look up, what can you see? Have you seen all the things in this book up in the sky?
node.esiss_activities: Lots of things go up in the air. Look through the book and make two groups. One group is for things that are living. The other group is for things that are not living. Write some other things that can go into each group.
node.esiss_hfw: up, a, and, me
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: "Who Took the Cake?"
node.content: Grandma bakes a cake for the family and it goes missing. No one knows who took it. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - the, Not, me, said, - Content words - Who, Took, Cake, took, cake, Dad, Mum, Lucy, Rose, Mark, Luke, Baby, - Repeated phrase - "Not me" said, - Phonics and phonemic awareness - M (Mum, Mark), m (me) L (Lucy, Luke) - Punctuation - quotation marks, question marks, commas, full stops, capital letters, exclamation marks - Special Feature - Punctuation associated with speech, Skills and Strategies - "Who Took the Cake?" can be used to introduce and reinforce: - reading using expression to increase understanding, - reading a variety of simple high-frequency words in context, - creating artwork or a piece of writing related to the text, - writing labels for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 98 (cut and ready for use), - copies of BLM, scissors, glue, markers, pencil, - paper, marker., For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Drawing conclusions|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Determining main ideas, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Someone has taken Grandma's chocolate cake. Dad did not take it. Mum did not take it. Who took the cake?
node.esiss_activities: Read this story to someone in your family. Make your voice sound interesting. Use a different voice for each character.
node.esiss_hfw: the, not, me, said
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: We Celebrate
node.content: Many different people live in my world.This simple text looks at some of the ways people celebrate. These include a birthday, Cinco de Mayo (Mexican-American heritage), Diwali (Indian/Nepalese heritage), Thanksgiving (American heritage), Hanukkah (Jewish heritage), Kwanzaa (African-American heritage), and New Year's Eve. Features of We Celebrate • the topic (celebrations), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • captions • photographs to convey information beyond the text • Word study: double medial sounds - different, Hanukkah • Short, repetitive sentence structure • Holiday names - Diwali, Hanukkah, New Year's Eve • Apostrophe - New Year's Eve • Exclamation mark on page 8 • Theme of light in the photos - candles, fireworks
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Forming opinions|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Activating prior knowledge, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: All around the world, people celebrate in many ways. This book shows six different ways that we celebrate special events.
node.esiss_activities: People celebrate many things. Talk to your family about how you celebrate together. Do you do anything like the people in the book? Draw a picture to show how you celebrate.
node.esiss_hfw: in, we
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Connections, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: I Can Write
node.content: This is a simple photographic story about small children writing in recognisable settings using a range of writing implements. Features of the Book • Bold, easy to read text. • Photographs to support the text. • Different settings for each picture. • Repetitive and predictable structure. • The high-frequency words - "I," "in," "the," and "on." • Silent "w" in "write." • Initial letter change from "in" to "on." • The compound word "anywhere." Purpose I Can Write can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • practicing pointing one-to-one as each word is read • reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context • introducing and discussing new words contained within the story • writing new words independently by hearing and recording the sounds in sequence.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: You can write on paper. You can write on the board. Where else can you write? Read this book to find out!
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book write in lots of ways. Think of three more ways to write. Draw a picture and write a sentence to show each way.
node.esiss_hfw: I , can , on, the, in
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELY1649 ACELY1650

node.title: I Can Draw
node.content: I Can Draw is the commentary given by a child as she draws each part of a bird. Features of the Book • The repetitive and predictable structure. • Clear letter spacing. • Photographs to support the text. • The change in the use of high-frequency words on pages 2 and 3 from "a" to "the." • The exclamation point on page 8. • The use of inset drawings and photographs to support the use of the labels. • The "dr" blend - "draw." • The hard "c" in "can." • The high-frequency words - "I," "a," and "the." Purpose I Can Draw can be used in a guided reading session to introduce the following skills and strategies: • reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: The girl in this book can draw a bird. Follow the steps and you can draw a bird, too.
node.esiss_activities: This book tells you how to draw a bird. Get a piece of paper and some pens. Draw your own bird using the instructions in this book. Can you draw another kind of animal? Write instructions for a friend to follow.
node.esiss_hfw: I, can, a, the

node.title: Off We Go!
node.content: Off We Go! provides a list of the requirements for a hiking trip. Features of the Book • The exclamation points in the title and on page 8. • Detailed illustrations that support the text. • The nouns that label the items. • The use of "s" in the plural form -"boots" and "friends." • Capital letters for each word in the title. • The high-frequency word "My." • Clear, well-spaced text with consistent placement. Purpose Off We Go! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: sharing own experiences related to the content of the story• recognising rhyming words• identifying onset and rime within words• recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds• writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Discussing point of view|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining cause and effect, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: When you're going on a long walk, it's best to check you have everything you need before you start.
node.esiss_activities: This book talks about the things you need if you are going on a long walk. Look at a map. Plan a walk for you and someone in your family. Draw a map to show where you would go.
node.esiss_hfw: my, we, go

node.title: All Kinds of Things
node.content: Rings and things and buttons and bows. In this book, seven children show us what they collect. Features of the Book • The use of nouns to label the collections. • The "ings" word ending - "rings" and "things." • The regular plural form using "s." • The blends - "th" and "sh." • Repetition of the words - "I collect." • The use of capital and lowercase letters as a title convention. • The high-frequency word "I." Purpose All Kinds of Things can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds • practising rereading own writing to an audience.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Comparing and contrasting|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Do you like to collect things? The children in this book each collect something special. What does the girl at the end of the book collect?
node.esiss_activities: What would you like to collect? Choose something and draw five of that kind of thing. Write a sentence to say why you want to collect it.

node.title: Shapes of the Land
node.content: The land has different shapes. This simple text explores the features of natural landscapes, such as a mountain, bay, and river. It provides opportunities to identify different shapes within the landscapes. Features of Shapes of the Land Opportunities for discussion using knowledge of different shapes • the topic (natural features of the land), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • picture glossary • familiar vocabulary (mountain, river, lake) and less familiar (canyon, bay)

node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making inferences, Sequencing, Making connections, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: When you look at the world around you, you can see a lot of different shapes. Look at the shapes in this book, and then take the test.
node.esiss_activities: Take a look at the last page of the book. How many shapes can you see? What makes each shape? Now look out the window. How many shapes can you see?

node.title: Look at Me
node.content: A pig dresses himself and then discovers that his colourful outfit has turned him into a rainbow! Visual sub-plots provide extra opportunities to stimulate the students' oral language. This book supports the comprehension strategies of using illustrations to understand text, Making connections with real-life experiences, forming hypotheses, and inferring. The high proportion of single-syllable words supports the development of one-to-one word matching. It also provides opportunities for students to practise cross-checking and recognition of high-frequency words. Text features • the high-frequency words - a, am, at, I, look, me, my, on • the variety of initial consonants • the one-line captions on pages 2 to 7 • the two text changes per page • the change in the text on page 8 which repeats the title of the book • the colour names • the visual sub-plots • the joyful ending • the exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making inferences|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Summarising, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Little Pig gets dressed in the morning. He puts on his brightest clothes. What does he look like when he is finished?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are going for a holiday in the snow. What clothes would you need to take? Write a list of what you would put in your bag.
node.esiss_hfw: a, am, at, I, look, me, my, on

node.title: I Can Open It
node.content: In this book, children show that there are many ways to open things. Vivid photographs show a range of everyday settings and objects, and the ending, though it breaks the repeated pattern, will be familiar to all children.

node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: There are all kinds of ways to open things. The children in this book will show you how!
node.esiss_activities: Look around your house. Find all the things that you can open. Think about how you open each thing. Now sort the things into groups.

node.title: Animals in Danger
node.content: People can help animals in danger. This simple text looks at animals that are in danger and need our help, such as whales, pandas, and manatees. (Big idea: People need to care for animals that are in danger.) Features of Animals in Danger • the topic (endangered animals), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • photographs that match text • picture glossary • initial consonants - bear, bird; tiger, turtle • initial consonant blends -"fr-", "wh-" • plural nouns - bears, birds, frogs, manatees, pandas, seals, tigers, turtles, whales • "er" word endings - danger, tiger • soft and hard "g" - danger, tiger • close look at visual discrimination of "d" (need) and "p" (help) • medial sound "ea" - seals
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Exploring text structure, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: The nine animals in this book are in danger. They all need our help to survive.
node.esiss_activities: Choose an animal that you would like to help. Make a badge that shows this animal. Write the name of the animal on the badge. Wear your badge and tell lots of people about it.

node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Keep Trying
node.content: This text is about achieving success through encouragement and practice. Dad supports his son by encouraging him to "keep trying", then the child returns the favour by helping Dad learn to skip. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of identifying the Determining main ideas, using prior knowledge, and Making connections with real-life experiences. It provides opportunities to practise cross-checking and to focus on irregular verbs. The repetitive structure provides support for less confi dent readers. Text features • the high-frequency words - and, can, do, I, it, said, you • the initial consonant blends - cl, sk, sw, tr • the contraction - can't • the irregular verb - could • the repetition in the text • the use of direct speech • the use of exclamation marks • the passage of time between the events of the left and the right pages • the safety equipment featured in the illustrations • the relationship between the boy and his father
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When something is hard, Dad tells me to keep trying. That's how you learn. Sometimes I tell Dad to keep trying too.
node.esiss_activities: At the end of the book, Dad tries something new. Draw a picture of Dad trying another thing that is new. Draw the boy beside him. Write a speech bubble for the boy. What will the boy be saying?
node.esiss_hfw: and, can, do, I, it, said, you
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning

node.title: Being a Scientist (Emergent)
node.content: What does a scientist do? Being a Scientist looks at what scientists do and the areas they study: plants and animals, space, what things are made from and how they change, and how things work. Features of Being a Scientist • general information about scientists (pages 2 to 7), followed by four double-page spreads about specific kinds of scientists • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text that supports and illustrates concepts • graph • initial consonant blends - "pl-","sp-" • digraphs - "st", "th", "wh", "wr" • silent "c" after "s" • Use of questions marks
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Scientists help us to understand the world. Find out about the skills a scientist needs to do a good job.
node.esiss_activities: Scientists ask questions about things in our world. Write three questions that you would like to ask a scientist. Make sure you use a question mark.
node.esiss_hfw: a, and, are, at, do, I, in, look, to, up
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Compare and Contrast

node.title: My Dad Makes Me Laugh
node.content: This story looks at the emotional value of laughter and how it can cheer up people when they are sad. A boy talks about the ways his dad makes him laugh, from telling jokes to singing funny songs. Near the end, the boy notices that his father is sad and realises he can make his dad feel better by making him laugh, too. Features of the Book - The link to emotional literacy - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion -feel, happy, laugh, sad - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends -th; short vowel sounds - dad/sad/that Purpose My Dad Makes Me Laugh introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making connections; - determining the author's purpose; - using texts as models for own writing.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Asking and answering questions|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Analysing themes, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Dad does lots of things to make the boy laugh. What does the boy do when his dad is sad?
node.esiss_activities: Dad makes the boy laugh. Write some sentences about how your dad makes you laugh. Then write how you make him happy when he is sad.
node.esiss_hfw: I, looks, make, me, my, plays, we

node.title: Discovering My World (Emergent)
node.content: What do you know about your world? Discovering My World looks at the shapes of the land, people finding their way, different people and places, and people getting along and helping one another. Features of Discovering My World • introduction of geography and civics concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about people and their place in the world (pages 2 to 7) followed by four double-page spreads about specific geographical and civic features • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support and illustrate concepts • captions

node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Drawing conclusions, Summarising, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: What do you know about the world around you? This book introduces you to the changing world and the things you can discover there.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of an important or interesting thing in our world. Write two sentences about it. Ask someone in your family what he or she finds interesting about our world.

node.title: Taking Care of Yourself (Emergent)
node.content: How do you take care of yourself? Taking Care of Yourself looks at how we keep healthy by keeping our teeth clean, eating healthy food, doing exercise, and keeping safe. Features of Taking Care of Yourself • general information about taking care of ourselves (pages 2-7), followed by four double-page spreads about specific things we can and should do to care for ourselves • personal statement that ends the book • photographs with text that supports and illustrates concepts • initial consonant blends - "cl", "pl" • preview question on the back cover
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: When you learn how to be healthy and safe, you're taking care of yourself. Check out how to eat a healthy diet and exercise safely, and how to deal with your feelings.
node.esiss_activities: These children can take care of themselves. Draw a picture showing one thing you do to look after yourself. Write a sentence about it.

node.title: How Does It Move?
node.content: This text describes how some vehicles, machines, and people move. It uses simple descriptive vocabulary such as "around and around", "back and forth", and "side to side". Captions and arrows help to guide the reader. (Big idea: Scientists look at how things move.) Features of How Does It Move? • the topic (how things move), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • arrows to show motion • question and answer format • picture glossary • initial blends - "sw-", "tr-" • word endings -"er"- roller coaster, under, over • digraph - "th", "wh" • opposite words - back/forth, under/over, up/down • prepositions - up, down, around, under, over
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Comparing and contrasting, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: A Ferris wheel goes around and around. How does a roller coaster move? How about a lift? Read about all kinds of moving things.
node.esiss_activities: The things in this book move in different ways. From the book, choose one way of moving. Make a list of four things that move in the same way.

node.title: Where Do Animals Live?
node.content: Scientists look at where animals and plants live. This simple text looks at the habitats of frogs, owls, rabbits, bats, and fish. (Big idea: Scientists study plants and animals to help us understand them.) Features of Where Do Animals Live? • the topic (habitats), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • opportunities to make predictions with the use of a question throughout the book • text supported by illustrations • matching of each animal to its habitat • glossary • repeated pairs of letters - rabbits, burrows, trees • plural nouns - bats, owls, frogs, rabbits • noun that is both singular and plural - fish • initial consonant blends - "wh-", "fr-", "tr-" • digraph - "th"
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Comparing and contrasting, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: You know that a fish lives in the sea, but what about an owl? Read about where animals live and then take the test.
node.esiss_activities: These animals live in interesting places. Choose another animal you know about. Draw the animal and show where it lives.

node.title: I Can See
node.content: This is a simple animal counting book, where the number of animals increases on each page. The final page breaks the pattern and provides a surprise.

node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: In this fun counting book, you'll get to meet a lot of animals. How many can you see?
node.esiss_activities: In this counting book there are lots of animals. How many zebras are there? How many puppies are there? How many animals can you see altogether? Don't count the bees!

node.title: I Can Find My Way
node.content: This book shows how a girl and her mum go to school. Using photographs and illustrations, the reader is invited to come along for the ride. (Big idea: People need help to find their way.) Features of I Can Find My Way • the topic (finding your way), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • step-by-step action photographs • initial consonant blends - "br-", "str-" • digraph sounds - "sch", "thr", "th" • medial sounds - "ee", "oo" • prepositions - across, around, down, into, out, over, past, through, up
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making inferences|Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making inferences, Sequencing, Making connections, Summarising, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: It's a long way to school, but this girl knows the way. Out, down, around, and up. And here we are!
node.esiss_activities: How do you get to school? Ask someone to help you draw a map of how you get there. Draw the interesting things you see on the way.
node.esiss_hfw: are, can, go, I, my, out, the, to, up, we
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Sequencing, Making Connections, Summarising

node.title: Traffic Jam
node.content: A boy talks to his mum about the different vehicles he can see in a traffic jam. Word Study - High-frequency words: I, can, see, a, in, the, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - T, t - Traffic, taxi, tow, traffic, truck, - - b - bike, bus, - - c - can, car, - - onsets and rimes - c/an, c/ar, - - compound word - motorcycle, , Skills and Strategies - Reading Traffic Jam will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify and read high-frequency words in context, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 122, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - photocopies of the pictures of each vehicle in the story, a long sheet of paper, crayons. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: All the traffic has stopped. The boy wonders why it isn't moving. He sees a bike, a car, and a bus. Then he sees the tow truck.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the traffic jam in this book. What is stuck in the jam? Why are they stuck? Make your own traffic jam with toy cars. Talk about where each car is in your traffic jam. Use words like "in front", "behind", "first", and "second".
node.esiss_hfw: I, can, see, a, in, the
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Making Inferences

node.title: The Wind Blows
node.content: A girl and her father observe the effect the wind has on a variety of outdoor activities. Word Study - High-frequency words: The, the, I, can, see, My, is, - Highlighting repeated phrases - The wind blows the ___ . I can see the ___ fly., - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonemic awareness: - - Th, th - The, the, - - b - balloon, blows, bubble, - - f - flag, fly, flying, - - onsets and rimes - c/an, h/at Skills and Strategies Reading The Wind Blows will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify onset and rime within new words, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 123, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - hand held fan, crayons, paper. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: It's a windy day. Lots of things are flying around. Hang onto your hat!
node.esiss_activities: In this story, the wind blows lots of things around. Think of two other things that the wind can blow around. Describe each thing in sentences like these. The wind blows the _______. I can see the _______ fly.
node.esiss_hfw: the, I, can, see, my, is

node.title: I Like That Horse
node.content: A group of children choose their favourite horse to ride on the merry-go-round. Word Study - High-frequency words: I, Like, That, like, that, It, has, is, a, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - I, i - I, It, is, - - h - has, horse, - - onsets and rimes - p/ink, th/at, - - plurals - spots, stripes, , - Use of capital and lowercase letters - I, i, Skills and Strategies - Reading I Like That Horse will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise sound/letter relationships, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - point one-to-one as each word is read to ensure an appropriate match, - prepare for independent writing using a variety of methods., Materials - copies of word and punctuation for this book from page 11, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - pieces of card, paint, string, scissors, a hoop shape. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The children all go to the fair. They want to ride on the merry-go-round. There are lots of horses with spots and one that has stripes. Is it really a horse?
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book ride on the merry-go-round. Which horse would you like to ride? Draw your own merry-go-round. Draw some animals that you would like to ride on. Put a different pattern on each animal.
node.esiss_hfw: I, like, that, it, has, is, a

node.title: Snowman Day
node.content: This story shows how children's activities are influenced by the weather conditions over a week. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - Day, a, day, We, went, to, - Punctuation - full stops, capital letters, exclamation mark - Phonics and phonemic awareness - S, s - Saturday, Snowman, Sunday, snowy, stayed, stormy, sunny - W, w - We, Wednesday, was, went, windy - onsets and rimes - d/ay, pl/ay Skills and Strategies: Snowman can be used to introduce and reinforce: - identify and read high-frequency or interest words, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - monitor reading and ensure that it is making sense, using meaning and structural and visual information from the story, - selection information from the story for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 118, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - newspaper weather pages, cards displaying days of the week and weather symbols, a cardboard box, a long strip of paper, scissors. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: You can play outside on a rainy day. You can play outside on a sunny day. Can you play outside on a snowy day?
node.esiss_activities: What day is it today? Write the name of the day. Write what the weather is like. Is it rainy or sunny? Is it windy? Write the names of the other days of the week. Make sure the first letter of each day has a capital letter. Every day when you get home, find the day on your list. Then write what the weather is like.
node.esiss_hfw: day, a, went, to
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Making Inferences

node.title: Dog on Holiday
node.content: The family dog goes on holiday and joins in with all the family fun. Word Study - High-frequency words: Dog, on, Mum, and, went, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - s - skating, surfing, swimming, - - sh - fishing, shopping, - - "ing" word ending - fishing, walking, shopping, skating, surfing, swimming Skills and Strategies Reading Dog on Holiday will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - hear and recall the sounds in words sequentially, - understand that the text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - locate information, - innovate on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 115, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - card, Popsicle sticks. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Dog goes on holiday with Mum and Jill. He likes to go everywhere with them. But do Mum and Jill like to go everywhere with Dog?
node.esiss_activities: You can change the word "fish" into "fishing". You do this by adding the letters "ing". Write "fish" on a piece of paper. Use a different colour to add "ing". Now write "walk". Use a different colour to add "ing". Write more words that end in "ing".
node.esiss_hfw: dog, on, mum, and, went

node.title: Bear Wakes Up
node.content: Bear is fast asleep while the other animals are busy playing then some bees wake him up. Word Study - High-frequency words: Up, Was, was, when, went, to, play, the, - Punctuation: full stops, question mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: W, w - Wakes, Was, was, went, when; - "ing" word ending - sleeping, - Use of capital letters for animal names, for example, Bear, Mouse, Rabbit, Skills and Strategies - Reading Bear Wakes Up will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify high-frequency or interest words, - understand that the text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - prepare for independent writing using a variety of methods, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 124, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - samples of honey. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: The animals go out to play but Bear is still asleep. Why is Bear waiting for the bees to go out to play?
node.esiss_activities: The words "sleeping" and "playing" end with "ing". Add "ing" to the end of the word "buzz". What does the word say now? Add "ing" to the end of the word "eat". What does the word say now? Write the other words you know that end in "ing".

node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising

node.title: The Party
node.content: A small boy tries to help his family prepare for a birthday party. Word Study - High-frequency words: The, Can, you, me, said, Yes, I, can, not, Dad, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, commas, quotation marks, question marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - Y, y - Yes, you, - - M, m - Max, me, - - onsets and rimes - c/an, d/ad, Skills and Strategies Reading The Party will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise simple consonant-vowel-consonant words, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - demonstrate an understanding of how print is organised and read, - innovate on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 130, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - a packet of party balloons. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: It's Mum's birthday party, and Max wants to help. He can help set the table. He can not help blow up the balloons. Can he help blow out the candles?
node.esiss_activities: We write question marks at the end of questions. Which of the sentences below need a question mark? • Can you help me • Yes I can • Where are you • Can you come to my party Put the question mark in the right place.

node.title: Little Ducklings
node.content: The little ducklings each set off down the track until Mother Duck calls them back. node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Summarising, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: Where are all the ducklings? They have gone down the track. How does Mother Duck get them all back?
node.esiss_activities: How many ducklings are in the story? If one duckling was lost, how many would be left? If three ducklings went swimming, how many would be left?

node.title: That Cat
node.content: A poem about a cat who likes to play with lots of things around the house before he settles down to eat. Word Study - High-frequency words: That, Cat, cat, likes, to, play, with, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - C, c - Cat, cat, - - Th, th - That, with, - - p - paper, play, - - onsets and rimes - b/ats, c/at, h/ats, th/at, - - plurals - hats, shoes, toys, , Skills and Strategies - Reading That Cat will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise rhyming words, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 131, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - paper, craft materials, an old hat, a soft toy, cat ,or a photograph of a real one. node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Making predictions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: This is a poem about a cat that likes to play. It plays with shoes, and it plays with hats. Does it play at dinner time?
node.esiss_activities: This cat likes to play with all kinds of things. Do you know someone who has a pet? What does it like to play with? Make a toy for the pet to play with.

node.title: Looking Down
node.content: A boy talks about the things he can see as he is looking down on the city from a balcony. Word Study - High-frequency words: Down, I, can, see, some, down, up, here, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - s - see, some, streets, shops - - c - can, cars, - - D, d - Down, dogs, down, - - th - there, - - onsets and rimes - c/an, w/ay, - - plurals - birds, cars, dogs, shops, streets, trees, , Skills and Strategies - Reading Looking Down will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - use knowledge of onset and rime to create new words, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - locate information, - write labels or captions for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 138, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, markers, - a large piece of paper, toy, cars, toy people, toy buildings, magazines, pictures of buildings, cars and people, photocopies of pictures of the children in the class. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: I live in a tall building. When I look down I can see lots of things. But what can I see up here?
node.esiss_activities: The boy can see lots of things when he looks down. What can you see when you look out the window? Draw a picture of the things you see. Write a label for each thing.

node.title: Edmond Went Splash!
node.content: Several animals go down a water slide with some enjoying the experience more than others. Word Study - High-frequency words: Went, went, down, the, My, said, - Punctuation: full stops, quotation marks, commas, exclamation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - M - Monkey, My, - - S s - Snake, Splash, said, slide, - - T t - Tiger, the, turn, - - onsets and rimes - d/own, sl/ide, w/ent, , - Use of capital letters for animal names, for example, Giraffe, Parrot, Tiger, Skills and Strategies - Reading Edmond Went Splash! will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - use knowledge of onset and rime to create new words, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - innovate on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 133, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - crayons, paper. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using graphic sources, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: The animals are having fun on the water slide. The all have a turn. What happens when Edmond the elephant slides down?
node.esiss_activities: Edmond made a big splash. Imagine you went down the slide next. Write your name in the sentence below. Edmond went down the slide. "My turn," said _________.

node.title: Hide-and-seek
node.content: A horse plays hide-and-seek with his farm animal friends. Word Study - High-frequency words: and, said, is, that, away, to, dog, cat, Here, I, come, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, commas, quotation marks, question mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - C, c - Cat, Cow, come, - - H, h - Here, Horse, hide, - - S, s - Seven, Sheep, Six, said, - - T, t - Three, Two, to, - - onsets and rimes - r/an, aw/ay, c/at, h/ide, , Skills and Strategies - Reading Hide-and-seek will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - discuss new words contained within the story, - understand that the text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense, - select information from the story for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 13six, - copies of BLM pencils scissors glue, - a selection of toy animals. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: It is fun to play hide-and-seek. Horse counts and the animals run away. Mouse finds the best place to hide.
node.esiss_activities: The animals played hide-and-seek. Play hide-and-seek with your family. Find the best hiding places in your house. When you are counting, count backwards from twenty.

node.title: Look at This Mess!
node.content: A story about two children trying to clear away their mess without success until Mum comes to help. Word Study - High-frequency words: Look, Mum, They, This, all, at, away, the, this, up, said, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation marks, quotation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - M, m - Mess, Mum, mess, - - Th, th - They, This, the, this, - - a - all, animals, at, away, - - b - blocks, books, - - c - cars, clothes, - - plurals - animals, blocks, books, cars, clothes, toys, - - onset and rime - a/way, , Skills and Strategies Reading Look at This Mess! will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - hear and recall the sounds in words sequentially, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - monitor reading and ensure that it is making sense using meaning and structural and visual information from the story, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 137, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - a selection of dress up clothes and toys, a small box or case, magnetic letters play dough, glue. node.esiss_blurb: There are blocks on the floor. There are toys on the floor. There are clothes on the floor. Can the children tidy up the mess?
node.esiss_activities: Mum says, "Look at this mess!" The exclamation mark tells you that Mum said this loudly. Which of these sentences need exclamation marks: I have a pet cat Help, I'm lost They put away the toys Look out for the car Write the exclamation marks.

node.title: Lunchtime at the Zoo
node.content: A boy and his mother visit the zoo with a picnic lunch for both themselves and the animals. However they are not allowed to feed the animals. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_activities: Draw a map of the zoo. Show where each animal lives. Draw the food that the zookeeper would feed to each animal. Write a sign that says, "Do not feed the animals."

node.title: Poor Puppy!
node.content: Puppy runs away with various items from around the house including a very prickly rose bush! Word Study - High-frequency words: The, away, with, the, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - P, p - Poor, Puppy, puppy, - - Th, th - The, the, with, - - onsets and rimes - tr/uck, d/uck; h/at, m/at, , Skills and Strategies - Reading Poor Puppy will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - use knowledge of onset and rime to create new words, - understand that the text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - read stories and rhymes independently, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 139, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - a large sheet of paper, smaller sheets of paper, crayons, dye, black waterproof markers. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Making judgments|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Making judgments, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: This is a poem. Puppy likes to run away with things. He takes the toys and the mat. Why does he say, "Ouch?"
node.esiss_activities: "Hose" and "nose" are rhyming words. Write two more lines for this poem about the puppy. Make the last word on each line a rhyming word. The puppy ran away with the __________. The puppy ran away with the __________.

node.title: Follow the Leader
node.content: Three children follow another child as he moves over playground equipment in different ways. Word Study - High-frequency words: the, When, went, up, they, all, down, in, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, commas, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - J - Joe, - - W, w - When, went, - - d - down, - - onsets and rimes - ar/ound w/ent Skills and Strategies - Reading Follow the Leader will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify and read high-frequency words in context, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - demonstrate an understanding of how print is organised and read, - write labels or captions for a specific purpose Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 135, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - paper, crayons. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: It's fun to play follow the leader. But be careful. What happens when the leader stops?
node.esiss_activities: Play "Simon Says" with someone at home. Say things like: • Simon says go in. • Simon says go under. • Simon says go over. • Simon says go down.

node.title: Stop That Noise!
node.content: Here is a family that really likes to sing. The two young children join in to sing with Mum, Dad, baby, and their grandparents. But when the family dog decides to show its talent ... Features of the Book • Clear, consistently placed text blocks. • A familiar setting. • The close picture/text match. • The names -"Mom," "Dad," "Grandma," and "Granddad." • The capital letters for the characters' names. • The repeated phrase -"When _____ sings, we sing too." • The quotation marks and exclamation point. • The "ing" letter cluster -"sing" and "sings." • The double "oo" vowel -"too." • The high-frequency words -"we" and "that." Purpose Stop That Noise! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: -predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story• -hearing the individual sounds in words• -encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding• -writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using graphic sources, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Everyone in this family likes to sing. But what happens when the dog wants to join in?
node.esiss_activities: This family loves to sing together. Choose a song for your family to sing. Maybe you could make some instruments, such as a shaker or a rubber band guitar and play along.
node.esiss_hfw: we, that
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice

node.title: Lights at Night
node.content: Clear photographs draw children's attention to the variety and necessity of night light. Features of the Book • Consistent placement of text. • Every sentence begins with the same word. • The "sh" blend - "shop." • The high-frequency words - "on," "in," "the," and "a." • The change from "on" to "in" on page 4. • The rhyme - "shop" and "stop." • The "Light" and "night" rhyme with a single consonant change. • The challenge of two lines of text on page 8. Purpose Lights at Night can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • hearing and recording the sounds in words sequentially • recognizing rhyming words • rereading the text at difficulty and searching for meaning • practicing writing using rhyme and rhythm.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Activating prior knowledge, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: When night comes, the lights go on. This book shows the many kinds of lights you can see after dark.
node.esiss_activities: We need lights to see at night. Why do you and your family use lights at night? Make a list. Draw each kind of light.
node.esiss_hfw: on, in, the, a
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Connections

node.title: Pedal Power
node.content: Pedal Power provides a close-up look at the mechanics of a bicycle. Features of the Book • Simple, explanatory text. • The repetition - "Around and around." • Consistent placement of text. • The text presented in two-line blocks requiring a left-to-right sweep and return. • The bisyllabic word "around," making one-to-one pointing more challenging. • Singular and plural nouns, with a corresponding change in the verb. • The high-frequency words - "and," "the," and "go." • The blends - "ch" and "wh." • The close-ups in the illustrations. Purpose Pedal Power can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • hearing and recording the sounds in words sequentially • monitoring the reading and ensuring it is making sense using meaning, structure, and • visual information from the story practicing writing using some poetic language.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: On a bike, there are a lot of things that go around and around. Find out who is riding the unusual bike in this story.
node.esiss_activities: This book describes how you make a bike move. What are the important parts of a bike? Draw a diagram to show how the parts work together.
node.esiss_hfw: and, the, go

node.title: We Dance
node.content: This story shows children from different countries dancing in different settings. Features of the Book • The clear sequence of steps for the children to identify and follow. • Photographs to support the text. • Clear, large print. • The high-frequency word "The." • Initial "b" consonant - "beach," "box," and "boat." • Long and short vowel - "box" and "boat." • Labeled diagram in the back of the book. Purpose The Boat can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • responding to simple questions about the story's content • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • writing labels for a specific purpose recording some simple directions.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Exploring writer's craft|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Comparing and contrasting, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Do you like to dance? This book shows people from all around the world. Some like to dance on ice. Some like to dance with fans. Some even like to dance in the dark.
node.esiss_activities: The children at the fair are wearing special costumes. Have you ever worn a costume? Draw a picture of it. Write a sentence to say what you were doing.

node.title: Time for a Change
node.content: Time for a Change explores changes in nature and the way that living things make transitions. Features of the Book • The predictable position and pattern of the text. • Two words change on pages 2, 4, and 6 to reflect the content in the illustrations. • The way the title is repeated on each new spread. • The high-frequency words - "for," "to," "And," "a," and "me." • Large, bold print for effect. • The way the illustrations blend in the middle of each spread. Purpose Time for a Change can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing ideas about the story with others in the group• • pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match• • collecting information relating to the story to share with others.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Things around us change all the time. What happens when it gets wet or when snow falls? How do you change?
node.esiss_activities: This book shows how things change. It shows how summer is different from winter. What is your favourite season? Draw a picture of your favourite season. Write a sentence to say why.
node.esiss_hfw: for, to, and, a, me

node.title: Who Is the Robot?
node.content: Who Is The Robot? is like a guessing game. Each new spread gives clues as to who the robot might be. Features of the Book • Repetition in pattern and position of text - "It has" and "Just like me!" • Illustrations closely aligned to text. • Clear, bold text. • The twist at the end of the story. • The high-frequency words - "It," "like," and "me." Purpose Who is the Robot? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the meanings of new or familiar words from the context of the story • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Following instructions|Making judgments|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Following instructions, Visualising, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: The robot in this story is just like the boy. To find out why, read to the end of the book.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of the robot. Label the different parts. How many of each part can you see?
node.esiss_hfw: it, like, me

node.title: What Plants Need
node.content: "What Plants Need is a short poem that focuses on how plants need the same things to survive. The idea is to tell students that, although the variety of plants is great, they all have certain things in common. In most cases, a trunk or stem holds leaves up, roots hold the plant firmly in the soil, and seeds are protected until they are ready for dispersal. All plants need light, warmth, nutrients, and water to grow. Features of the Text - Assonance (repetition of vowel sounds) - strong, along; light, right - The very regular and repeated rhythm, which accentuates the rhyme. Purpose of the Book The book draws students' attention to the functions of the various parts of a plant, which perform the same functions in all plants, despite their different sizes and shapes. Although plants vary a lot, they all need light, warmth, nutrients, and water."
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: This book will tell you about the different parts of plants, and why each part is important to help the plant stay alive.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of a plant. Now read the book again. Find the things that the plant needs to grow. Add these to your picture.
node.esiss_hfw: to, be, make, of, up
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas

node.title: What's in the Tin?
node.content: One after another, family members help themselves to a chocolate brownie from the tin. When Dad arrives home, only crumbs are left. This is a cheerful family story that will be familiar to many children. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of using illustrations to make predictions, noting details in illustrations, and Summarising the Determining main ideas. It provides many opportunities to practise cross-checking and to focus on regular and irregular past-tense verbs. The repetitive structure provides support for less confident readers. Text features • the high-frequency words - and, in, the • the initial consonant blends - cr, sn • the digraph -ch - chocolate • the rime -um - Mum, yum, crumbs • the contraction - what's • the use of "asked" rather than "said" • the use of a question as the title • the repetition in the text • the use of exclamation marks • the use of direct speech • the children's names
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: What is in the tin? Everyone looks in the tin. What does Dad find when he gets home?
node.esiss_activities: The word "crumbs" starts with the blend "cr". What other words start with "cr"? Write a list of "cr" words.
node.esiss_hfw: and, in, the

node.title: Training Ruby
node.content: In this text, a boy proudly demonstrates how well trained his dog is until temptation proves too strong, and the dog runs away with some sausages. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of Making connections to real-life experiences, using illustrations to understand the text, analysing and Synthesising, and evaluating. It provides many opportunities to practise cross-checking and to focus on regular and irregular past-tense verbs. The repetitive structure provides support for less confident readers. Text features • the high-frequency words - and, come, good, I, said • the rimes in "jump", "sat", "sit" • the initial consonant blend -st - stay, stayed • the verbs ending in -ed - jumped, stayed, walked • the irregular past-tense verbs - ate, came, sat • the use of imperative verbs • the use of speech marks • the opportunities for expressive reading • the repetitive structure • the relationship between the boy and his dog • the story line carried by the illustrations • the humorous ending
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Ruby is a good dog. If you tell her to sit, she sits. If you tell her to stay, she stays. But be careful when you tell her to eat!
node.esiss_activities: We can change the middle letter of a word to make new words. Which letter changed in "sit" and "sat"? Which letter changed in "pot" and "pat"? Write four words that you can change into other words like this.
node.esiss_hfw: and, come, good, I, said
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect

node.title: My Balloon Man
node.content: A girl makes a balloon man using craft paper cardboard and a balloon. Word Study - High-frequency words: a, at, big, has, little, Look, my, My, He, on, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - M, m - My, man, mouth, my, - - b - balloon, big, body, - - h - hands, has, he, his, - - l - lands, look, - - onsets and rimes - h/ands, m/an, , Skills and Strategies - Reading My Balloon Man will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify and read high-frequency words in context, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - use visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense, - select information from the story for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 128, - copies of BLM, markers, pencils, - balloons, craft paper, cardboard, glue or tape. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: A boy helps his sister to make a balloon man. When they throw it up into the air, something funny happens.
node.esiss_activities: Make your own balloon man with big feet. You will need: • some glue and scissors • some cardboard and a balloon. Does your balloon man land on his feet?

node.title: My New Truck
node.content: A father and son work together to build a toy truck, using a variety of equipment. Word Study - High-frequency words: I, My, a, dad, has, - Punctuation: full stops, commas, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonetic awareness: - - h - hammer, has, have, - - s - saw, screwdriver, some, - - p - paintbrush, pencil, - - compound words - paintbrush, screwdriver, , Skills and Strategies - Reading My New Truck will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - discuss new words contained within the story, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - demonstrate an understanding of how print is organised and read, - write labels or captions for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 117, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - a variety of cardboard boxes craft materials a selection of tools, e.g. hammer, paintbrush, screwdriver. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.title: The Toys' Picnic
node.content: A girl enjoys a picnic with her toys choosing food she likes from their plates. Word Study - High-frequency words: That, The, I, said, to, This, Mum, me, looks, - Punctuation: full stops, commas, quotation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - C, c - Clown, carrot, cookie, - - Th - That, The, This, , - Personal pronouns, for example, Clown, Mum, Skills and Strategies - Reading The Toys' Picnic will reinforce The following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise sound/letter relationships, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - develop an awareness of the directionality of the print on the page, - write labels or captions for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 121, - copies of BLM, pencils, scissors, glue, - plastic/paper cups and plates, coloured paper, scissors, glue. node.title: Too Big for Me!
node.content: A mother and her daughter sort out some clothes and find that they need to be passed on to the younger daughter Kate. Word Study - High-frequency words: Big, My, are, big, can, for, is, it, said, me, Me, Mum, - Punctuation: full stops, commas, quotation marks, exclamation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - M - Mum, My, - - s - said, shoes, skirt, small, sweatshirt - - Th, th - These, them, - Use of capital letters for names - Kate, Mum, Skills and Strategies - Reading Too Big for Me! will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise sound/letter relationships, - emphasise left-to-right movement and reinforce directionality, - point one-to-one as each word is read to ensure an appropriate match, - prepare for independent writing using a variety of methods, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 120, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - a collection of different sized children's clothing (hat, coat, sweatshirt, shoes) For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: I am getting big and my clothes are too small. Mum gives them to my little sister. Will they fit her?

node.esiss_blurb: You can cut open an apple. You can cut open a grape. You can cut open a kiwifruit. What do you find inside? Little seeds!
node.esiss_activities: Get a piece of fruit. Ask someone to help you cut the fruit in half. How many pieces are there? How many seeds can you see? Draw the fruit and the seeds.

node.title: Is It an Insect?
node.content: Many creatures are insects and have six legs, but watch out! Some creatures that look like insects aren't insects at all! Word Study - High-frequency words: Is, It, Yes, A, No, is, has, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation marks, question marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - I, i - Insect, Is, It, insect, is, - - A, a - A, an, ant, - - onset and rime - n/ot, - - compound words - dragonfly, ladybird, butterfly, , Skills and Strategies Reading Is It an Insect? will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - discuss new words contained within the story, - understand that the text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - demonstrate an understanding of how print is organised and read, - select information from the story for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 125, - copies of BLM, pencils, - a large sheet of paper, photocopies of the insects from the book, construction card, pape,r scissors, glue, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Science and technology
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining main ideas|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Is a fly an insect? Yes it is. Is a spider an insect? No it is not. How can you tell?
node.esiss_activities: Look in your garden for some creepy-crawlies. How many legs does each one have? Draw your favourite creepy-crawly. Count the legs. Is it an insect?
node.esiss_hfw: is, it, a, yes, has, no
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion

node.title: Playing
node.content: Two children play together co-operatively with various toys. Word Study - High-frequency words: We, like, to, play, with, the, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - d - dinosaur, doll's house, dough, drums, - - p - play, puzzle, - - plurals - blocks, cars, dinosaurs, drums, - - onsets and rimes - b/lock, c/ars Skills and Strategies - Reading Playing will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify onset and rime within new words, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - monitor reading to ensure that it is making sense, using meaning and structural and visual information from the story, - use text as a model for writing Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 130, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - a camera, a variety of toys - blocks, cars, dinosaurs etc. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: It's fun to play with the blocks. It's fun to play with the cars. But best of all it's fun to play together.
node.esiss_activities: Do you have a friend that you play with? What do you like to play? Think of three things. Write three sentences. We like to play with the ________.

node.title: Stew for Dinner
node.content: A girl and her grandfather make a stew using vegetables and meat. Word Study - High-frequency words: All, for, Some, the, went, We, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - p - peas, pepper, pot, potato, - - S s - Stew, Some, salt stew - - - th - the, - onsets and rimes - p/ot, - - compound word - into, , Skills and Strategies Reading Stew for Dinner will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify high-frequency or interest words, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - monitor reading and ensure that it is making sense using meaning and structural and visual information from the story, - prepare for independent writing using a variety of methods, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 134, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - paper, scissors, glue, examples of some of the vegetables described in the story. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: The vegetables go into the pot. The meat goes into the pot. Soon, there is stew for dinner. Yum!
node.esiss_activities: The girl and her grandad make some stew. They put eight things into the pot. Write a shopping list for making the stew. Read it to someone in your family.

node.title: Look at My Weaving
node.content: A child demonstrates the process of paper weaving. Word Study - High-frequency words: Look, at, My, I, some, It, and, my, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - b - black, blue, - - g - goes, green, - - p - paper, purple, - - I - I, It, - - onsets and rimes - bl/ack, , - Use of capital and lowercase letters - I, i, Skills and Strategies Reading Look at My Weaving will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - recognise sound/letter relationships, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context of the story to make predictions, - innovate on the story to create own versions of stories plays and songs, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 137, - copies of BLM pencils markers, - coloured paper, a camera, examples of a woven mat or basket, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: Weaving is fun. You go under and over, under and over. Soon, you have a pattern.
node.esiss_activities: Can you see how the boy does his weaving? Follow the steps in the book to make your own pattern. Use lots of colours. Remember to go over and under.

node.title: My School Bag
node.content: A boy shows a variety of things he takes to school in his school bag. Word Study - High-frequency words: My, my, I, some, When, when, go, to, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation mark, comma, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - s - school, some, sweatshirt - - b - bag, books, - - p - paper, pencils, - - plural "s" - books, markers, pencils, - - onsets and rimes - b/ooks, c/ap, , Skills and Strategies Reading My School Bag will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - use knowledge of onset and rime to create new words, - understand that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - use the context for the story to make predictions, - write labels or captions for a specific purpose, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 138, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - examples of school bags from the group, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: The boy is getting ready for school. He takes his paper. He takes his pencil. He takes his apple. What does he carry them in?
node.esiss_activities: What do you put in your school bag? Draw the boy's school bag and colour it in. Now write a list of the things the boy put in his bag.

node.title: Buttons
node.content: Children talk about buttons of various colours and shapes. Word Study - High-frequency words: All, my, are, some, of, all, - Punctuation: full stops, exclamation marks, capital letters, - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - A, a - All and are, - - B, b - Buttons, black, blue, buttons , - - S, s - Some, squares, stars, - - bl - black, blue, , Skills and Strategies Reading Buttons will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - discuss new words contained within the story, - emphasise left-to-right movement to reinforce directionality, - locate information, - use text as a model for writing, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 132, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - card, material, a selection of button,s scissors, glue, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: Buttons come in all shapes and sizes. They come in all kinds of colours too. Can you say the shape and colour of these buttons?
node.esiss_activities: Ask if you have any buttons at home._x000D_ Sort the buttons into colours._x000D_ Now sort the buttons into shapes._x000D_ How many groups can you make?

node.title: A Fish Picture
node.content: A boy explains what he uses to make a fish picture. node.esiss_blurb: This boy can make a picture. He puts on string. He puts on glitter. What is the last thing he puts on his picture?
node.esiss_activities: Find some things that you can use to make a picture. Make your picture. Write your name on it.

node.title: Toast
node.content: Eight children talk about the toast toppings they like best. Word Study - High-frequency words: I, on, my, like, it, that, this, - Punctuation: capital letters, full stops, exclamation marks - Phonics and phonemic awareness: - - b - banana, butter, - - h - have, honey, - - l - like, - - t - toast, tomato, - - onset and rime - th/at, , Skills and Strategies Reading Toast will reinforce the following objectives and give the children the opportunity to: - identify and read high-frequency or interest words, - understands that text must make sense and use cues to monitor this, - point one-to-one as each word is read to ensure an appropriate match, - prepare for independent writing using a variety of methods, Materials - copies of words and punctuation for this book from page 119, - copies of BLM, pencils, markers, - a toaster, bread, toppings, plates, knives, spoons, napkins For more information please see our Lesson Plan.

node.esiss_blurb: Some people like eggs on toast. Some people like jam on toast. Some people like banana on toast. Some people like everything on toast.
node.esiss_activities: What do you like to eat on toast? Ask each member of your family what they like on their toast. Draw a picture of each person's toast. Write a sentence for each one. • I like ________ on my toast. • Mum likes ________ on her toast. • My brother likes ________ on his toast.

node.title: In the Dark Forest
node.content: Deep in an enchanting "3-D" forest, a bevy of creepy crawlies cavort by the light of the moon untill danger lurks ... Features of the Book • Clearly structured beginning, middle, and end. • The high-frequency words -"It," "is," "in," and "the." • The double vowels -"ee" and "oo." • The clear text in two-line blocks encourages a return sweep. • Consistent placement of text, with a change at the climax on page 7. • The use of repetition • The clay-modelling technique the illustrator has used. Purpose In the Dark Forest can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context • sharing ideas about the story with others in the group • hearing the individual sounds in words• innovating on the story to create own versions of stories, plays, and songs.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure|Making judgments
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making judgments, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: When it gets dark in the forest, all kinds of animals come out. What happens when owl wakes up?
node.esiss_activities: When it's dark, lots of animals come out. What animals or insects can you see outside when it's dark? Draw a picture of them coming out from their hiding places.
node.esiss_hfw: it, is, in, the
