node.title: Make It Spin
node.content: In this story, a child makes a simple paper helicopter. The clear procedural text and accompanying diagram for the project will enable the readers to create their own helicopters. Features of the Book • The clear sequence of steps in the text. • The close picture/text match. • Well-spaced text in a consistent position on the page. • The high-frequency words - "it," "up," "go," and "a." • The exclamation point on the final page. • The word endings - "in" and "ip." • The rhyme - "hold" and "fold." • The apostrophe in the contraction "it's." • The simple, labeled diagram to help build skills in content reading. Purpose Make It Spin can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • introducing and discussing new words contained within the story • retelling the sequence of the story using own words • writing a simple set of instructions.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making connections, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: You can make your own tiny paper helicopter. Follow the steps in this book to make your helicopter spin.
node.esiss_activities: This book tells you how to make a paper helicopter. Make your own helicopter. Follow the instructions. Use different kinds of paper. Which kind of paper works best?

node.title: Helping Planet Earth (Emergent)
node.content: Who can help? Helping Planet Earth looks at how we can help planet Earth by using resources carefully, caring for animals in danger, keeping it clean, and reusing things. Features of Helping Planet Earth • introduction of environmental concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about environmental issues (pages 2 to 7) followed by four double-page spreads about specific issues and suggested solutions • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text that supports and illustrates concepts • captions initial consonant blends - "pl-","cl-" • digraph - "th"
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Drawing conclusions|Forming opinions|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Forming opinions, Drawing conclusions, Determining cause and effect, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: One day, the resources on planet Earth may run out. Find out how you can help to take care of Earth's air, water, forests, and animals.
node.esiss_activities: There are many different ways we can help planet Earth. Make a poster to remind people of one way that they can help our planet. Look through the book for some ideas.
node.esiss_hfw: and, can, do, I, in, to, you
node.wushka_comprehension: Sequencing, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Summarising

node.title: Open It!
node.content: Two children demonstrate a variety of ways to open things. Features of the Book • The strong visual cues in the photographs. • The high-frequency words - "it," "the," and "to." • The use of challenging verbs to describe opening action. • The identical format of text and photographs in the first three spreads. • The rhyme and rhythm in the text - "rings" and "things." Purpose Open It! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • monitoring the reading and ensuring it is • making sense using meaning, structure, and visual information from the story • responding to simple questions about the story's content • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Exploring text structure, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: How do you open a jar or a box? How about a can? The children in this book know how to open all kinds of things.
node.esiss_activities: Look around your house and find all the things that you can open. Put them in groups. Write a sentence to explain how to open the things in each group.
node.esiss_hfw: it, the, to

node.title: Leaves, Fruits, Seeds, and Roots
node.content: The detailed illustrations in this book show a grandfather and his granddaughter working in a vegetable garden. Each spread focuses on one of four words in the title. Features of the Book • One word change per spread until page 9. • The rhyme - "fruits" and "roots." • The blends - "pl" and "fr." • The double vowels - "oo" and "ee." • The high-frequency word "A." • Clear, bold text, consistently placed. • Colorful, double-paged illustrations. Purpose Leaves, Fruits, Seeds, and Roots can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • responding to simple questions about the story's content • collecting information relating to the story to share with others.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Making inferences|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Making inferences, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: There are lots of different plants in this garden. How many of them can you put into a salad?
node.esiss_activities: Write down the headings - leaves, fruits, seeds, and roots. Read the book again. There are different vegetables and fruits in the pictures. Write their names under the correct heading. Can you add any other names to the groups?

node.title: Things That Help Me
node.content: This book takes a look at the simple technology that children use every day at school. Each common tool is named, and the sound it makes is emphasised by the creative use of typography. Features of the Book • The elements of rhythm and rhyme. • The blends - "sn," "ch," and "cl." • The high-frequency word "My." • The repetitive element in the text. • Strong photographs provide visual clues to meaning. • The use of creative typography as a support. • Clear, bold text, consistently placed. Purpose Things That Help Me can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the story line using illustrations from the book • identifying onset and rime within words • recognizing the relationship between letters and their sounds • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining importance|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Determining importance, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: What kinds of things help this girl with her project? And what has she made at the end of the book?
node.esiss_activities: What does the girl use to make her book? Can you make a book like she does? Use a stapler, some pencils, and some scissors. Ask someone in the family to help you.

node.title: Up Went Edmond
node.content: Edmond the elephant wants a see-saw ride. He is much larger than any of his friends, so he needs a lot of help to get off the ground.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Science and technology
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Making predictions|Using texts as models

node.esiss_blurb: Playing on a see-saw is fun. But what if you're a heavy elephant like Edmond? How can you have a see-saw ride?
node.esiss_activities: Make a see-saw using a ruler and something for it to balance on, such as a stone. Put a toy on one end. What can you put on the other end so the see-saw is balanced?

node.title: We All Play Sports
node.content: This story shows family members of all ages actively involved in different sporting activities. Features of the Book • The strong illustration support. • The high-frequency words - "My," "with," and "me." • Clear, consistently placed text. • The compound words - "baseball," "basketball," and "football." • The "ays" word ending - "plays." • The names for family members - "mom" and "dad." • The ellipsis on page 7, indicating anticipation. • The use of bold type for effect. Purpose We All Play Sports can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: -monitoring the reading and ensuring it is making sense using meaning, structure, and visual information from the story• -sharing own experiences related to the content of the story• -introducing and discussing new words contained within the story• -writing a simple set of instructions.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Forming opinions|Making connections|Making judgments|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Making connections, Sequencing, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: How many different sports can you name. In the family in this book, everyone plays a different kind of sport.
node.esiss_activities: Do you play any of the sports in this book? Think of a sport you want to play or invent a new one. Write some rules for the sport. Draw the things you need to play the sport.

node.title: Keeping Cool
node.content: Keeping Cool shows a family at home on a hot and sunny day. The text and illustrations combine to show a problem-solving approach when the children become too hot and go in doors to make iceblocks. Features of the Book - The high-frequency words "We". - The way the third word relates to the illustration. - The blends - "pl" and "sq". - The "ing" word ending - "playing", "pouring", and "waiting". - The double vowels - "ee" and "oo". - Large text with consistent placement. - The additional information in the illustrations. Purpose Keeping Cool can be used in a guided reading lesson to introduce the following skills and strategies: - sharing own experiences related to the content of the book; - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match; - writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: On a hot day, it's nice to eat something cold. Read about how some children help each other to make ice blocks.
node.esiss_activities: These children make ice blocks to keep themselves cool. Follow the steps to make your own ice blocks. What could you put in your ice blocks? Ask an adult to help you.
node.esiss_hfw: we, are

node.title: Holes
node.content: This book shows all kinds of animals that live in holes. The last page breaks the repeated sentence pattern and provides a surprise ending. Features of the Book - High-frequency words - This, in, a - Content words - hole, owl, fish, crab, spider, wasp, meerkat, bear, baby - Repeated phrase - This ____ lives in a hole. - Phonics and phonemic awareness - H, h (Holes, hole), b (baby, bear) - Punctuation - fullstop, exclamation mark Purpose of the Book Holes can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - reading a variety of simple high frequency words in context.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Synthesising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Visualising, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: A rabbit lives in a hole called a burrow. What other animals live in holes?
node.esiss_activities: Look at all the animals in the book. They all live in holes. Can you think of two more animals. that live in holes. Put each one in a sentence. The _______ lives in a hole.
node.esiss_hfw: this, in, a
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Where Are My Socks?
node.content: The familiar character Mo scrambles through a zany collection of clothes looking for his socks. Features of the Book • The question in the title. • A story with a known character. • Sentences split over two pages. • The high-frequency words - "is," "my," and "and." • The rhymes - "cat hat," "frog togs," and "winker blinker." • Clear, consistently placed text. • The text in the illustrations. • The use of bold type for effect. Purpose Where Are My Socks? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story • recognizing rhyming words • practicing writing using rhyme and rhythm.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Forming opinions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making connections, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Poor Mo. He can't find his socks. When he does find them, he sees that he's lost something else.
node.esiss_activities: Mo has a winker-blinker and frog togs! Think about the clothes at your house. Use them to create a crazy outfit like Mo's.
node.esiss_hfw: is, my, and
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Predictions, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELT1579 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: It Can Float
node.content: This book shows objects and animals that are able to float on water. The last page breaks the repeated sentence pattern and provides a surprise ending. Building Basic Skills - High-frequency words - This, is, a, It, can, on, the, I - Content words - alligator, crane, duck, insect, leaf, plane, ship, water - Repeated phrases - This is a ____ . It can float on the water. - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends: fl, cr, pl, sh - Punctuation - full stops, comma, exclamation mark Skills and Strategies It Can Float can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - asking questions of the text - making connections with own experiences.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Forming opinions|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Determining cause and effect, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever seen a leaf floating in a puddle? What other kinds of things can float?
node.esiss_activities: Fill up the sink with water. What little things can you find that will float? What little things can you find that will sink? Write a list of things that float and things that sink.
node.esiss_hfw: this, is, a, it, can, on, the, I
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Recognising Cause and Effect, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Look at the Eyes
node.content: In the animal kingdom, there are eyes of all shapes, sizes, and colours. This book explores some of the more unusual examples, and ends with a twist in the storyline. Features of the Book - High-frequency words - Look, at, the, It, has, little, big, of, no - Content words - cat, crab, eyes, fish, frog, owl, snail, spider, worm - Repeated phrases - Look at the ___. It has ___ eyes. - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends - cr, fr, sn, sp - Punctuation - fullstops, exclamation mark Look at the Eyes can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - using texts as models for own writing - asking questions of the text.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: How many eyes does a spider have? How about a worm? This book will tell you about amazing animal eyes.
node.esiss_activities: Make up an animal. Draw a picture of it. How many eyes will it have? Will the eyes be big or small? What colour will they be? Write a sentence: This ________ has _______ eyes.

node.title: Shapes
node.content: There are shapes all around us. This book encourages children to look at the shapes that make up everyday objects. Features of the Book - High-frequency words - can, you, see, I, a, and - Content words - circle, shape, square, triangle - Repeated phrases - What shape can you see? I can see a ___. - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends - sh, sq, tr; double vowel sound oo - Punctuation - fullstops, question marks Shapes can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - using texts as models for own writing - asking questions of the text.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring writer's craft, Exploring text structure, Making inferences, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Can you spot the shapes in this book? The last one is good to eat!
node.esiss_activities: Look inside your house. Then look outside your house. What things have a circle shape? What things have a square shape? What other shapes do you see around your house? Draw a picture using all these shapes.

node.title: We Can Bake
node.content: Baking is fun. In this book, two children help each other bake some biscuits. When when all the hard work is done, they get to have the first taste! Features of the Text - High-frequency words - I, in, the, We - Content words, butter, egg, flour, raisins, salt, sugar - Repeated phrases - I can put in the ___ - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blend: fl; short vowel sound: egg, butter - Punctuation - fullstops Purpose We Can Bake can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising the relationship between words and images; - pointing one-to-one at each word to ensure an appropriate match - phonemic awareness; - making predictions.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Following instructions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Following instructions, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Baking is fun, especially when you help each other. When when all the hard work is done, you get to have the first taste!
node.esiss_activities: Ask someone to help you bake some biscuits. Find a recipe together. Take turns to put things into the bowl. Make the biscuits and share them with the family.
node.esiss_hfw: I, in, the, we
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Happy Birthday, Estela
node.content: In this counting story, Estela and her friends show how much work, and fun, it is to break a pinata and reap the rewards. Features of the Book • The use of text as simple captions. • The use of bold text for effect. • Creative typography for effect -"CRACK." • The use of counting numbers as part of the story line. • Illustrations that show the same character more than once in the same scene. • The blends -"st," "sp," "sw," "fr," and "cr." • The use of imagery -"candy falling like rain." • The high-frequency words -"on," "the," "in," and "like." Purpose "Happy Birthday, Estela!" can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • predicting outcomes from illustrations • developing the skill of writing instructions.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: It's Estela's birthday and time to open the pinata. How will the five friends get the treats inside?
node.esiss_activities: This is a birthday counting book. Write your own book like this. Use the same numbers to write about five things that happen at your party.
node.esiss_hfw: on, the, in, like
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Hogboggit
node.content: The hogboggit is the main character's shadow, doing whatever she does but looking much larger and more fearsome. Features of the Book • The use of italics for emphasis. • The use of exclamation points. • The high-frequency words -are, see, me, my, and has. • The word endings - er and ing. • The blends - sc and cl. • The verb change from see to sees. • The child's control over the action in the story. Purpose The Hogboggit can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • discussing the Cause and effect of specific events within the story • using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense • innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Is the hogboggit real? I can see its teeth. I can see its eyes. How do I make it go away?
node.esiss_activities: The girl in this book changes her shadows. Find a place where you can see your shadow. Watch how your shadow moves as you move. Try to make teeth and ears like the hogboggit. What other shapes can you make?
node.esiss_hfw: are, see, me, my, has

node.title: What Is It Made Of?
node.content: Scientists look at what things are made from. This book uses colourful photographs and simple text to explore the materials that a variety of objects are made from. Objects include a bat, some letters, a bottle, a hat, and a ball. (Determining main idea: Scientists study what things are made from.) Features of What Is It Made From/. the topic (what things are made from), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • picture of material at the bottom of each page • picture glossary • initial consonant "b" - bat, ball, bottle • initial blends - "cl-", "gl-", "pl-" • digraph - "th" • rhyming words - bat, hat • silent "e" - made • double letters - bottle, letters, rubber, wood • one-syllable words - ball, bat, can, cloth, glass, wood • two-syllable words - bottle, letters, metal, plastic • change from "this" to "these" and "is" to "are" Combination of familiar (bat, letters, bottle, can, hat, ball) and less familiar (cloth, glass, plastic, metal, rubber, wood)
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Visualising, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: You can make a ball from rubber. What can you use to make a cricket bat? Read about the kinds of materials that are used to make the things around us.
node.esiss_activities: Look around your house. What can you see?_x000D_ Find three things that are made of metal._x000D_ Draw each thing. Make a label for each one.
node.esiss_hfw: are, is, it, this
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Visualising, Making Predictions

node.title: Boots for Toots
node.content: The delightful Toots (pronounced with the long "oo" sound) sets about buying a pair of boots. She tries on different kinds, telling the reader why they are not suitable, until she finds the perfect pair. Features of the Book • Word building in the rhyming words - "boots" and "Toots." • The high-frequency word "are." • Repeated vocabulary - "These boots are." • Clear, consistently placed text with good word spacing. • The additional text on the store signs. • The use of "too" as an adverb. • Exaggeration as a means of focusing the reader's attention. • The use of picture clues with initial consonants and blends to read unknown words. Purpose Boots for Toots can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: -reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context• -predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story• -recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds• -collecting information relating to the story to share with others.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Spotty boots, tall boots, big boots, and small boots. How many boots does Toots have to try before she finds a pair that is just right?
node.esiss_activities: Toots tries on many different boots. Which ones did you like best? Draw and colour in a pair of boots that are just right for you.
node.esiss_hfw: are
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Fun with Mo and Toots
node.content: The familiar character, Toots, and her friend, Mo, tell readers about the activities they enjoy. Features of the Book • The element of repetition supported by strong, clear images. • The use of text within the illustrations -By Toots and Mighty Mo. • The high-frequency words -in, I, We, like, to, and my. • The change from I to We when both characters are talking. • The use of a question mark to engage the reader. • The use of bold type for emphasis. Purpose Fun with Mo and Toots can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • reading a range of simple, high-frequency words in context; • developing an awareness of the directionality of the print on each page; • identifying onset and rime within words; • writing a story that incorporates a similar model to that of the book.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Mo and Toots love to play and have fun. They like to play some games by themselves, and they like to play some games together.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, Mo and Toots talk about the things they like to do. Make your own book that shows the things you like to do. What is the same about what you like? What is different about what you like?
node.esiss_hfw: in, I, we, like, to, my
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Rumble, Rumble, Boom!
node.content: This is a simple story focusing on the effects of the heat that comes from within the Earth. Features of the Book • The use of onomatopoeia. • Type that fits within the layout of the illustrations. • The high-frequency words - "The" and "is." • The double vowels and consonants in the onomatopoeic words - "bubble," "gloop," and "hiss." Purpose Rumble, Rumble, BOOM! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • using knowledge of letters and sounds for recognizing simple consonant-vowel-consonant words • predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story • practising rereading own writing to an audience.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Sequencing|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Sequencing, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: The places in this book are all very hot. What will happen when the volcano gets hot?
node.esiss_activities: This book uses words like "rumble" and "boom". These words show what the volcano sounds like. Draw a picture of something else that makes loud noises. Draw words on the picture to show the sounds.
node.esiss_hfw: the, is
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Sequencing

node.title: Ready to Ride
node.content: How do I take care of myself? I keep safe.In this book, a father and daughter decide to go for a bike ride. They know it is important to keep safe, so they check their tyres, brakes, and helmets. Once their bikes are safe, they go for a ride. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping safe.) Features of Ready to Ride • the topic (safety), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • background illustrations of bike parts • photographs that support the text • initial blend -"br" • digraph - "ch'' • words beginning with "r" - ready, ride • medial sound - "ck" • vowel sounds "oo" and "ee" - looks, good, need, keep • "ed" word endings - checked • silent "e" - safe (changing the short "a" sound to long) to support the text • labels
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Drawing conclusions|Forming opinions|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Forming opinions, Drawing conclusions, Determining cause and effect, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Before you go for a bike ride, you need to check that your bike is safe. And you need to keep yourself safe, too.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of the girl's bike. Add a label to each part that you know. Draw yourself on the bike. Make sure you're wearing a helmet!
node.esiss_hfw: a, are, good, I, on, said, to, we, went, with
node.wushka_comprehension: Sequencing, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Summarising

node.title: I Can Save ...
node.content: I can use things carefully. In this book, a girl shows how she can save things such as water, paper, and electricity by using them carefully. (Big idea: People need to use resources carefully.) Features of I Can Save • the topic (saving energy), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • summary on final page • photographs that support understanding of the text • initial consonant blends - "cl-", "st-", "sw-" • digraphs - "th", "wh" • silent letters - write • word ending "er" - paper, water
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Discussing point of view, Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: How can you save water? How about paper? The girl in this book has some great ideas about using things carefully. You'll learn how to save things, too.
node.esiss_activities: Fold a piece of paper to make three columns. In each column, draw something you can do to save electricity, paper, or water. Share your ideas with your family.
node.esiss_hfw: a, can, go, I, my, of, out, the, you
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: How Can I Use It Again?
node.content: I can help to use things again and again. This informational text describes how a boy recycles everyday household items (a can, box, bottle, and an egg carton) by making them into something useful (money bank, pencil box, plant pot, game). The photographs invite the reader to think about how everyday items can be recycled. (Big idea: People need to reuse things instead of throwing them away.) Features of How Can I Use It Again? • the topic (celebrations), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • question and response format • picture glossary • initial blend -"pl-" • digraph - "th" • initial consonants - "p" (plant, pot), "b" (bank, box) • challenging vocabulary - bank, money, box, pencils, pot, plant, game
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Discussing point of view, Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: You don't need to throw that tin in the rubbish. You can use it again. Read this book if you like making things.
node.esiss_activities: Find something that you can recycle. What can you make from it? You could look in the book for ideas.
node.esiss_hfw: a, and, can, for, I, is, my, this, to

node.title: In the Garden
node.content: This narrative text, written in the first person, captures a young girl's delight as she picks produce from the garden and puts it into a variety of containers. The climax to the story is predictable - and delicious! This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge, making connections using real-life experiences, making and confirming predictions, and inferring. It builds on the sentence structure in the Dragonflies emergent title The Picnic and provides opportunities for practising one-to-one word matching, recognising high-frequency words, and cross-checking the print with the illustration. Text features • the high-frequency words - I, in, my, the • the plural nouns - apples, beans, flowers, potatoes, strawberries, tomatoes • the repeated initial consonant "b" - bag, basket, beans, box, bucket • the initial consonant blends - -fl , -str • the first-person narration • the use of the ellipsis for anticipation • the repetitive sentence structure, with a change on pages 7 and 8 • the two word changes per page • the visual sub-plot - the bee, butterfly, and dog appear on most pages
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: There are lots of vegetables in the garden. Some go in the bucket. Some go in the box. Where do the strawberries go?
node.esiss_activities: The girl put the potatoes into a box. Draw a: • bucket • jar • basket • bag. Write what the girl put into each thing.
node.esiss_hfw: I, in, my, the
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Summarising, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Where Is Sam?
node.content: In this humorous story, the reader follows Sam through the house, finding item after item of discarded clothing, until the answer to the question posed in the title is revealed. The keyhole illustration on the cover provides an intriguing clue, while the detailed illustrations provide many opportunities for discussion. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of predicting using illustrations and making connections with real-life experiences. It provides opportunities for consolidating the high-frequency words "here", "where", "is", and "are" and for monitoring the students' use of return sweep. Look at Me is another Dragonflies emergent title about clothes. Text features (Focus on only one or two per session.) • the high-frequency words - I, in, my, the • the plural nouns - apples, beans, fl owers, potatoes, strawberries, tomatoes • the repeated initial consonant "b" - bag, basket, beans, box, bucket • the initial consonant blends - -fl , -str • the first-person narration • the use of the ellipsis for anticipation • the repetitive sentence structure, with a change on pages 7 and 8 • the two word changes per page • the visual sub-plot - the bee, butterfly, and dog appear on most pages
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: You can see Sam's shoes. You can see Sam's socks. You can see his T-shirt too. But where is Sam?
node.esiss_activities: Make up a story like this one. Put your name in the story. Try to write a different ending. For example, you could be in bed at the end of the book.
node.esiss_hfw: are, he, is, this
