node.title: Car Shopping
node.content: A family visits a car yard and looks at a variety of cars before deciding on one to buy. Humorous touches pepper the lively illustrations. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using illustrations to understand text, making and confirming predictions, and Summarising the Determining main ideas. The repetitive text offers strong support for the reader. This book is particularly useful for observing the students' attention to word endings and their use of initial sounds to decode content words. Text features • the high-frequency words - a, at, I, it, like, said, we • the repeated initial consonants - c, l, w • the initial consonant blends - bl, gr • the digraph -sh - shopping • the contraction - we'll • the adverb - too • the -ed ending - looked • the opposites - little and big, old and new • the colour words • the use of direct speech • the exclamation marks on pages 7 and 8 • the feelings of the family members shown in the illustrations • the text in the illustrations
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: This family needs a new car. It needs to be the right size and the right colour. What is the best kind of car to buy?
node.esiss_activities: Big is the opposite of little. Old is the opposite of new. What is the opposite of happy? What is the opposite of fast? What other opposites can you think of?
node.esiss_hfw: a, at, I, it, like, said, we
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Going Swimming
node.content: On a hot day, a family goes swimming, but they visit a variety of places before they decide on one that suits them all. This book supports the comprehension skills of Making connections with real-life experiences and making and confirming predictions. A repetitive Text structure provides support, allowing the students an understanding of speech and expressive phrases. Text features • the high-frequency words - can, go, like, said, we, went • the repeated initial consonants - w, m, d • the contraction - we'll • the adverb - too • the opposites - hot, cool • the use of direct speech • the family members' feelings expressed clearly through the illustrations • the relationship between the text and illustrations (finding cues for unfamiliar words)
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: It's a hot day. The family want to go swimming. They go to the pool. They go to the beach. Where is the best place to swim?
node.esiss_activities: The word "It's" is a contraction, which means "it is". Write what these contractions mean. • we'll • can't • you'll What other contractions can you write?
node.esiss_hfw: can, go, like, said, we, went
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Thank-you Day
node.content: This book is about a class that is having a special thank-you day. The teacher, Mr Thomas, explains to the class that he would like them all to remember to say thank you when someone does something nice for them. The Thank-You Day introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making connections • recognising cause and effect. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: idea, Miss, Mr, remember, special, thank • Phonics and phonemic awareness: digraph th (thank, that's, the, Thomas) • word endings ("ing, "ed, "s)
node.strategies: Making Connections |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Mr Thomas's class is having a thank-you day. When do you say thank you and why?
node.esiss_activities: Try having a thank-you day at home. Make a list of the things your family or carers do for you.
node.esiss_hfw: day, for, my, said, that’s, we, you
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1447 ACELA1448 ACELA1449 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1457 ACELA1458 ACELA1459 ACELA1778 ACELA1455 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1583 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Cooking with Dad
node.content: People take care of themselves by eating good food. Good food gives you energy and helps you grow. In this book, a young girl and her father make pizza bagels. Vivid photographs and a simple first-person narrative convey the message that preparing a tasty, nutritious meal is fun. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by eating good food.) Features of Cooking With Dad • the topic (healthy food), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • procedural words - first, then, now, next, finally, last • step-by-step action photographs • flow diagram • initial consonant blends - "gr-", "st-" • final consonant blend - "-st" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "ch" • contractions - I'm, it's • speech marks • exclamation marks • descriptive language - "My stomach is rumbling" • dialogue
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: How do you make healthy pizza bagels for lunch? Follow the steps in this book and soon your stomach will be rumbling.
node.esiss_activities: What kinds of things do you like to eat? Design a delicious meal. Draw the meal. List the ingredients. Make sure there are some vegetables or fruit.
node.esiss_hfw: get, make, our, put, some, them, then, too, with
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Machines That Move
node.content: Some scientists help us to understand how machines work. This book explains how machines move, using vivid photographs and easy-to-read diagrams. The simple text outlines the common elements that enable trucks, ships, and planes to move. (Big idea: Scientists study how things move and work.) Features of Machines That Move • the topic, which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • repetitive use of question marks to focus the explanatory text structure • captions and labels • layout (photographs of machines adjacent to diagrams) • labels and arrows which highlight the location of important parts • simple diagrams • use of coloured arrows to indicate movement • picture glossary • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "fl-", "tr-", "pr-" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "wh" • verbs - sail, fly, turn, spin, make, push, move • repetitive sentence structure to give concise information ("A ... has an engine." "The engine makes the ..." "The ... moves the ...")
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: What makes a plane move? What about a bulldozer? Different machines move in different ways. Find out how!
node.esiss_activities: Make three headings - Air, Land, and Sea. Think of as many machines as you can. Think about how each machines moves. Put it under either "Air", "Land", or "Sea". Talk to someone about how each thing moves.
node.esiss_hfw: all, an, has, have, that, they, what
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Half Moon Bay
node.content: This poetic text, perfect for reading aloud, evokes the sights, sounds, and feelings of a summer's day at the beach. Text features such as alliteration, repetition, and lively verbs provide a stimulating model for writing. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of Making connections with real-life experiences and Visualising. The high proportion of content words means that it may be better to share this text before using it for guided reading. Although the word count for this book is low because it is a poem, this is compensated for by the greater degree of difficulty. Text features • the variety of initial consonant blends • the -sh digraph - shines, shout • the -ou digraph - out, round, shout• the -ee digraph - breeze, green • the "I" sound for "y" - cry and fly • the rhythmic nature of the text • the use of alliteration, repetition, and rhyme • the use of commas for phrasing • the Descriptive language (adjectives, lively verbs, and vivid combinations of ideas) • the compound words "lawnmower", "sailboats", and "seagulls" • the details in the illustrations (the fi sh and bird cloud shapes on page 5, the safety aspects, and the changing perspectives) • the title included in the sign
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: If you want to have a holiday, come to Half Moon Bay. It's warm and sunny and we love swimming there. This is a poem you can read aloud.
node.esiss_activities: What can you see, feel, and hear at your house? Write a sentence about each thing. Use the sentences to write a poem like the one in the book. Draw a picture to go with your poem.
node.esiss_hfw: down, out
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The School Band
node.content: This text is about a boy's admiration for his older brother, who plays the drums. Conveyed through the illustrations, but not mentioned in the text, is the added dimension of the older brother having Down's syndrome. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of making inferences and understanding the author's purpose. This is a rich text with layers of meaning and is a good model of reflective personal experience writing. Text features • the theme of inclusiveness • the little brother's feelings of admiration and pride • the unnamed first-person narrator • the changes of tense, moving from the present to the past and then to the future • the way the author creates impact through the use of short sentences, repetition (of words and phrases), the use of the adverb "too", and exclamation marks • the variety of verbs and verb endings • the "flying" drumsticks in the illustrations • the initial consonant blends - br, cl, dr, fr, pl, pr, sch • the digraphs -ch - cheered, teach, teacher• -sh - showed• -th - brother, the, they, together, there, then
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making inferences|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Making inferences, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: This boy's brother plays the drums. He makes a lot of noise. He's in the school band, and the boy wants to be just like him.
node.esiss_activities: What instruments do the band play? What instruments are the children playing? Choose three instruments. Draw your own band. Write a label for each instrument.
node.esiss_hfw: her, him, how, our, over, then, there, they, too, us, with
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELA1822 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Gardener
node.content: This humorous narrative portrays a gardener's delight in expressing his creativity. The students will enjoy making predictions from the intriguing illustrations. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions and Drawing conclusions. The repetitive sentence structure provides strong support for students who lack confidence in their reading. Text features • the high-frequency words - a, and, are, he, is, my, said, the • the initial consonant blends - cl, fr, sm, tr • the digraphs -ph - elephant• -th - the, this • the different endings for the word "clip" - clippers, clipped • the adverb "too" • the use of direct speech to begin and end the story • the clear beginning, middle, and end • the use of repetition • the question mark on page 2 • the humorous, exaggerated illustrations
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Drawing conclusions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The gardener loves to use his clippers. He clips and clips, and he makes all kinds of animal shapes. The bush gets smaller and smaller. What animal can he make?
node.esiss_activities: What letters are at the end of the word "clipped"? Add the same letters to "look". What other words end in "ed"? Make a list of "ed" words that you know.
node.esiss_hfw: a, and, are, he, is, my, said, the
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: I Want an Ice Cream
node.content: This book looks at the difference between people's needs and wants. Rabbit's mother takes him shopping for new clothes that he needs. Rabbit would rather have an ice cream than go shopping, but his mother repeatedly reminds him that he doesn't need an ice cream, but he does need new clothes. At the end, when their shopping is done, Rabbit's mother realises she wants an ice cream, too. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - needs and wants - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - need, want - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - sh, cr Purpose I Want an Ice Cream can be used to reinforce the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - using prior knowlege; - making predictions; - discussing point of view; - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Rabbit wants an ice cream but his mother says he needs new clothes. Will Rabbit get what he wants?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, Rabbit and his mum go shopping because he needs new clothes, but Rabbit wants an ice cream. Imagine you are going shopping for clothes with your mum or dad. Write a list of the clothes you might buy. Which clothes do you need? Which clothes would you like to buy, but don't really need?
node.esiss_hfw: but, do, new, this, too, want, yes
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Let's Go to the Market
node.content: This story looks at how money is a limited resource that can be used to purchase goods. A girl and her father go to an outdoor market to shop for fresh produce. They have a limited amount of money to spend. They make several purchases, but find that the tomatoes they want are too expensive. At the end, they realise they do have enough money left to buy strawberries. Features of the Text - The link to financial literacy - money - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - dollars, spend - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends -cr, gr; long vowel sounds - need/green/sweet Purpose Let's Go to the Market introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - using prior knowledge; - making predictions; - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining cause and effect|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Using graphic sources, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: The girl and her father have ten dollars to spend at the market. Can they buy everything they want?
node.esiss_activities: Do you like helping your mum or dad do the shopping? Write a shopping list for the week. What things do you need? What things would you like, but don't really need?
node.esiss_hfw: but, go, have, now, yes
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Questioning, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELA1822 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Nick's Glasses
node.content: Nick is so used to wearing his glasses that he does not notice them on the end of his nose. His family tries to help him without giving the show away. Features of the Book • The frequent use of direct speech and questions. • The use of illustrations to answer the question. • The prepositions -"under," "in," "on," and "behind." • The high-frequency words -"Have" "Where," "your," and "you." • The "gl" blend -"glasses." • Large text blocks of two and three lines. Purpose Nick's Glasses can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • predicting outcomes from illustrations • hearing the individual sounds in a word • incorporating diagrams, maps, and tables to support information.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Following instructions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Following instructions, Using texts as models, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: Poor Nick! He can't find his glasses. Luckily his brother helps him find them!
node.esiss_activities: This book uses speech marks to show when someone is talking. Dad says, "Have you looked in your backpack?" Look through the book and find the speech marks. Now write two questions about looking for Nick's glasses. Make sure you use speech marks.
node.esiss_hfw: have, where, your, you
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Look at My Face
node.content: This book looks at the different kinds of feelings that people have and how they express them. A young girl visits the beach with her mother and little brother. Throughout the outing, she experiences a range of emotions that are reflected in her facial expressions and body language. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations; - Content words for discussion - face, look - Phonics and phonemic awareness: long vowel sounds - excited/like/tired Purpose Look at My Face reintroduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - asking and answering questions; - using texts as models.
node.strategies: Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: The girl's face shows how she is feeling at the beach. She is happy, then she is sad. How will she feel when she sees the big wave?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, the girl's face shows how she is feeling when she is at the beach. Draw pictures of your face to show what you look like when you're happy, sad, excited, scared, grumpy, tired, and asleep.
node.esiss_hfw: like, this
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: My Sister
node.content: A young girl, who has just started school, is too scared to go down the slide. Her older sister describes how they finally solve this problem. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of making connections with real-life experiences and identifying the Determining main ideas. The high-frequency words repeated in different sentence structures provide opportunities to practise rapid word recognition and develop fluency. The lively dialogue supports expressive reading. This text is a good model of personal experience writing. Text features • the theme of overcoming fear and the idea that people can help when you're feeling scared • the sisters' relationship • the way that the big sister solves the problem • the feelings of the characters conveyed through the expressive dialogue and illustrations • the large amount of dialogue, including some that isn't explicitly attributed to a particular character • the first-person narrator • the high-frequency vocabulary • the initial consonant blends - cl, cr, pl, sl, st • the digraphs -th - the, they, thanks• -ch - teacher• -sh - shouted • the verbs ending in -ed - played, started, shouted • the imperative verbs - come, help, jump, don't, hurry • the use of the adverb "too" for emphasis • the contractions - can't, it's, I'm, I'll, don't • the rimes -y - my, cry• -ound - ground, around • the use of exclamation marks and upper-case letters for emphasis
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining main ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: The girl's sister is at the top of the slide, and she is scared. How will the girl help get her sister down?
node.esiss_activities: The word "won't" is a contraction. It means "will not". Read the book again and find four contractions. Write what each contraction means.
node.esiss_hfw: back, down, get, put, too
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELA1822 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Don't Forget Grandma
node.content: This simple play has a cast of four characters. Grandma is coming to dinner, and the children have a lively, entertaining time preparing for her arrival. This simple play has a cast of four characters. This text supports the comprehension strategies of following the steps in a process, noting details in illustrations, and summarising the Determining main ideas. It introduces the students to the conventions involved with reading and performing plays. Text features • the initial consonant blends - gr, sp, pl • the contractions - don't, let's, didn't, I'll • the compound words - forget, Grandma • the silent "k" in "knife" • the use of repetition • the use of exclamation marks • the use of questions • the play-writing conventions of a list of characters and the use of capital letters and coloured type • the humour in the illustrations • the visual sub-plots involving a spider and the family cat
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Grandma is coming for dinner. George and Rosanna are helping to set the table. Everything has to be just right for Grandma's visit. This is a play you can read with three friends.
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book know how to set the table for dinner. Ask if you can set the table for dinner. How many people will be at the table? How many forks, knives, and spoons will you need?
node.esiss_hfw: one, some, too, were, what
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Hello, Dad!
node.content: This simple text focuses on modern communication technology and on one of the oldest methods of all, a simple painted message to welcome Dad home. Feature of the Book • The strong, rhythmic pattern in the text. • The high-frequency words -"it," "a," "on," and "the." • The clear depiction of a fax, telephone, and computer as items of technology. • Rhythmic, repetitive text. • The rhyme -"call" and "wall." • Clear, well-spaced text. • The two levels of text -the story text and the written messages. • The Chinese script in the girl's letter. Purpose Hello, Dad! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • monitoring the reading and ensuring it is making sense using meaning, structure, and visual information from the story • sharing ideas about the story with others in the group • introducing and discussing new words contained within the story • illustrating a story to support the meaning of the text.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The girl in this book is missing her dad. How many different ways can she say hello?
node.esiss_activities: Write a list of the ways that this girl keeps in touch with her dad. Choose one way of keeping in touch with someone you know. Write down what you would write or say to them.
node.esiss_hfw: it, at, on, the, make
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Shush!
node.content: Shush! is a lively narrative about getting up in the morning and making breakfast in bed and as a surprise for Mum. Features of the Book • The range of punctuation -question mark, exclamation point, and quotation marks. • The high-frequency words -"He," "the," "said, "this," and "a." • The adjectives -"big" and "long." • Sequence of events in a narrative. • The different uses of apostrophes -"who's" and "Sam's." Purpose Sam's Mask can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: responding to simple questions about the story's content• encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding• retelling the sequence of the story using own words• writing a simple set of instructions.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Making predictions, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: When you want to make mum a surprise breakfast in bed, you have to do it quietly. You can make a fruit salad like this too. Just follow the recipe.
node.esiss_activities: What does this family eat for breakfast? Make a breakfast menu for your house. What breakfast foods do you already have? What will you need to make or buy?
node.esiss_hfw: he, the, said,
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: "Smile!" said Dad
node.content: Somebody manages to spoil the shot every time Dad attempts to take a family photograph. Features of the Book • The variety of vocabulary and punctuation. • The use of repetition. • The use of direct speech. • The format of the cover -photograph and beach creatures. • The use of contractions -"I'll" and "I've." • The use of creative typography and onomatopoeia -"click." • The blends -"cl," "ck," and "sm." • The high-frequency words -"have," "her," "again," "said," "had," and "was." Purpose "Smile!" said Dad can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story • using knowledge of onset and rime to create new words from those in the story • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Comparing and contrasting|Determining importance|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Determining importance, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Dad is trying to take a family picture at the beach. Each time he pushes the button, someone ruins the photo.
node.esiss_activities: Ask a family member if you can use a camera or phone to take some family photos. Take the photos. What kinds of funny faces can you see? Choose your favourite face. Write a sentence about why the person made the funny face.
node.esiss_hfw: this, a
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: In the Desert
node.content: In The Desert follows the experience of a young boy as he explores the features of a desert. Features of the Book • The high-frequency words - "you," from," and "can." • The repetitive and predictable structure. • Clear letter spacing. • The contraction "don't." • The use of senses to describe experiences. • The use of ellipses, exclamation point, and commas. • Photographs to support the text. • The multisyllabic words - "mountains," "desert," "flowers," "candy," and "cactus." Purpose In the Desert can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • blending sounds together to make a word • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making predictions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: In the desert, there are things you can hear, smell, taste, and touch. There is also something that you should not touch!
node.esiss_activities: What different things can you see in this desert? What can you see outside your house? What can you see on the street? Make a list for each place. What is the same? What is different? You can use a Venn diagram to show this.
node.esiss_hfw: you, from, can
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1458 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Bumble Bee
node.content: Bumble Bee is a simple explanatory text that describes how a bumble bee flies. Features of the Book • The use of ellipses. • Initial "b" consonant - "bumble bee," "big," and "body." • The contraction "that's." • The high-frequency words - "very," "can," "has," "but," "are," and "How." • Capital letters for emphasis - "VERY." • The labeled diagram at the back of the book. • The difference between the diagram and photographs. Purpose Bumble Bee can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • summarising the main parts of the story• discussing several books that relate to the same theme • gathering information and sorting it into categories.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Analysing themes|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: A bumble bee is round and furry. How can it fly with such tiny wings? This book explains how.
node.esiss_activities: A bumble bee has a big body and tiny wings. Draw a picture of a bumble bee. Use the book to help you label all the parts.
node.esiss_hfw: very, can, has, but, are, how
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Chinese New Year
node.content: Chinese New Year is a time of celebration and festivities. This story looks at how a mother and her son prepare for holiday. Features of the Book • The use of repetition as a support. • The variety of verbs - "make," "sweep," "put," "change," "hang," and "wait." • The Chinese cultural content. • The blends - "sw," "ch," "cl," and "bl." • The challenge in the vocabulary - "dumplings," "blankets," "lanterns," and "envelopes." • The change from "We" to "I" on page 7. • The high-frequency words - "We," "the," "out," "to," "my," and "for." Purpose Chinese New Year can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • hearing the individual sounds in a word • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • encouraging rereading of the text to clarify understanding • practising using poetic language to write poems.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making connections|Making predictions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Making connections, Making predictions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: There's lots to do to get ready for Chinese New Year. The boy in this book helps his mum do all of the jobs.
node.esiss_activities: What does this family do to celebrate Chinese New Year? What does your family do to celebrate the new year? What is the same? What is different?
node.esiss_hfw: we, the, out, to, my, for
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Making Connections, Making Predictions, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: My Bike
node.content: A young girl puts her bike through its paces as she rides through varied terrain faster and faster - to the inevitable conclusion. Features of the Book • The structure provided by the diary layout. • The cumulative story form. • The high incidence of prepositions - "around," "under," "over," and "through." • A close picture/text match to encourage picture-search skills. • The blends - "tr" and "br." • The days of the week. • The high-frequency words - "On," "my," "and," "the," and "up." Purpose My Bike can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense • incorporating diagrams and tables into pieces of writing to support ideas • innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Exploring writer's craft|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Exploring writer's craft, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: You can ride a bike in all kinds of places, but be careful how fast you ride down the hill!
node.esiss_activities: This book uses words that tell where something is. Can you find the words "around", "under", and "over". Draw a plan of your own bike course. Use these words to describe how you ride around it.
node.esiss_hfw: on, my, and, the, up
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Praying Mantis
node.content: Close-up photos follow a praying mantis as it hunts its prey. "Features of the Book • The circular nature of the story. • The blends - "pr" and "th." • The rhymes - " fly," "by" and "munch," "crunch." • The use of photographs to convey information. • The onomatopoeia - "Bzzzz," "Pow!" and "Munch, crunch." • The use of ellipses to indicate anticipation. • The creative use of type - "Bzzzz" and "POW!" • The high-frequency words - "very," "comes," "this," and "that." Purpose The Praying Mantis can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • discussing the motives of specific characters • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • writing labels for a specific purpose."
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring writer's craft|Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences, Drawing conclusions, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: The praying mantis doesn't move, but don't be fooled. Watch what happens when a fly comes by!
node.esiss_activities: Some words in this book are rhyming words. "Munch" and "crunch" are rhyming words. Choose three other words from the book, such as "pow". Make a list of words that rhyme with your three words.
node.esiss_hfw: very, comes, this, that
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Ready, Steady, Jump!
node.content: Ready, Steady, JUMP! is a non-fiction text describing how the jumping spider builds its web. Features of the Book • Everyday language - "Made it!" and "Missed!" • The use of exclamation points, ellipses, and a question mark. • The format that shows frame-by-frame action. • The close-up photography. • The "er" word ending - "over," "spider," and "other." • The labeled diagram and additional information for study. • The high-frequency words - "Can," "the," "get," and "She." Purpose Ready, Steady, JUMP! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • predicting outcomes from illustrations • comparing and contrasting two books by the same author • innovating on familiar songs and writing another version.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Science and technology
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Exploring writer's craft|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring writer's craft, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Spider wants to jump from one leaf to another, but she misses. Will she give up, or will she try again?
node.esiss_activities: This book uses a question mark._x000D_ A question mark shows that someone is asking a question._x000D_ Write some questions you would like to ask the spider _x000D_ about how it jumps using its web.
node.esiss_hfw: can, the, get, she
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Off Went the Light
node.content: Two brothers share a bedroom. Andy can't get to sleep because he can't find his toys. William can't get to sleep because Andy keeps asking for help. When all the toys are found, Andy happily goes to sleep, leaving William awake and grumpy. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of using Prior knowledge and noting details in illustrations. It provides opportunities for monitoring the student's integration of reading strategies. Text features • the initial consonant blends - fl , sl • the digraphs -ph - elephant; -sh - she's, wish; -th - they, them • the contractions - can't, she's, he's • the names of the children and their toys • the prepositions - on, off , under, in • the repetition • the use of direct speech • the use of italics for emphasis on page 7 • the use of the ellipsis to indicate a pause on page 8 • the use of an exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the underlying theme of opposites - on and off; light and dark; awake and asleep; happy and unhappy • the feelings of the children, expressed through the illustrations and their conversation
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining main ideas, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Andy needs all of his toys before he can go to sleep. William helps him find them. What happens when Andy goes to sleep?
node.esiss_activities: Andy says, "I can't find Ted." The speech marks show that Andy is talking. Look at the book again. Find four sentences where someone is talking. Write the sentences. Make sure you use speech marks for each one.
node.esiss_hfw: them, your
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Don't Throw That Out!
node.content: A resourceful and determined child rescues rubbish to create a magnificent dragon. This text supports the comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions, noting details in illustrations, and using illustrations to understand text. This text is constructed completely through direct speech and the use of speech bubbles, providing good opportunities for expressive reading. Text features l• the initial consonant blends - br, dr, gl, gr, pr, st • the digraph -th - that, that's, the, those, everything, brother • the letter cluster -thr - throw • the contractions - don't, that's, I've • the compound word - everything • the past and future forms of the verbs - made, make • the adjectives - cool, good, sticky • the use of direct speech • the repeated sentence structure on pages 2 to six • the speech bubbles on pages 10 to 12 • the characters' names • the varied text placement • the use of a variety of punctuation
node.strategies: Making predictions|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Making predictions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: The girl in this book doesn't like to throw things out. She collects all the rubbish from the class. What does she make?
node.esiss_activities: Look around your house for something old that you can make into something new. You could make a dragon like the girl in the book. Share it with your family.
node.esiss_hfw: have, make, now, one, some, that, too
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Purr-Fect!
node.content: This fable is about a vain and gullible bird and a clever cat. Its dramatic ending is sure to stimulate lively discussion. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions, drawing conclusions, and making judgments. This text provides many opportunities for the students to practise their decoding and cross-checking strategies. It's a useful text for monitoring the students' integration of reading strategies and their comprehension at this level. Text features • the high-frequency words • the initial consonant blends - cl, fl , pl, tr • the digraphs -sh - she, washed• -th - that, the, feathers • the verbs ending -ed - fluffed, jumped, liked, pleased, wanted, washed • the forms of the verb "to fly" - flew, fly, flying • the irregular past-tense verbs - could, flew, said, sang, saw • the pronouns - he, she • the play on words in "Purr-fect" • the use of direct speech • the use of the ellipsis to indicate a pause • the feeling of anticipation • the gory (but unspecified) ending • the characterisation • the implied moral about the perils of flattery • the visual language features - the changing perspective• the way the bird moves closer
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: The cat in this book is clever. It gets the bird to come closer and closer. What happens when it gets too close?
node.esiss_activities: The cat tricked the bird by saying nice things. Write another story where one animal tricks another. Use speech marks to show when the animals are talking.
node.esiss_hfw: down, his, saw, that, you
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Ling Lee's Surprise
node.content: A girl brings a mystery package to school to share with her class. Ling Lee becomes the focus of attention as her gift is revealed. This story has an underlying theme about valuing other cultures, gaining confidence, and developing a sense of belonging. This text supports the comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions and summarising the Determining main ideas. This text is particularly suitable for monitoring children's integration of reading strategies at the end of the Blue level. Text features • the initial consonant blends - br , cl, pl, sm, st, sw • the digraphs -ch - China, teacher; -sh - goldfish, she, show; -th - mother, the, there; -thr - three • the present-tense verbs - holds, opens, says, sits, smiles, takes • the -y ending - carefully, happy, story, very • the -ou sound - our, out, round • the variety of adjectives - beautiful, big, brown, little, paper, plastic • the compound words - classroom, goldfish • the use of direct speech • the use of changing perspective over pages 5 to 8 as Ling Lee reveals her surprise • the familiar classroom scenes
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: There is a goldfish in Ling Lee's classroom. She thinks it is bad luck to have just one fish. One day, Ling Lee brings a paper bag to school. What could be inside?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a big fish bowl with two goldfish. Then draw Ling Lee's fish. Write a sentence about why Ling Lee wanted three goldfish in the class fishbowl.
node.esiss_hfw: her, little, our, put, see, too, two, us
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: City Scenes
node.content: This text takes the form of a poem expressing a child's view of the city as he and his mother walk home. Features of the Book • Division of text into non rhyming introduction and conclusion, with a rhyming middle section. • The use of an ellipsis. • Playful illustrations to support text. • The "ing" word ending - "dashing" and "rushing." • Couplets with internal and end rhymes based on assonance. • The plurals in the text are not matched by plurals in the illustrations. • The high frequency word "the." Purpose City Scenes can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • hearing the individual sounds in a word; • visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense; • practicing using poetic language to write poems; • comparing the story to events in our own lives.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Using graphic sources, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: The city is a busy, noisy place. Find out why this boy loves the city.
node.esiss_activities: This book uses verbs to describe what the boy can see. He sees "runners dashing". Imagine taking a walk around your neighbourhood or city. What would you see? Write the verbs that describe what is happening.
node.esiss_hfw: I, in, the, see
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Down at the River
node.content: This is a simple rythmic text about the pastime of skipping stones. Features of the Book • The poetic quality of the text. • The blends -"sk," "st," "sm," "fl," and "pl." • The "thr" letter cluster -"three." • The use of numbers. • The way the illustrator has shown movement. • The close-up illustrations to show detail. • The use of onomatopoeia -"skip" and "plop." • The use of exaggeration for effect -"We skip a million stones." • The high-frequency words -"We," "at," and "the." Purpose Down at the River can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense• using knowledge of onset and rime to create new words from those in the story• Summarising the main parts of the story• comparing the story to events in own lives.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Skipping stones at the river is fun. Don skips one. How many do the other children skip?
node.esiss_activities: What sounds did the stones make when they bounced across the the water? Write a list of five more sounds the stones might make.
node.esiss_hfw: we, at, one, the, two, a, down
