node.title: Sam's Mask
node.content: In this story, Sam makes a mask at school and then wears it home to give his mother a surprise. Features of the Book • The range of punctuation - question mark, exclamation point, and quotation marks. • The high-frequency words - "He," "the," "said, "this," and "a." • The adjectives - "big" and "long." • Sequence of events in a narrative. • The different uses of apostrophes - "who's" and "Sam's." Purpose Sam's Mask can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • responding to simple questions about the story's content • encouraging reading using expression to increase understanding • retelling the sequence of the story using own words • writing a simple set of instructions.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Sam makes a mask and wears it home from school. What happens when his mother opens the door?
node.esiss_activities: Sam makes a mask and surprises his mum. Using some paper or card, make your own mask. Who can you surprise?
node.esiss_hfw: he, the, this, a, made, who, said
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: My Shadow
node.content: My Shadow is a series of simple, reflective statements about the attributes of shadows. Features of the Book - The rhythm of the central pages of text when changed. - The way the lengths of the shadows in the illustrations vary from page to page. - The rhyme - day and away. - The sh blend - shadow and short. - The different sounds of the o vowel in long, short, morning, and comes. - The ellipsis on page 11 indicating that text continues on the next page. - The high-frequency words - can, be, up, and going. Purpose My Shadow can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - discussing the cause and effect of specific events within the story; - hearing the individual sounds in a word; - innovating on an original storyline to write another version.
node.strategies: Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: How does your shadow change during the day? And what happens to it at night?
node.esiss_activities: Shadows have all kinds of shapes. Look around your house for shadows. Write down the different shadows you see. Draw the shadows.
node.esiss_hfw: in, the, my, when
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1440 ACELA1438 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: A New Balloon
node.content: A New Balloon chronicles a child's delight in exploring the properties of a balloon - until an inquisitive kitten takes the story to its predictable conclusion. Features of the Book - The logical sequence of the events helps the reader to predict the outcome. - The clear, well-spaced text. - The double oo vowel - look and balloon. - The rhyme - blow and grow. - The high-frequency words - look, A, it, and, like, and up. - The text in the illustration - POP. - The ellipses to indicate suspense. - The simple, clear illustrations. - The placement of the balloon on pages 8 and 9 to provide strong visual clues to the meaning. Purpose A New Balloon can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - predicting the storyline using illustrations from the book; - recognising rhyming words; - recognising the relationship between letters and their sounds; - writing using the conventions of capital letters and fullstops.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Activating prior knowledge, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: What happens when the boy gets a new balloon? He blows it up until it's like a big pink ball. Watch out for the cat!
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you have a balloon and it goes on a trip. Write about where it would go. What happens to it in the end?
node.esiss_hfw: it
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Signs Keep Us Safe
node.content: Following the road rules and looking for signs helps to keep you safe. This book uses clear graphics and simple text to show how signs in the community help keep people safe. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping safe.) Features of Signs Keep Us Safe • the topic (signs), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • graphic illustrations, photographs, and symbols • glossary • opportunities to build knowledge of letter-sound patterns and practise cross-checking decoding strategies • digraphs - "th" and "wh" • "ee" rime in "keep" • italicised word "not" on page 9
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Forming opinions|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Forming opinions, Exploring writer's craft, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Look around you. There are signs everywhere. Some tell you what to do. Some tell you what not to do. Signs keep us all safe.
node.esiss_activities: The signs in this book help keep people safe. Make a sign for your house to help keep visitors safe. Your sign can have pictures or words, or both.
node.esiss_hfw: have, made, not, some, they, us, what
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Welcome to the Desert
node.content: Some scientists help us understand different habitats. This book is a simple scientific report on how animals and plants survive in the desert. Vivid desert photographs are overlaid with images of a boy acting as a guide. (Big idea: Scientists study plants and animals to help us understand them.) Features of Welcome to the Desert • the topic (habitats), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • questions that focus attention on the topic • bold text for glossary words • cutaway photographs • photographs of a boy who acts as the reader's guide • picture glossary • contractions - it's, doesn't, there's • medial sound "ee" - need, deep
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: There's not a lot of water in a desert, so it's hard to imagine many animals living there. This book explains how desert animals get a drink when they're thirsty.
node.esiss_activities: There isn't much water in the desert. Imagine you are an animal that lives in the desert. Draw a picture of yourself as an animal. Write about how you would find food and water. Tell someone your ideas.
node.esiss_hfw: from, get, has, here, not, there, too, when
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Gardener and the Scarecrow
node.content: In this humorous narrative, the gardener needs to find a way to stop the greedy birds from pecking at his vegetables. The students will enjoy working out what the gardener has in mind! This text supports the comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions and noting details in illustrations. It's also useful for monitoring children's integration of reading strategies at the end of the Blue level. There are two other Dragonflies books about this character: The Gardener (emergent) and The Gardener's Maze (early). Text features (Focus on only one or two per session.) • the initial consonant blends - cl, sc, st, tr • the digraphs -ch - chased, stitch; -sh - shed, shouted; -th - that, the, they, this • the lively verbs - chased, clipped, pecked, shouted • the irregular verbs - came, kept, made, put, said, was, went • the variety of word endings - clipped, clipping; garden, gardener, gardening • the -y ending - scary • the compound word - scarecrow • the use of repetition for effect and to suggest time passing on page 7 • the sentences running over two lines • the use of commas for phrasing • the clear narrative structure • the markers of time - in the morning, that night • the gardener's imperative language • the way the gardener talks to himself • the battle between the gardener and the birds • the clues in the illustrations • the surprise ending
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: The birds are eating the gardener's vegetables. He hammers and cuts and stitches. What does he make?
node.esiss_activities: The gardener's scarecrow looks just like him. Draw a scarecrow that looks like you. Write a list of the things you would need to make the scarecrow.
node.esiss_hfw: away, back, came, from, his, not, put, some, that
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Lonely Bull
node.content: Bull thinks nobody wants to be his friend, but it's just that all the other farm animals are scared of him. Features of the Book • The strong element of repetition for reader support. • The frequent use of the blends -"sh" and "fr." • The paper collage medium used by the artist. • Illustrations that span two pages. • The frequency of direct quotes and the question mark. • The capital letter that begins each animal's name. • The high-frequency words -"was," "will," "you," "came," and "your." Purpose The Lonely Bull can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: using structural cues to monitor that reading is making sense• predicting outcomes from illustrations• Summarising the main parts of the story• selecting information from the story for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Making judgments|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Sequencing, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Bull is lonely. No one will be his friend, until Duck comes along to help him.
node.esiss_activities: At the end of this story, the lonely bull makes a friend. If you met the lonely bull, how would you be a friend to him? Write some sentences about the things you would do together.
node.esiss_hfw: was, will, you, your, be, my, and, she
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Wind
node.content: The Wind is a poem that describes a very windy day. Features of the Book • The rhyme - "dirt," "shirt," and "skirt." • The addition of "s" to make the plural form. • The high-frequency words - "The," "and," "my," and "me." • The use of an ellipsis. • The "sh" blend - "shirt." • Clear, consistent placement of text. • The use of italics to emphasize the learning elements in the illustrations. • The flag image used with the page numbers. • The complete poem printed on the final page. Purpose The Wind can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • sharing own experiences related to the content of the story • recognizing rhyming words • practicing writing using some poetic language • practicing writing using rhyme and rhythm.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Exploring text structure, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: On a windy day, everything gets blown around. Even me! This is a poem you can read aloud.
node.esiss_activities: The wind can blow around lots of things. Sometimes it is helpful and sometimes it is not. Make two headings - "Helpful Wind" and "Unhelpful Wind". Now look through the book and find three things to go under each heading.
node.esiss_hfw: the, and, my, me
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELT1579 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Peter's Collection
node.content: Peter's Collection follows a young boy who searches in various places in and around his house to find a collection of items he needs to take to school. needs to take to school. Features of the Book • Photographs supporting the text. • Repetitive text reinforcing vocabulary. • The use of regular, past-tense verbs - "counted," "looked," and "shouted." • Counting patterns. • The use of quotation marks, exclamation points, and ellipses. • The use of natural language. • The use of common objects. • The use of prepositions - "inside," "outside," "under," and "in." • The high-frequency words - "looked," "his," "them," "away," "put," "not," "some," "were," and "but." Purpose Francisco's Collection can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense • incorporating diagrams and maps to support ideas.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Peter needs to bring a collection of one hundred things to school. He looks in his toy box, he looks under his bed. Where will he find one hundred things?
node.esiss_activities: Peter makes a collection of different things. Take a look around your house and make your own collection. How many things are in your collection? Write some sentences to tell a friend about your collection.
node.esiss_hfw: looked, his, them, away, put, not, some, were, but
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: People Are Working
node.content: A non-fiction story about the work that needs to be done before a museum can open to the public. Features of the Book • Clear photographs, including insets, to support the text and show close-up detail. • Repetitive text using two main structures. • The use of question marks, exclamation points, and quotation marks. • The depiction of different occupations. • The high-frequency words - "are" and "you." Purpose People Are Working can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • using visual clues to monitor that reading is making sense • summarising the main parts of the story • innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Analysing themes|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making connections, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: A lot of people work in the museum. How do they all get ready for opening time?
node.esiss_activities: Lots of people work in this museum. They need to work together. Write about what would happen if someone was late for work. What would happen at the museum?
node.esiss_hfw: are, you
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Walking the Dog
node.content: This is a fun text about taking a dog for a walk in the park. The lively illustrations convey what is "really" happening in the story. This book supports the Comprehension strategies of noting details in illustrations, making connections with real-life experiences, and using illustrations to understand text. The sentences are structured so that the content words from the last half of each sentence are repeated in the beginning of the next sentence. This provides strong support for the students who lack confidence in their reading. Text features • the high-frequency words - and, the, we • the initial consonant blends - br, fl , thr, tr • the word ending -er - under, over, flower(s) • the compound word - into • the plural nouns - ducks, flowers • the prepositions - after, along, into, over, round, through, under • the consistent sentence structure • the use of an ellipsis to heighten anticipation on page 7 • the use of an exclamation mark for emphasis on page 8 • the significant development of the story line that occurs in the illustrations
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: When you walk the dog, you see lots of things. You see cats and ducks. You see flowers. What do you see when you get home?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a map of the route the girl and the dog walked. Draw a sign beside each place. Write the name of the place on each sign.
node.esiss_hfw: and, the, we
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: My Special Job
node.content: This is young boy's simple recount of the daily routine of removing his fathers boots. Features of the Book • Text running over spreads. • The use of contractions -"don't" and "you've." • Descriptive language -"like a bucking bronco." • The blends -"br," "sp," "ch," and "sl." • The ellipses on pages 7, 9, and 10. • The idea of a simple tradition. • The use of the large text and exclamation point for effect. • The use of the past tense on the final page. • The high-frequency words -"when," "from," "his," "then," "that," and "for." Purpose My Special Job can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • predicting outcomes from illustrations • developing the skill of writing instructions • comparing the story to events in own lives.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Drawing conclusions|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Sequencing, Discussing point of view, Drawing conclusions,
node.esiss_blurb: When Dad comes home from work feeling tired, someone has to loosen his bootlaces. Guess who does that job?
node.esiss_activities: This boy had a special job to do each day. What is the special job you do at home? Write a description of what you have to do. You could also draw a diagram to help explain it.
node.esiss_hfw: when, from, his, then, that, for
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Skipper's Happy Tail
node.content: In this humorous narrative, Skipper's tail causes all kinds of trouble, but finally comes in useful. The dramatic events and lively dialogue encourage expressive reading. This text supports the comprehension strategies of makingconnections to real-life experiences and making and confirming predictions. It is useful for monitoring children's integration of reading strategies at the end of Blue level. There is another early book about this character, No, Skipper! Text features - thevariety of initial consonant blends - the digraphs -ch - children; -ph - Sophie; -sh - brushed, crash, shed, shopping,shouted; -th - the, there, they - the repeated rimes - here, there; get, wet; flicked, pick, tickled; out, shouted - the strong narrative structure - the dramaticstyle - thecharacterisation of Skipper - theexpressive illustrations - thepossessive apostrophes in "Greg's", "Skipper's", and "Sophie's" - the lively verbs - the expressive dialogue, including imperative language - the use of exclamation marks for emphasis - the use of onomatopoeia - crash!
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Skipper's tail is always getting him into trouble. But if a bee lands on your arm, Skipper's tail is very useful!
node.esiss_activities: Skipper's tail gets him into trouble. In the end, his tail scares away a bee. Write about two other good things that Skipper's tail could do.
node.esiss_hfw: away, get, have, here, his, off, over, them, there, what, your
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Going to the Moon
node.content: This book describes how people have gazed at the moon for hundreds of years. It features the three American astronauts who travelled to the moon in 1969 and discusses how long the trip took and what they did while away from Earth. It also features diagrams of the spacecraft and module Apollo 11 and Eagle. Going to the Moon introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • questioning • monitoring and clarifying. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: astronaut, command, crater, module • Phonics and phonemic awareness: -ee (long /e/sound)
node.strategies: Questioning|Monitoring and Clarifying |Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Questioning, Monitoring and Clarifying, Sequencing, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: When did you last look up at the moon? Do you know anything about the moon and the people who have visited it?
node.esiss_activities: When the three astronauts landed on the moon, it was big news. Write a short TV report about the moon landing as if you were a reporter in 1969. Remember that reports can be more informal than a non-fiction book.
node.esiss_hfw: back, big, call, can, down, look, see, take, there
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1447 ACELA14478 ACELA1449 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1457 ACELA1458 ACELA1459 ACELA11778 ACELA1455 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1583 ACELT1584 ACELT1581 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Pushy Pete
node.content: This book looks at some penguins that built a slide out of ice. Pete wants to go first down the slide, but the other penguins don't think that is fair. He doesn't listen to them and goes down anyway. But then Pete needs their help when he gets stuck on the slide. The other penguins come to the rescue and Pete learns the value of fairness and sharing. Pushy Pete introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • visualising • compare and contrast. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: push, first, fair, turn • Phonics and phonemic awareness: trigraph /air/
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Visualising |Sequencing |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Visualising, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Pushy Pete wants to go down the slide first. Have you ever met someone who is pushy? How did it make you feel?
node.esiss_activities: Create a short conversation between Pete, Lila and Freda about who is going to go first down a new ice rollercoaster that the penguins have just built. Perform the conversation to the rest of the class.
node.esiss_hfw: all, down, fast, get, go,more, no, said, say, want
node.wushka_comprehension: Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect, Compare and Contrast, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1447 ACELA14478 ACELA1449 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1457 ACELA1458 ACELA1459 ACELA11778 ACELA1455 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1583 ACELT1584 ACELT1581 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: True Bugs
node.content: This book looks at true bugs and how to identify them from other bugs. It explains how true bugs, which come in different shapes and sizes, catch prey and move. It also explains the different ways that true bugs defend themselves against predators. True Bugs introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • activating prior knowledge • summarising • comparing and contrasting. Features of the Book: • The information contained in photographs • Content words for discussion: beak, disguise, predators, prey • Phonics and phonemic awareness: -ue (long /u/sound)
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Comparing and contrasting, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: True bugs come in all shapes and sizes. What do you know about true bugs? Do you have a favourite?
node.esiss_activities: Choose two true bugs from the book to compare and contrast. Use a Venn diagram. Remember that in a Venn diagram, the things that the two bugs have in common go in the middle and the things that are different between the two bugs go in the outer part.
node.esiss_hfw: are, at, but, eat, have, like, on, so, that, there, they, this, with
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1447 ACELA14478 ACELA1449 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1457 ACELA1458 ACELA1459 ACELA11778 ACELA1455 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1583 ACELT1584 ACELT1581 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Grace's Game
node.content: This book looks at when a girl named Grace starts her first day at after-school care. She waits and waits for the other children to ask her to play. When they don't, her mother suggests that she begins her own game. So she does! Soon, the other children join in Grace's game. Grace's Game introduces and reinforces the following strategies: making predictions & sequencing. Features of the book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: hoop, game, join, throw • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend pl
node.strategies: Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Grace wants to play with the other children at after-school care but she's too nervous to ask them. Can she find a way to join in?
node.esiss_activities: Re-read page 10. Imagine that Kayla and Grace invent a new game together. Write some instructions for someone who has not played the game before. Write the instructions in the correct sequence or order.
node.esiss_hfw: after, again, away, day, get, go, one, said, soon, want
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Penny the Firefighter
node.content: This story looks at the job of a firefighter. Penny is a firefighter who works the night shift. She arrives at the fire station and checks her gear and the fire engine to make sure everything is working. After she exercises, she has dinner, but the fire bell interrupts her meal. She and the other firefighters rush off to put out a fire in a garden shed. After they put out the fire, they return to the fire station and check their gear again before sitting down to eat. However, the fire bell rings again. Penny and the other firefighters head out to another emergency.Penny the Firefighter introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making connections; • determining main ideas; • determining cause and effect. Features of the text: • The information contained in the illustrations • Content words: checks, fire engine, firefighter, fit, gear, helmet, night, siren, station, training, works • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends"st; long vowel sounds"drives/fire/firefighter/night/siren/time; short vowel sounds" fit/is/rings/sit/this
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making connections|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: When the fire bell rings, Penny the firefighter jumps into action, ready to respond to any emergency!
node.esiss_activities: Write a job advert for a firefighter. Think about the kind of person that would like this job as well as the skills that person would need.
node.esiss_hfw: at, be, have, into, now, on, out, she, soon, they, this
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELA1822 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Fit for the Game
node.content: People take care of themselves by doing exercise. Exercise is fun and keeps you healthy. In this book, a girl describes her love of playing soccer. Vivid photographs and a simple first-person narrative convey the message that preparing for a game helps you improve and stay in shape as well as have fun. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by doing exercise.) Features of Fit for the Game • the topic (exercise), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • specialised vocabulary - defenders, forwards, midfielders, position • timetable • diagram • glossary (boldface type) • labels • close-up photos • initial consonant blends - "sp-", "pl-", "bl-", "sc-", "dr-", "tr-", "pr-","br-", "gr-", "st-", "fr-", "sl-"
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: If you play a sport like soccer, it pays to be fit for the game. Here's how you can make sure you train, eat, and play well. Sleep is important as well.
node.esiss_activities: The girl in this book is getting ready for a soccer game. Make a list for someone who wants to play soccer. What do they need to do to get ready? What do they need to wear? What do they need to eat?
node.esiss_hfw: all, as, down, from, has, have, make, now, see, some, too, what, when
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Healthy Teeth
node.content: People take care of themselves by keeping clean. This book discusses keeping teeth clean. The simple text, diagram, and photographs engage the reader and emphasize the importance of taking care of teeth. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping clean.) Features of Healthy Teeth • the topic (keeping teeth healthy), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • close-up photographs • cross section diagram • specialised vocabulary • glossary (boldface type) • final consonant blend - "-st" • digraphs - "th", "sh", "ch" • contraction - let's • compound word - sometimes
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: Clean teeth are healthy teeth, and healthy teeth are happy teeth! How do you make sure your teeth are happy? Read this book for some great tips!
node.esiss_activities: Draw a big tooth. Write "Healthy Teeth" inside the tooth. Around the tooth, write five things people can do to keep their teeth healthy. Hang your poster in the bathroom.
node.esiss_hfw: all, away, from, get, have, how, make, some, that, them, they, too, your
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: My Brother
node.content: Big brother is impatient to get to school and doesn't realise that his school bag has fallen open and things are falling out. His little brother rescues the fallen items and tries to get his brother to slow down. Suggested purposes This text supports the Comprehension strategies of using illustrations to understand text and making and confirming predictions. It can also be used to practise recalling details in a narrative. Text features • the large amount of decodable text, including repeated rimes • the variety of initial consonant blends • the digraphs -ch - lunch, lunchbox; -sh - shops, shouted; -th - brother, footpath, path, they, through, thump • the use of dialogue • the use of imperative verbs • the lively verbs - bounced, called, fell, ran, shouted • the irregular verbs - fell, gave, ran • the alternatives to "said" - called, shouted • the prepositions - along, down, out, past, through • the use of onomatopoeia - bump, clunk, flop, huff, pop, puff, thump • the compound words - footpath, homework, lunchbox • the contractions - didn't, we'll, we're, where's • the repetition of words for effect • the use of rhetorical questions • the relationship between the brothers • the multiple illustrations on page 11 • the visual sub-plot
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: The brothers are late for school. When the big brother gets to school, he sees that everything has fallen out of his bag. He's lucky that his little brother was running behind him.
node.esiss_activities: In the book, there are compound words. "Footpath" is a compound word. It is made up of the words "foot" and "path". Can you find another compound word in the book? Make a list of other compound words you know.
node.esiss_hfw: did, down, here, him, his, not
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: I Felt Angry
node.content: This story looks at the ways a person can calm down when he or she is angry. Amy spills her milk, gets hit by a ball, has trouble doing her class work, and loses her backpack. Each incident makes her feel angry. Her mother, her friends, and her teacher suggest ways that Amy can calm down, but none of these methods work. In the end, a friend's suggestion to run when she feels angry works for Amy. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion: angry, calm, felt - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - gr; short vowel sounds - and/any/backpack/ran/tagged Strategies I Felt Angry introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - determining author's purpose; - visualising.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Amy's mother, her friends, and her teacher suggest ways that Amy can calm down when she feels angry. Which way will work for Amy?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, Amy tries different ways to stop being angry. What do you do to calm down when you feel angry? Write some sentences that describe what you do. Do these ways work for you?
node.esiss_hfw: but, do, on, ran, she
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1447 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Hissing Bush
node.content: Two children come across a bush that makes hissing sounds, and they gradually get close enough to discover a goose guarding her nest. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions, noting details in illustrations, and summarising the Determining main ideas. Dynamic illustrations, lively dialogue, and the anticipation of solving the mystery encourage expressive reading. This is a good text to use for monitoring the integration of children's reading strategies at the end of the Blue level. Text features • the framework of high-frequency words • the initial consonant blends - cl, fl , pl, sn, st • the digraphs -ch - children, each; -sh - bush, she, show• -th - feathers, that, the, they, this • the words ending in -ed - asked, fluffed, hissed, jumped, laughed, looked, walked, whispered, yelled; -ing - hissing, playing; -er - closer, feather(s), other • the irregular past-tense verbs - heard, ran, said, took, went • the strong narrative structure and the dramatic climax • the lively dialogue that includes colloquial language - don't be silly, so weird • the contractions - didn't, doesn't, don't, I'll, it's • the alternatives to "said" - asked, whispered, yelled • the use of the ellipsis to build anticipation on page 9 • the use of italics and exclamation marks to support expressive reading • the use of onomatopoeia - hiss, hissss • the visual language features - the thought bubble on page 8, text in the illustrations, and the "noise" lines on page six Setting the scene
node.strategies: Making predictions|Summarising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Making predictions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: There's a strange hissing sound coming from the bush. The children investigate. Is it a snake? Is it a cat? No, this animal has feathers.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, the author uses different words to show how Nicola or James are talking. Which words has the author used instead of "said"? Choose three sentences from the book where "said" is used. Change "said" to another word to show how James or Nicola are talking.
node.esiss_hfw: her, here, that, were, what
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Way It Was
node.content: In this story, a young girl compares her life with her great-grandmother's. The illustrations are based on the period 1910-1920, but be aware that the dates of birth of your students' great-grandparents will vary. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of comparing and contrasting and drawing conclusions. The historical information has strong links to social studies. Text features • the comparisons between the present and the past, emphasised by the contrast between the sepia and the coloured illustrations • the first-person narrator • the indicators of time - every day, once a week, when it got dark, when it gets dark • the changes from the past to the present tense • the short, humorous ending • the irregular past-tense verbs - did, had, lit, made, rode, were • the compound words - grandma, sometimes • the variety of initial consonant blends • the use of an ellipsis for impact on page 9 • the use of commas for phrasing • the use of the adverb "too"
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: It was hard for great-grandma to do the washing or cook the dinner. These days, it's a lot easier. This book shows you why.
node.esiss_activities: Things were different long ago. Think of three things you do today. Now write to describe how someone would have done these things long ago.
node.esiss_hfw: did, gets, her, not, them, too, were, when
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1433 ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1449 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: What Can I Play?
node.content: This story looks at different kinds of instruments and their features. Lilly wants to play in the school band. She asks the music teacher Miss Timms what instrument she can play. Miss Timms introduces Lilly to different instruments, but she doesn't like something about each one. Lilly finally finds an instrument that she likes. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion -play, sound, try - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - tr; combined vowel sounds - loud/sound Purpose What Can I Play? reintroduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - making predictions; - comparing and contrasting.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Miss Timms shows Lilly lots of different instruments. Which one will Lilly want to play?
node.esiss_activities: Which instrument would you like to play? It could be from the book or it could be another instrument that you know. Write the name of the instrument. Describe why you would like to play it. What kind of sound would it make?
node.esiss_hfw: do, have, like, no, that, this, too, want, what
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELA1822 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: No, Skipper!
node.content: This humorous story features a boisterous family pet who would love to be allowed inside. When rain sets in, Skipper gets his opportunity. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of noting details in illustrations and summarising the Determining main ideas. This text requires the students to pay close attention to visual information. The lively nature of the narrative provides opportunities for expressive reading. This text is a companion to the Dragonflies early title Skipper's Happy Tail. Text features • the variety of initial consonant blends • the digraphs -ch - chase, chased, chair• -ph - Sophie • the -er word ending - corner, dinner, Skipper • the -ed verb ending - chased, fl ooded, opened, rained, stopped, wanted• -ing - looking, wagging • the irregular past-tense verbs - ate, came, said, sat, slept • the compound words - inside, outside, sometimes • the possessive apostrophe - Dad's, Greg's, Sophie's • the use of direct speech • the exclamation marks for emphasis • the use of commas for phrasing • the strong narrative structure • the characterisation of Skipper • the expressive illustrations
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Synthesising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Poor Skipper. He's always in trouble, so the family send him outside. But it rains and rains, and Skipper gets into more trouble than ever.
node.esiss_activities: Skipper is an outside dog, but he is allowed inside sometimes. Write some rules for when Skipper can come inside. Make a sign to put on the door for Skipper.
node.esiss_hfw: an, down, from, his, that
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Skeletons
node.content: Skeletons uses text and illustrations to show the various skeletons that animals have. Features of the Book • The use of photographs and diagrams to support the text. • The repetitive structure of the text to reinforce vocabulary. • The use of compound words - "inside" and "outside." • The repetition of the "sk" blend - "skeleton." • The use of the exclamation point. • The high-frequency words - "A," "it," "on," and "the." Purpose Skeletons can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce the following skills and strategies: • using visual clues to monitor that reading is making sense • blending sounds together to make a word • using knowledge of onset and rime to create new words from those in the story • incorporating diagrams to support information.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: A fish has a skeleton. So does a chicken. What about a bat or a snake? Read this book to see what animal skeletons look like.
node.esiss_activities: These animals have interesting skeletons._x000D_ Use plastic straws to make one of the skeletons._x000D_ Use scissors to cut the straws into bones._x000D_ Now glue the bones onto paper to match the skeleton.
node.esiss_hfw: a, it, on, the
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1439 ACELA1440 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Far Away Moon
node.content: Far Away Moon looks at how we see the Moon from Earth. Features of the Book • Clear, informational statements. • Capital letters for the proper names of the Sun, Earth, and Moon. • The use of light and shadow in the illustrations that helps convey the information in the text. • Italics used for emphasis. • Compound words - "moonlight," "daytime," and "sometimes." • The use of storybook language - "Far, far away." • The high-frequency words - "away," "can," "see," and "We." Purpose Far Away Moon can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • encouraging rereading of the text to clarify understanding • discussing the Cause and effect of specific events within the story • practising using poetic language to write poems.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: Find out some interesting facts about the moon, and why we can't see the other side of it.
node.esiss_activities: Sometimes you can see the whole moon, sometimes you can see only part of the moon, and sometimes you can't even see the moon. Draw four pictures that show the different shapes of the moon.
node.esiss_hfw: away, can, see, we
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Discovering My World (Early)
node.content: What do you know about your world? Discovering My World looks at the shapes of the land, people finding their way, different people and places, and people getting along and helping one another. Features of Discovering My World • general information about people and their place in the world (pages 2 to 7) followed by four • double-page spreads about specific geographical and civic features • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support and illustrate concepts • captions • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "st-", "tr" • digraphs - "sh", "ch" • plural ending "ies" - stories, countries
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: What do you know about the world around you? This book introduces you to the changing world and the things you can discover there.
node.esiss_activities: We live in an interesting world. Fold a piece of paper in half. On one side, draw a picture of something that is interesting about the land. On the other side, draw something that is interesting about the people. Write about your pictures. Use the book to help you.
node.esiss_hfw: and, is, it, my, the, this, to
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Make a Lei
node.content: In this simple procedural text, a young girl makes a lei, or necklace, of flowers. Features of the Book • Clear sequence of steps for children to identify and follow. • The "fl" blend - "flowers." • The "thr" letter cluster - "thread" and "through." • The double "ee" vowel - "need" and "needle." • Double consonants - "ribbon" and "scissors." • The silent "c" in "scissors." • The high-frequency words - "some," "You," and "your." Purpose Make a Lei can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • hearing the individual sounds in a word • retelling the sequence of the story using own words • developing the skill of writing instructions.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: A lei is a colourful flower necklace. This book shows you how to make one for yourself.
node.esiss_activities: Use the book to help you make your own lei. Ask an adult to help you. If you don't have any flowers, you could make some paper flowers. Wear your lei!"
node.esiss_hfw: some, you, your
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Paper Patchwork
node.content: This procedural text presents a method for constructing patchwork squares and putting them together to make a paper patchwork quilt. Features of the Book • Nouns for one-to-one matching. • The blends - "gl," "sc," "pl," "sq," "tr," and "fr." • Mathematical language - naming shapes, identifying numbers, using measuring skills, and sequencing actions. • Imperative verb form in the sentences. • The high-frequency words - "with," "Get," "some," "You," and "your." Purpose Paper Patchwork can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • hearing the individual sounds in a word • using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense • developing the skill of writing instructions • developing the skill of giving directions.
node.theme: Art and activity|Physical science|School and community
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Discussing point of view|Forming opinions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Discussing point of view, Forming opinions, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: It's easy to make a paper patchwork. This book shows you what you need, and gives you the steps to follow. This is a fun activity to do with your friends.
node.esiss_activities: Make your own paper patchwork. The book shows you what you need. Ask someone in your family or a friend to help you.
node.esiss_hfw: get, some, you, your
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Taking Care of Yourself (Early)
node.content: Taking Care of Yourself looks at how we keep healthy by keeping our teeth clean, eating healthy food, doing exercise, and keeping safe. Features of Taking Care of Yourself • introduction of health concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about taking care of ourselves (pages 2 to 7), followed by four double-page spreads about specific things we can do to care for ourselves • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text that supports and illustrates concepts • use of captions • initial consonant blends - "br-", "sch-", "pl-", "sc-", "sp-" • digraph - "th"
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Exploring text structure, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: When you learn how to be healthy and safe, you're taking care of yourself. Check out how to eat a healthy diet and exercise safely, and how to deal with your feelings.
node.esiss_activities: We do many things to take care of ourselves. Make a list of the things you do to get ready for school. How many things can you do on your own?
node.esiss_hfw: how, that, they, when, your
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: The Gardener's Maze
node.content: In this humorous narrative, the gardener designs and builds a maze with help from a friendly hedgehog! This text is a follow-up to the Dragonflies emergent title The Gardener and a companion text to the early title The Gardener and the Scarecrow. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of setting a purpose for reading and following steps in a process. Variety in the sentence structures means that the students will need to attend to punctuation to develop phrasing and fluent reading. Text features • the variety of initial consonant blends • the "all" rime - all, small, tall • the silent "e" - made, make, maze • the -ed verb ending • the irregular past-tense verbs - came, got, grew, made, put, sat, saw, went • the variety of word endings - garden, gardener, gardening, planting, plants • the possessive apostrophe in the title • the use of repetition for effect • the use of commas to support phrasing • the variety of sentence beginnings - at last , day after day , one wet day , the next day • the element of fantasy introduced by the cartoon style illustrations and the hedgehog companion • the humorous ending
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: The gardener shows how to build a maze. He planted the plants, and watered and clipped them. When the maze grew he went inside it. He found that it's hard to get back out of a maze!
node.esiss_activities: The gardener takes a long time to make his maze. You can make a maze on a sheet of paper. Get some ideas from the shapes in the gardener's maze. Give the maze to your family to solve.
node.esiss_hfw: get, here, his, one, put, saw, that, too, with

node.title: I Can Play Tangram
node.content: A story about two young children playing the traditional Chinese game of tangram. Features of the Book • Repetitive structure of the text. • Copymaster for tangram shapes on the inside back cover. • Inclusion of factual information. • The use of inset photos to assist text meaning. • The blends - "sq" and "ch." • Photographs supporting the text. • Regular use of a question. • The high-frequency words - "can," "from," "with," "this," "what," " you," and "them." Purpose I Can Play Tangram can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • reading everyday text within the classroom, for example, instructions • predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story • predicting outcomes from illustrations • developing the skill of writing instructions.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making inferences, Making predictions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: The boy and girl in this book can both play tangram. They can make a goose and a cat and lots of other shapes. Look in the back of the book to find out how they make them.
node.esiss_activities: You can make lots of different pictures with a tangram. Draw your own tangram. Use the pattern in the book. Can you make all the patterns in the book?
node.esiss_hfw: can, from, with, this, what, you, them
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Inferences, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Two Simple Rules
node.content: In my world, people need to get along and treat others with respect. In this book, a teacher and students discuss classroom rules. Vivid photographs and lively text help to place the reader in the classroom. (Big idea: People need to get along and help one another.) Features of Two Simple Rules • the topic (classroom rules), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • lists and charts • action photographs • questions • glossary (boldface type) • initial consonant blends - "gr-", "wr-", "sp-", "br-", "cl-" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "kn" • contraction - that's
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Discussing point of view, Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Ms Jones' class needed some rules, but they ended up with too many to remember. Find out how they got the list down to two simple rules.
node.esiss_activities: What rules do you have at home? Fold a piece of paper in half. On one side, draw a picture of something you do to respect people. On the other side, draw something you do to respect things. Share your pictures with your family.
node.esiss_hfw: after, all, down, had, her, how, our, put, some, them, two, us, what, your
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: One Hundred Acorns
node.content: This story is a version of Aesop's fable, Ant and Grasshopper, in which we see the benefits of thinking ahead. Squirrel notices the signs that winter is on its way and thinks of the food she will need during that time. Rabbit isn't concerned and is living for the moment. He is eating the grass and not thinking ahead to when it will be scarce. Features of the Book - A story with a message, taken from an Aesop's fable - The link to financial literacy - scarcity and choice - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - acorns, shiver, Squirrel, winter - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - gr, sh, sq, sw, tr; long vowel sound: week, green Purpose One Hundred Acorns can be used to reinforce the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - making predictions; - discussing point of view; - character study; - determining the author's purpose.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Financial Literacy|Food|Habitats|Problem solving
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Making inferences, Determining author's purpose, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Squirrel is busy getting ready for winter, while Rabbit is busy lying in the sun. Will Rabbit get ready in time?
node.esiss_activities: Rabbit didn't think ahead and plan for winter when there won't be much food. What might have happened if he had? Write a different ending to the story. Share it with a member of your family.
node.esiss_hfw: all, he, into, saw, was, went
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELT1575 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: Can We Have Some Bread?
node.content: This story looks at how people can "earn" goods by providing a service. Red Hen makes some bread, which Dog and Duck want to share. Red Hen points out that they didn't help make the bread, so they haven't earned a share of it. Dog and Duck help Red Hen by washing her dishes and sweeping her floor. This earns them some bread. At the end of the story, Dog, Duck, and Cat all help Red Hen make some more bread, and so they earn their share of it. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - goods and services - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - help, make - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends - br, sh; short vowel sounds - help/red/smell Purpose Can We Have Some Bread? introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - using prior knowledge; - making predictions; - discussing point of view; - determining the author's purpose.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Financial Literacy|Problem solving
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining cause and effect|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge , Determining cause and effect, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Red Hen has made some bread and Dog and Duck want some. How will they earn some bread?
node.esiss_activities: The animals in this story do some jobs to earn some bread. What jobs could you do around your home to earn something? Write a list of the things you could do. What would you like to earn for doing the jobs?
node.esiss_hfw: all, came, did, have, now, there, they, was, who, will
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Cause and Effect, Sequencing

node.title: Snap! Splash!
node.content: In this informational text, heron stalks a crab. Predictably, there is a happy ending for only one of the creatures - but which one will it be? Features of the Book • The use of onomatopoeia - "plip," "snap," and "splash." • The use of large, bold type for effect. • Creative placement of text for effect on page 9. • The rhymes - "wriggle," "jiggles" and "crab," "grabs." • The blends - "cr," "pl," "gr," "sn," "wr," "sl," and "sp." • The use of ellipses to indicate anticipation. • The range of verbs - "comes," "walks," "sees," "grabs," "wriggles," and "jiggles." • The high-frequency words - "for," "look," and "out." Purpose Snap! Splash! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story • hearing the individual sounds in a word • discussing several books that relate to the same theme • writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Comparing and contrasting, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: The heron is good at fishing. It has caught a crab. But what happens when the crab wriggles and jiggles?
node.esiss_activities: This book uses words like "snap" and "splash" to tell you that something is making a sound. What other sounds would the heron or the crab make. Write some words to show these sounds.
node.esiss_hfw: for, look, out
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Compare and Contrast, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1437 ACELA1440 ACELA1817 ACELA1818 ACELY1648 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELY1659 ACELY1660

node.title: T Shirts
node.content: Here's a family who's wild about their T shirts! A child narrates a sing-songy description of the family collection. Features of the Book • The rhyme -"he," "me," and "she." • The contractions -"I've," "we've," "there's," "Dad's," and "Mom's." • The ellipsis used to link the text. • The use of capital letters in the story to reflect the text in the illustrations. • The visual summary on the final page. • The use of a limited range of colors for effect. • The repeated blends -"gr" and "sh." • The cover text incorporated into the illustration. • The high-frequency words -"got," "his," "her," "she," and "has." Purpose T-shirts can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense • comparing the story to events in own lives.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining importance, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: All the people in this family have T shirts, and they are all different. When you hang them on the line, you get a poem!
node.esiss_activities: Some of the T-shirts in this story rhyme - he, she, me, three. What would your family have on its T-shirts? Choose a word and brainstorm all the words you know that rhyme with it. Draw T-shirts for your family with the words on them.
node.esiss_hfw: got, his, her, she, has, big

node.title: My New Red Rocket
node.content: Billie wants to buy a toy rocket, but she needs to earn more money to pay for it. She has enough money to buy the blue boat, but would rather wait for the toy she really wants. At the end of the story, Billie has the satisfaction of knowing that she helped pay for the toy. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - earning and spending - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - buy, earn, jobs, money, pay - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - bl, cl, gr, pl; long and short vowel sounds: shop/rocket/got/jobs: money/some Purpose My New Rocket can be used to reinforce the following skills and strategies: - making connections; - using prior knowlege; - making predictions; - making inferences; - determining the author's purpose
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Discussing point of view|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Making inferences, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: Billie wants to buy a new toy, but she doesn't have enough money to pay for it. How will she earn the money to buy the red rocket?
node.esiss_activities: Billie saves up for a toy that she really wants. What toy would you really like to buy? How could you earn the money to pay for it?
node.esiss_hfw: day, do, have, now, please, they, want, was, went, what

node.title: Let's Go Exploring
node.content: This story looks at exploring the world around you. A brother and sister decide to explore their garden on a sunny day. They discover a spider making a web, some strawberries growing, a bird's nest, and their cat's new kittens. These small discoveries are so enjoyable that they want to continue exploring the next day. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - discover, explore - Phonics and phonemic awareness: short vowel sounds -explore/let's/nest/next/then/web and long vowel sounds - discover/some/wonder Purpose Let's Go Exploring introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making connections; - making predictions - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: A girl and a boy explore their garden. They find a bird's nest and a spider making a web. What do they find in the shed?
node.esiss_activities: Explore your garden or backyard. First, make a map so you know where to go. Then explore the garden. What do you discover?
node.esiss_hfw: all, at, our, so, what, will
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice

node.title: Blackbird's Nest
node.content: Black bird's Nest is an expository text about the life cycle of the blackbird, presented as a poem. Features of the Book • The rhythm and rhyme. • The possessive apostrophe -"blackbird's." • The use of commas to mark a pause. • The use of Descriptive language -"lonely," "empty," "speckled," "big," "bare," "small," and "dead." • The rhyme -"grew" and "flew." • The distinct sequence of events in the blackbird's life cycle. • The use of alliteration -"Four bare babies in a blackbird's nest." • A variety of blends -"bl," "sp," "gr," "fl," and "sm." • The challenge of the verb change on page 7. • The high-frequency words -"sat," "from," and "what." Purpose Blackbird's Nest can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: -predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story• -using structural cues to monitor that reading is making sense• -using a range of punctuation to read expressively• -incorporating diagrams and tables into pieces of writing to support ideas.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Science and technology
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Watch as the baby blackbirds hatch, grow, and fly away from the nest their mother has made.
node.esiss_activities: This book is about an animal that hatches from an egg. What other animals do you know that hatch from eggs? Make a little book about these animals. Write the name of each animal. Draw a picture of each animal.
node.esiss_hfw: sat, from, what, one, big
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Recognising Bias and Prejudice

node.title: Slides
node.content: A small boy explores the features of a good slide. Features of the Book • The frequent use of the blends - "sl" and "th." • The repetitive structure of the text to reinforce vocabulary. • The use of photographs to support the text. • The use of question marks and exclamation points. • The high-frequency words - "you," "That," "This," and "has." Purpose Slides can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • blending sounds together to make a word • using visual clues to monitor that reading is • making sense • using a variety of punctuation to read expressively • selecting information from the story for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Making predictions, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Slides come in all shapes and sizes. Some even have water. Read this book to find out how slides work.
node.esiss_activities: This book is about all kinds of slides. Use the pictures to draw your own exciting slide. Write three sentences to say why the slide is exciting.
node.esiss_hfw: you, that, this, has

node.title: New Paper, Everyone
node.content: "New Paper, Everyone! is a simple procedural text about the process of making new paper from old paper. Features of the Book - The identifiable beginning, middle, and end. - The text gives instructions even though its form is poetic. - The illustrations give the reader more information than is stated in the text. - The text changes from a statement on page 2 to a rhythmic, rhyming poem over the following pages. - The repetitive sentence patterns on pages 4 to 9. - The repetitive sentence form - We ... - The use of ellipses on pages 10 and 11 to indicate time passing. - The sc blend - scoop. - The squ letter cluster - squish and squeeze. The high-frequency words - from, We, can, all, and up. Purpose New Paper, Everyone can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - blending sounds together to make a word; - predicting the meanings of new or unfamiliar words from the context of the story; - using a range of punctuation to read expressively. - developing the skill of writing instructions.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Summarising|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: It's easy to make new paper from old paper. This simple poem tells you how.
node.esiss_activities: Look through the book. What do you need to make paper? Write the instructions for making paper. Be sure to use your own words.
node.esiss_hfw: we, it

node.title: This Is My Family
node.content: In my world, there are many different countries. There are many different people. This simple first-person narrative is the story of one boy's family heritage, from his great grandfather to his immediate family. Historical and present-day photographs depict each generation and are supported by brief stories. (Big idea: There are many different countries and people in the world.) Features of This Is My Family • the topic (family heritage), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • black-and-white and color photographs • family tree diagram • specialised vocabulary • labels • glossary (boldface type) • compound words - grandparents, grandmother, grandfather, sometimes, became • plurals - daughters, stories • family words - grandparents, father.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Forming opinions|Sequencing|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Forming opinions, Synthesising, Asking and answering questions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Do you know where your grandmother was born? How about your great grandmother? The boy in this book knows a lot about his relatives and how they came from Italy to live in a new land.
node.esiss_activities: How many people are in your family? Where have they come from? Ask a family member to help you draw your family tree.
node.esiss_hfw: came, did, from, had, her, here, his, how, made, now, one, out, see, they, too, two, what, when, with
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Questioning, Sequencing

node.title: Volcano
node.content: In my world, the land has many different shapes. This book explains how a volcano can change the shape of the land. Vivid photographs and cross-section diagrams show how a volcanic eruption can create a mountain, island, or lake. (Big idea: The land can change shape over time.) Features of Volcano • the topic (landforms), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • subject-specific vocabulary - crater, eruption, lava, ash • vivid photographs • cross-section diagrams • captions that describe photographs • glossary (boldface type) • descriptive language - The ground shakes and rumbles initial consonant blends - "gr-", "bl-", "sl-", "cr-" • final blend - "-st" • digraph sounds - " th", "sh", "ch" • repetition of "bigger"
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Drawing conclusions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: When a volcano erupts it can change the shape of the land. What shapes can be formed from the lava of a volcano?
node.esiss_activities: This book has lots of facts about volcanoes. Look through the book and find three interesting facts. Write down the facts. Then tell someone in your family what you have learnt.
node.esiss_hfw: an, made, make, off, out, with
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Making Inferences, Compare and Contrast

node.title: Helping Planet Earth (Early)
node.content: Helping Planet Earth looks at how we can help planet Earth by using resources carefully, caring for animals in danger, keeping Earth clean, and reusing things. Features of Helping Planet Earth • introduction of environmental concepts and anchor words associated with the topic - general information about environmental issues (pages 2 to 7) followed by four double-page spreads about specific issues and suggested solutions • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with close text matches to support and illustrate concepts • captions • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "cl-", "gl-" • digraph - "th", "sh", "wh"
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Forming opinions|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Distinguishing fact from opinion, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: One day, the resources on planet Earth may run out. Find out how you can help to take care of the air, water, forests, and animals on Earth.
node.esiss_activities: Make a booklet to show people how to take care of Earth. Think of four things that people can do to help planet Earth. Share your booklet with your family and friends.

node.title: Pocket Money Day
node.content: This story looks at the way some children earn pocket money. Josh and his sister help their family by doing jobs around their home. They walk the dog, unload and put away groceries, set the table, clean up after meals, and work in the garden. At the end of the week they receive their pocket money, some of which they save and some of which they spend. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - earning and saving - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - jobs, pocket money, save - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends -kn, pl; short vowel sounds - jobs/pocket/shopping Purpose Pocket Money Day introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - using prior knowledge; - making connections; - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining main ideas|Discussing point of view
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Josh and his sister do jobs around their home and earn pocket money. What jobs do they do?
node.esiss_activities: The girl and the boy in the story do jobs around the house and earn pocket money. What jobs do you do around your home? Write a description of each job you do. You could draw a diagram to explain it, too.
node.esiss_hfw: all, do, get, our, out, with
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Finding Main Ideas
