node.title: How Long Do Animals Live?
node.content: How Long Do Animals Live? makes factual statements about the life spans of variety of animals. Features of the Book • Two lines of text per page. • Photographs to support the word change on each page. • The use of a timeline. • The use of question marks on each page. • The word "can" to show onset and rime. • The high frequency words - "can," "just," "for," and "like." • The blends - "ch," "gr," and "wh." Purpose How Long Do Animals Live? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • using knowledge of onset and rime to create new words from those in the story • using a range of punctuation to read expressively • predicting outcomes from the illustrations writing labels for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Activating prior knowledge, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Do you know how long a rooster lives? How about a duck? Read this book to find out how long all kinds of animals live.
node.esiss_activities: Make a timeline of the ages of the people in your family. Draw a picture of each person and write a label to show their age. Write factual statements comparing the life spans of two animals, for example, "A horse can live longer than a duck."
node.esiss_hfw: can, just, for, like

node.title: 1, 2, 3 ... Recycle
node.content: People need to recycle things. Using things again helps to save resources. This book explores how recycling can help solve the problem of plastic. It explains the recycling process in simple numbered steps, using photographs, fact boxes, charts, and diagrams. It includes tips on how the reader can help solve the plastic problem. (Big idea: People need to recycle things instead of throwing them away. Features of 1, 2, 3 Recycle • the topic (recycling), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • step-by-step action photographs • flow diagram • charts • captions • close-up photographs • glossary (boldface type) • specialised vocabulary • initial consonant blends - "sk-", "st-", "pl-", "cl-", "st-", "tr-", "sl-" • final consonant blend - "-st" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "ch", "thr" • contractions - it's • compound words - landfills, around, yourself, skateboard • procedural words - first, next, then • exclamation mark, question marks • descriptive language - Is plastic fantastic?
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Exploring text structure, Drawing conclusions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: You don't have to throw out paper, glass, plastic, and metal. You can recycle it so it can be used again and again. It's as easy as 1, 2, 3.
node.esiss_activities: Make three headings - Good, Bad, and Questions. Write three things that are good about plastic. Write three things that are bad about plastic. Write three questions you have about plastic. Ask someone in the family to answer your questions.
node.esiss_hfw: as, away, from, how, if, made, make, not, our, put, some, them, then, they, two, when
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Sequencing

node.title: How Many Legs
node.content: This book talks about how many legs different animals have, including animals that have no legs. How Many Legs? introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • compare and contrast • finding main ideas. Features of the Book • The information contained in the photographs and diagrams • Content words for discussion: fly, gallop, legs, predators, prey, sticky, suckers, toes, vibrations • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant digraph /ck/ • Synonyms for the word run
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Life science|Scientific investigation
node.strategies: Compare and Contrast|Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: How many legs does a spider have? Let’s find out which animals have 2, 4, 6, 8 and more legs!
node.esiss_activities: Geckos have four legs and spiders have eight legs. Compare and contrast a gecko and a spider. Use the book and the Internet to help you.

node.title: Where Are My Keys
node.content: This book looks at how Mum keeps losing her keys. Her daughter Gina has an idea to build something that will prevent mum from losing them. Mum also helps Gina remember something, too! Where Are My Keys? introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • sequencing • recognising cause and effect. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: pulley, idea, string, build • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant digraph wh-
node.strategies: Sequencing|Making Predictions |Recognising Cause and Effect |Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever lost something in the house? How did you find it?
node.esiss_activities: Re-read the two signs in the story. Write your own two signs: one from Gina to Mum and one from Mum to Gina.

node.title: Feeding Tiger
node.content: This book looks at how Tiger the cat is going to be fed when Oliver and Dad go away for the weekend. Dad buys a new feeder, but Oliver thinks that Tiger will be greedy and eat all the food at once. Oliver sets about inventing a customised cat feeder and teaches Tiger to tap his paw against the feeder whenever he is hungry. Feeding Tiger introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making connections • making predictions. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: feeder, paw, greedy, hungry • Phonics and phonemic awareness: aw (/aw/ sound))
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: In this story Oliver invents a new feeder for his pet cat Tiger. Have you ever invented anything?
node.esiss_activities: Think of an invention you could create that would solve a problem in your home. For example, an invention that would help you to tidy your room. Explain what the invention would do and how it would work.

node.title: Mum's Present
node.content: This book looks at two children who go shopping with their dad to find a present for their mum. When neither of the two children has enough money to buy a present individually, they come up with a good idea to resolve their problem. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: enough, cost, spend, money • Phonics and phonemic awareness: final consonant blend - nt. Mum's Present introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making inferences • making predictions
node.strategies: Making inferences, Making predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Making predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Ana and Joe are shopping for a birthday present for their mum. Will they have enough money to buy what they want?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine Ana or Joe are talking to the owner of one of the shops they visited. Write a conversation between either Ana or Joe and the owner. The owner might be able to offer solutions to the children's problem.

node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect

node.title: Can You Help Me?
node.content: This book looks at how we can overcome obstacles and solve problems with the help of friends. Farmer Dan sets off for the market to sell his cabbages. Along the way, his cart runs over a big stone and one of the wheels comes off. An old woman with a walking stick offers to help. The man thinks the woman is too old to help but she is clever and kind and manages to put the wheel back on. Can You Help Me? introduces and reinforces the following strategies: recognising cause and effect making predictions. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: farmer, cart, help, wheel • Phonics and phonemic awareness: words with the ee digraph and long /e/ sound (wheel, need/needed)
node.strategies: Recognising cause and effect, Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising cause and effect, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: The wheels have fallen off an old man's cart on his way to market. A man, a girl and an old woman pass by - who will be able to help him fix his cart?
node.esiss_activities: Re-read page 3 and think up different cause and effect situations. For instance, the horse might stand on a stone and hurt its hoof, or the cart could bounce up and down and Farmer Dan might drop the reins or slide off the cart.
node.esiss_hfw: ask, back, can, off, please, put, said, say

node.title: Meet the Grey Wolf
node.content: People need to care for animals that are in danger. Every animal needs to live in a safe habitat. This book introduces the grey wolf. The author uses humour and interesting facts to dispel myths about wolves and to describe the plight of this endangered animal. (Big idea: People need to care for animals that are in danger.) Features of Meet the Grey Wolf • the topic (endangered animals), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • historical photographs • bulleted list of facts • large photograph with labels • comparative maps with key • glossary (boldface type) • compound words - grandma, eyesight • varied word endings - "ing", "ed", "s" • initial consonant blends - "gr-", "sc-", "bl-", "pl-", "sm-", "str-", "cl-" • singular and plural forms - wolf, wolves
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting|Exploring text structure|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Synthesising, Asking and answering questions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Many people think that wolves are scary animals, but the grey wolf is really quite shy. Find out why this species nearly became extinct.
node.esiss_activities: Make a list of facts about the grey wolf. Which information is the most important? Try to find five big ideas. Draw the grey wolf. Add labels.

node.title: Being a Scientist (Early)
node.content: What does a scientist do? Being a Scientist looks at what scientists do and the areas they study: plants and animals, space, what things are made from and how they change, and how things work. Features of Being a Scientist • introduction of science concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about scientists (pages 2 to 7), followed by four double-page spreads about specific kinds of scientists • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with close text matches to support and illustrate concepts • captioned photographs • graph • initial consonant blends - "qu-", "st-", "pl-", "sp-", "dr-" • digraph sounds - "ch", "th" • multisyllabic words - understand, experiments, discover
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Scientists help us to understand the world. Find out about the skills a scientist needs to do a good job.
node.esiss_activities: Some scientists study plants and animals. The scientist in the book has made a bar graph. Look under a rock or under some leaves in your garden. What kinds of bugs do you see? Count the numbers of each kind. Make a bar graph like the one in the book.

node.title: All Aboard
node.content: Grandpa's boat, The Queen of Spain, lies unused on his front lawn. The boat hasn't completed its final sea voyage, however, as Kate and her friends take it on a magnificent journey to Spain. This is a lively and imaginative poetic text. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of identifying the Determining main ideas, visualising, and using illustrations to understand text. Text Features • the theme of co-operation and working together for enjoyment; the importance of dreaming and using your imagination • the unnamed, first-person narrator • the use of dialogue to drive the narrative • the -ing word ending - coming, moving, swishing, splashing, barking, cooking • the contractions - they're, we're, I'll, there's • the Descriptive language to create images in the reader's mind and enhance the story • the compound words - Grandpa, lookout • the exclamation marks for emphasis • the imperative verbs - look, listen • the use of rhyme - Spain, again • the content words • the action verbs - beat, whips • the simile - the sea is like a dangerous monster • the blends - oa, ai, ee, st, sl, sp.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The children in this book love playing on Grandpa's boat. They pretend to sail to Spain. The clouds become castles. The dogs become dolphins. They sail through a storm. Do they get home in time for dinner?
node.esiss_activities: The children use their imagination to go on a journey. They pretend to sail away in the boat in their backyard. Choose something at home, such as a chair or a bed. Pretend it is something else like a spaceship. Write your own imaginary adventure.

node.title: The Hole in the King's Sock
node.content: In this humorous fairy tale, the King commands his servants to mend a hole in his sock. Nothing works until, finally, the Queen comes up with a solution that means the King will never have cold feet again. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions and noting details in illustrations. Repetition in the text provides support for less confident readers. Text Features • the clear narrative structure • the fairy tale elements • the characterisation of the King and Queen • the use of imperative language by the King • the humour and in the inclusion of modern elements in the illustrations • the visual sub-plot involving the mouse • the use of exclamation marks for emphasis • the possessive apostrophe in the title • the irregular verbs - came, gave, made, wove • the double consonants - knitted, knitting, running • the words with silent letters - dough, knit, knitted, knitting, stitched, wriggled • the "old" rime - cold, gold Possible challenges • the words - learned, thread • the concept of knitting.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Asking and answering questions, Monitoring for meaning, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: There is a hole in the King's sock, and he is not happy. The cooks try to fix it. The gardeners try to fix it. In the end, the Queen helps the King solve the problem.
node.esiss_activities: The servants tried all kinds of things to fix the King's sock. Imagine the King has a hole in his shoe. Think of three clever ways you could fix the hole. Think of three funny ways to do it.
node.esiss_hfw: around, into, yourself

node.title: Every Drop Counts!
node.content: People need to use resources carefully. They should only use what they need. This book explores water, one of Earth's most essential natural resources. The simple text is displayed in a visually appealing way with highlighted facts, a diagram, and bold headings. (Big idea: People need to use resources carefully.) Features of Every Drop Counts • the topic (conserving water), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • layout shape of a water drop • bold type • photographs • flow diagram to explain water cycle • captions give startling facts in photos • close-up photos • glossary • bulleted list • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "st-","fr-", "dr-", "cl-" • final consonant blend - "-st" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "ch", "wh" • contractions - can't, don't, doesn't • compound words - whenever, everyone, inside, without, everywhere, sometimes • questions to introduce the topic
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Exploring text structure, Drawing conclusions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Every living thing needs water. There are more and more people on Earth, so we need to be careful about how we use, save, and take care of our water.
node.esiss_activities: We are lucky to have fresh water for drinking and washing. Make a list of the things you do that need fresh water. Talk to someone about why it is important to take care of the fresh water on Earth.

node.title: The Ant and the Grasshoppers
node.content: In this fable, retold as a play, Ant busily stores corn for the winter while the grasshoppers sing and dance in the sunshine, only to be caught out when winter strikes. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making and confirming predictions and summarising the Determining main ideas. You can use this text to familiarise the students with the format of a play and to encourage fluent, expressive reading. Please note that the word count for this play is high because there are character names and stage directions. Text Features • the conventions of a play - the list of characters, the role of the reader, the stage directions (written in the present tense and in italics) • the use of colour coding and upper-case letters to indicate the speaker; the repetition • the indicators of time - a long time away, day after day, the next day, winter is here • the use of italics (page 8) and exclamation marks for emphasis • the sense of tension as the grasshoppers' predicament unfolds; the lively illustrations • the cross section of Ant's home on page six • the word endings - -ing, -er, -y (as an "e" sound) • the "k" sound for "ch" in "characters" • the compound words - grasshoppers, everywhere, pancakes • the contractions - didn't, don't, I'll, I'm, it's, she'll, we'll, where's.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Food|History|Science and technology
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Ant is a hard worker. She tries to save plenty of food for winter. The grasshoppers just want to have fun. What will happen when winter comes?
node.esiss_activities: This story is told as a play. There are no speech marks. Choose one page of the play. Write this part of the play using speech marks. For example, "Ant! Ant!" said Grasshopper Two. "Come and dance with us!"

node.title: Wibble Wobble, Albatross
node.content: This informational text is an explanation of why the largest seabird in the world finds it hard to walk. Features of the Book • The information in the photographs. • The use of creative typography in the title. • The addition of the "s" to the verbs "glide," "leave," "live," "rest," "look," "come," "learn," and "wobble." • The use of exclamation points. • The words "Wibble wobble." • The diagram and text on the inside back cover. Purpose Wibble Wobble, Albatross! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • using punctuation cues to read instructionallevel books with expression • discussing the relationship between cause and effect within the story • using diagrams, charts, and
node.strategies: Determining importance|Discussing point of view|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Discussing point of view, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: The albatross spends a lot of its life at sea. It glides on the wind and catches fish. Why does it wibble wobble when it comes back to land again?
node.esiss_activities: An albatross is a very big bird. Think of five other birds. Write their names in order from the smallest to the largest. Draw the bird you like most. Write a label for it.

node.title: My Name Is Shoshana
node.content: Shoshana is starting a new school, and her mother explains that some of the children may have difficulty pronouncing her name. Shoshana's teacher introduces a game to help the class learn to say her name correctly and ensure that Shoshana feels welcomed and happy. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of summarising the Determining main ideas, noting details in illustrations, and understanding the author's purpose. The sensitive illustrations are a rich source of meaning. Text Features • the themes of inclusiveness and of the importance of pronouncing names correctly • the feelings of the characters revealed in the text and in the illustrations • the roles of Shoshana's mother and the teacher • the satisfying ending that links back to page 5 • the third-person narrator • the inclusion of dialogue • the indicators of time - as they walked, at last, after school, that night • the adjectives - beautiful, excited, favourite, new, pink, pleased, proud, strong, yellow • the contractions - couldn't, didn't, I'm, I'll, it's • the possessive apostrophe - Shoshana's • the single quote marks for "near, far, shining star" on page 7 • the rhyme in "near, far, shining star" • the instructions for the game on page 7.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Visualising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: It is Shoshana's first day at school. She is worried that people won't be able to say her name. Luckily, the teacher has a game that helps the class to learn everyone's names.
node.esiss_activities: Shoshana has an interesting name. Ask your parents how they chose your name. Find out what your name means.

node.title: Our Solar System
node.content: Some scientists study the stars and planets up in space. They help us to understand our solar system. This simple text is presented as if written by a child as part of a school project. Using photographs and childlike drawings, it includes brief descriptions of our solar system, such as the hottest planets, the biggest planets, moons, asteroids, and comets. (Big idea: Scientists study Earth and space.) Features of Our Solar System • the topic (our solar system), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • labelled diagrams • photographs • comparison chart • glossary (boldface type) • fact boxes • bulleted list • captions • proper nouns - Mars, Saturn • initial consonant blends - "sp-", "pl-", "dr-", "thr-" • final blend - "-st" • initial digraphs - "ch-", "th-", "sh-" • contractions - it's, that's • exclamation marks • comparative language - bigger than, more than, biggest, closest, hottest • report format as if written by a child
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Martin knows a lot about our solar system. He hopes to discover his own planet one day. If you want to know more about planets, moons, and comets, read Martin's special school report.
node.esiss_activities: Martin has made a book about our solar system. Make your own fact book about space. What are your favourite things in space? Which facts are the most interesting? Include these in your book, as well as some drawings.

node.title: My Skin Looks After Me
node.content: While Granddad and his grandson are out exploring, they find a lizard's skin, and so the story of skin and its characteristics begins. Features of the Book - The use of speech bubbles. - The exclamation marks and questions marks used in the speech bubbles. - The way the speech bubbles and text convey independent storylines. - The informative nature of the text. - The high-frequency words - My, all, me, and for. - The double vowels - oo and ee. - The blends - ex, pl, sk, sw, bl, and cl. The scr letter cluster - scrape. Purpose My Skin Looks after Me can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - using a range of punctuation to read expressively; - summarising the main parts of the story; - reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context; - developing an awareness of new vocabulary and what it means.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Using graphic sources, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever wondered why you have skin? The girl in this book knows a lot about skin and how it looks after her.
node.esiss_activities: This story uses speech bubbles to show people talking. Make up your own story. Use speech bubbles to show what the characters say.

node.title: Making Glass
node.content: Some scientists study what things are made from. They help us understand how materials change. In this book, the author interviews a glassblower about making glass. The questions structure the text while Katie's responses and the vivid photographs detail the procedure of making a drinking glass. (Big idea: Scientists study materials and how they change.) Features of Making Glass • the topic (making glass), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • procedural words - first, then, now, next, finally • step-by-step action photographs with labels • flow chart • inset photographs showing close-ups to highlight important details • interview format • labels • glossary (boldface type) • initial consonant blends - "st-", "sp-", "fr-", "gl-", "bl-", "dr-", "cr-", "qu-", "sl-", "str-" • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "ch" • contractions - I'm, it's • question marks • exclamation marks • verbs - use, make, heat, bend, stretch, blow • adverbs - slowly, quickly
node.strategies: Making inferences|Making predictions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Visualising, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Did you know that glass starts out as soft, white sand? Join Katie Brown as she shows you how this sand becomes smooth, clear glass.
node.esiss_activities: Look through the book and write a set of instructions explaining how to make a glass. Make sure your ideas are in order. Look at page 11 if you need help.

node.title: What's That Smell?
node.content: What's That Smell? is set in a family home just before dinner. Each new spread introduce a different kind of smell and the responses of the two main characters. Features of the Book - The use of speech bubbles to expand upon the narrative. - The use of interjections - Mmmm, Yumm, and Yummy. - Varied use of ellipsis, signaling a pause in speech within speech bubbles, but a change of focus when used in text sentences. - The blends - sm, pl, sn, and fl. - The blends occurring at the end of words - lp, lf, and ld. - The high-frequency words - that, can, his, has, for. Purpose What's That Smell? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense; - using a range of punctuation to read expressively; - discussing the cause and effect of specific events within the story; - incorporating diagrams, maps, and tables to support information.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Comparing and contrasting|Determining importance|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Determining importance, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: There are all kinds of smells around the house. What is the most delicious smell? The family and the dog have different ideas about that!
node.esiss_activities: Draw a map of Jessie and Rolf's travels and label the things they could smell at each location.

node.title: Moon Moon
node.content: "Moon Moon is a simple story about the full Moon, which is so bright that night-time is like daytime. Crabs and developing seedlings are sensitive to the full Moon's glow and become more active at that time of the month. On a cloudy night, the Moon can't be seen - because Nana has scared it into hiding with her ghost stories. Features of the Book - Rhyming words, such as ""glows"" and ""grows"". - Repetition of ""... when the full Moon glows above."" - Pages 2 to 7 follow a rhythmic pattern. - Contrast: the change of rhythm and sentence pattern on page 8 and a change of focus from a powerful Moon that makes things happen to a Moon that appears to be scared. (Now it's Nana who's powerful!) Purpose of the Book This book can be used to stimulate students' awareness and interest in the lunar cycle. The students will get the idea that the Moon influences living things even though it's a very long way off. The Moon has been the subject of much scientific research. It has also been central to folk traditions around the world. In Pacific folklore, ghosts become active when the Moon is full. The book ends on a whimsical note when the students find out that Nana can seemingly scare the moon with her ghost stories."
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: This is a short poem that explains why plants, crabs, and people like the shiny, full moon.
node.esiss_activities: Write the headings "Same" and "Different". What is the same about Earth and the Moon? What is different about Earth and the Moon? Write your ideas under the headings.

node.title: A Good Idea
node.content: On a hot day, four African animals are inspired with good ideas about how to stay cool. They soon realise that they need to co-operate so that they can all get what they want. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of identifying the Determining main ideas and making and confirming predictions. The repetition provides support for less confident readers, while the engaging vocabulary provides rich opportunities for exploring language and practising strategies to unlock new words. Text Features • the theme of co-operation • the cumulative, repetitive text • the high proportion of dialogue • the content words and animal names • the thought bubbles • the adjectives - hot, shady, good, sweet, nice, bubbly, perfect, big • the use of italics and exclamation marks for emphasis • the use of commas to support phrasing • the lively, humorous illustrations • the infinitive and past-tense verbs - lie, lay• nibble, nibbled• drink, drank• roll, rolled • the contractions - I'm, that's, I'll, you've • the words with "y" as an "ee" sound - bubbly, everyone, plenty, sadly, shady, very • the initial consonant blends - cr, dr, fr, gr, pl, spr, sw, tr • the digraphs - sh, th.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining main ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: It's a hot day and all the animals want to lie under the shady tree. Will there be enough room? Rhinoceros has a good idea.
node.esiss_activities: Some of the pictures in this book have thought bubbles. They show what each animal is thinking. Imagine that a giraffe and a hippo also wanted to go with the animals. Draw pictures that use speech bubbles to show what the giraffe and the hippo are thinking.

node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising

node.title: I Want to Be the Fox
node.content: In this narrative, Josh is disappointed when he is given the part of the rabbit in the school play, but no one seems to understand his problem. Finally, Josh comes up with a way to make the best of the situation. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of understanding the author's purpose; summarising the Determining main ideas; and interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions. It's a good model of a narrative with a satisfying and positive conclusion. The lively dialogue encourages expressive reading. Text Features • the themes of co-operation and determination, of overcoming challenges, and of self-discovery • the relationships between the characters and the different points of view • the way Josh resolves the situation • the high proportion of dialogue • the strong narrative structure and the satisfying ending • the indicators of time - the next day, that evening, when, on the night, soon, later • the change in pace from page 8 once Josh has made his decision; the large number of lively verbs - talked, hopped, bobbed, flapped, tricked, laughed, cheered, clapped • the imperative verbs - hop, make, try • the variety of word endings - ed, ing, er • the comparative adjectives - higher, faster, later • the irregular past-tense verbs - had, said, chose, put • the contractions - couldn't, didn't, don't, that's, I'd, I'll, you'll, you're • the singular and plural forms on page 4 - that and those, and this and these • the initial consonant blends - cl, fl, pl, sch, sm, st, tr • the digraphs -ch - chose, children, cheered; -gh - laugh, laughed, laughs • the doubled consonants - hopped, bobbed, flapped, clapped • the possessive apostrophe - Josh's • the "y" with an "ee" sound - funny, everyone, silly; with a long "I" sound - try, why • the "ight" rime - right, night.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Josh is in the school play, but he isn't happy. He is the rabbit, and he has to wear a tail and big ears. He worries that people will think he is silly.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, the rabbit has to hop, bob, and flap. What words would you use to describe how the fox moves? Draw the fox. Write the words around your picture.

node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning

node.title: Finding Tibs
node.content: Zara's cat Tibs is a special member of her family. One morning, Tibs goes missing. Being blind doesn't stop Zara from helping, and she uses her special skills to find Tibs. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making connections with real-life experiences, noting details in illustrations, and understanding the author's purpose. The repetition provides support for less confident readers, while the engaging and descriptive vocabulary provides rich opportunities for exploring language. Text Features • the theme of the importance of family, including pets, co-operation and of overcoming difficulties • the use of simile for description - his tongue was rough, like sandpaper • the relationship between Zara and her cat • the way Zara resolves the situation through perseverance • the strong narrative and satisfying ending • the word endings - ed, ing • the contractions - I've, isn't, wasn't, didn't • the repetitive text • the adjectives - dark, fuzzy, silky, soft • the content related/cat verbs - rubbed, nudged, licked, sleeping, pat • the diagraphs -gh - rough• -dg - nudged• -tch - scratch, switched • the soft detailed illustrations • the use of commas for phrasing • the compound words - sandpaper, cupboard.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Zara is blind. Her special friend is her cat Tibs. When Tibs goes missing, Zara looks for him. How does she do that?
node.esiss_activities: Zara is blind, but she finds Tibs. She listens very carefully. Make a list of the sounds that Zara would hear in the morning. What would each sound tell her?

node.title: Is That an Earthquake?
node.content: A young boy wonders anxiously whether the movements around his home could be an earthquake. Suddenly there is an earthquake, and the family act quickly. The story incorporates a recount and an explanation and is told through dialogue. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making connections with real-life experiences and using illustrations to understand text. It includes a high number of descriptive verbs that encourage attention to initial blends, onomatopoeia, and rhyme. Text Features • the use of dialogue to tell the story • the portrayal of the characters' feelings through the text and the illustrations • the repetitive Text structure and the dramatic change on pages 8 to 11 • the change in tense on page 11 • the lively, descriptive verbs; the imperative verbs on page 9 • the italics and exclamation marks for emphasis • the rhyme; the personification on pages 3 and 6 • the inclusion of the family dog in most of the illustrations • the examples of verbs that involve dropping the final "e" before adding "ing" Possible challenges • the use of dialogue to tell the story • the poetic language • the terms "logging truck" and "goods train" on pages 3 and 5.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Every time the house shakes, the family asks if it is an earthquake. First a train passes. Then the wind blows. What happens when there is a real earthquake?
node.esiss_activities: The author has used different words to describe the sounds in the story For example, "rattling and "chattering". Make a list of the other "sound" words in the story. Choose two words. Write them in a sentence that describes the earthquake.

node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Summarising, Monitoring and Clarifying

node.title: The Big Game
node.content: A young boy compares the experience of watching soccer in his old country with that of watching football in his new country, the United States. Features of the Book • The specialised vocabulary - "stadium," "cheerleaders," "buses," "musicians," and "crowd." • Language signaling change - "I used to" and "But here." • The use of commas to separate phrases. • Plural forms of nouns, especially "families." • The blends - "dr," "st," "sh," "pl," "ch," and "cr." Purpose The Big Game can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the themes of the book • discussing the organisational structure of the text • recognising and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • writing using a variety of sentence patterns.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making connections, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: The boy in this story tells you how they play the big game in his old country and his new country. The games are different, but he loves them both.
node.esiss_activities: What kinds of sports do you know? Choose the sport you like best. Write down the things you need to play the game. Draw a picture of them.

node.title: Football Fever
node.content: Football Fever conveys the enthusiasm that young children often feel about their chosen sport - revealing a final surprise. Features of the Book • The use of clipped sentences to indicate speed. • The use of exclamation marks. • The repeated phrase - coach always says. • The use of capital letters for emphasis. • The use of ellipses to indicate pauses. • The language associated with football - winger, coach, referee, centre, full-back, midfielder, trap, dribble, and goalkeeper. • The blends - gr, br, dr, wh, ch, and sc. • The contractions - We've, Let's, It's, goal's, we'll, and you're. • The simile - like a tiger. Purpose Football Fever can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding; • encouraging prediction and developing questioning skills; • recognising and reading a variety of word patterns, including different word endings; • developing the skills of writing using direct speech.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: On Saturday mornings, these kids can't wait to get to their football game. Why do they play so well? They have a very special coach!
node.esiss_activities: The words "Football" and "Fever" both start with the letter "f". Find an old magazine or newspaper. Cut out things that begin with an "f". Make a poster with the pictures.

node.title: Car Smart
node.content: People need to keep planet Earth clean. This book explores the problem of air pollution caused by cars. It describes new kinds of cars and fuels that help reduce air pollution and suggests simple, everyday ways to help to clear the air. (Big idea: People need to stop polluting the air.) Features of Car Smart • the topic (reducing air pollution), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • specialised vocabulary • photographs and captions • bullet points • captions • glossary (boldface type) • initial consonant blends - "cl-", "sp-", "tr-", "sk-", "sm-", "fr-", "sc-" • final consonant blend - "st"
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Discussing point of view|Making inferences|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Discussing point of view, Comparing and contrasting, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Cars are useful if you want to go somewhere, but most cars pollute the air. How can we be smart about driving cars and keeping the air clean?
node.esiss_activities: Most cars make a lot of pollution. But some cars are better for the environment. Design your own car. What will it run on? How can you make it good for the environment?

node.title: The Big Surprise
node.content: This is the story of two little girls awaiting the arrival of a new baby in their family and their suprise when they discover that their mum has had twins. Features of the Book • The final "y" consonant - "lovely," "baby," and "ready." • The use of the contractive apostrophe - "I'm," "I'll," "it's," and "she's." • The use of direct speech. • The use of italics for emphasis. • The use of bold type for effect. • The visual interest in the photographs. Purpose The Big Surprise can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • encouraging prediction and developing questioning skills • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • developing the skill of writing in a sequential manner.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making predictions

node.esiss_blurb: There's going to be a new baby in the house, and Neela and Hersha are excited. When the girls visit Mum in the hospital, she has a big surprise for them.
node.esiss_activities: Some words in this book end with the letter "y". Look through the book and find the words that end with "y". Make a poster of words you know that end with the letter "y". You will need a piece of paper and some coloured pens.

node.title: Let's Swap
node.content: This story looks at two ways to obtain a good - by swapping (bartering) another good for it or by buying it with money. Some children want slices of watermelon from Mr Moon's stand at the outdoor market. One swaps his hat for a slice, another trades some flowers, and another exchanges some feathers. The last child in line has no goods to swap, but has enough money to buy a slice of watermelon. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - money - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - money, swap - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - fl, sl; long vowel sounds - moon/cool and short vowel sounds - good/look Purpose Let's Swap introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - using prior knowledge; - making predictions; - discussing point of view; - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Some children want to buy watermelon at the market, but some of them don't have any money. What will they swap for a slice of watermelon?
node.esiss_activities: In the play, the children swap the things they have for a slice of watermelon, except for Nic who has some money. Write a list of other things the children in the play might swap for a slice of watermelon.

node.title: The Lunchbox
node.content: This is a story of food left in a lunchbox - an experience many readers may have had. Features of the Book - The alliterative language. - The high-frequency words - "by", "her", "went", "she", "look", "said", and "this". - Sequence of days in the week. - Repetition of the phrase "Bobby Bently's lunchbox". - Repetition of verbs for rhythmic effect. - The visual changes in the illustrations that signal the food's gradual decay. - The rhythmic pattern of the text from page to page. - The use of the possessive apostrophe. - The use of ellipses. Purpose The Lunchbox can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - predicting outcomes from illustrations; - discussing the cause and effect of specific events within the story. - planning how to sequence the events of a piece of narrative writing.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: What happens when Bobby Bently leaves her lunchbox under the bed. Watch as her leftover lunch changes from day to day.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are one of the ants that finds the lunchbox. Write a sentence about each kind of food the ant finds. If you were an ant, what would you eat from the lunchbox?
node.esiss_hfw: in, the, they, went

node.title: The Tricky Sticky Problem
node.content: The Tricky Sticky Problem shows children using a problem-solving approach - identifying a dilemma and making a series of attempts to remedy the situation. Features of the Book - The design strip running down the left-hand edge of page 2. - The type for emphasis on the initial word in the story. - The rhymes - drain, again, and stick, trick. - The use of direct speech. - The way the illustrator has added extra touches to the story that are not mentioned in the text. - The high-frequency words - said, get, them, his, her, and she. Purpose The Tricky Sticky Problem can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - hearing the individual sounds in a word; - predicting outcomes from illustrations; - summarising the main parts of a story; - developing an awareness of the specific structure within story.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Activating prior knowledge, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: How do you get car keys out of a drain. The children try everything, until Katie uses science to solve the problem.
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book do some clever problem solving. What problem are they trying to solve? Invent something else you could use to solve the problem. Draw a picture of what it would look like.

node.title: My World from Above
node.content: Sometimes, people need help to find their way from one place to another. In this book, a boy describes his neighbourhood, city, county, state, and country. Simple maps depict his home from each perspective. (Big idea: People need help to find their way from one place to another.) Features of My World From Above • the topic (finding your way), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • maps with and without keys • captions, labels, and arrows to show and describe locations • glossary • some repetitive simple sentence structure • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "gr-" • contraction - it's
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Drawing conclusions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine looking down on your street, town, or even country from above? What would you see?
node.esiss_activities: Ask someone to help you make a map of your neighbourhood. Draw your house. Draw the streets. Draw any parks or shops. Use your map to tell someone how you would get from your house to somewhere else in the neighbourhood.

node.title: Hay for Ambrosia
node.content: This infomational text explains how hay is made and why it is needed. Features of the Book • The high-frequency words - for, with, have, will, come, and out • The rural setting • The distinct sequence of the process • The special function of each machine • The use of specialised verbs - dries, turns, gathers, presses, and, ties • The additional information on page 12. • The "er" word ending - weather, mower, other Purpose Hay for Ambrosia can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • Introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story; • Using visual cues to monitor that reading is making sense; • Encouraging rereading of the text to clarify understanding; • Selecting information from the story for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: When it's hot and sunny, it's time to make hay. A lot of farm animals eat hay over the winter, including Ambrosia the donkey.
node.esiss_activities: This book talks about how hay is made. What are the steps for making hay? Write the steps in order on a flow chart.

node.title: The Best Bike of All
node.content: This story looks at how people distinguish between the things they need and the things they would like to have. A family goes shopping for a new bike for Mum. In looking at the different features of each bike, there are some she needs and some that aren't necessary, but are appealing. At the end of the story, the family finds a bike that has all the features they need, plus some great extras. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - needs and wants - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - need, want - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends - br, fl; long vowel sounds - bike/find/ride; word endings - ing Purpose The Best Bike of All introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - using prior knowledge; - making predictions; - determining the author's purpose.
node.strategies: Summarising|Synthesising

node.esiss_blurb: Mum needs a new bike. What things does she need on her bike? What kind of bike will Mum buy?
node.esiss_activities: What kind of bike would you like to own? Think about the things you would need on your bike. What things would you like on your bike, but not really need? Draw a picture of your bike. Write labels for each part.

node.title: A New Cage for Nutmeg
node.content: This story looks at ways to raise money by selling goods and services. Nutmeg, the class pet rabbit, needs a new cage. The students think of ways to raise money. Some make muffins and lemonade to sell at lunch time. Two students sell a service (dog walking) instead of goods. At the end, the class has raised enough money to buy a new cage for Nutmeg. Features of the Book - The link to financial literacy - goods and services - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - job, pay, raise, sell - Phonics and phonemic awareness - consonant blends - gr, th; short vowel sounds - but, fun, lunch, up, us Purpose A New Cage for Nutmeg introduces and reinforces the following skills and strategies: - making inferences; - using prior knowledge; - making predictions; - discussing point of view - determining the author's purpose.

node.esiss_activities: The children in this book think of different ways to raise money to buy a new cage for Nutmeg. What other things could they do to raise money? Which thing would you like to make or do?

node.title: Doctor Fox
node.content: This story follows a father and son who visit the doctor, and takes a humorous look at their different reactions to the situation. Maxwell Meerkat cuts his foot and his father takes him to the doctor. Maxwell is comfortable at the doctor's. In contrast, Maxwell's father is increasingly anxious. When the doctor decides that Maxwell needs stitches and an injection, the news doesn't bother Maxwell, but it makes his father feel unwell. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - doctor, nurse, team, worried - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - cl; long vowel sounds - between/feel/knees/meerkat/needle/see Purpose Doctor Fox introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - discussing point of view; - determining author's purpose; - sequencing.

node.esiss_blurb: Maxwell cuts his foot and his father takes him to the doctor. Why does Maxwell act as a nurse to his father at the doctor's?
node.esiss_activities: Maxwell cuts his foot and goes to the doctor. Make a timeline of the story. Write down the events that happen. Make sure they are in order. Start when Maxwell cuts his foot. Finish with the nurse thanking Maxwell for helping his father. Share your timeline with a family member.

node.title: The Safe Place
node.content: A small brown hen looks for a safe place to hide on the Pacific atoll where she lives. Features of the Book • A story from the Pacific Islands of Tokelau. • The conventional storybook beginning - "Once upon a time." • The contraction "isn't." • The use of compound words - "herself," "inside," and "everyone." • The use of direct speech - "Is this a safe place?" • Repetitive phrasing on pages 4 to 7. • The map on the inside back cover. Purpose The Safe Place can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • encouraging prediction and developing questioning skills • Summarising the ideas from a story • writing to support the events in the story • developing the ability to write specifically about the detail of the setting of the story.

node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Making connections, Sequencing, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: On the tiny island of Fenuafala, a hen is looking for somewhere safe. She looks in all kinds of places, then finds the perfect spot. Why does she need a safe place?
node.esiss_activities: Where could Hen safely hide at your place? Draw a picture of the safe place. Write about why it is safe.

node.title: A Bright Idea
node.content: This story looks at how to solve problems by thinking of new ideas. Mickey Mole is lost in the woods at night. His family want to search for him, but they are worried about losing their way in the dark. At first, they think the moonlight might light their way, but the night is cloudy. Mindy then suggests that they use fire sticks. The family sets out by firelight and finally finds Mickey. A sudden downpour puts out the fire sticks leaving the family unsure of how to find their way back home. In the end, Mindy has another idea to solve the problem. She catches fireflies in a jar and uses them to light their way back through the dark woods.A Bright Idea introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • determining main ideas; • determining cause and effect; • making predictions. Features of the text: • The information contained in the illustrations • Content words: bright, find, fireflies, help, idea, know, look, Mole, use, work • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends" fl and th; long vowel sounds" moon/too/poor and meeting/see/tree; word endings" -ight
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Recognising Cause and Effect| Making Predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Predictions
node.esiss_blurb: It's dark and Mickey Mole is lost in the woods. How can his family light their way to find Mickey and bring him home?
node.esiss_activities: List the problems the mole family faced in the story and then list the solutions they found.

node.title: Wash It Away
node.content: People take care of themselves by keeping clean. Washing or showering helps to remove dirt and bacteria from your skin. This book explains the importance of keeping clean. A straightforward second-person narrative explains how keeping clean helps us feel good about ourselves, and vivid photographs show that keeping clean can be fun. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping clean.) Features of Wash It Away • the topic (keeping clean), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • contents and index pages • close-up pictures to show detail • flow diagram • captions • bulleted list • fact boxes • glossary (boldface type) • digraph sounds - "th", "sh" • initial blend - "br-", "sw-", "cl-", "pl-", "spr-" • contractions - don't, it's, you're • compound words - bathroom, fingernails, yourself, into
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making inferences|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Making inferences, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Germs and bacteria live in warm damp places, like your sneakers. Learn how you can take charge of germs that live on your skin, in your hair, and in your clothes.
node.esiss_activities: The author explains how you can take charge of germs. Make a poster that you can share with the class. Write the heading, "Take Charge of Germs". Use words and pictures to explain how to take charge of germs on your clothes and skin, and in your hair.

node.title: Panda Emergency
node.content: Some animals are endangered. People need to keep these animals safe and care for their habitats. This text looks at the endangered giant panda and explains what is being done to help keep it safe from extinction. (Big idea: People need to keep endangered animals safe and care for their habitats.) Features of Panda Emergency • the topic (endangered animals), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • maps • close-up photographs • captions • contents page, glossary (bold type), index • bulleted lists • fact boxes • digraph sounds - "wh", "sh", "th" • contractions - can't • exclamation mark • question marks • compound words - everyone, without, behave, highways, outside, forever, together

node.esiss_blurb: There are only around 1600 pandas left in the wild. People have cut down much of the bamboo that pandas like to eat. Now the species is endangered. Read about how scientists and the World Wildlife Fund are working to save the panda from extinction.
node.esiss_activities: Scientists are helping to protect pandas. Make a poster called "Protect Pandas". Using the book, write about three problems that pandas face. Explain how people are helping to solve these problems. Share your poster with your family.

node.title: Fix It, Annie
node.content: This story looks at a day in the life of a car mechanic. Annie works as a car mechanic. She is good at her job and many people take their cars to her to be repaired. Annie likes her job so much that on weekends, she works on her own car. Purpose - The information contained in the illustrations. - Content words for discussion - fix, like, problem, wrong - Phonics and phonemic awareness: long vowel sounds - broken/hold/old/won't; short vowel sounds - job/lots/on/problem/wobble/wrong Strategies Fix It, Annie introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - comparing and contrasting; - determining author's purpose.

node.esiss_blurb: Annie fixes cars. Some cars are easy to fix, others are hard to fix. Why won't Emma's car start?
node.esiss_activities: Lots of people bring their cars to Annie to fix. She has to work out what the problem is and then fix it. What skills would a car mechanic need to do their job well? What kind of person would be good at this job? Write a list of skills and qualities a car mechanic would need.

node.title: I Like that Beat!
node.content: This story looks at the benefits of practice and perseverance when learning to play an instrument. Bear is learning to play the drums, but the other animals complain about the noise. Parrot tells him he must go and practice where no one can hear him. A few days later, the animals discover Bear playing the drums. His playing has greatly improved. Bear's perseverance inspires Gorilla to learn to play the guitar. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - beat, played, practice - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - scr; str; vowel blends - beat/ears/hear/really; word endings - ed Purpose I Like that Beat! introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - asking and answering questions; - determining author's purpose.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining author's purpose|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Bear is learning to play the drums, but the noise sounds terrible and the animals tell him to go and play somewhere else. Does Bear's playing get better with practice?
node.esiss_activities: When Bear starts playing the drums, the sounds he makes are described as "Bang, Boom, Bang!" or "Bash, Bang, Boom!" When his playing is better, the sounds are described as "Ba-da-boom!" and "Ba-da-boom-boom!" Choose one or two instruments such as the guitar or the violin. How do you think the instrument would sound when it is played badly. Write the sounds. How do you think it would sound when it is played after practice. Write the sounds.

node.title: A Very Clever Possum
node.content: This narrative describes how Mr Lees sets a trap to save his favourite rosebush from a hungry possum. Night after night, the wily possum manages to get the food out of the trap and escape. Information about possums is included on page 12. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making inferences, drawing conclusions, and understanding the author's purpose. Text Features • the narrative structure • the way the possum outwits Mr Lees • the open ending • the fact that the possum is never actually seen by the characters • the possum "thought bubble" on page 12 • the information about possums on page 12 • the indicators of time - at last, after a while, in the morning, that night, the next morning • the use of a first-person narrator • the relatively long sections of direct speech • the poetic language on pages 2, 3, six, and 8 • the irregular verbs - heard, hid, lay, ran, woke • the verbs that involve doubling the final consonant - slammed, stopped • the verbs that involve dropping the final "e" - dancing, shaking, using • the use of onomatopoeia (page 2) • the image of the possum dancing (pages 3 and six) • the use of personification (page 8) Possible challenges • the ideas of the possum and the branches "dancing" and the wind "singing" • the reasoning behind Mr Lee's solution to the problem (on page 11) • the reason for the possum "thought bubble" on page 12.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Mr Lees is grumpy because a possum is eating his roses. He wants to trap the possum, but it's too clever. In the end, he thinks of a plan that suits everyone.
node.esiss_activities: Mr Lees makes a trap to catch the clever possum, but it doesn't work. Design a trap that won't hurt the possum. Write labels to show what each part of the trap does.
node.esiss_hfw: could, into, under
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1450 ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1464 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Pop! Pop! Pop!
node.content: This is a humorous family narrative about a girl and her older sister who find out how not to make popcorn. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making connections with real-life experiences, making and confirming predictions, and following the steps in a process. It lends itself to expressive reading and provides excellent models for writing. Text Features • the dramatic and lively nature of the text • the strong narrative structure • the humour in the text and the illustrations • the characterisation of Jo • the expressive dialogue • the use of italics, repetition, onomatopoeia, and exclamation marks for effect • the lively verbs - jumping, pushing, popped, squealed • the many alternatives for "said" - asked, called, cried, laughed, squealed • the indicators of time - after a few minutes, after dinner, after a while, soon • the contractions - couldn't, I'm, it's, won't, wouldn't • the variety of forms of some verbs - laugh, laughed• make, making• popped, popping• tried, trying • the possessive apostrophe in "Toni's" • the "ur" sound - burn, turned Possible challenges • the words - enough, spoonfuls, squealed • the idea of a gas stove • the term "popping corn".
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Toni and her sister Jo make popcorn together. Jo says she is good at making popcorn, but she puts too much corn in the pot. What do you think happens?
node.esiss_activities: Write a list of instructions for making popcorn. Use a number for each step. Make sure the steps are in order. Ask a family member if you can make some popcorn with them.
node.esiss_hfw: again, around, into
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Why Cats Wash after Dinner
node.content: In this folktale from South Carolina, a tiny mouse uses its wits to escape from a hungry cat. Features of the Book • The classic opening phrase. • The blends - "sh," "st," "cr," "tr," and "fr." • The letter clusters - "str" and "squ." • The graphic by each page number. • The use of the possessive apostrophe. • The close picture/text match. • The use of direct quotes. Purpose Why Cats Wash After Dinner can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: identifying the plot and characters within the story • encouraging prediction and developing questioning skills • recognizing the features of different genres • developing the ability to write specifically about characters and their feelings within the story.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Do you know why cats clean their paws when they've finished eating their dinner? It all started with a clever mouse!
node.esiss_activities: This book has lots of blends in it. Find the words that start with st, cr, fr, and str. Make a list of other words you know that use these blends.
node.esiss_hfw: her, going, when, what, before, your, here, all, from, off
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Shimbir
node.content: This delightful narrative describes a Somali girl's experience of her first day at school in a new country. When her teacher asks her about the wonderful bird she has painted, all Anisa says is "shimbir", the Somali word for bird. The class supports Anisa by creating a report about Anisa's bird and making it into a class big book. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of identifying the Determining main ideas and understanding the author's purpose. This story will encourage discussion about facing new situations and provides an excellent model of using descriptive language. Text Features • the first-person narrator • the way the class supports Anisa • the inclusion of a text within a text • the shift in time from page 5 to page 6 with the children having written and illustrated the big book • the change from past tense in the main text to present tense in the big book • the relatively complex sentences • the unusual perspective in the illustration on page 12 • the adverb "proudly" on page 4 • the adjectives - beautiful, big, blue, bright, first, green, happy, little, red, wonderful, yellow • the suffix "ful" - beautiful, wonderful • the possessive apostrophes on pages 2, 3, and 5 • the "ies" plural ending - berries • the -ea digraph - eats, leaves, speak, teacher Possible challenges • the sentences that include more than one idea • recognising and understanding why Anisa feels more confident by the end of the story.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Monitoring for meaning, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: On her first day at school, Anisa draws a beautiful bird. She says it is a bird called Shimbir. It is from her country. The class decides to make a book about Anisa's bird.
node.esiss_activities: Anisa's favourite bird is called Shimbir. Draw a picture of your favourite bird. Write about the bird using interesting words that describe what it does, what it looks like, and what it sounds like.
node.esiss_hfw: because, book, first, home, into, mother, our, red, school, three
node.wushka_comprehension: Monitoring for Meaning, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The King's Birthday
node.content: The King is upset because no one has remembered his birthday. Lunch, however, reveals a lovely surprise. This is a sequel (with lots of delightful links) to the Dragonflies fluent title The Hole in the King's Sock. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of using prior knowledge, making and confirming predictions, and noting details in illustrations. The "royal" vocabulary and complex sentences provide opportunities for exploring language, while the repetitive structure provides support for less confident readers. Text Features • the clear narrative structure • the fairy tale elements • the characterisation of the King and Queen • the humour and the medieval illustrations • the inclusion of complex sentences • the expressive dialogue, including italics for emphasis, an imperative (on page 7), questions, and contractions • the alternatives to "said" - asked, ordered, sang, thought, wondered • the compound words - birthday, courtyard, everywhere, footmen, into, maybe, something, today, upset, upstairs • the possessive apostrophe in the title.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: It is the King's birthday, but nobody seems to have remembered. They are all too busy. When the lunch bell rings, the King sees why everyone is busy.
node.esiss_activities: The King's birthday party is a secret. Everyone is doing something special. Imagine you have been invited to the party. Write about three things you would bring or do to make the party fun.
node.esiss_hfw: into, know, their, where
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Making Connections

node.title: Lift-off!
node.content: Two characters leave Earth far behind as they explore outer space in their rocket.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making inferences|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Making inferences, Using graphic sources, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: In this story, two children go on an imaginary journey into space. They find out about all of the things that orbit around Earth, and then decide to explore even further.
node.esiss_activities: What would you see if you were in the rocket? Use your imagination. Choose three things you would see. Draw each thing. Write a sentence about each one.

node.title: The Clumsy Tiger
node.content: This text is a folk tale in which a tiger is outwitted by a cat. The tiger asks the cat to teach him to become quick and clever. The tiger learns quickly, but there is one thing the clever cat realises he shouldn't teach him! This text is a companion to the Dragonflies fluent title The Competition. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of summarising the Determining main ideas, understanding the author's purpose, using illustrations to understand text, and comparing and contrasting. The storyline, illustration style, and features of a traditional tale provide opportunities for making connections between this text and The Competition. It provides excellent opportunities for expressive reading and retelling. Text Features• the clear narrative structure • the features of a traditional tale • the way the illustrations support and extend the ideas • the direct address of the reader at the end of the story and the layout features (text placement, italics, and bold print) that add emphasis • the traditional opening that sets the tale in a distant time • the talking animals • the battle of wits between the characters • the humorous ending • the lively verbs - climb, hide, jump, pounce, prowl, run • the adjectives - big, clever, clumsy, quick, slow, strong, tall • the use of commas to support phrasing • the indicators of time - once, one day, every day, soon, then • the use of idiom on page 8 • the opposites - slow, quick • the silent letters - climb, climbed, could, know, right, taught, through, who • the different sounds for "ou" - could, enough, pounce, through Possible challenges • the irregular verb "taught" • students for whom English is a second language may need support with the idiom "that's it!" (meaning "that's the end") on page 8 and "just as well" (meaning "that's a good thing") on page 11.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making connections|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Tiger is clumsy and can't catch anything to eat. He asks Cat to teach him to hunt. When he has learnt to pounce and prowl, Tiger decides that Cat might make a nice dinner.
node.esiss_activities: The word "climb" has a silent "b" at the end. Look through the book for other words that have silent letters. Write the words in a list. What other words do you know that have silent letters?
node.esiss_hfw: could, eat, know, soon, through
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Competition
node.content: This text is a folk tale in which a tiger is outwitted by a frog. It describes how the small but clever frog is able to scare away a tiger. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of using illustrations to understand text and comparing and contrasting. The storyline, illustration style, and features of a traditional tale provide opportunities for making connections between this text and The Clumsy Tiger (and with tales from other cultures). It provides excellent opportunities for expressive reading and retelling. Text Features • the clear narrative structure • the features of a traditional tale • the way the illustrations support and extend the ideas • the direct address to the reader and the layout features (text placement, italics, and bold print) that add emphasis • the traditional opening that sets the tale in a distant time • the talking animals • the battle of wits between the characters • the humorous ending • the tricky nature of the frog • the adjectives - busy, loud, small, tiny, yellow • the comparative and superlative adjectives - higher, highest; farther, farthest • the confrontational dialogue • the colloquial language - right • the use of ellipses on page 5 to indicate a pause • the "tion" ending in "competition" Possible challenges • the words - farther, farthest • the "air" sound for "ar" in "scaring" • the colloquial language (for the students whom English is a second language) • Visualising how the frog tricks the tiger.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Tiger and Frog argue about who owns the pool in the jungle. They decide to have a competition to see who can jump highest and farthest. Each time, Frog plays a trick on Tiger.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine that you are the frog in this story. Write another ending where you use a different trick to beat Tiger.
node.esiss_hfw: again, around, could, eat, find, first, found, got, live, take, three
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1665 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Brave Ben
node.content: This story looks at the anxiety some people feel when they visit the doctor. Ben needs to go to the doctor to have an injection. He is worried that the injection will hurt, even though his mother reassures him that it will hurt only a little. In the end, Ben finds the injection is just as his mother and the nurse described it, and he feels brave for having had it. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - brave, injection, nurse, worried - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - sm; long vowel sounds - don't/go/going/home/know/won't Purpose Brave Ben introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - making predictions; - determining author's purpose.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Ben is worried because he has to go to the doctor for an injection. What will happen when he goes to the doctor's?
node.esiss_activities: Ben is worried that the injection will hurt. He asks his mother lots of questions about it. When he is waiting to see the doctor, he reads a book to keep himself calm. Think about how you keep calm when you have to do something that you are worried about. Write down the ways you keep calm.
