node.title: A Tree Is a Home
node.content: A tree is a home to many kinds of animals. A Tree Is a Home explores a variety of creatures that make their homes in trees. Features of the Book • Dramatic photographs providing a close match to the text. • The words in capitals at the beginning of the text. • The use of different creatures on each spread. • The frequent use of the "tr" blend. • The use of "s" to form regular plurals - "birds," "trees," "animals," "homes," "eggs," and "frogs." • Irregular forms of the plural - "leaves," "branches," and "lives." • The model for expository writing. Purpose A Tree Is a Home can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • developing effective strategies for reading unknown words within a story • writing in a similar style to that of the story.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Forming opinions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Forming opinions, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: When you look at a tree, what do you see? A tree is more than branches and leaves. All kinds of animals live in trees. Find out why frogs, snakes, and insects all make their homes in trees.
node.esiss_activities: A tree can be a home to many different creatures. Draw a picture of a tree. Show some of the animals that live in it. Write a label for each animal.
node.esiss_hfw: other, home, eat, some, like, their, very
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Sweet to Eat
node.content: The first page introduces a family's grapevine, that was planted by the children's grandfather. The following pages record the growth of the grapes over one season. Features of the Book - Text used to show the passing of time - Every year and At first. - Changes in season shown in the illustrations. - The decorative border repeated on each spread. - The repetition of Sweet to eat in two different contexts. - Evocative language - old, twisty grapevine and fat and juicy. - The silent w in wrinkly. - The blends - tw and gr. - The use of letter clusters in both medial and final positions - tch in stretches and ght in night. - The use of compound words - grapevine and grandfather. Purpose Sweet to Eat can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings; - identifying the themes of the book; - developing the skill of writing using a range of word choices.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The grapes that grow along the wall are juicy and sweet. What happens when autumn comes and the leaves fall off the grapevine? Is the fruit still sweet to eat?
node.esiss_activities: An adjective is a describing word. Look through the book. Find the adjectives that describe the grapes. Choose another fruit that is sweet to eat. Write three adjectives to describe this fruit.
node.esiss_hfw: our, now, along, first, right, eat, every, look, very

node.title: What a Noise!
node.content: This procedural text shows how some children make musical instruments from materials they find around the house. Features of the Book - The side-by-side storylines on each page - one narrative, one procedural. - The way Harry the baby appears incidental to the story but becomes pivotal. - The use of onomatopoeia - Boom!, Zzzzz!, and Ting! - The use of text in the illustrations to convey noise. - The abbreviated language and lack of punctuation in some of the procedural text. - High-interest names of unusual instruments - kazoo and xylophone. - The frequent use of exclamation marks. - The high-frequency words - have and some. Purpose What a Noise! can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - hearing the individual sounds in a word; - blending sounds together to make a word; - introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story; - developing the skill of writing instructions.
node.strategies: Making inferences|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Some children decide to have a band. They each make an instrument to play. Who makes the loudest noise?
node.esiss_activities: These children make their own instruments. They use things they find at home. Ask someone to help you make an instrument. Look in the book for ideas. What noise does your instrument make?

node.title: Discovering My World (Fluent)
node.content: What do you know about your world? Discovering My World looks at the shapes of the land, people finding their way, different people and places, and people getting along and helping one another. Features of Discovering My World • introduction of geography and civics concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about people and their place in the world (pages 2 to 7) followed by four double-page spreads about specific geographical and civic features • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs that support and illustrate concepts • captions • multisyllablic words - country, different, family, people, ideas, respect, together, travel, water
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: What do you know about the world around you? This book introduces you to the changing world and the things you can discover there.
node.esiss_activities: Our world is an interesting place to live. Write a story about an interesting place you have been. Explain why you found it interesting.

node.title: A Name for a Rabbit
node.content: When the class gets a new pet rabbit, they can't wait to give it a name. Features of the Book - The repetitive pattern in the story line. - Speech bubbles supporting the parallel storyline. - The charts and lists in the illustrations. - The inference required on the final page. - The regular use of direct speech. - The double consonants - class, rabbit, all, bubbles, snuff, and called. - The double vowels - oo and ee. Purpose A Name for Rabbit can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - identifying the themes of the book; - recognising and reading one- and two-syllable words; - writing to support the events in the story.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Discussing point of view|Exploring text structure|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Exploring text structure, Discussing point of view, Determining author's purpose, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: The class has a new rabbit. First they need to learn how to look after it properly. Then they need to find the best name.
node.esiss_activities: Make a list of ten names that would be good for a rabbit. You could look in the book for some ideas. Ask the people in your family which name they like best. Which name got the most votes?

node.title: The Changing Land
node.content: The Changing Land explores how the elements can affect Earth and change its shape. Features of the Book • The common focal sentence with one key word change. • The common pattern of the additional sentence per spread. • The different text size used to emphasize the key noun on the page. • The way information can be gained from a close study of the photographs. • The concluding collage that recalls all the elements. • The blends - "ch," "dr," "cr," "bl," and "pl." • The verbs that describe particular changes - "dries," "blows," "washes," and "cracks." • The two forms of the verb "change." • The high-frequency words - "can" and "from." Purpose The Changing Land can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: discussing the Cause and effect of specific events within the story• predicting outcomes from illustrations• innovating on an original story line to write another version.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making predictions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: This book will tell you how wind, sun, water, and ice can change the shape of the land.
node.esiss_activities: This book shows how the land changes shape. Choose one thing that changes the land, such as water. Draw a picture of a place before it has changed shape. Then draw the place after it has changed shape.

node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect

node.title: Taking Care of Yourself (Fluent)
node.content: How do you take care of yourself? Taking Care of Yourself looks at how we keep healthy by keeping our teeth clean, eating healthy food, doing exercise, and keeping safe. Features of Taking Care of Youself • introduction of health concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about taking care of ourselves (pages 2-7), followed by four doublepage spreads about specific things we can do to care for ourselves • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text to support and illustrate concepts • use of captions • initial consonant blends - "st-", "sk-", "br-", "tw-", "gr-", "sp-", "pl-", "str-" • digraphs - "th", "wh", "ch", "sh"
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Synthesising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: When you learn how to be healthy and safe, you're taking care of yourself. Check out how to eat a healthy diet and exercise safely, and how to deal with your feelings.
node.esiss_activities: We do many things to take care of ourselves. Make a list of the things you do to get ready for school. How many things can you do on your own?

node.title: Happy Hiking
node.content: When people do things safely, they are taking care of themselves. Following rules helps to keep you safe. This book presents a step-by-step approach to planning a hiking trip. The lively yet straightforward text and photographs will encourage the students to explore the outdoors. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by keeping safe.) Features of Happy Hiking • contents page and index • procedural markers - step 1, step 2, step 3, first, now, next, finally • bold headings • checklists • fact boxes • photographs • chart • digraphs - "th", "sh", "ch" • initial blends - "cl-", "pr-", "wh-", "f-", "pr-" • contractions - let's, you'll, youre
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Drawing conclusions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: If you want to go on a hiking adventure, it pays to think about safety first. You need to check the weather, your clothing, the food and drink you're taking, and of course, your safety gear. This book is a good guide for a safe hike.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, there are five steps for safe and happy hiking. Using these rules, make a poster for hikers. For each step, write two sentences and draw a picture or diagram.

node.title: A Mongoose Tale
node.content: In this book, the narrator explains a natural phenomenon by retelling a personal memory. The phenomenon is the raggedness of the tails of mongooses from Nakelo; other mongooses have soft, furry tails. The narrator tells of how he worked out why the mongooses of Nakelo were different - by observing them in the wild. Features of the Book - The word play in the title, which is based on the different meanings of tale and tail. - Contrast expressed through adjectives: "thick and furry" compared with "ragged". - Contrast expressed through verbs and adverbs: "sat very still and waited" compared with "rushed forwards". - Simile (describing something by comparing it with something else): The mongoose was using its tail like a fishing line. Purpose of the Book - Reading this story will help students to become aware that there are reasons for the observable features of living things - in this case the raggedness of a mongoose's tail.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Forming opinions, Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Most mongooses have smooth tales, but not the mongooses from Nakelo? How did that come to be?
node.esiss_activities: Pretend you are a mongoose. Write a story about about how you use your tail to catch mud lobsters. Write about what you see, hear, feel, and taste.

node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections

node.title: The Little House
node.content: In this play, retold from a Russian folktale, Various animals find an empty house that they all want to live in - until Mr. Bear comes along, sits on it, and squashes it flat! Features of the Book • The character list and introduction. • Repetition as a support for the reader. • The stage directions. • The frequent use of question marks, exclamation points, and commas. • The high-frequency contraction "I'm." • The use of capitals for characters' names. • The use of parentheses and/or italics for stage directions. • The photographs of the children preparing for the play. • The interviews with the actors. Purpose The Little House can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the plot and characters within the story • understanding the punctuation cues and their meanings • discussing the organizational structure of the text • writing a recount of the story using own words.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: This is a play you can read with your friends. Who will you be? Mouse, Hare, or Frog? And how will Bear fit into the little house with all the other animals?
node.esiss_activities: Ask someone to help you make a small house. You could use an old box or a sheet. Choose some soft toys to be the animals that go to live in the house. You can be the bear and squash them all flat!
node.esiss_hfw: this, more, there, who, inside, house, little, live, what, who

node.title: Being a Scientist (Fluent)
node.content: What does a scientist do? Being a Scientists looks at what scientists do and the areas they study: plants and animals, space, what things are made from and how they change, and how things work. Features of being a Scientist • introduction of science concepts and anchor words associated with the topic • general information about scientists (pages 2 to 7), followed by four double-page spreads about specific kinds of scientists • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text that supports and illustrates the concepts • captioned photographs • graph • silent "c" after "s" • initial consonant blends - "dr-", "gr-", "pl-", "pr-", "sh-", "sp-"
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Discussing point of view|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Discussing point of view, Making predictions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Scientists help us to understand the world. Find out about the skills a scientist needs to do a good job.
node.esiss_activities: Some scientists study plants and animals. They look at the features that help living things survive. Choose a plant or an animal that you find interesting. Draw a picture of it. Label the features that help this living thing survive.
node.esiss_hfw: another, find, into, live, look, many, some, their, use, want, work
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELT1582 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: It's Not Fair
node.content: This story looks at how feelings of jealousy can make a person unhappy. The girl in the story is jealous because Jack has a new ball. She watches Jack and his friends play soccer with the new ball. When Jack asks her to join the game, the girl realises that the important thing is to have fun and enjoy herself. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion: fair, jealous, want - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - th; letter combinations - ough; long vowel sounds - school/smooth Strategies It's Not Fair introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - determining author's purpose; - comparing and contrasting.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: The girl thinks that it's not fair that Jack has a new ball. Why is she jealous of his ball?
node.esiss_activities: The girl in the story is jealous because Jack has a new ball. Write a story about a time when you felt jealous. How did it make you feel? What did you do? Did you stop feeling jealous?
node.esiss_hfw: around, both, would, your
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1589 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: All Kinds of Eyes
node.content: All Kinds of Eyes is a close-up look at some very unusual eyes. Features of the Book • The variation in text size. • The way the text wraps around some of the illustrations. • The use of adjectives - "big," "small," "tiny," "round," "bulgy," and "bright." • The irregular pronunciation of "eyes." • The two spellings and meanings of "its" and "it's." • The blends - "th," "cr," "st," "sl," and "sn." • The challenge of the text layout. • The high-frequency words - "has," "have," "for," "look," and "like." Purpose All Kinds of Eyes can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: encouraging rereading of the text to clarify understanding• using a range of punctuation to read expressively• reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context• selecting information from the story for a specific purpose.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Animals have all kinds of eyes. Some have big eyes, some have small eyes. Some have lots of eyes! What do your eyes look like?
node.esiss_activities: This book has big eyes and tiny eyes. Find the animal that you like the most. Draw the animal. Make a label for its eyes.
node.esiss_hfw: has, have, for, look, like
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Dragon
node.content: Before they leave school, a class makes a "dragon" to keep together on their walk to the library. Features of the Book • The large, bold capital letter that begins the text. • The personification of the dragon - "The dragon squeezes close," "The dragon curls up," and "It takes off its shoes." • The range of major and minor characters in the story. • Examples of commas, exclamation points, and quotation marks. • The thought bubbles that support the children's fantasy. • The high-frequency word "says" and the interest word "dragon." • The use of contractions - "don't," "she's," and "there's." • The text in the illustrations. • The dragon images in the illustrations Purpose Dragon can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the plot and characters within the story • Summarising the ideas from a story • writing a conclusion.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Going to the public library with the class is exciting when you go as a dragon. Who is the head and who is the tail? And what happens when the dragon wants to go inside the library?
node.esiss_activities: Walking like a dragon looks like fun. Write what the dragon would see and do on the way back to school.
node.esiss_hfw: says
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Swing, Monkey, Swing
node.content: Monkey is learning to swing, but she keeps falling. She is ready to give up, but her mother and Snake encourage her to keep trying. After a snack and a nap, she tries again. She swings farther, but still falls. Her mother and Snake once again encourage her to persevere. Monkey keeps trying until she learns to swing, and she feels proud of her accomplishment. Purpose - The information contained in the illustrations. - Content words for discussion - again, learning, try - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - sw; short vowel sounds - belly/better/fell/get/listen/when/yelled Strategies Swing, Monkey, Swing introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - determining author's purpose; - visualising.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Overcoming obstacles
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Monkey is learning to swing. It's hard, and she wants to give up. Monkey's mother and Snake tell her to keep trying.
node.esiss_activities: Monkey finds it hard to learn to swing and wants to give up. Think of a time when you were learning to do something. Who helped you? How did you feel? Did you want to give up? Write a story about what happened.

node.title: The Lion and the Mouse
node.content: This story looks at friendship and how the most unlikely individuals can become friends. When Lion traps Mouse, he considers eating her, but Mouse begs for mercy and offers him friendship instead. Lion decides that Mouse is too small to eat or to be his friend, and he lets her go. The next day, Lion is caught in a trap, and Mouse frees him by chewing through the ropes. In the end, Lion realises that the size of a friend doesn't matter. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - friend, help, laughed - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - th; short vowel sounds - but/hungry/lunch/much/us Purpose The Lion and the Mouse reintroduces and reinforces the following strategies: - activating prior knowledge; - using graphic sources; - making connections.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining cause and effect|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: When Lion captures Mouse, she asks him to let her go and be her friend. Will Lion want to be Mouse's friend?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, Monkey traps Lion in a net. Imagine Lion asks Monkey to let him go. Write a story from Lion's point of view. Why should Monkey let Lion go. What will Lion do to be Monkey's friend?

node.title: Let's Build a Bridge
node.content: This book is about a group of ants who work together to solve a problem – how to get some seeds across a stream and back to their nest. They come up with an idea, but will their solution fix their problem? Let's Build a Bridge introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • sequencing • visualising • recognising cause and effect. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: ants, bridge, roll, seeds, stream, twig • Phonics and phonemic awareness: three"letter consonant blend str
node.strategies: Sequencing|Visualising|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Visualising, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: In this story, the ants are trying to get some food back to their nest. Have you ever watched a group of ants move an object? Why do you think ants work together?
node.esiss_activities: Can you think of a time you worked as part of a team to get something done? Describe what you did and how it made you feel.

node.title: Clever Mr Goodyear
node.content: This book is about Charles Goodyear who, in 1830, wanted to improve a new product called rubber. For many years, he experimented in his kitchen on a hot stove. Thanks to possibly a lucky accident, he achieved his goal. Clever Mr Goodyear introduces and reinforces the following strategies:• making connections• finding main ideas• recognising cause and effect. Features of the Book:• The information contained in the illustrations, text, timeline, diagram, Contents page and glossary • Content words for discussion: rubber, sticky, stove, sulphur, waterproof • Phonics and phonemic awareness: vowel digraph ee
node.strategies: Making Connections|Finding Main Ideas|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Ever heard of the inventor Mr Goodyear? This recount explains what he invented and how!
node.esiss_activities: Look at the timeline on pages 10 and 11 again. Research another famous inventor and create a timeline for their life and invention.

node.title: The Scarecrow
node.content: Mum is driving Henry and Simon to school when the boys notice that she's wearing her dressing gown. When the car has a flat tyre, they have to think quickly to make sure that nobody sees their mum in her dressing gown. Features of the Book: • Direct speech • First-person narrative • Humour • Specialised vocabulary - award, contest, creative, judging, results • Written from a child's point of view. The Scarecrow can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: • inferring meaning • paraphrasing and summarising information • relating the characters, settings, and plot to real-life situations • exploring initial blends Ph • understanding common antonyms and synonyms • writing text that has a beginning, middle, and end
node.strategies: Making connections|Making inferences|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Henry and Simon's mum is changing a flat tyre on their car, in her dressing gown! Have your parents ever embarrassed you?
node.esiss_activities: Ask the children to think of a time when something embarrassing happened to them. Use one of the children's suggestions to write a story as a class. Draw three columns on chart paper, headed Beginning, Middle, and End. In the appropriate columns on the chart, record the main ideas for the story. Encourage the children to think about their own stories in this way.

node.title: Rain Forest Adventure
node.content: This story looks at exploration and problem solving. Scientists Lisa and Matt go on an expedition to the rain forest to look for tree frogs. On their first day, they collect plants and insects and photograph rain forest animals, but they don't see any tree frogs. A rain shower causes them to take cover, and Matt takes off his backpack. Afterwards, Matt realises that a monkey has taken his pack with the tent inside. Luckily, Lisa's pack has the food and other supplies. In the end, Lisa and Matt make a shelter from branches and ferns, allowing them to stay overnight in the rain forest and finish their expedition. Rain Forest Adventure introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making predictions; • determining cause and effect; • visualising. Features of the text: • The information contained in the illustrations • Content words: collected, expedition, explored, gear, maps, scientists, shelter, study, supplies, survive • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends " ck"; long vowel sounds "food/moon"; short vowel sounds " good/look/took"
node.strategies: Making predictions|Recognising Cause and Effect|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Lisa and Matt are on an expedition in the rain forest when a cheeky monkey steals their tent. What will they do for shelter now?
node.esiss_activities: What would you take on a trip to the rain forest? Make a list of items and explain why you have chosen them.

node.title: Coyote in Trouble
node.content: In this collection of Native American tales, Coyote is tricked or makes mistakes and he ends up in trouble. The book ends with a letter from Coyote to Grandma in which he apologises for being so much trouble. Features of the Book - A collection of separate but related tales, - Native American tales from an oral tradition, - Direct speech, - Predictable outcomes, - Exclamations and onomatopoeia - Crash!, Ouch!, Kerplunk!, - Punctuation - ellipses, - Contractions - he'll, he's, didn't, don't, wasn't, I'll, I'm, can't, that's, what's, it's, you'll, they'll Purpose - Coyote in Trouble can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring the genre of traditional tales, - making confirming and revising predictions, - reading and writing procedural texts, - describing a sequence of events, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: That Coyote! He thinks he's clever. But he's always in trouble. Some other animals play tricks on Coyote. He tries to be clever and trick them, too, but then he gets into more trouble. Do you think he'll ever learn?
node.esiss_activities: Coyote thinks he is clever. How would these stories be different if Coyote listened to the other animals instead of thinking he is always right? Retell the "Coyote and Turtle" story from pages 14 to 19. Imagine that Coyote believes Turtle when she says that she wasn't singing. What would happen next? Write a new ending.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELA1469 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669

node.title: Stars
node.content: Scientists study the sun to help them understand more about stars. Using photographs and diagrams, Stars provides information about discovering and studying stars. The book concludes that scientists still have a lot to learn about stars! (Big idea: Scientists study Earth and space.) Features of Stars • the topic (Earth and space), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • headings that introduce new aspects of the topic and support students to read sections • captions and labels that highlight features of the visuals • questions that focus attention on the topic • diagrams • cutaway diagram • comparative picture • fact file • glossary (boldface type) • index • multisyllabic words - Galileo, photographs, telescope, universe, faraway, scientists, understand, discovered, factory, Antares • comparative adjectives - closest, hottest, big, small, biggest, bigger, smallest, smaller • descriptive language: • similes - like tiny dots of light, like a giant factory making heat and light
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Discussing point of view, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: When you look up on a clear night, you can see stars dotting the sky. How much do you know about these huge energy factories? Did you know there are stars that are millions of times bigger than our sun? Read this book to become an expert on stars.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, there is a pattern of stars called Orion. Find another star pattern or make one of your own. Draw some stars on a sheet of paper. Can you make a pattern? Join the stars with lines. Show someone the pattern. Can he or she guess what it is?

node.title: From Tree to Paper
node.content: Some scientists help us to understand how materials can change from one state to another. This book provides a clear, concise explanation of how paper is made. The photographs and diagrams support an understanding of the process and invite the reader to ask questions. (Big idea: Scientists study materials and how they change.) Features of From Tree to Paper the topic (how paper is made), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • step-by-step action photographs • procedural words • flow diagram • chart • fact boxes, captions, and labels • magnified pictures • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "scr-", "sp-", "tr-", "gl-", "cl-", "dr-", "st-", "squ-" • final consonant blend - "-st" • digraph sounds - "th", "ch", "thr-" • contraction - it's • procedural words - first, next, now, after that, finally • adjectives - huge, new, small, solid, soft, lumpy, thin, grey, tiny, wet, flat, giant • compound word - dishwater
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: When you pick up a book, it's hard to imagine that the pages in it started their life as a tree. This book tells you about every step in the process, from a load of logs to a roll of smooth white paper.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the diagram on page 14. Write a sentence that describes each step in the process of making a tree into paper. Read the steps to someone in your family. Does he or she understand how a tree is made into paper?
node.esiss_hfw: after, around, book, each, first, into, left, look, next, such, their, through
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and effect, Making Predictions

node.title: Dinosaur Detectives
node.content: Denzel's allowance is not enough to buy the computer game that he wants so he starts work as a detective. With the help of his friend Tere he finds Helen Hadrosaur's missing eggs and is rewarded with a generous fee. Features of the Book - Colloquial language - awesome, cool, wild-goose chase, - Direct speech, - Humour, - Labelled diagram, - Specialised vocabulary - Allosaur, detectives, Hadrosaur, Word Study Opportunities - Blends "gr" and "pl" - grocery, growled; please playing, - Contractions - I'll, here's, you're, it's, - Compound words - backyard, sunglasses, bathroom, footprint, - Double vowels "ee" and "oo" - need, see, tree; zoo, looked, footprint, good, shook, - Digraph "th" - these, thief, this Purpose - Dinosaur Detectives can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - making confirming and revising predictions, - exploring dialogue, - reading aloud with expression, - exploring digraphs, - identifying the features of different genres, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When Helen Hadrosaur's eggs go missing, she asks Daniel and Terry to help find them. A footprint outside Helen's house is their first clue to solving the mystery.
node.esiss_activities: Denzel wanted to buy Swamp Monster 2. He decided to become a dinosaur detective so he could earn the money to pay for it. What game or toy would you like to buy? How could you raise the money? Make a flyer like Denzel's to advertise your service.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669

node.title: The Weather Spider
node.content: "People whose livelihood depends on the weather know that some animals have a kind of sixth sense about it. The children in the story know this and consult Hina the spider. She spins her web low in the bushes, thereby telling the children that it's going to be a stormy season. A warning to farmers and fishermen is necessary, for the children know the value of paying attention to Hina's activities. Features of the Book - The story is fold in the form of questions and answers. - Some poetic devices are used: repetition and alliteration (weave my web) - Different text styles tell the reader whose voice is indicated. Roman text indicates the narrators' voice, whereas italic text indicates Hina the spider's voice. Purpose of the Book The story highlights the fact that animals may know things that we humans don't. The students will begin to understand the concept of animal instinct. The question/answer style of presenting the story to the students will challenge them to try to answer the questions. They will therefore be intellectually involved as they read the story."
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Forming opinions, Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The children in this book know how to talk to Hina the weather spider. She tells them when the storm is coming.
node.esiss_activities: When Hina the spider is in the bushes, it means that a storm is coming. Where might Hina go when it is sunny or windy?
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Magpie Tree
node.content: Maddy and her friends are having trouble with a pesky magpie that seems to like their shiny possessions. Using some clever problem solving, Maddy works out a way to keep her friends, and the magpie, happy. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of determining cause and effect, forming and supporting opinions, and retrieving relevant information from a text. It is a lively narrative that holds the readers' attention through suspense and prediction and has a positive and satisfying ending. Text Features • the themes of problem solving and co-operation • the way Maddy resolves the situation successfully thinking of everyone involved • the complex narrative structure • the use of dialogue to reveal information slowly and keep the readers' interest • the indicators of time - the next day, that night, next morning, at playtime, the next day • the variety of word endings - ed, ing • the large number of lively verbs - swooped, twinkled, warbled • the doubled consonants - swoop, swooped • the commas for phrasing • the bright, colourful, and lively illustrations • the adjectives - shiny, silver, silvery • the replacement of an "e" with a "y" - shine, shiny • the compound words - hairclip, playtime, classroom, earrings, teapot • the possessive apostrophes - Dan's, children's, Maddy's • the blends - fl, br, ch, sh • the digraph - bl • the use of hyperbole - good idea!
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|School and community
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models,
node.esiss_blurb: Magpies like shiny things. They swoop and warble around the children. Maddy has an idea about how to get the birds to leave them alone.
node.esiss_activities: There are some unusual words in this story. For example, "warbled", "swooped", and "silvery". Write a glossary showing what these words mean. Find three other words in the book and add them to your glossary.

node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Rock in the Road
node.content: This is a simple play of how the collective strength of a group of animals solves a problem that none of them could solve individually. Features of the Book • The character list and introduction. • Repetition as a support for the reader. • The use of increasing numbers - "What are you four doing?" • The frequent use of question marks, exclamation points, and commas. • The high-frequency contractions - "What's," "won't," "You're," "can't," and "it's." • The use of parentheses and/or italics for stage directions. • The support in the illustrations. Purpose The Rock in the Road can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the plot and characters within the story • comparing books by different authors written in the same genre • discussing the organisational structure of the text • writing a recount of the story using own words.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|School and community
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Following instructions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using graphic sources, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: A rock has fallen on to the road and it's causing problems for the animals. Each animal tries to move it, but it's too heavy. Working together might be the answer!
node.esiss_activities: The rock on the road was a tricky thing to move. Invent something that could help move the rock. Draw your invention. Write about how it works.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1450 ACELA1821 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1655 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1463 ACELA1466 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Wind Power
node.content: This book looks at all the things that the wind powers. Features • The specialised vocabulary - "generator," "blades," "turbine," "electricity," and "machine." • The root word "wind" - "windy" and "windmill." • The labeled diagram to summarize the concepts in the story. • The imagery of the wind in the illustrations and in the cover text. • The final "y" consonant - "city," "electricity," "every," and "windy." • The "bl" blend - "blades" and "blows." Purpose Wind Power can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • using diagrams, charts, and other illustrations to support writing.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining importance|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Exploring text structure, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: A walk to the top of a hill to see a wind turbine helps this girl understand how electricity works. When they race home to miss the storm, she sees that electricity is used for all kinds of things.
node.esiss_activities: The last page in this book shows a house. What things can you see in the picture that use electricity? Take a look around your home. Make a list of the things that need electricity to work.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Walter's Worries
node.content: Walter is a dragon who has trouble holding down a job because he's always causing fires. Walter and his friend Maisie go from place to place, and job to job, until Walter finds the perfect job. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Onomatopoeia - woosh, hssss, crash!, Ahh!, - Alternative verbs for said - grumbled, shouted, yelled, advised, cried, called, - Punctuation - ellipses, question marks, - Question and answer format, - Repetition Purpose - Walter's Worries can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making confirming and revising predictions, - using illustrations to gain and deepen meaning, - identifying the plot and characters within a story, - extending vocabulary, - reading aloud with expression, - writing to communicate information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using graphic sources, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: Walter the dragon has a lot of worries. Walter's friend Maisie tries to help him find a job that won't make him worry. They have a few disasters along the way.
node.esiss_activities: Let's help Walter find a job. Brainstorm all the good things about Walter. Include the things that he is good at doing. Use your ideas to write a letter that asks someone to give Walter a job.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELA1469 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669

node.title: The Painting Lesson
node.content: When Mr Toggins discovers the secret behind Mrs Zest's ever-changing, colourful house, he can't wait to try her special paintbrush himself. However, things don't work out the way Mr Toggins expects! Features of the Book - Fantasy in a neighbourhood setting, - Repetition in a refrain, - Interesting vocabulary, - crept, peered, slipped, - Contractions - what's didn't, couldn't, wouldn't, you'll, - Blends - fl, br, str, sp, - Expressive illustrations, - Alliteration - bristly brush, flicking and flowing, streaks, stripes, and spots, - Poetic rhyme Purpose - The Painting Lesson can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - - exploring the use of poetic language, - examining the consequences of character's actions, - exploring initial blends, - using illustrations to gain and deepen meaning. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Mrs Zest has the most colourful house on Main Street. Mr Toggins discovers Mrs Zest's magic paintbrush and wants to paint his house, too. But how will he stop the brush from painting?
node.esiss_activities: The author has used interesting words in the story. She has used pairs of words that start with the same letter. This is called alliteration. "Bristly brush" and "flicking and flowing" are examples of alliteration. Write down any other examples you can find in the story.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1659 ACELA1464 ACELA1469 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669

node.title: The Funny Fish
node.content: This story is about a young girl who overcomes a personal obstacle and finds confidence in her artistic ability. Jess and her classmates are painting fish. Jess doesn't feel confident. As the other children work, she reluctantly starts to paint, but is dissatisfied with the result. She is surprised that her teacher and classmates recognise her painting as a fish and are supportive of her. The experience gives Jess confidence, and she wants to continue painting. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - colours, great, paint - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - sh, sp; short vowel sounds - and/any/asked/can/can't/classroom/had/sat Purpose The Funny FIsh introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - visualising; - comparing and contrasting.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Jess doesn't like painting fish, and she isn't happy with the fish she paints. What does the class think of her painting?
node.esiss_activities: What kind of animal would you like to paint? Choose an animal like a dog or a horse, or a wild animal like a tiger or an elephant. What will it look like? Paint your animal. Share your painting with your family.
node.esiss_hfw: again, an, any, going, had, her, his, of, some, then, were
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Visualising, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1589 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Guard Dog Diggory
node.content: Diggory is Mac's dog. One day, Mac leaves Diggory eating a bone in the back of his truck. The truck is stolen. Diggory protects the truck (and his bone) while the police help Mac catch the robbers and find the truck. Features of the Book - Colloquial language - Yikes crime busters, - Direct speech, - Includes characters from A Home for Diggory, - Humor, - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - sometimes, footpath - Contractions - don't, we're, I'll, he's, isn't, - Past tense verbs - barked, wagged, stopped, - Silent "b" - climbed Purpose - Guard Dog Diggory can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings relationships and actions, - making confirming and revising predictions, - inferring meaning, - exploring the silent "e", - writing to record ideas. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Monitoring for meaning, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Diggory is a big dog, but he isn't very fierce. When Mack's truck is stolen, the robbers think that Diggory won't cause any trouble. But they are wrong.
node.esiss_activities: Design a poster that asks for help to find Mac's truck and Diggory. Draw a picture of the truck and Diggory. Write what the truck and Diggory look like. Describe where Diggory was last seen.
node.wushka_comprehension: Monitoring and Clarifying, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELA1469 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669

node.title: Hands Up, Wolf!
node.content: This play is a contemporary version of Little Red Riding Hood. It also contains references to Goldilocks and the Three Bears. Features of the Book - Based on Little Red Riding Hood, - Colloquial language - break-in, cheer her up, cops; Hands up, Here we go again, So long, Sarge, take a look, - Humor, - specialised vocabulary - All Points Bulletin, arrest, crook, custody, sergeant, suspect, - Stage directions Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - Downtown Honeybee, onstage, offstage, woodshed, - Contractions - I'll, can't, she's, ma'am, you've - Multisyllabic words - bulletin, custody dangerous officers, - Silent letters - know climbs Purpose - Hands Up Wolf! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - Comparing and contrasting different versions of the same story, - identifying the features of different genres, - reading aloud with expression, - recognizing silent letters, - using alternative endings for a story. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Comparing and contrasting, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Red is on her way to visit Grandma and someone is following her. She calls the police and they come to investigate. What do you think they will find?
node.esiss_activities: Sometimes we use abbreviations when we write and talk. An abbreviation makes a word or group of words shorter. Write a list of these abbreviations and what they mean: • Why is the girl called Red? • Why is the wolf called BB Wolf? • What does APB mean?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Compare and Contrast, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1450 ACELA1454 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1463 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1669

node.title: The Perfect Pet
node.content: This story looks at how even the perfect job can have advantages and disadvantages. Petunia owns a pet shop. She loves working with animals, but she wishes she didn't have to sell them. She would rather keep them all. In the end, Pete helps Petunia solve her problem by finding her a perfect pet, too. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion: customers, perfect, sell - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - sh; short vowel sounds - apartment/checked/opened/pet/Petunia/sell/them/there Purpose The Perfect Pet introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making connections; - determining author's purpose - summarising.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Petunia wishes that she could keep some of her pets rather than selling them all in her pet shop. Pete thinks of a good way to solve Petunia's problem.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine two other people come to Petunia's pet shop looking for a pet to buy. Choose two more pets from Petunia's Pet Shop. Make up two characters and write about the kind of pet he or she would like, the pet that Pete suggests, and then the pet that Petunia thinks is perfect for them.
node.esiss_hfw: an, could, every, had, her, his, just, let, take, them, then, were
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Glide, Wriggle, Zoom
node.content: A dance instructor works with a group of children. She shows them different moves they can use to create their own dance. Features of the Book - List, - Specialised vocabulary - balance, break dancing, choreography, float, flutter, glide, hip-hop, hover, isolation, rhythm, soar, style, tap-dancing, tumble, weight, transfer, wriggle, zoom, - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Initial blends "bl", "pr", "sh", and "sn" - blown, blades; practises, pretends; shifts, shoulder, shows; snapping, snake, - Multisyllabic words - balancing, choreography, helicopter, hovering, imagining, important, instructor, isolation, - Regular and irregular plurals - bodies, branches, dancers, dances, feet, friends Purpose - Glide, Wriggle, Zoom can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - using the illustrations to gain a deeper understanding of the text, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - paraphrasing and Summarising information, - using onsets and rimes to learn new words, - using descriptive language. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Tom likes to dance, but he isn't sure which style of dance he likes best. Then an instructor shows Tom some new moves.
node.esiss_activities: In Chapter 2, the dance instructor talks to the children about different kinds of movement. Try out some different moves for: • balance • isolation • weight transfer • theme words Play some music and add your moves to make a dance. Perform the dance for your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1660 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Going up the Wall
node.content: This book is about Rory, Anna, and Phebe learning to climb an indoor climbing wall with their instructor, Deb. We read about climbing equipment, climbing skills, and various types of climbing. Features of the Book - Speech bubbles, - Fact boxes, - Labelled diagram, - Specialised vocabulary - foothold, handhold, instructor, route, sole belayer, bouldering, Word Study Opportunities - Verb ending "ing" - climbing, raining, training, falling, bouldering, - Silent "b" - climb, - Compound words - handholds, footholds, indoor, outdoor, sometimes, inside, outside Purpose - Going up the Wall can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - using the table of contents, chapter headings, glossary, and index to locate information, - identifying the Determining main idea and supporting detail, - developing strategies for reading unknown words, - exploring word families, - paraphrasing and Summarising information, - using charts and diagrams to record information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Rory, Anna, and Phebe are about to have some fun at the climbing wall. Their instructor, Deb, is teaching them some climbing skills. Join them to find out how they go up the wall safely.
node.esiss_activities: Compound words are made up of two words that are joined together. The word "foothold" is a compound word. Look through the text and make a list of all the compound words. Beside each compound word, write the two words that have been joined.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Know Where to Go
node.content: Jessica and Sam use a variety of maps to help them plan a visit to the zoo. Detailed maps and diagrams are a feature of this book. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Fact boxes, - Maps, - Question-and-answer format, - Specialised vocabulary - compass rose, key, route, scale, symbols, transit, - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Double consonants - compass, different, Jessica, tells , - Double vowels - between, kangaroo, look, need, zoo, - Verb ending "ing" - eating, walking, watching, reading, - Initial blends "pl" and "tr" - place, plan; train, transit, travel, treasure, - Regular and irregular plurals - bison, buses, people, routes Purpose - Know Where to Go can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - Making connections between own experiences and the text, - interpreting the information in diagrams, charts, and graphs, - paraphrasing and Summarising information, - exploring initial blends, - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - using charts and diagrams to record information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Some maps help us find our way between one place and another. These maps are really useful when you're planning a trip to the zoo.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the map of Sam's treasure hunt on page 30. Draw a map of your home. Use symbols and a key. Hide some "treasure". Add arrows to show where the treasure is. Give the map to someone in your family. Can they follow the directions to find the treasure?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Pets
node.content: The narrator can't understand why people laugh at the pet giraffe when so many of the neighbours have pets that are just as wacky. Features of the Book • The regular rhythm. • The use of rhyme on every page. • The similarities between the pets and their owners. • The humor in the illustrations. • The convention of beginning each line with a capital letter. • The use of boxes to define the text. • The double "oo" vowel - "pool" and "door." • The repeated "wh" blend - "why," "what," "when," and "white." • The blends - "sh," "ch," "sn," "th," and "st." Purpose Pets can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the plot and characters within the story • developing effective strategies for reading unknown words within a story • discussing the organisational structure of the text • writing using capital letters for the beginning of sentences and the names of people and places.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Making predictions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: There are some strange pets in this book. The man down the road has a yak and a toad. So what's so unusual about my pet?
node.esiss_activities: The pets in this book are like their owners. What would be the best pet for you? Write some sentences to say how you would be like your pet.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1581 ACELT1582 ACELT1584 ACELT1585 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1463 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Go, Slow, Whoa!
node.content: When people eat a balanced diet, they are taking care of themselves. Healthy foods give you energy to grow and learn. This book encourages us to eat a balanced diet. The phrase "Go, Slow, Whoa!" reminds us about foods we can eat all the time (Go), some of the time (Slow), and occasionally (Whoa). (Big idea: People take care of themselves by eating a balanced diet.) Features of Go, Slow, Whoa! • the topic (healthy food), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • questions in titles • pinboard effect on photographs • captions • labels • chart • initial consonant blends - "tr-", "sl-", "pl-", "fr-", "br-", "dr-", "str-", "gr-", "sp-" • final consonant blend - "st" • digraphs - "th", "sh", "wh" • contractions - it's, they're, don't, doesn't, wouldn't
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Synthesising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Some foods are good to eat any time, others are sometimes treats. This book tells you how to use go, slow, and whoa to make sure you eat a healthy diet.
node.esiss_activities: Healthy food gives you the energy to grow and learn. Use the information in the book to make a menu for each night of the week. Make sure you create a balanced diet. You can include "sometimes" food on one of the nights.
node.esiss_hfw: about, eat, first, feel, give, know, last, long, next, think, yourself
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1821 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Cupboard Full of Summer
node.content: The McVittys are a family who work together to perform the enormous task of preserving a tree full of peaches into a cupboard full of summer. Features of the Book - The evocative language - wild summer wind, Heave ho, peach-dream sleep, and sparkling like bright sunshine. - The use of repetition for effect - No more! No more! More, more! But still the wind blew, and Pop! - Information about preserving food conveyed in a literary, imaginative context. - Illustrative devices to indicate speed (page 8), the popping lids (page 10), and dreams (pages 12 and 13). - The use of onomatopoeia - cans clattering and Pop! - The ght letter cluster in both medial and final positions - lightning and bright. - Silent letters in words - whistled and cupboard. Purpose A Cupboard Full of Summer can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - recognising and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings; - using punctuation cues to read instructional level books with expression; - developing the ability to write specifically about characters and their feelings within the story.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Drawing conclusions|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Sequencing, Discussing point of view, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: When summer comes and the peach tree is dripping with fruit, this family knows what to do. Then in the winter, they'll still have a reminder of the sunny summer days.
node.esiss_activities: This book uses different verbs to describe how the McVitty family talked. They shouted, sang, and mumbled. Write as many verbs as you can to describe other ways that people talk.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Next Stop, Space!
node.content: This story looks at a day in the life of some astronauts on the space station. Blake, Hana, and Ike fly to the space station. Viktor, Ray, and Jane have been on the space station for six months. They show Blake, Hana, and Ike around the station. Then it's time for Viktor, Ray, and Jane to return to Earth. Their journey is delayed by a malfunction in the spacecraft, but the astronauts repair the problem on a space walk and head home to Earth. Purpose - The information contained in the illustrations. - Content words for discussion - astronauts, Earth, explore, spacecraft - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - sp; long vowel sounds - Jane/later/Ray/space; short vowel sounds - back/had/handed/hatch Strategies Fix It, Annie introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - activating prior knowledge; - determining author's purpose; - sequencing; - visualising.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Sequencing|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Determining author's purpose, Sequencing, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: The astronauts are returning to Earth after six months on the space station. But something is wrong with their spacecraft. Will they be able to fix the problem and return to Earth?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine the astronauts couldn't fix the problem with the spacecraft. Write another ending to the story. What might happen if they can't fix the spacecraft themselves?
node.esiss_hfw: around, been, cold, sleep, their, would
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Sequencing, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1453 ACELA1454 ACELT1584 ACELY1658 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Sailing Away
node.content: This book describes early boats, how traders used boats and ships to transport goods and how traders sometimes became raiders! It also features ships used for scientific exploration. Sailing Away introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • finding main ideas • compare and contrast. Features of the Book: • The information contained in the photographs, illustrations, text, map, diagrams, Contents page and glossary • Content words for discussion: boats, built, explorers, people, voyage • Words that are both nouns and verbs: sail, fish, row, land, plant, watch, pass
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_strategy: Finding Main Ideas, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_blurb: Have you seen any boats or ships near where you live or where you've been on holiday? What did they look like and what were they used for?
node.esiss_activities: Skim the book again for facts about Captain James Cook and his ship and crew. Create a fact file, add a heading and bullet point each of the facts.
node.esiss_hfw: from, of, on, in, there, these, they, to, were
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1659 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1463 ACELA1466 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589

node.title: Sing Sarah
node.content: This book looks at a girl named Sarah who doesn't like speaking in front of her class because she has a stutter. One day, her teacher, Ms Kelly, wants them to read out poetry. Poor Sarah's heart sinks. Her best friend, Kaylee, after hearing Sarah sing her poem at lunchtime, has a good idea. Sing, Sarah! introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • compare and contrast • summarising. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: poem, stutter, practise, brave • Phonics and phonemic awareness: -oo (short /oo/ and long /oo/).
node.strategies: Summarising |Compare and contrast |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Compare and contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Does speaking in front of people make you nervous? Do you use any strategies to help you?
node.esiss_activities: Find a poem or write a poem to perform in front of the class or group. You might even want to sing the poem to your classmates!
node.esiss_hfw: after, always, ask, back, can, down, have, know, like, said, say
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Compare and Contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1463 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1474 ACELA1471 ACELA1823 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591

node.title: Smash!
node.content: This book looks at friends and how people need to take responsibility for their actions. One day after school, three friends (Edie, Max and Finn) are playing soccer in the park. Max kicks the ball to Finn, but he misses it. The ball flies over the fence and smashes into the window of a house. Smash! introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making predictions • identifying compare and contrast. Features of the Book: • The information contained in illustrations • Content words for discussion: fault, annoyed, blame, own up • Phonics and phonemic awareness: words with the /sh/ sound.
node.theme: Character education| Friends| Responsibility| School and community
node.strategies: Making predictions |Compare and contrast |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Compare and contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Max, Finn and Edie accidentally break their neighbour's window whilst playing soccer. Have you ever accidentally broken something belonging to someone else? How did you feel and who did you tell?
node.esiss_activities: Re-read page 7. Imagine that it was Edie who ran away and Max and Finn who stayed to face Mrs West and then write a new conversation between Edie, Max and Finn.
node.esiss_hfw: away, day, get, have, run, said, say, tell

node.title: Stories in the Sky
node.content: This book looks at the patterns of stars called constellations. It features the different names and stories about different constellations and how people from long ago used stars to find their way. The book also features the legend of the constellations Perseus and Andromeda. Stories in the Sky introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making connections • visualising • distinguishing fact from opinion. Features of the Book: • The information contained in photographs and illustrations • Content words for discussion: compasses, constellations, patterns, stargazers • Phonics and phonemic awareness: -ar (r-controlled sound /ar/)
node.theme: Ancient people| Scientific investigation| Space| History
node.strategies: Making connections|Visualising|Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: This book explains the stories behind the names of various constellations. Have you ever looked up at the sky on a clear night? Do you know the names of any stars or constellations?
node.esiss_activities: Reread page 7 and write three facts about the constellation on this page. Find three extra facts using the Internet.
node.esiss_hfw: always, before, call, gave, made, or, their, these
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Visualising, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
