node.title: The Eiffel Tower
node.content: This book looks at the history of the Eiffel Tower in Paris, France. It focuses on why and who built this iconic tower and features people's different reactions before and after the tower's completion. The book presents facts and figures in chart form, as well as important dates and events on a pictorial timeline, which illustrates four stages of the construction process. The author gives a glimpse into what the Eiffel Tower's opening night was like, then ends the book with how people have enjoyed and interacted with the tower over the years. Features of the Book: • The information contained in photographs and illustrations. • Content words for discussion: design, foundations, iron, sections • Phonics and phonemic awareness: ow (long /o/ sound - show, owned, flown and the /ou/ sound - tower, down)
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: The Eiffel Tower is the most famous building in Paris but when, how and why was it built?
node.esiss_activities: With a partner, discuss what you have learned about the Eiffel Tower. Then, summarise the book using key words and your own words. Don't forget to use the main ideas in the book to help you summarise.
node.esiss_hfw: first, made, many, off, or, their, why, would
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Barking Dog
node.content: This story looks at the fear some people have of dogs and how to overcome that fear. Hera lives next door to Mrs Frost, whose small dog Lexi barks at everyone and everything. Even though Lexi is little, she scares Hera. One day, Hera hears a change in Lexi's bark. She looks over the fence and sees that Mrs Frost has hurt her ankle and needs Hera's help. Hera is worried about coming near Lexi, but she wants to help her neighbour. Lexi senses that Hera is there to help Mrs Frost and stops barking. Over time, Hera gets to know Lexi and learns that Lexi barks for different reasons. In the end, Hera and Mrs Frost take Lexi to a dog show together, and Hera feels comfortable around not only Lexi but also the other dogs. The Barking Dog introduces and reinforces the following strategies: • making connections • determining author's purpose • asking and answering questions Features of the text: The information contained in the illustrations • Content words for discussion: barking, proud, scared • Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends " fr and pr"; vowel blends " around/ ground/house/outside/proud/sounded"
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Neighbourhood|Overcoming obstacles|Pets
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Questioning, Making Inferences, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Hera is scared of her neighbour's dog, Lexi. When her neighbour has an accident, Hera learns to overcome her fear.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are nervous about meeting a dog you've never met before. Write a list of questions you could ask the dog's owner before you approach it.
node.esiss_hfw: always, around, made, right, why
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Amazing Amazon
node.content: My world has many different shapes. Wind and water can change the shape of the land. Using dramatic photographs and simple diagrams, this book provides information about the Amazon River, which is forever changing shape as water flows along it. (Big idea: The land can change shape over time). Features of Amazing Amazon • the topic (Amazon), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • headings that introduce new aspects of the topic and support students to read information in sections • contents page, glossary (boldface type), index • captions that add information • aerial views • chart to show comparison • map with a key • captions to give information about photographs • diagram that explains the action of the river • labels to help read features on aerial maps • digraphs - "ch", "th", "sh" • compound words - everywhere, riverbank • contractions - it's • suffixes - "-ly", "-est", "-ing" • silent letters - high, through, mighty, climb, knocks
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring writer's craft|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Drawing conclusions, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: The Amazon is not the longest river in the world, but it is the widest, flowing for more than 6000 kilometres from the Andes Mountains to the Atlantic Ocean. Along the way, it travels through the largest rain forest on Earth.
node.esiss_activities: The Amazon is the second longest river in the world. Why is the Amazon so amazing? Find five facts about the Amazon. Explain why each fact makes the Amazon special.
node.esiss_hfw: again, along, around, behind, eat, into, live, long, look, many, other, people, their, through, where
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Let the Games Begin
node.content: Many different people live in my world. This book looks at the way people from all around the world gather together to compete in the Olympic Games. It focuses on the changes that have occurred since the first Olympic Games. (Big idea: Many different people live in the world. Features of Let the Games Begin • topic (Olympics), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • headings • captions that add information • historical information • digraphs - "th", "sh", "ch", "wh" • silent "e" - flame • words with repeated letters - different, millions, winner, allowed, winning, summer • compound words - worldwide, wheelchairs, basketball
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: The Olympic Games began as a small event in Greece. Today, over 200 nations take part in over 300 different events. This book explains how the Olympics are about more than just sport.
node.esiss_activities: The Olympic Games began over three thousand years ago. Since then there have been many changes. Look through the book and find four changes that have been made to the games. Have these changes made the Olympics better? Write some sentences to explain why.
node.esiss_hfw: another, each, first, into, many, other, such, take, three, where
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Transform It!
node.content: You can recycle metal, glass, paper, and plastic. This cuts down on rubbish and saves space in landfills. This book looks at some of the items that can be made from recycled glass, paper, plastic, and aluminium. Interesting photographs and lively text create many opportunities for discussion. (Big idea: People need to recycle things instead of throwing them away.) Features of Transform It • the topic (recycling), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • contents page and index • questions as headings • photographs • flowcharts • captions • glossary (boldface type) • labels • initial consonant blends - "tr-", "sp-", "pr-", "gl-", "str-", "cr-", "st-", "fr-", "pl-", "cl-", "br-" • procedural words - first, next, then, now, finally • compound words - landfill, windscreen, everywhere, newspaper, cardboard, skateboards
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: You don't have to throw glass, paper, metal, or plastic into the rubbish. You can recycle them. Or you can transform them! Here are some great ideas for turning rubbish into treasures.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, diagrams show how glass and plastic are recycled. Draw another diagram that shows how paper is recycled to make new paper. If you have any questions, ask a family member to help you.
node.esiss_hfw: about, again, around, because, before, each, find, first, into, long, look, many, next, other, think, use
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1459 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Dragon's Egg
node.content: Nick shows his brother and sister a "dragon's" egg and tells them that if they want to see it hatch, they'll have to do his chores. When Mum finds out, Nick discovers what it's like to have a trick played on him. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of setting a purpose for reading, identifying the Determining main ideas, and forming and supporting opinions. Text Features • the theme of honesty and fairness • the way Nick tricked his brother and sister and the way Mum solved the problem in a clever way • the complex narrative structure • the dialogue to show the characters' thoughts, feelings, and actions • the indicators of time - one day, every afternoon, after a few days • the word endings - y, ing, ly • the commas for phrasing • the colourful and lively illustrations • the compound words - shoebox, eyelashes, eyebrows • the hyperbole - it's not fair! • the exclamation marks for emphasis • the similes for description • the lively vocabulary - fiery, nestled, chomping • the contractions - weren't, there's, they'd, it'll, wouldn't, it's, we'd, he's, won't, there's, let's, he'll, I'll, couldn't, wasn't, I'm, egg's • the possessive apostrophe - Nick's, Sam's.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Nick says he has a real dragon's egg and that he's going to hatch it under his bed. If his brother and sister want to be in on the secret, they have to do his chores. But in the end, Nick gets a taste of his own medicine.
node.esiss_activities: Nick tricked his brother and sister. The dragon's egg was really a rock. Write another ending for the story where the egg really hatches. Draw a picture of Nick with a speech bubble. What would he be saying when he sees the dragon?
node.esiss_hfw: because, before, their, under, unless, want, where
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Great Bean Race
node.content: Mr Moffat's purple racing beans cause instant excitement in the class as each child tries to coax their bean to reach the finish line first. Features of the Book - The use of dialogue and the question-and answer format. - Innovative uses of print to convey emphasis and increasing excitement. - The development of the children's characters through direct speech. - Effective use of punctuation to build momentum - ellipses, exclamation marks, and question marks. - The use of storybook language - In went the stones and Up, up, up they grew. - Detailed information about growing beans conveyed through an exciting narrative. - Alliteration in the names - Mr Moffat and Hester Hewitt. - The concept of time passing. Purpose The Great Bean Race can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding; - identifying the plot and characters within the story; - recognising and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings; - writing about the steps in a procedure.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining cause and effect, Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: The class is having a great bean race. Whose bean will be the tallest? After the holidays, the children find out that measuring all of the beans might be difficult!
node.esiss_activities: Write the story as a set of instructions for growing beans successfully.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Recognising Cause and Effect, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Home for Diggory
node.content: When his owner goes overseas, Diggory has to go to the dogs' home. Many unsuitable people take Diggory home with them, but they always return him. At last, the right person for Diggory is found. Features of the Book • The style of the illustrations • The appeal of a dog as the main character • Repetitive pattern of events • Contractions - I'm, can't, don't, isn't • Double consonants - Diggory, Anna, worry, biggest • Direct speech, - Double oo spelling pattern - stood, toot, looked Purpose A Home for Diggory can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - tracing story development across multiple episodes, - exploring problem resolution, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - reading aloud with expression, - using charts and diagrams to record important details. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Diggory is a big, friendly dog who needs a new home. But sometimes Diggory is too big and too friendly, and it gets him into trouble. Who will give Diggory a new home?
node.esiss_activities: Create an advertisement to find a home for Diggory. What kind of dog is Diggory? What kind of person would he suit. Use words that will help "sell" Diggory. Draw a picture of Diggory.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: All About Me!
node.content: Eight-year-old Lesley E. Thompson has written a school project that looks at some of the events that have happened to her. Amusing descriptions and anecdotes accompany photos of her family, friends, holidays, and school. Features of the Book - Dedication page, - A fictionalised memoir, - Events familiar to eight-year-old children, - School project format, - First-person narrative, - Questions addressed to the reader, - Humour Purpose - All about Me can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making connections between children's experiences and the text, - understanding the features of an autobiographical project, - understanding the parts of a book, - using text as a model for writing.
node.strategies: Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Hi, I'm Lesley E. Thompson. This book is about me and my life, so far. I don't mind if you skip the bits about my brother, because he's boring. But make sure you read about my best friend, Stinky.
node.esiss_activities: Ask your parents for a photo of you when you were younger. What were you doing? What might you have been thinking or saying? Draw the photo. Write some sentences that describe what was happening in the photo. Add speech bubbles to your photo. Share it with your family and friends.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: There's a Boy under the Bed
node.content: This is a humorous and lively version of a traditional tale. Smook is a monster, and one day he finds a boy under his bed. Despite his fear of humans, a friendship develops between the boy and Smook. They realise they have a lot in common. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of making connections with real-life experiences, making and confirming predictions, and comparing and contrasting. Text Features • the themes of tolerance, celebrating differences, and not judging what is feared • the relationship between the characters and the friendship that develops despite their differences • the narrative structure and use of dialogue to drive it • the indicators of time - next morning, at dinner time • the interesting past-tense action verbs - snatched, croaked, flopped, squeaked, poked, flip-flopped, crawled, shoved, wriggled • the words ending in "ed" • the opposites - locked and unlocked, up and down • the irregular past-tense verbs • the contractions - there's, don't, couldn't, he'd, didn't, can't, boy's, I'll • the onomatopoeia - aaargh, boo, thump, ouch, icky • the colloquial language • the punctuation for emphasis and direction • the compound words - trapdoor, marshmallow, snailburgers, seaweed, upset • the interesting imaginative content words - snailburgers, Smook, slug slime, Colossa.
node.strategies: Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Smook is an ordinary monster. Like all monsters, he's afraid that there's a boy under his bed. When it turns out to be true, Smook's mother is terrified and wants to get rid of the boy. But Smook has grown to like his new friend.
node.esiss_activities: What are the differences between the boy and Smook? How are they the same? Now design your own creature. How are you and your creature the same? How are you different?
node.esiss_hfw: because, behind, such, their, through, under
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Brother Trouble
node.content: This is a personal narrative about a sister and a brother who act in TV advertisments, and Lauren is tired of Justin always having the more powerful role. When a solution is found, Justin learns what it feels like to change roles. Features of the Book -Familiar family theme, -Problem resolution, -Specialised vocabulary - studio, wrap, director, script, - Contractions - that's, didn't, it's, I'm, you'll, everyone's, he's, you're, - Examples of TV scripts, - Colloquial language - shoe's on the other foot Purpose - Brother Trouble can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - Making connections between children's experiences and the text, - exploring problemresolution, - recognizing and understanding colloquial language, - reading aloud with expression, - using charts and diagrams to record important details, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Drawing conclusions|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Sequencing, Discussing point of view, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Lauren is tired of her brother, Justin. He's a big bully. With a little help, she teaches Justin a lesson and has some fun too!
node.esiss_activities: Anita solves Lauren's problem in this story by swapping the children's roles. In what other ways could Lauren and her family solve the problem? Rewrite the ending of the story.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Ready, Set, Go!
node.content: This is a personal narrative about Jenny, who features in Rainbows all Around (Skyrider Chapter Books). In this story, Jenny makes a new friend by teaching him how to make a racing car. Features of the Book - Narrative and procedural text, - Incidental inclusion of disability, - Development of the characters through the story, - Problem resolution, - Contractions - how's, it's, I'l,l what's, haven't, I'm, don't, - Compound words - hallway, pancakes, breakfast, - Blends - cl, pl, cr, sh, tr, ch, wh, gr, th, - Features Jenny from Rainbows All Around a Skyrider chapter book Purpose - Ready Set Go! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationship,s and actions, - writing contractions, - following written instructions, - recognising and understanding compound words, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Jenny meets the new boy next door and teaches him how to make a racing car. They have fun racing together. Who do you think will be the winner?
node.esiss_activities: Read the instructions on page 32 on how to make a racing car. Get the equipment you will need. You could use a milk carton instead of a juice box. Follow the steps to make your own racing car. Ask an adult to help you make the holes. Decorate your car and race it!
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Any Excuse
node.content: This is a lively and entertaining play about a boy who forgets his homework. His friends try to resolve the problem by thinking of the best excuse. Their teacher appears to have the best excuse, tricking them all in the end. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of noting details in illustrations and drawing conclusions. This is a good text for studying a narrative in play format and how a script is written and performed. Text Features • the theme of being honest • the relationship between the characters and the way they each try to resolve the problem • the conventions of a play - the list of characters, the scene instructions, the stage directions (written in the present tense and in italics) • the detailed and humorous illustrations • the commas to support phrasing • the contractions - she'd, you'll, I'm, don't, that's, hadn't, I've, what'll, haven't • the content words • the word endings - ing, ed, ly • the exclamation marks for emphasis • the colloquial language - cool, this'll be boring • The compound words - butterflies, grandchildren, homework, spaceship, otherwise, somewhere • the onomatopoeia - shhhhh!, wow! • the hyperbole • the lively and descriptive past-tense verbs - trampled, stampeded, snatched, carried, hovering, swooped, kidnapped.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making connections|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Sam has forgotten his homework. His friends think of excuses that become more and more unbelievable. In the end, he doesn't need an excuse at all.
node.esiss_activities: Sam has forgotten his homework. All the children help him think up a good excuse. What excuse would you think up for Sam? Write the funniest excuse you can.
node.esiss_hfw: above, around, before, could, find, give, going, got, home, into, know, last, left, say, school, think, through
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Concrete Jungle
node.content: Maria is envious of her cousin's school in the country because it has trees to climb and a garden while her school is all concrete. She comes up with a plan to make her school playground colourful and exciting. Features of the Book - Colloquial language - awesome, concrete jungle, wow, - Direct speech, - Ellipses and hyphens, - Specialised vocabulary - artist, brainstorm, creek, hardware, mural, permission, waterfall, - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - brainstorm, outline, paintbrushes, waterfall, - Double vowel "oo" - good, school, shook, - Verb ending "ed" - asked, grumbled, painted, - "ly" word ending - excitedly, nervously, - Multisyllabic words - colourful, dinosaurs, grandparents, permission, - Regular and irregular plurals - bushes, butterflies, houses, ideas Purpose - Concrete Jungle can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - relating the characters settings and plot to real-life situations, - reading aloud with expression, - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - exploring dialogue, - exploring word families, - writing using capital letters and punctuation appropriately. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Maria's school has a concrete playground with concrete walls. Her cousin's playground in the country has grass and trees. Maria thinks it's time her class did something to change their school.
node.esiss_activities: Go outside and look at your backyard. What things would you like to change? You might want to weed the garden or make a mural for the fence or wall. Make a plan and show it to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Walter and the Inventor's Garden
node.content: Ivan the inventor is hoping that his vegetables will win a blue ribbon at the show, but Walter the dragon burns them with his fiery breath. Walter, Maisie, and Ivan work together to make new things to take to the show. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Features characters from Walter's Worries, - Humour, - Onomatopoeia - aaahhh CHOOO!, clunk, whir, - Specialised vocabulary - inventor, inventions, - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Hyphens - mini-sew, odd-looking, - Initial blend "fl" - flames, flowers, flying, - Digraphs "th" and "wh" - that, then, they, Thanks, thinking, what's, when, whir, - Multisyllabic words - anything, happening, inventor, measuring, tomatoes, vegetables Purpose - Walter and the Inventor's Garden can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - using the illustrations to gain a deeper understanding of the text, - making confirming and revising predictions, - exploring the digraph "th", - writing a response to literature. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Ivan the inventor is hoping his vegetables will win a blue ribbon at the show, but Walter's fiery breath gets out of control and burns them. What will they take to the show?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, Walter, Ivan, and Maisie come up with some clever ideas to use Ivan's beans, tomatoes, and corn. What would you make with the beans, tomatoes and corn? Explain how you would do it.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Where Is White Rabbit?
node.content: Things are going missing at Anna's house - her white rabbit, her brother's sweatshirt, and her mum's gardening glove. Anna and her brother set out to solve the mystery. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - New clues revealed throughout the plot, - Third-person narrative, - Mystery, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - bedroom, footprints, lookout, - Contractions - can't, don't, he's, she'd, they'll, we'll, where's, - Verb endings "ed" and "ing" - asked, checked, peered• boring, digging, gardening, missing Purpose - Where Is White Rabbit? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - Making connections between own experiences and the text, - recognising the structural features of a text, for example, theme, plot, setting, characters, - paraphrasing and Summarising information, - exploring the digraph "ch", - writing a letter. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: White rabbit goes missing, and then other things start to disappear. Who or what is taking them? Maybe the answer is closer than you think!
node.esiss_activities: The children in the story want to keep one of the kittens. Imagine you are Anna or Leo. Write a letter to your mother asking if you can keep a kitten. Remember to include how you will look after the kitten and why it would be good to have a cat in the family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Zoo Overnight
node.content: Jenny and Ben win a Zoo Overnight contest in the library's holiday programme. Jenny is worried about spending the night away from her mum, but Ben has a solution. Instructions for making a diorama are included in the book. Features of the Book - Activity with instructions, - Direct speech, - The characters Jenny and Ben from Ready Set Go!, - Third-person narrative, - Specialised vocabulary - contest, cubs, diorama, enclosure, grassland, habitat, savanna, scene Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - grassland, maybe, overnight, - Contractions - couldn't, I'll, that'd, who'd, you'll, - Initial blend "br" - bring, breath, - Digraphs "ch" and "sh" - chair, chattered, choose• shook Purpose - Zoo Overnight can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - asking and answering questions about a text, - inferring meaning, - using onsets and rimes to learn new words, - following written instructions, - writing simple instructions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: Jenny and Ben enter a contest, and the winners will spend a night at the zoo. Jenny is worried about being away from her mum for the night. Will Jenny go if they win the contest?
node.esiss_activities: Choose an animal habitat that you find interesting. Make a diorama for your animal habitat or follow the instructions on page 32 to make an African savannah diorama.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Poppy
node.content: This non-fiction title traces the life cycle of a poppy, from bud to new seedlings. Features of the Book • The poetic quality of the text. • The sequential, cyclical nature of the story line. • The use of imagery - "morning sun" and "crinkled red poppy." • The use of colors - "red," "yellow," "gold," and "brown." • The frequent use of adjectives. • The passing of time and how this is shown in the text. • The diagram of the life cycle of the poppy. • The use of evocative language - "bend its head" and "soft brown earth." Purpose The Poppy can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • summarising the ideas from a story • encouraging prediction and developing questioning skills • developing the skill of writing using a range of word choices.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences, Drawing conclusions, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: The poppy in her grandfather's garden helps this girl understand the life cycle of a flower.
node.esiss_activities: This book shows the life cycle of a poppy. Draw six boxes. In each box, draw what the poppy looks like at a stage of its life cycle. Put a number on each box to show which is first and last.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Cat and the Stars
node.content: This is a touching narrative about Evie and her grandmother. Evie is tired of sharing her home with her grandmother. One day, Evie discovers that they are both interested in the stars, and she finds out that her grandmother can teach her more than she realised. This text supports the Comprehension strategies of summarising the Determining main ideas; interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships and actions; and making judgments. Text Features • the themes of co-operation and tolerance and finding things in common to share • the relationship between the characters and the different points of view • the narrative structure and the use of dialogue to drive it • the indicators of time - on Saturday morning, that night, the next morning, that afternoon • the action verbs that provide interest to the writing - banging, crept, whispered, rubbed, shining • the word endings - ed, ing ,ly, est • the comparative adjective - tallest • the irregular past-tense verbs - had, said • the contractions - don't, I'm, didn't, there's, they're, you're • the alliteration - clever cat, seven stars shining • the onomatopoeia - meow, purr • the colloquial language - OK, oh! • the commas for phrasing • the compound words - Grandmother, forever, downstairs, pancakes, strawberries, everything, upstairs, afternoon, sometimes, goodnight • the blends -gr - grandmother; -sh - shiny, shadow; -wh - whispered; -br - bright, brave; -cl - climbing; -st - storm.
node.strategies: Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Evie is annoyed. Her grandmother is staying in her bedroom. Evie has to sleep in the sewing room. One night, Grandma shows Evie her favourite stars and tells her a story. Evie realises that her Grandma is an important part of the family.
node.esiss_activities: There are a lot of compound words in this story. Can you find them all? Write each compound word. Next to the word, write the two words that are joined up.
node.esiss_hfw: around, behind, had, herself, who
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: How to Weigh an Elephant
node.content: Clever Little Tsao has the solution to the problem perplexing her elders. Her biggest problem is getting them to listen to her! Features of the Book • A clear introduction, middle, and conclusion. • A clear sequence of problem-solving skills - problem identified, possible solutions considered, one course of action chosen, success gained, and success acknowledged. • The use of mathematical language - "weigh," "add," "heavier," "scales," and "numbers." • The math concepts involving weight and displacement. • The use of Various word endings - "ier," "ed," and "ing." • Present and past forms of verbs - "take" and "took," "weigh" and "weighed," "work" and "worked," "pay" and "paid." • The use of italics for emphasis. Purpose How to Weigh an Elephant can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • comparing books by different authors written in the same genre • understanding the function, feature, and category of new words encountered in the story • developing the skill of writing in a sequential manner.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The elephant is far too big for the tiny scales. How can the rich man weigh it? Everyone has clever ideas, but it is a small girl who solves the problem.
node.esiss_activities: Use your problem solving skills. What is another way that the people could have weighed the elephant? Draw a picture of your idea. Write the steps in order.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1665 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Quilt for Kiri
node.content: A quilt from the Cook Islands becomes the connection between Kiri and the grandmother she will never meet. Features of the Book • The use of imagery - "moonlight lay spread across her bed" and "like a big, wet rainbow." • The use of adjectives - "old," "torn," "tiny," "crinkly," and "lovely." • The similes - "like the words of a song" and "like a big, wet rainbow." • The compound words - "rainbow," "moonlight," "grandmother," and "birthday." • The concepts of distance, death, and dreams. • The design of the quilt and its connection to the flower. • The additional information and map. Purpose A Quilt for Kiri can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: -identifying the themes of the book• -identifying the plot and characters within the story• -developing the skill of writing in a sequential manner.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Kiri's grandmother lives far across the sea. When her grandmother dies, Kiri gets a parcel in the mail. It's a special quilt that will help her to learn a lot about her grandmother.
node.esiss_activities: Think of someone you know that lives far away. Write a letter to them. Tell them about something interesting that happened to you this week.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: At the End of the Day
node.content: At the End of the Day describes how a class of children enjoy a message circle at the end of their school day. Features of the Book • The sequence of steps involved in making a massage circle. • The use of photographs to support the text. • The use of "s" to denote the plural form - "hands," "shapes," "circles," "fingers," and "hugs." • The contractions - "we're," "we've," and "it's." • The use of question marks and exclamation points. Purpose At the End of the Day can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • understanding the punctuation cues and their meanings • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • writing using pictures to support the events in the story.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Visualising, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: What's the best way to end the day? This class has the answer. Read this book to discover the perfect activity to finish your school day.
node.esiss_activities: Would you like to have a massage circle at the end of the day? Write some instructions that explain what to do in a massage circle. Ask your teacher if you can try them out.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Playhouse
node.content: This book explores how the past can be pieced together from artefacts and oral history. Features of the Book - The clear beginning, middle, and end of the story. - Illustrations that support the text and provide additional information. - The use of descriptive narrative in the first person. - The use of past and present tense. - The use of a historical perspective on pages 8 and 9. - The use of sepia tones in the illustration on page 8 to indicate a scene from the past. - The range of prepositions - in, by, on, beside, from, after, next to, and under. - The compound word - playhouse. - The concept of pretending. Purpose The Playhouse can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: - discussing the organisational structure of the text; - discussing the relationship between cause and effect within the story; - developing the ability to write specifically about the detail of the setting of the story.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: A girl finds some treasures in the dirt near the stone wall. Her grandparents use the objects to tell her about the history of Mrs McCoy's shop.
node.esiss_activities: This story has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Find someone in your family and tell them the story in your own words. Make sure you tell the beginning, the middle, and the end.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Dave's Food Truck
node.content: This story is about a man named Dave who runs a successful food truck. Dave has a schedule of places where he sets up his food truck - the city, the beach, and the park. He enjoys interacting with the customers and cares about them and their preferences. One day, his regular customers surprise him with flowers to thank him for his friendly service. Features of the Book - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - customer, liked, ready - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - ck; long vowel sounds - balloon/food/smoothies/soon Purpose Dave's Food Truck introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making predictions; - determining main ideas; - asking and answering questions.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Dave runs a food truck. One day, his customers give him a surprise to thank him for his friendly service.
node.esiss_activities: Dave's customers bring him flowers to thank him for his friendly service. Dave then changes the name of his food truck to "The Happy Food Truck". Write a different ending to the story. In what other way could the customers have thanked Dave? What could Dave have then done to show that he likes his customers.
node.esiss_hfw: always, before, first, made, off, their, these, us, work, your
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Amazing Bug Trap
node.content: Mr Harris is an avid vegetable gardener who is proud of his prize-winning cabbages. One day, he finds holes in the cabbages, made by earwigs. His neighbours Maria and Tom build a bug trap to catch the earwigs, but it doesn't work. Maria and Tom revise their trap by adding a light stick to attract the earwigs. Mr Harris tries the improved trap and it works. Then all the neighbours want one of Maria and Tom's bug traps for their gardens. Purpose - The information contained in the illustrations. - Content words for discussion - invention, problem, solve, trap - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blend - tr; short vowel sounds - bug/but/munching/suddenly; word ending - ly Strategies The Amazing Bug Catcher introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making predictions; - determining cause and effect; - determining main ideas.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining cause and effect, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Mr Harris's cabbages are being eaten by earwigs. How will Maria and Tom help him catch the earwigs?
node.esiss_activities: To catch the earwigs that are eating the cabbages, Maria and Tom invent a bug trap that uses light sticks. What other natural ways could they have tried to catch the earwigs? Use library books or the Internet to do your research. Write a short report about what you found.
node.esiss_hfw: been, gave, made, read, their, those, very, work
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Cooped Up
node.content: This is a story about a young boy who moves to the city and finds his new life lonely. Josh thinks of the perfect solution for his loneliness - a pet. But first Josh and Grandad need to convince Mum that a pet is a good idea. Features of the Book - Multisyllabic words - backyard, apartment, understood, cockatoo, parakeet, - Blends - st, br, tr, bl, th, sh, pl, dr, - Contractions - he'd wouldn't you've don't, - Direct speech, - Expressive illustrations, - Italics and punctuation for emphasis, - The -ing word ending - zooming, amazing, - Double oo spelling pattern - cooped Purpose - Cooped Up can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - tracing the development of a character, - using illustrations to gain and deepen meaning, - identifying the setting of a story, - exploring the use of direct speech, - recognising and reading multisyllabic words, - identifying a problem and its resolution. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Personal change|Problem solving
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Josh is lonely and misses his old house, especially his dog, Duchess. They can't have pets in their apartment, but Grandad finds a way to keep everyone happy.
node.esiss_activities: Authors use speech marks to show when someone is talking. Find the sentences in the book where someone is talking. Notice the speech marks. Where would you put speech marks in these sentences? • Look at them zooming around out there, he thought. • I'm going to make a feeding station, Josh said. • This is great, said Grandad when they got home.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Freeze, Goldilocks!
node.content: This play gives a modern twist to the familiar Goldilocks story. The three bears and the police search the bears' house, looking for the intruder. Features of the Book - - Dialogue with stage directions, supported by illustrations that show costume and staging suggestions, - Based on the traditional story of The Three Bears, - Use of humour and a familiar, modern setting to liven up the old story, - Questions to progress the plot, - Specialised and colloquial language - kid, tag, MO, - Contractions - I'd, don't, you're, I've, that's, doesn't, we'll, it's, she's, they've, we'd, isn't. For more information, please see our lesson plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When the Bear family discover someone has been in their house, Father Bear calls the police. Who would eat the Bears' porridge? A golden, curly hair might be a clue.
node.esiss_activities: This Goldilocks story is different to the fairy tale. What changes have been made? Write a list of the words that have been used to make the story sound modern. If Goldilocks was caught in this story, what would happen to her? Write a new ending.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Going on Safari
node.content: Joanne and her friend Devina are on safari in the Masai Mara National Park in Kenya, Africa. We learn about the wild animals that they see, including giraffes, elephants, leopards, and hippos. Features of the Book - Captioned photographs, - Comparisons to convey meaning - as fast as a car; four times as long as your foot, - Fact boxes, - Maps, - Specialised vocabulary - clearing, herd, illegal, kill, mammal, predator, pride, roam, safari, vicious, Word study opportunities - Compound words - highlands, flatlands, backyard, sometimes, afternoon, backbone, nearby, - Contractions - we're, they're, it's, aren't, doesn't, we've, I've, can't, he's, she's, - Initial blends "fr," "pr", and "gr"- friend, free, from, protects, predators, pride, grass, grumpy, great Purpose - Going on Safari can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - using the table of contents, chapter headings, glossary and index to locate information, - using visual structural and meaning cues to read unknown words, - exploring initial blends, - writing to communicate information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Determining main ideas, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Devina and Joanne are on holiday in Africa. They see all kinds of animals, including their favourites.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are Joanne or Devina. Write a postcard home to your family to tell them about your adventure. Include at least two facts about your favourite safari animal.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Making Yoghurt
node.content: This book contains step-by-step instructions for making yoghurt and suggests different ways that it can be eaten. It also includes the history and health benefits of this food. Features of the Book - Instructions for an activity, - Captioned photographs, - Question-and-answer format, - Specialised vocabulary - bacteria, calcium, Celsius, degrees, ingredients, liquid, multiply, powdered protein, sterilises temperature, thermometer, Word Study Opportunities - Double vowels - cool, smoothie; sweet, freeze, - Word ending "est" - creamiest, thickest, - Digraph "ch" - change, children, check, - Initial blend "fr" - Francesca, fresh, from, - Multisyllabic words - bacteria, dishwasher, everything, ingredients, multiplying, temperature Purpose - Making Yoghurt can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - recognising the features of non-fiction text, - using context clues and prior knowledge to understand unfamiliar words, - reading and writing procedural text, - exploring multisyllabic words through segmenting, - writing simple instructions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Try making your own yoghurt. It's easy and fun, and it tastes good.
node.esiss_activities: The children wanted to find out more about yoghurt. They wrote some questions. What else do you want to know about yoghurt? Find the answers by asking someone or looking in books or on the Internet .
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Maze Craze
node.content: This book looks at the minotaur maze in ancient Greece, the huge hedge mazes of Hawaii and Hampton Court, and modern mirror and maize mazes. Features of The Maze Craze • comparisons to convey meaning • maps • measurements • specialised vocabulary - challenge, craze, hedge, image, maize, maze, reflections Purpose The Maze Craze can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: • identify the use of repetition, rhythm, rhyme, and alliteration in texts • using visual, structural, and meaning cues to read unknown words • making connections between own experiences and the text • understanding the author's purpose; writing text that has a beginning, middle, and end
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Activating prior knowledge, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: There's usually only one way in and out of a maze. The challenge is to find your way to the end without getting lost!
node.esiss_activities: Solve the maze on page 15. Now design your own maze. Make as many twists and turns as you can. Give your maze to someone in your family. Can they find their way to the middle of the maze?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: On the Ball
node.content: How do balls bounce? What are they made from? How do they help us? The answers to all these questions, and a poem, are found in the text. Features of the Book - Activity with instructions, - Labelled diagrams, - Poetic language, - Hyphens - Different-shaped in-between table-tennis, - Specialised vocabulary - ball bearings, cork, latex, natural, pulu tree, rubber, sealed, synthetic, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - baseball, basketball, snowballs, - Double consonants - ball, collected, filled, flattens, hitting, less, passing, rolling, - Initial blend "spr" - spreads, springs, - Digraph "ch" - change, children Purpose - On the Ball can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - using the table of contents, chapter headings, glossary, and index to locate information, - paraphrasing and Summarising information, - interpreting the information in diagrams charts and graphs, - identifying the use of repetition, rhythm, rhyme, and alliteration in texts, - using descriptive language. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Sports balls come in different sizes, shapes, and weights. They are fun to throw, hit, and kick. You can have fun bouncing sports balls, too!
node.esiss_activities: Try the drop test on page 15. You will need two balls. Drop each ball and see which one bounces the highest. Were your results similar to those in the book? Write a short report about your results.
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Tracks on the Ground
node.content: Animals and people make all kinds of tracks. This book explains what different tracks look like and what we can learn from them. The text includes instructions for making footprints using paint and "feet" from plaster of Paris. Features of Tracks on the Ground • Instructions for an activity • Captioned photographs • Labeled diagrams • Specialized vocabulary - cloven, coyote, fossil, hind, hoof, imprint, plaster cast, plaster of Paris, search-and-rescue, webbed Tracks on the Ground can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: • identifying the Determining main idea and supporting detail; • extending vocabulary by using a glossary; • reading and writing procedural text; • using knowledge of the words in compound words to predict meaning; • writing simple instructions.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|School and community
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Animals and people make all kinds of tracks. Find out what different tracks look like, and learn how to make your own tracks too.
node.esiss_activities: Draw some of the animal tracks in the book. Ask members of your family to name the animal that each track belongs to. Did they guess correctly?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Four Friends
node.content: This story looks at friendship and how good friends help and look out for each other. Crow, Deer, Rat, and Turtle are friends. One day, Deer is caught in a hunter's trap. Crow, Rat, and Turtle find her, but just after they rescue her, the hunter returns and captures Turtle. Deer, Crow, and Rat refuse to abandon their friend. They trick the hunter and are able to free Turtle. In the end, the four animals realise the value of good friends. Features of the Text - The information contained in the illustrations - Content words for discussion - friend, rescue, thank - Phonics and phonemic awareness: consonant blends - sh, st; long vowel sounds - arrived/behind/hiding/high/idea/nice/tied; short vowel sounds - hid/it/in/is/limped/tricking/until/vanished/whispered; word endings - ed Purpose The Four Friends introduces and reinforces the following strategies: - making predictions; - forming opinions; - determining author's purpose.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Discussing point of view
node.esiss_strategy: Analysing themes, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: The hunter has caught Turtle. Will Crow, Deer, and Rat be able to save their friend?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are one of the four friends in the story. Write a different ending. In what other way could you trick the hunter?
node.esiss_hfw: around, best, don't, found, these, very
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Walk Tall
node.content: This book includes an interview with a stilt walker and infomation about the contemporary and historical use of stilts in different countries. There are fact boxes and maps throughout the text. Features of the Book - Diagrams and maps, - Fact boxes, - Measurements, - Specialised vocabulary - marathons, stilts, and maps throughout the text, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - Bigfoot, footrests, - Double consonants - borrow, called, carry, slipped, stepped, wobble, - Initial blends "pr", "sp", and "str" - practise, products, pruners• special, spirits• Stretch, strong, - Multisyllabic words - California, centimetres, kilometres, marathons, important Purpose - Walk Tall can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - using visual, structural, and meaning cues to read unknown words, - using the illustrations to gain a deeper understanding of the text, - exploring problem, resolution, - exploring word families, - using charts and diagrams to record information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever walked on stilts? It can be a lot of fun. The author did some research and found some amazing facts about stilt walking.
node.esiss_activities: Reread page 14. Make a chart with the heading "Jobs for Stilt Walkers". On the chart, write the jobs that people can do on stilts. Next to each job, write how you think stilts make the job easier.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Rats, Bats, and Black Pudding
node.content: Small Cat, frustrated by her size, asks Black Bull how to grow bigger, with interesting results. Features of the Book • The humor in the text, especially the sayings of Small Cat. • The use of large type for expression and characters' names. • The range of punctuation - ellipses, question marks, exclamation points, and quotation marks. • The adjectives - "big," "little," "small," "quick," and "clever." • The character list. • Repetition as a support for the reader. • The use of parentheses and/or italics for stage directions. Purpose Rats, Bats, and Black Puddings can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • understanding the punctuation cues and their meanings • discussing the organizational structure of the text • identifying the plot and characters within the story • writing a recount of the story using own words.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Small Cat asks Black Bull how she can grow bigger. Aunt Penty thinks Small Cat is fine just the way she is.
node.esiss_activities: Find some pieces of card, some markers, and some string. Make a mask for each character in the play. Ask two friends or family members to act out the play with you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Why There Are Shooting Stars
node.content: In this folktale, successive coyotes take on more than they can cope with when they insist that they can dance with the stars. When, at last, they fall, the people on Earth see them as shooting stars. Features of the Book • Paragraphs to indicate a change in place, time, or speaker. • The Various forms of the word "dance" - "danced," "dancing," and "dancer." • The word endings - "ed" and "ing." • The use of the words "moons" and "snows" to measure time. • The contractions - "can't" and "couldn't." • The style of the framed illustrations. Purpose Why There Are Shooting Stars can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: -recognizing the features of different genres• -comparing the opinions of the author with those of the reader• -comparing books by different authors written in the same genre• -writing in a similar style to that of the story.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Following instructions|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Making inferences, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: In this folk tale, Coyote wants to dance with the stars. When he becomes tired, he discovers that getting back to the ground is harder than he thinks!
node.esiss_activities: This book uses question marks to show when someone_x000D_ is asking a question. Look through the book to find _x000D_ the questions marks._x000D_ Now write the questions you have about shooting stars.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Let's Build a Bike
node.content: Scientists can explain how the different parts of a machine work together. Machines have different parts that work together. This text looks closely at the parts of a bike and explains how they make the bike move. (Big idea: Scientists study how things move and work.) Features of Let's Build a Bike • the topic (machines), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • headings • close-up photographs and visual features that highlight the bike parts (blue highlight, arrows, labels) • captions • pedal and brake diagrams • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • prefixes - "bi-", "tri-", "uni-" • vowel combinations - too, smoothly, wheels, steep, squeezing, treads, road • contractions - it's, let's, there's, won't, you'll, you're • prepositions - around, on, onto, over, to, without
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Bikes are amazing machines. They have so many moving parts. What's the best way to learn how all of these parts fit together? Let's build a bike!
node.esiss_activities: This book shows how to build a bike. Draw a diagram of the finished bike. Write a label for each part.
node.esiss_hfw: along, around, next, people, some, such
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: I Can See the Leaves
node.content: This story follows the procedure at an optometrist as a young boy is examined and fitted for glasses. Features of the Book • The use of reported speech. • Large blocks of text. • The boy's observations - "The clouds had edges! I could see the leaves." • Indirectly reported speech on pages 4, 7, and 10. • Specialized language - "optometrist," "chart," "glasses," "lenses," and "frames." • The contractions - "won't," "I'll," and "I'm." • The extensive use of punctuation - question marks, ellipses, and exclamation points. Purpose I Can See the Leaves can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding; understanding the punctuation cues and their meanings; writing in a similar style to that of the story; developing the skill of writing using direct speech.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Making predictions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: When things start to look blurry it's time to get your eyes checked. This father takes his son to get his first pair of glasses. He looks different, but now he can see the leaves on the trees.
node.esiss_activities: This boy has new glasses. He describes all the things he can see. Imagine you are the boy. Look out your window and write about something you can see. Describe it carefully.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Predictions, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Black Sock Swamp
node.content: This is a story about the consequences of destroying a mangrove swamp. It's told as a first-person narrative. The narrator is a child who loved the swamp because she loved walking in the squishy mud. Her grandmother and the old people of the village valued it as a breeding place for mullet, a fish the villagers relied on every year for food. However, others neither loved nor valued the swamp. When the swamp was filled in and the site was used for a hotel, everyone was happy except for the grandmother and the narrator. The following year, there were few mullet in the sea. So while the businessman who ran the hotel made money, the villagers lost out because they now had to buy fish from the market. Features of the Book - The strong match between the illustrations and the text. - The rich detail in the illustrations that provide clues to help students interpret the text. - Verbs that describe the texture of the mud and the sound when one walks on it, for example, squished and glugged. - The ellipsis on page 2 indicating a suspenseful pause. - The ellipsis on page 10 indicating time passing. - The black sock theme, which is repeated at the end. On page 12, the businessman's socks ironically remind the narrator of the mangrove swamp, her love of walking in it, and the way she would emerge with mud socks. Purpose of the Book - This book could be used to arouse an awareness of the importance of the mangrove swamp. Perhaps it will change the attitudes of students who think of tidal swamps as muddy places. - It could also provide an opportunity to think about the natural environment more generally, as a habitat (home) for many different living things that depend on each other. Destroying a habitat can have far-reaching consequences, and these consequences
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Determining cause and effect, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: When an old swamp is filled in to build a new hotel, Grandma knows that something bad will happen. When the fish don't return the next season, she knows she was right.
node.esiss_activities: Make a sign to explain to the village why it is important to save the swamp. Then write a list of things the people in the village need to do to keep the swamp safe.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Staying in Shape
node.content: Exercise keeps the different parts of your body in shape. Exercising every day keeps you healthy and strong. This text explores the importance of healthy eating and regular exercise. It looks at the benefits of staying in shape and offers advice on how to get started. (Big idea: People take care of themselves by doing exercise.) Features of Staying in Shape • the topic (keeping healthy), which expands on pages 12 and 13 from the anchor book Taking Care of Yourself • preview question on the back cover • headings as questions to focus attention and support students to read in sections • subheadings • bulleted lists • charts and tables • fitness plan • contents page • glossary • index • contractions - doesn't, you're, it's, let's • silent "g"- tongue • multisyllabic words - carbohydrate, energy, exercise, flexible, nutrients, recover
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Synthesising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Staying in shape means that your body will run well, like a shiny new car. This book explains how to eat healthily and excercise safely, so you feel great!
node.esiss_activities: In this book, the author writes about three kinds of exercise. Create a list of exercises that you can do each day. Include exercises that keep you flexible, strong, and healthy.
node.esiss_hfw: about, eat, feel, first, give, next, school, some, such, take, work
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1435 ACELA1434 ACELA1786 ACELA1818 ACELT1578 ACELT1785 ACELY1649 ACELY1650 ACELA1453 ACELA1459 ACELT1584 ACELY1659 ACELY1660 ACELA1464 ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Be an Energy Expert
node.content: People have to use Earth's natural resources very carefully. They should use only what they need. This book provides information about the natural resources we use to make energy. It suggests ways to use these resources carefully and better ways of making energy so Earth's resources will last longer. (Big idea: People need to use resources carefully.) Features of Be An Energy Expert • the topic (sustainability), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • headings that introduce new aspects of the topic and support students to read information in sections • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • captions • boxes that summarise each section • graph • turbine diagram • summary box of the positives and negatives about different energy resources • compound words - everything, forever, meantime, outdoors, sometimes, windmills • silent "k"- knowledge • digraphs - "sh", "th", "wh"
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: How much do you know about the energy we use every day? How do wind, oil, and the sun fit in to the picture? Take the test and see if you are an energy expert!
node.esiss_activities: In this book, there is a diagram that shows how water is used to make electricity. Draw another diagram to show how wind is used to make electricity. Write three steps. Add labels to the diagram.
node.esiss_hfw: because, find, home, know, last, morning, school, take, their, think, through
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Magic Noodle Show
node.content: Mrs Pole has invited the children's parents to come to school to talk about their jobs. Hong thinks this is a bad idea. His parents don't speak English very well, and they're too busy at the restaurant. This story shows how Hong changed his opinion when he learned about his grandfather's special skill. Features of the Book - Information about different cultures, - Character development within the story, - The use of adjectives to describe feelings - worried, surprised, nervous, puzzled, proud, - Exploring a problem and its resolution in a story, Purpose - The Magic Noodle Show can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying a problem and predicting outcomes, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - forming and supporting opinions, - exploring the conventions of direct speech, - writing for a specific purpose or audience, - exploring the ways in which different skills are valued in communities. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Hong's class is having a careers afternoon, and his grandfather is coming to talk about his job. Hong's family runs a Chinese restaurant, and he's nervous about what his classmates will think when his grandfather speaks.
node.esiss_activities: Hong's grandfather learnt special skills to make noodles. Ask an adult in your family about their job. What skills do they need to do their job well? Why is their job important for the community?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Fishy Mystery
node.content: A group of children discover some dead fish floating in the local river, and they're determined to find out what's killing the fish. They start by asking their teacher for help, then they conduct some research of their own. Features of the Book - Mystery, - Specialised vocabulary - water pollution, thermal pollution, chemicals, fertiliser, - Cause and effect, - Alternative verbs for "said" - shouted, asked, laughed, Purpose The Fishy Mystery can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making, confirming, and revising predictions, - exploring word families, - identifying Cause and effect in a story line, - using a text as a springboard for further research, - retelling events in sequence, - identifying how people's activities affect the environment. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Fish are dying in the river near Taylor's school, and Taylor and her friends want to find out why. With some help from their teacher, they try to solve the fishy mystery.
node.esiss_activities: The children in this story discover that fish are dying in the river. They set out to solve the mystery. On page 32, there are steps that show how to do research. Use these four steps to research something you would like to know more about. Share your report with your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Air I Breathe
node.content: People need to stop polluting the air. We need to breathe clean air to stay healthy. This book explores the different ways that people pollute the air. It looks at the importance of air and the ways people can help keep the air clear. (Big idea: We need to breathe clean air to stay healthy.) Features of The Air I Breathe • the topic (air), which expands on pages 12 and 13 of the anchor book Helping Planet Earth • preview question on the back cover • contents page, glossary (bold type), index • questions as headings • captions • fact box • table • close-up view to see details • initial consonant blends - "br-", "cl-", "sm-", "pl-", "str-", "tr-", "pr-", "gr-", "fl-" • final consonant blend - "-sh"
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: You can't see air, but it's everywhere and we need it to stay alive. This book explains how pollution has made air dangerous to breathe. It gives some good tips for making sure that the air of the future is clean and safe.
node.esiss_activities: This book describes how cars, ships, planes, and factories put fumes and chemicals into the air. Choose one of these things. Explain how it causes pollution. Write three things that people can do to make less pollution.

node.title: Fantastic Dragonflies
node.content: Creatures have many special features that help them to survive. This informational text focuses on the fantastic features and facts about dragonflies that have enabled them to survive since the dinosaurs. Features of Fantastic Dragonflies • the topic (dragonflies), which expands on pages 8 and 9 of the anchor book Being a Scientist • preview question on the back cover • headings, which support students to read in sections • labelled diagrams • life cycle • flow chart • fact boxes • close-up photographs • compound words - dragonflies, somewhere • plural endings - dragonflies • similes - like a basket, like a dragon's mouth, like a robot arm • adjectives - strong, scary

node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Dragonflies have lived on Earth for 300 million years. They were flying around when dinosaurs were alive. How have they survived so long? Read some fantastic dragonfly facts.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, there are fact boxes called "Fantastic Facts". Reread the book, and find five more fantastic facts. For example, "Dragonflies can fly 60 kilometres an hour." Put each fact into an interesting shape.
