node.title: Lost in Space
node.content: Brad's dad is an astronaut, and his spaceship needs to be repaired. Meanwhile, back on Earth, Brad is planning to make end-of-mission waffles to celebrate Dad's return. But Big Jaws, the waffle maker, is missing! Book Summary Brad's dad is an astronaut and his spaceship needs to be repaired. Meanwhile back on Earth Brad is planning to make end-of-mission waffles to celebrate Dad's return. But Big Jaws the waffle maker is missing! Features of the Book - Two linked story lines told in different formats, - Two different settings, - Specialised vocabulary - alias space junk landing legs mission mission control Big Jaws, - Speech bubbles in the cartoons, - Email, - Punctuation - ellipses capital letters Purpose - Lost in Space can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognizing and discussing the organisation of a book, - using charts and diagrams to record important details, - discussing and writing about the themes of a book, - extending vocabulary, - describing a sequence of events. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Captain Earl's spaceship has been hit by space junk. Back on Earth, his son, Brad, can't find the waffle maker. How are these events connected?
node.esiss_activities: Captain Earl had a successful space mission, but he had to solve some problems before he could return to Earth. Write a news report that retells Captain Earl's adventures in space. Write about Captain Earl's problems, and how he solved them. Give your report an interesting title.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Cephalopods
node.content: Cephalopods are animals that have a soft body and arms that grow out of their head. This book has detailed information about squid, octopi, and nautiluses. Photographs and diagrams provide added interest. Features of the Book - Captioned photographs and illustrations, - Comparisons to convey meaning - giant squid can grow to be the same length as a bus; has eyes the same size as the lights on a bus, - Measurements, - Similes - like a balloon full of air• like a wrinkled sack, - Specialised vocabulary - coral reef, cuttlefish, gastropods, nautilus, octopus, predators, prey, squid, suckers, Word Study Opportunities - Double consonants - bottle, cuttlefish, fills, shells, - Double vowel "oo" - balloon, zoom, - Initial blend "squ" - squeezed, squid, - Prefix "sub" - submarine Purpose - Cephalopods can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - asking and answering questions about a text, - using context clues and prior knowledge to understand unfamiliar words, - inferring meaning, - exploring multisyllabic words through segmenting, - interpreting information in diagrams charts and graphs, - using Descriptive language. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Cephalopods have soft bodies, and some have shells. They also have arms that grow out of their heads!
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Summarising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Chameleons of the Rain Forest
node.content: More than half the world's chameleons live on Madagascar. Chameleons of the Rain Forest contains many interesting facts about these creatures and how they live, as well as information about other animals that live on the island. Features of the Book - Captioned photographs, - Labelled diagram, - Life cycle, - Measurements, - Specialised vocabulary - absorb, endangered, extinct, mammal, predator, prey, reptile, scales Purpose - Chameleons of the Rain Forest can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - asking and answering questions about the text, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - using visual, structural, and meaning cues to read unknown words, - exploring rhyming words, - interpreting information in diagrams, charts, and graphs, - showing an awareness of the topic and audience. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: Why do some people call chameleons tiny dragons? What is the chameleon's secret weapon? Visit Madagascar with Rod Morris to find out about these amazing animals.
node.esiss_activities: This story has lots of facts about different kinds of chameleons. Choose two kinds of chameleon. Use a Venn diagram to show what is the same and what is different about the chameleons. Explain the diagram to someone in your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: When Foxes Came to Stay
node.content: When a family of foxes makes a den near their house, Jenna and Kyle decide to watch and protect them. Not everyone in the household wants to help the foxes though! Features of When Foxes came to Stay • Realistic fiction • Problem and solution • Direct speech • Factual information about foxes • Descriptive writing and a poem Purpose When Foxes Came to Stay can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the use of Descriptive language • identifying the problems and solutions in a narrative • finding factual information in fiction texts • Making inferences
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Determining cause and effect|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Determining cause and effect, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Jenna and Kyle are excited when a fox and her cubs come to live on their property. Jenna wants to watch the cubs grow, but Mum has other ideas. Can Jenna save the foxes before the animal control officer arrives?
node.esiss_activities: Jenna wrote some interesting words to describe the foxes. Choose an animal you like. It could be your family pet. What words or sentences can you use to describe it? Write down your ideas and share them with your family. Can they guess the animal you are describing?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: At the Pond
node.content: In this text you will learn about all kinds of animals and insects that live in on and around the pond. There are different areas of the pond that provide the animals that live there with food and shelter. Features of At the Pond • Report • Headings and subheadings • Close-up photographs with captions • Food chain diagram • Contents page, glossary, index Purpose At the Pond can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information from the text and graphic features • using a glossary and an index to build understanding • summarising information.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Determining main ideas|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Using texts as models, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: Many kinds of animals live in, on, and around the pond. These animals are all different, but they depend on each other for food.
node.esiss_activities: Reread page 2 of the book. Then draw a diagram of the four parts of the pond habitat. Draw the animals from the book in their habitat. Draw arrows between each animal and its prey. Which animals have more then one predator?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Two Tricky Tales
node.content: Sally Murphy retells two of Aesop's fables. In the first fable, Fox uses flattery to trick Crow. In the second, Mosquito learns that no matter how great you are, there will always be somebody better than you. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Humour, - Similes - They are black like midnight; They look like diamonds, - Specialised vocabulary - Aesop, storyteller, fable, - Third-person narrative, - Retelling of two traditional stories Word Study Opportunities - Contractions - didn't, he'd, I'm, she's, - Verb endings "ed" and "ing" - decided, hopped, ruffled; looking, spinning, waiting, - Multisyllabic words - beautiful, diamonds, Mosquito Purpose - Two Tricky Tales can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - understanding the author's purpose, - Making connections between own experiences and the text, - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - exploring onsets and rimes, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: In these stories, Crow and Mosquito both have lessons to learn about life. But will they be smart enough to learn from them?
node.esiss_activities: Fox told Crow that her feathers were "black like midnight" and her eyes were "shiny like diamonds". The author has used a simile to show that something is like something else. Finish these similies in your own words: • The wind was as cold as • The pillow was as soft as a • The parrot was cheeky like • The feathers were green like
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Once upon a Rhyme
node.content: Once upon a Rhyme tells us some of the fascinating stories behind familiar nursery rhymes. The book includes four rhymes from non-English traditions. Features of the Book - Familiar nursery rhymes, - Double-page illustrations, - Full text of nursery rhymes, - Explanations of the origins of nursery rhymes, - Rhymes from different cultures, - Interactive text that asks questions of the reader, - Poetic language including imagery and rhyme, - Index Purpose - Once upon a Rhyme can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making, confirming, and revising predictions, - discussing or writing about the ideas presented in the reading, - using illustrations to gain and deepen meaning, - exploring the use of poetic language, - exploring the genre of nursery rhymes. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Why did Mary's little lamb follow her to school? Who were Jack and Jill? In this book, you will find out some of the interesting stories behind nursery rhymes.
node.esiss_activities: Which nursery rhymes from this book do you know? What other nursery rhymes do you know? Say out loud your favourite rhyme and add some actions. What do you think the rhyme means? Share your rhyme with your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Voice for Children
node.content: People work together to solve problems and make a difference. This book tells the story of Craig Kielburger, a young boy who worked to help children in other countries who were forced to work long hours for little money. His efforts have made a big difference to many children's lives. (Big idea: People need to get along and help one another.) Features of A Voice for Children • the topic (helping people), which expands on pages 14 and 15 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • headings that support students to read in sections • timeline • maps • captions • contents page, glossary, index • pronunciation guide - Iqbal • the suffix "tion" - attention, petition • compound words - newspaper, something, shoebox • digraphs - "th", "ch", "sh" • vowel sounds - "ea", "ee", "oo" • the silent letters "g", "h", and "k" - sign, bright, Delhi, knew

node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: When Craig Kielburger looked at the newspaper one day, he saw a picture of a small boy who was speaking out for children who were forced to work in factories in India. That day he started a life-long crusade, fighting for the rights of children.
node.esiss_activities: Craig Kielburger did some brave things to help poor children. Read the book again. Find four things you think were brave or hard to do. How did each action help children?

node.title: Go, Annie, Go!
node.content: Annie has always been teased by her classmates for being different. When she unexpectedly joins in the 1500 metre race, the older girls mock her until the narrator decides to cheer her on. Features of the Book - First-person narrative, - Themes and settings that are familiar to children, - Issues of difference teasing and special needs, - Powerful story on an emotional level, - Direct speech, - Hyphenation - after-school one-mile, - Colloquial language - way to go• Go Annie go! Purpose - Go Annie Go! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - Making connections between children's experiences and the text, - distinguishing between the narrator and author of a story, - inferring Cause and effect relationships, - exploring point of view, - making comparisons within a book. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: One day, Annie does something that surprises the other children. She teaches them that a winner is not always the person who comes first.
node.esiss_activities: Annie is teased at school for being different, but her friend helps and encourages her. What does the friend do to show she cares? What other things can friends do to support each other? Use your ideas to make a poster that reminds people of how to be a good friend.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Quilt with a Difference
node.content: When some children plan to brighten up their school, one class decides to make a quilt from cardboard print to hang on the wall. This non-fiction text takes the reader through the whole process, from concept to finished product. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - instructional text materials list design brief glossary index, - Narrative procedure illustrated with photographs, - First-person narrative from a child's point of view, - Illustrates printmaking techniques, - Problem solving, - specialised vocabulary - design brief checklist stencils templates draft, - The link to math skills Purpose - A Quilt with a Difference can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - formulating questions, - reading and writing procedural texts, - exploring problem resolution, - using charts and diagrams to record important details. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.theme: Art and activity|Problem solving|School and community

node.esiss_blurb: How do you make a quilt with no fabric or thread? Find out how the children in this book make a beautiful patchwork quilt, with a difference.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are making a quilt using crayons instead of ink. Make your cardboard pattern following the steps in the book. Rub over your cardboard pattern with crayons. Make lots of copies of the crayon print. Join them to make a crayon quilt.

node.title: The New Girl
node.content: Amy is at home with a broken ankle. While she is away, her best friend Kim, makes friends with the new girl. Amy finds this unsettling, then she finds out that she and the new girl have something in common. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Written from a child's point of view, - Specialised vocabulary - cast crutches hobbled , - Third-person narrative, Word Study Opportunities - Double consonants - stopped worried hobbled, - Contractions - wasn't, didn't, she's, how's, couldn't, can't, - Silent "w" - write, wrong, wrote - Compound words - classroom, everyone, anymore, outside, lunchtime Purpose - The New Girl can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings relationships and actions, - making confirming and revising predictions, - using context clues and Prior knowledge to understand unfamiliar words, - exploring problem resolution, - recognizing the silent letter "w", - writing compositions that have interesting subjects. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making connections, Making predictions,
node.esiss_blurb: Amy has broken her ankle, and she can't go to school. She's missing her best friend, Kim. Then a new girl starts school, and the teacher asks Kim to look after her. How will Amy feel?
node.esiss_activities: The word "write" starts with a silent "w". What other words in the book start with a silent "w"? Write a list. The word "knock" starts with a silent "k". Make a list of other words that start with a silent "k".
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Connections, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Pot of Gold/The Clever Farmer
node.content: These folk tales tell the stories of two farmers. The first farmer is an honest man who finds a pot of gold and has to decide whether to keep it. The second farmer is a clever man who needs to escape from a hungry tiger. Features of the Book - Direct speech, - Short stories, - Specialised vocabulary - Vietnam, clay, lesson, plough, buffalo, cleverness, - Third-person narrative Word Study Opportunities - Irregular plural - thieves, - Verb endings "ed" and "ing" - looked, lifted, buried; ploughing, talking, watching, - Compound words - something, someone, without, inside, outside, anything Purpose - A Pot of Gold/The Clever Farmer can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - identifying the features of different genres, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - exploring problem resolution, - exploring homophones, - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - writing alternative endings for a story. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Communication|Problem solving
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Comparing and contrasting, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: These folk tales from Vietnam tell the stories of two farmers. The first farmer is an honest man who finds a pot of gold. The second farmer is a clever man who needs to escape from a tiger.
node.esiss_activities: In the second story, the farmer fooled the tiger. He said he had left his cleverness at home and that he could go and get it. Imagine you are the farmer. How would you fool the tiger? Write a different ending to the tale.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Rhythm and Shoes
node.content: Jasmine is a tap dancer. This story follows Jasmine and her dance class as they prepare to perform in a show. It includes tap facts and history. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - glossary, index, fact boxes, labelled diagrams, photographs, - Sequential text, - Present tense, - Speech bubbles, - Specialised vocabulary - performance, shoemakers, rhythm, stagedoor, onstage, - Variations on roots words such as dance practice Purpose - Rhythm and Shoes can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - forming and supporting opinions about a text, - recognising the features of non-fiction texts, - exploring root words, - describing a sequence of events, - exploring the use of direct speech, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Join Jasmine and her friends as they put on their tap shoes and get ready for a show.
node.esiss_activities: The author tells us that Tap Dogs is an amazing tap group. Ask a member of your family to help you look on the Internet for video clips of Tap Dogs performing. Watch the clips of them tap dancing. Try out some moves yourself!
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Wacky Wheels
node.content: This fast-paced book takes a light-hearted look at some unusual ways in which wheels are used. It also explains the development of many recreational and sporting uses for wheels. Features of the Book - Alliteration - wheels that are weird, wonderful, or just downright wacky, - Specialised vocabulary - pedal, hobbyhorse, handle, steering, boneshaker, penny-farthing bicycle, unicycle, - Historical and present-day photographs as well as illustrations to support the text, - Contractions - here's, they've, it's, you're, there's, aren't, - Compound words - hobbyhorse, boneshaker, skateboard, footpath, grandparents, handlebars, - Hyphenation - penny-farthing, ice-skating, in-line, - "Wh" blend - wheels, when, what, why, - Non-fiction text features - introduction, glossary, index Purpose - Wacky Wheels can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising the features of non-fiction texts, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - exploring root words, - using text as a model for writing, - exploring compound words. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making inferences|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making inferences, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: If you want to check out some wheels that are weird, wonderful, or downright wacky, then read this book.
node.esiss_activities: Some words in this book are in bold. These words are in the glossary on page 31. Look in the glossary and find out what these words mean: • invention • steering Write a glossary for five more words in the book, such as "penny farthing" and " wacky".
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Horse Power
node.content: In Horse Power, readers learn about the different parts of a horse, how horses communicate, and the kinds of jobs that horses do. The last chapter is about taking a horse for a ride Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - fact boxes, sub-headings, labelled diagrams, glossary, index, - Compound words - fingernails, horseshoes, workhorses, cowboys, cowgirls, wheelchairs, racehorses, - Contractions - they'll, I'll, it's, don't, doesn't, - Opportunities to make comparisons such as past and present, wild and tame Purpose - Horse Power can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making connections between children's experiences and the text, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - exploring compound words, - forming and supporting opinions, - finding information in a non-fiction text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Distinguishing fact from opinion, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Horses have been helping people for thousands of years. This book tells you how. It also shows how we use horses for recreation and for fun.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Bagels for Kids
node.content: Charlie is a bagel maker. Lydia is a regular customer at Charlie's store. Lydia and her classmates help Charlie to decide which new bagel flavour he will make especially for kids. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - glossary, index, flow charts, sub-headings, - Procedural text and a recipe, - Survey and voting procedure, - Question-and-answer format, Problem posing and solving, - Similes - like play dough• like a bagel, apartment building, Purpose - Bagels for Kids can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising and discussing the organisation of a book, - describing a problem and its solution, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - using text as a model for writing, - describing a sequence of events. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making judgments|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making judgments, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Charlie is a bagel maker. He wants to make a new bagel flavour for kids. Lydia and her classmates help him choose the new flavour. What will it be?
node.esiss_activities: Lydia helps Charlie decide on a new bagel flavour. Think of four more flavours that your friends might like. Think of some names for your flavours and write a list. Ask your family which flavour they like. Which flavour is the most popular?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The World of Dummies
node.content: From crash test dummies and store mannequins to Egyptian kings, a Chinese emperor, and Madame Tussaud's waxworks, this book provides an informative look at the different ways dummies have helped people since ancient times. Features of the Book - Humour, - Interactive text that asks questions of the reader, - Specialised vocabulary - dummy, Egyptian, mannequins, museum, pyramid, tomb, wax, Word Study Opportunities - Compound words - crossbows, dressmakers, wetsuit, - Initial blends "cr", "pr", and "tr" - crash, cross; practise, pretend; train, treasure, - Multisyllabic words - complaining, information, interesting, mannequins, practising, pyramid Purpose The World of Dummies can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - recognising the features of non-fiction text, - identifying the Determining main idea and supporting detail, - using the photographs and illustrations to gain a deeper understanding of the text, - using knowledge of the words in compound words to predict meaning, - writing to communicate information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Take a peek into the world of dummies. They are used for all kinds of jobs. Dentists drill their teeth, and car makers use them in crashes when they are learning how to make cars safe. They can also be used to make us laugh.
node.esiss_activities: Dummies are used for dangerous jobs, such as testing the safety of a car. Choose a job that people use dummies for. Write a job description, explaining why the job is important.
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Best Birthday
node.content: Jamie has a special birthday present to send his cousin Rachel in England. The only problem is that she'll have to wait six months to find out what it is! Features of the Book - Email formats, - Exchange of written communications that progress the story, - Illustrations that provide extra information and support, - Contractions - I've, That's, can't, I'll, it's, don't, won't, There's, Here's Purpose - The Best Birthday can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising and understanding colloquial language, - making, confirming, and revising predictions, - using charts and diagrams to record important details, - reading aloud with expression, - using text as a model for writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Jamie has a cool birthday present to send to her friend Rachel in England. The only problem is that she'll have to wait six months to find out what it is.
node.esiss_activities: In this story, two children write emails to each other. What friend or family member would you like to write to? Write an email to tell them about something that happened today. Send your email.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Wooden Drum
node.content: "The Wooden Drum looks at an aspect of village life that has changed. The wooden drum has always been a means of sending messages to everyone in the village. It has been a symbol of unity. However, it has finally become too rotten to use and has been replaced by a metal bell in a new church. The new bell does the same thing as the old drum, but the narrator regrets the loss of the wooden drum. The story is about the passing of time and how things cannot last• about change and attitudes to change. Features of the Text - The word ""would"" is used to express habitual actions: ""we would hear a special sound"" and ""the drum would tell us the time"". - The drum is made to seem like a living thing, a friend, telling the villagers what was happening and what to do. This makes readers feel sad when the drum is left out in the open to rot and finally becomes useless. - Page 6 describes the cyclone, using descriptive verbs: the wind howled, the rain lashed, lightning flashed. The verb ""howled"" describes a particular type of noise. ""Lashed"" is a word that usually describes hitting something with a whip. Here it is a metaphor: the rain is compared with a whip, that fell so hard that it whipped the trees and anything else it landed on. These language features may be challenging for your students. Allow for plenty of discussion time. - The change of present tense of page 11 signals a shift to the present day. - The expression ""used to"" on page 11, is another way of expressing past habitual action, but on page 12, it means ""to become familiary with or accepting of"" something. - There are other sound words in the story besides ""howled"", for example, buzz, chirp, thud, and ding. Purpose of the Book The story highlights the fact that things do not stay the same. They grow old and cannot do their job as well as they did when they were new. Finally, they become useless. This is something that the writer regrets, but perhaps not everyone feels this way. Some people like change and get used to new things quickly. The story also focuses on how the drum, and later the bell, bring people together. Today, there are many new forms of mass communication (like radio, TV, and newspapers). The drum was a traditional medium, and it's still used in many places in the Pacific to bring people together for different occasions."
node.theme: Change over time|Communication|School and community|Science and technology
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Asking and answering questions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: The wooden drum has always been used to call the people of the village to meetings. What happens when it becomes too rotten to use?
node.esiss_activities: The wooden drum in this book is used to send messages to people in the village. How do people in your family send messages? Write a list of your ideas.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Paper Pots
node.content: "Paper Pots is a narrative text that describes a process. Mobrig and his mother make pots out of paper, but it's not until the end of the story that the students learn why these pots are special. Each page takes the students one step further in the process, and they will enjoy the satisfaction of creating something new with Mobrig if they try the activity themselves. Features of the Book - The way the process follows a time sequence (""One Saturday"", ""One hour ... two hours ... three hours."", ""A week later""). The use of ellipsis to show time passing. - The words used to to describe texture (the adjectives ""soft"", ""soggy"", and ""dry"" and the noun ""mush"".) - The many important details that are given (""small pieces"" - the size of Mobrig's thumb, ""hot water"", ""plastic yoghurt cups"", ""three spoons"", ""Mobrig carefully pulled out"") - The comparisons (""the same size as your thumb"", ""like thick porridge!"" Purpose of the Book The students are introduced to a recycling process through a story that is made interesting by direct speech, a little humour, the suspense of waiting until the end before learning how the pots are going to be used. Their curiosity is aroused right from the start. ""Why are the plant pots described as special""?) They will also be stimulated to think about the changes taking place during the recycling process and the reasons for these changes. They will see the connections between the causes (water, mashing, and warmth) and effects (changes in the paper at various stages)."
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Morbrig and his mother make plant pots out of paper. At the end of the story, you'll discover why the pots are so special.
node.esiss_activities: The people in this book recycle old paper to make special pots. Ask someone to help you to make your own paper pots. Use them to plant some seeds.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Hunting the Moon
node.content: "Naga, a brave hunter, wants to catch the full Moon but can't because trees are in the way. Then he sees a second Moon in the lake and tries to catch it, first with a spear, then with a line, and finally with a net. But this Moon is just a reflection, and his efforts are in vain. Narrated in the present tense, the text evokes the forest night and Naga's thoughts and feelings as he tries to catch the Moon. Readers will enjoy the mix of suspense and humour and will be drawn into the atmosphere of the forest at night. Features of the Text - The use of the present tense, making the story more immediate. - The use of many descriptive words: verbs (rustling, stretches, rubs, glowing, floating); adjectives (tall, dark, wriggling); and adverbs (slowly, quickly). - The use of words that sound like the noises they describe (""rustling"" leaves, ""hooting"" owl). - The contrast between Naga's knowledge of the forest and his ignorance about what the Moon is and the conception of reflection. - The amusing anticlimax of the last page. - The concept of disappointment. Purpose of the Book This book is intended to arouse the students' imaginations and stimulate them to think about natural phenomena. The humour of Naga's attempts to catch the Moon's reflection and his failure to do so will get the students thinking and talking about the reason for his failure. The concept of reflection is the key. "
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using texts as models, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Naga the brave hunter wants to catch the moon, but he's not having much luck. Is that the real moon he's hunting?
node.esiss_activities: Naga tries different ways to catch the moon. Invent a machine that you could use to catch the moon. Draw your machine. Tell someone how it works.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Long Journey
node.content: A child tells the story of how her ancestors came to settle on the island where she lives. It's a story that has been handed down through the generations. The story has made an impression on her, and she imagines that she can hear the voices of her ancestors as they enter the passage through the reef after their long journey. The story is full of details that show that this voyage was one for which a great deal of planning, preparation, and skill were needed. It was a voyage full of danger and uncertainty, yet the travellers were optimistic of finding a land that offered all they needed - a land that would become their new home. Features of the Book - The story is told in the past tense, except for the last page, which brings us to the present day. - The story is very detailed. It includes adjectives that describe size - huge, tall, great, big, bigger, high, large, deep; distance - far, long; quality - strong, fresh, new, special, right, wrong. - Time expressions help to convey the idea that this was a long-term undertaking - long ago, a long time, weeks, finally. Specialised vocabulary to do with ancestral voyages - ancestors, voyage, pandanus, hulls, fibre, woven, off course, currents. The use of the passive voice - ... parts of the canoe were tied together.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Exploring writer's craft|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Exploring writer's craft, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: This story has been handed down through the generations. It tells of how a girl's family came to travel to a land that would become their new home.
node.esiss_activities: Use the information from the story to help you write some instructions that explain how to make a canoe. What do you need? What are the steps?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Magpie's Tail
node.content: A magpic loses his tail while arguing over milk and must satisfy the demands of various characters before he can get it back. Features of the Book • The cumulative nature of the story line. • The major and minor characters in the text. • The frequent use of direct speech. • The text in the illustrations. • The storybook language - "Please, Hen, give me an egg." • The contractions - "I'll" and "she'll." • The use of possessive apostrophes - "magpie's" and "water carrier's." Purpose The Magpie's Tail can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • identifying the plot and characters within the story • identifying the themes of the book comparing the opinions of the author with those of the reader • writing a conclusion.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: The cheeky magpie wouldn't stop drinking milk from the old woman's pail, so she pulls off its tail. How will the magpie get its tail back?
node.esiss_activities: The magpie had to collect four different things to get her tail back. Draw a map and show where the magpie had to go. Write a label to show what she had to collect there.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Whizz! Click!
node.content: The toys in Rose's bedroom are truly anazing! But when they stop turning, winding, and winching, they must visit Uncle Max, who's a whizz at keeping things clicking.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect|Determining importance|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Determining importance, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Uncle Max loves to invent things. When Rose's unusual toys all break, he comes up with his best invention yet. But how will it fit in Rose's room?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are Uncle Max. What would you make from Rose's broken toys? Draw your invention. Write about how it works. Make sure you use cogs and wheels.
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Mapping It
node.content: My world has many places to learn about. Maps can help people to find their way. This book explores a variety of maps - including mall maps, online maps, and GPS - and how they help people find their way. (Big idea: People need help to find their way around). Features of Mapping It • the topic (maps), which expands on pages 10 and 11 of the anchor book Discovering My World • preview question on the back cover • maps • captions • close-up visuals • contents page, glossary (boldface type), and index • digraph sounds - "th", "sh", "wh" • medial vowels - route • plural endings - roads, buildings, rivers, parks, places, things, maps, cities, oceans, rocks, islands, sailors, buses, trains, malls, stores, directions, cars, landmarks • contractions - didn't, I'm, let's, don't
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: The first sailors didn't have maps. They used stars to find their way. Now there seems to be a map for everything, even the shopping mall. This book tells you all you need to know about finding your way with a map.
node.esiss_activities: Some words in this book are in the glossary. Find five other words and make your own glossary. Show the glossary to someone in your family. Does your glossary help them understand the meaning of the words?
node.esiss_hfw: about, around, each, find, first, give, know, left, look, many, other, people, such, take, their, use, want, where, would
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Helping Planet Earth (Fluent)
node.content: Who can help? Helping Planet Earth looks at how we can help planet Earth by using resources carefully, caring for animals in danger, keeping it clean, and reusing things. Features of Helping Planet Earth • introduction of environmental concepts and anchor words associated with the topic - general information about environmental issues (pages 2 to 7) followed by four double-page spreads about specific issues and suggested solutions • personal statement that ends the book • preview question on the back cover • photographs with text that supports and illustrates concepts • captions • initial consonant blends - "pl-", "cl-", "tr-", "gl-", "sm-", "br-"
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: One day, the resources on planet Earth may run out. Find out how you can help to take care of Earth's air, water, forests, and animals.
node.esiss_activities: On a piece of paper, write the headings - Good, Not So Good, Questions. Write three things that are good about the environment of our planet. Write three things that are not so good about our environment. Write three questions you have about our environment. Show the questions to someone in your family. Ask him or her to help you answer them.
node.esiss_hfw: about, could, had, into, last, left, live, many, other, some, soon, that, think, work
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Mouse in the House
node.content: A visiting mouse saves a young girl from having to wear the new sneakers she dislikes. In return the girl helps the mouse to escape the mousetraps her mother has hidden around the house. Features of A Mouse in the House • Realistic fiction • Direct speech • Problem-solving focus • Information about animal behavior Purpose A Mouse in the House can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making predictions • identifying the problems and solutions in a narrative
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Life science|Neighbourhood|Problem solving
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Following instructions|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Using texts as models, Sequencing, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: When a mouse eats the insides of Jamie's sneakers, she doesn't care at all. She didn't like them anyway. But Mum is horrified that there's a mouse in the house. Can Jamie get to the mouse before Dad sets the traps?
node.esiss_activities: Mice can cause problems when they make nests in people's homes. Make a small poster to show the ways we can stop mice from coming into our houses as well as the ways we can get rid of them. Use ideas from the story to help you. Which solutions are the best?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Backyard Jungle
node.content: Many animals and insects live in and around our homes. Some of these creatures are more helpful to us than others. Features of Backyard Jungle • Headings and subheadings • Labelled illustrations and diagrams • Fact files and charts • Photographs (including closeups) • Activity with instructions Purpose Backyard Jungle can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring and using the features of non-fiction texts • using prior knowledge • summarising and synthesising information
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Life science|Neighbourhood|Problem solving
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Distinguishing fact from opinion, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: Believe it or not, your backyard is like a jungle. There are animals and insects that are helpful and harmful. Find out which is which.
node.esiss_activities: Divide a piece of paper in half. On the top half, draw a garden and the insects you might see there. On the bottom half, show the animals that live under the ground. Label each animal and describe it as a "pest" or "not pest".
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Fact from Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Blast to the Past
node.content: Omega and Zip's class is investigating accidental discoveries. The two students use the class time travel pod to go back in time to witness the discovery of vulcanised rubber. While they're there, they accidentally change the future. Features of the Book - Science fiction, - Specialised vocabulary - time travel pod, milliseconds, vulcanisation, chemicals, sulphur, - Invented names and objects - Ms Umax, Zip, Omega, buzz-bike, quotch, robo-cook, proton brain, Purpose - Blast to the Past can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - discussing the plot and setting of a story, - considering the consequences of characters' actions, - exploring the language specific to science fiction texts, - innovating on a text, - locating and using sources of information for research, - gaining an understanding of what scientists do. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Zip and Omega need to choose a discovery to investigate for their science project. Then they're going to travel back in time to watch what really happened as the discovery was made. But will they remember the most important rule of time travel?
node.esiss_activities: Omega and Zip travel back in time to research an invention. Choose an invention you are interested in. Use books or the Internet to research how this invention was discovered. Draw a diagram of the invention. Label it. Share your research with your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELT1833 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Brilliant Bugologist
node.content: Archibald is a bugologist. He travels the world to find rare and interesting insects. With his two helpers, Daisy and Bobby, Archibald goes in search of the rare blue butterfly of Mooloomunga. Features of the Book - Life cycle of a butterfly - eggs, caterpillars, cocoons, butterfly, - Similes - as slowly as a snail; like giant wings; as annoying as ants at a picnic, - Alliteration - brilliant bugologist; blue butterfly; great grasshoppers; jumping jitterbugs; bouncing bug of Bangaloola, - Invented names - Bugologist, Mooloomunga, Teepeepunga, Bangaloola, - Recurring description - shimmering and glimmering Purpose - The Brilliant Bugologist can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - determining the similarities and differences in characters, - studying the use of adjectives, - identifying the use of figurative language such as similes, - exploring multisyllabic words through segmenting, - using graphic aids such as diagrams to convey information, - developing an understanding of life cycles. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Forming opinions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Forming opinions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Archibald B. Moon is looking for the rare blue butterfly of Mooloomunga. He's certain he'll find it, but his helpers, Daisy and Rob, aren't so sure.
node.esiss_activities: In this story, the author has used alliteration to describe things. Alliteration is the repetition of sounds at the beginning of words, for example, "great grasshoppers" and "brilliant bugologist". Practise using alliteration by adding words that start with the same letter: • firefly • jungle • campsite
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Cool Treasure
node.content: Mike finds a diary in the basement of his new house. It was written in 1944 and contains clues about some treasure that was hidden by the writer of the diary. Mike reads the diary and follows the clues. Features of the Book - Mystery, - Excerpts from a diary in letter form, - Descriptions of life in the 1940s, - Play on words - cool treasure, - Specialised vocabulary - blackout, coal chute, food stamps, electric icebox, Purpose The Cool Treasure can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making, confirming, and revising predictions, - determining an author's purpose, - interpreting specialised vocabulary in context, - using a text as a springboard for further research, - using a diary to record information feelings and ideas, - identifying and using Various sources such as diaries to reconstruct the past, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Mike finds a diary in the basement of his new house, and he reads about a boy's life in the 1940s. What is the boy's treasure, and where has he hidden it? There are many mysteries to solve before the diary makes sense.
node.esiss_activities: Mike finds a diary that leads him to a treasure. He also finds out what his neighbourhood was like when his father was a boy. Things had changed a lot. Interview an adult about the neighbourhood where he or she grew up. Ask how things have changed there.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Day and Night
node.content: These two tales retell myths about the origin of day and night. In the first tale, Maui slows down the sun to make the days longer. In the second tale, Big Snake's daughter releases night so that her husband can sleep. Features of the Book - Myths from Hawaii and the Amazon, - Personification of the sun and night, - Descriptive language - the evening star; a blanket of cloud; the dawn star; pale morning light, - Mythical concepts - controlling the sun; capturing night; dividing night from day, Purpose - Day and Night can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - recognising the features of myths, - identifying a problem and predicting outcomes, - considering the consequences of characters' actions, - exploring antonyms, - identifying Cause and effect in a story line, - developing an argument based on information in the text, - understanding that objects in the sky have patterns of movement. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: A long time ago, the people in Hawaii were unhappy. The days were too short, and no one could get any work done. The people in the Amazon were also unhappy. They had no night, and their days never ended. Read the two myths in this book to find out how these peoples resolved their problems.
node.esiss_activities: A word that has an opposite meaning to another word is called an antonym. "Day" and "Night" are antonyms. Write the antonyms for these words: • fast • short • light • expensive
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Visualising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Going to Canada: Li-Ming's Diary
node.content: Li-ming's family is moving from China to Toronto. Going to Canada is Li-ming's diary account of how she prepares for the move and how she feels about it. The text compares life in China with life in Toronto. Features of the Book - Personal account, - Chinese names - Li-ming, Tag-hee, - Diary format, - Questions posed by the writer, - The use of similes - like the world is wrapped in a big white quilt; they all honk like geese, Purpose - Going to Canada can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - Making inferences, - Making inferences, - identifying the use of figurative language such as similes, - writing using figurative language such as similes, - exploring the reasons why people move from one country to another. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: "My father called last night. He sounded so far away. I felt shy and didn't know what to say." Li-ming's father and brother have moved from China to Toronto, and Li-ming and her mother will soon join them there. She's nervous about living in a new country, and it helps to write her feelings in her diary.
node.esiss_activities: What do you know about China? Make a chart with two columns. Write the headings, "China" and "My Home". In the "China" column, write a list of the things you know about China. Use information in the book to help you. Under the "My Home" heading, write a list of the things in your home that are different from China. Are any things the same in both places?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Irniq and the Eagles
node.content: This traditional tale from the Pacific North-west is about three brothers. When the two older brothers do not return from hunting, the eagle man appears to help the youngest brother, Irniq, find them. But first Irniq must help the eagle man and his own people. Features of the Book - Retelling of a traditional tale, - The message of sharing, helping each other, and enjoying life, - Descriptive language - the snow began to whirl; letting the sound wash over him; the rushing wind; young and full of life, - Phrases that indicate time - A long time ago; One day; After that; And that night, Purpose - Irniq and the Eagles can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - recognising the features of traditional tales, - considering the consequences of characters' actions, - determining the author's underlying message, - writing to express and support an opinion, - exploring the use of apostrophes, - exploring the ways in which individuals in a community can support each other. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Irniq lives with his two brothers near the sea. Although people live nearby, Irniq never sees them. One day, after his brothers go missing, Irniq meets some mysterious eagles. Life for Irniq and his neighbours changes forever.
node.esiss_activities: Many traditional stories are hundreds of years old. People used to retell stories before writing was invented. The stories were handed down from generation to generation. Retell this traditional story to someone in your family. Talk about the message of the story and how sharing and helping one another makes the world a better place.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Summarising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Bread for Life
node.content: People all over the world eat bread. There are many kinds of bread and many ways to eat it. This book explains the history of bread and how some breads are made. Features of the Book - Historical and cultural information about bread, - Labelled diagrams, - Photographs with captions, - Specialised vocabulary - dough, grain, husk, knead, nutrient, staple, yeast, - Recipe for tortillas, Purpose - Bread for Life can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Making connections between the text and students' own experiences, - exploring verb tenses, - reading and following procedural text, - Summarising important ideas, - writing procedural text, - exploring and describing similarities and differences in staple foods across cultures. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Did you know that people all over the world eat bread? There are many different ways to make it. There are even more ways to eat it. Find out how ...
node.esiss_activities: Ask an adult to help you make some bread. Decide if you would like to make the bread on page 18 or tortillas on page 23. Check that you have all the ingredients you need. Follow the instructions in the book to make your bread. Eat it with your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Summarising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Maui and the Sun
node.content: This is a traditional legend from the Maori people of New Zealand. It explains how Maui and his brothers slow the sun's journey across the sky to make the days longer for eating, fishing, and hunting. Features of the Book • The use of paragraphs to break the text. • The use of direct speech. • The personification of the sun. • The possessive apostrophes - "Maui's" and "sun's." • The distinctive narrative pattern of the story line that allows exploration of characters, plot, and setting. • The frequent use of the contractive apostrophe - "can't," "don't," and "we'll." • The word endings - "ed," "ness," and "ly." • The additional information and map on the inside back cover. • The stylised illustrations. Purpose Maui and the Sun can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: -identifying the plot and characters within the story• -recognising the features of different genres• -recognising and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings• -writing using a variety of sentence patterns.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: The days are too short and people don't have time to do their work. They ask Maui for help. How will he slow down the sun?
node.esiss_activities: Draw pictures of Maui and the Sun. Glue them onto small sticks to make puppets. Use the puppets to retell the story, or create your own adventure for Maui and the Sun.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Fire! Fire!
node.content: Fire is a source of heat and light that has been used by people in different ways for thousands of years. This book explores what fire is, how it can be used, the dangers of fire, and how people can keep safe from fire. Features of the Book - Legends about fire, - Historical information, - Statistical information, - Specialised vocabulary - bellow, ember, generate, source, fuel, oxygen, smelt, Purpose Fire! Fire! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Asking questions of the text, - retrieving and classifying the information in a text, - using electronic media for further research, - exploring homophones, - writing a report that has an organisational structure, - understanding that fire is a source of heat and light. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making Predictions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Long ago, people didn't know how to use fire. Today, we use fire for all kinds of things, but it can also cause damage. It's important that we know how to control fire so that we keep safe.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the escape plan on page 27. Draw a map of your house. Plan a safe escape route. Decide where your meeting place will be and label it on your map. Share the map with everyone in your family so that they know the safety plan.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The First Builders
node.content: Animals were the first builders. They built nests, tunnels, dams, and towers long before the first people were around. This book explores how and why animals build different structures and what people have learned about building from animals. Features of the Book • Compares the structures built by animals with those built by people • Explanations • Compound words - hilltops, underground, earthworms • Specialised vocabulary - shelters, lodges, foundations Purpose The First Builders can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: asking questions of the text; • synthesising information from two or more sources; • using knowledge of the words in compound words to predict meaning; • using the text as a springboard for further research; • writing a narrative procedure; • identifying the similarities between the structures built by animals and those built by people.
node.theme: Change over time|Habitats|Life science|Science and technology
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Animals have been building tunnels, dams, and towers for millions of years. They were the first builders. People build these things as well. We've learnt a lot from animal builders.
node.esiss_activities: Reread pages 19 and 20. Imagine you are a beaver. Write instructions that explain how to build a beaver dam. Number the steps and read them to someone in your family. Would they understand how to build a beaver dam?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1665 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: A Place to Live
node.content: People live in all kinds of homes. This book explores the different materials and structures used to build homes and shows how houses reflect people's needs and the environment. It also explores how houses have changed over time. Features of the Book - Fact box - Sequence - Labelled diagram - Cause and effect Purpose Parasites: Nature's Stowaways can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Asking questions, - analysing - Text structure, - forming opinions based on evidence from text, - comparing the characteristics of organisms. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Everyone needs a home. It's a place where we feel safe. All around the world, people build all kinds of shelters - from huts made of grass to tall buildings.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the glossary on page 31. Find out the meaning of these words: • air conditioning • aluminium • insulation • shutter Now look in the book for four words you think could be in the glossary. Write the meaning of these words. Ask your family if they can guess what the words are.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Pet Vet
node.content: A busy vet talks about his work. Readers follow a day in his life as he treats pets that children have brought in. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - glossary, index, definitions, case studies, photographs, labels, - First-person narrative in the present tense, - Case studies of animals, - Descriptions of people's occupations, - Specialised vocabulary - veterinarian, clinic, receptionist, schedule, temperature, - Animal words - parakeet, turtle, rabbit, cat, dog, guinea pig Purpose - Pet Vet can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - Making connections between children's experiences and the text, - formulating questions, - developing strategies for reading unknown words, - using charts and diagrams to record important details. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Monitoring for meaning, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Buster the budgie has hurt his wing. Hoppy the rabbit is limping. Zac the cat needs his teeth cleaned. How does a vet help all these animals?
node.esiss_activities: Write a schedule for the vet's day. Include the times and the different jobs. Don't forget his lunch break! Make sure each job is in the right place on the vet's schedule.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Monitoring and Clarifying, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Flicking the Switch
node.content: This text invites the reader to imagine what life was like 150 years ago when there was no electricity. It does this by comparing the life of a boy born in 1851 with the lives of children today. It also provides information about how some modern electrical appliances work. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - diagrams, photographs, glossary, index, - Written instructions, - Different illustrative styles for past and present, - Familiar activities and events used to compare past and present, - Use of a child to evoke past times, - Simple explanations of familiar technology, - Specialised vocabulary - electricity, refrigerators, stove, ignition, spiral signals, - Punctuation - question marks, exclamation marks for emphasis Purpose Flicking the Switch can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising and discussing the organisation of a book, - finding the Determining main idea, - locating important information, - reading and writing procedural text, - making comparisons within a book. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When Jack Peterson was a boy there was no electricity. Learn about Jack's life and how things were done differently a hundred and fifty years ago.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1824 ACELA1825 ACELT1589 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Iaru's Lullaby
node.content: "Iaru's Lullaby tells the story of a volcanic eruption and how the sights, sounds, smell, and other sensations affect a young boy as he lies in bed waiting for his bedtime lullaby. On this dramatic night, the distant rumbling of the erupting volcano eventually becomes his lullaby. Features of the Book - The way the story appeals to the senses. First come the terrifying noises, then colourful sights. There is an awful smell and irritating ash. - Descriptive words are used (""angry"" and ""awful""). - Metaphor is used (burrowed) and so is simile (""like big, grey ghosts"" and ""like village drums"".) Purpose of the Book The story depicts the power of natural forces. It will stimulate the senses and arouse the reader's feelings: fear and awe, concern for Iaru and his parents, and relief when all ends well and Iaru is lulled to sleep. "
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: What's that rumbling sound? How can a baby be expected to sleep when a volcano is erupting?
node.esiss_activities: The sounds of the volcano made a lullaby for Iaru. What noise would a storm make? Write the different sounds. Use these sounds to make up a new lullaby.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: How Do You Measure a Dinosaur?
node.content: A group of children use math to help them draw a life-sized tyrannosaurus. Features of the Book • Compound adjectives - "full-sized" and "hardtop." • Past-tense verbs - "wrote," "drew," and "copied." • The list of items needed for the task on page 3. • The definite introduction, middle, and conclusion. • The text presented as numbered steps. • The use of mathematical language - "grid," "squares," "inch," "yard," "bigger," "smaller," and "taller." • The use of multisyllabic words - "dinosaur," "tyrannosaurus," and "disappeared." • The use of parentheses. Purpose How Do You Measure a Dinosaur? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: -discussing the organizational structure of the text• -critically analysing the information contained within a non-fiction story for accuracy• -developing the skill of writing in a sequential manner• -writing about the steps in a procedure.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: A dinosaur is big. Is it too big to measure? Not if you follow the five steps in this book. The children at Island Bay School find out just how huge a tyrannosaurus was!
node.esiss_activities: In this book, the children use a grid to make a big picture. Use the steps in the book to make a big picture of something you like. If you have chalk, draw the picture outside on the footpath.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Most Terrible Creature in the World
node.content: This East African folktale is retold as a play, set outside the house of the hare, where a terrible creature has taken residence. Ideas are shown for the simple prop and masks. Features of the Book • The character list and introduction. • Repetition as a support for the reader. • The frequent use of question marks, exclamation points, and commas. • The contractions - "I'm," "I'll," "that's," "don't," and "what's." • The use of capitals for characters' names. • The use of parentheses and/or italics for stage directions. • The photographs of the children preparing. • The interviews with the actors. Purpose The Most Terrible Creature in the World can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognising the features of different genres • understanding the punctuation cues and their meanings • discussing the organisational structure of the text • writing a recount of the story using own words.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using graphic sources, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: What is the most terrible creature in the world? The children in this book will tell you. This is a play you can read with your friends.
node.esiss_activities: Ask your family to help you act out this play. Make masks to show who is playing each animal. Write a sentence to say why you liked being your animal.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Butterfly Day
node.content: Butterfly Day describes a special day at Maungawhau School and includes facts about butterflies Features of the Book • Photographs to support the text. • The contractions - "can't," "wouldn't," and "wasn't." • The use of compound words - "butterfly," "sunshine," "classroom," and "themselves." • The blends - "cl," "fl," and "cr." • The word endings - "er," "ed," "ly," and "ing." • Factual information about butterflies. Purpose Butterfly Day can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing features of different genres • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • using diagrams, charts, and other illustrations to support writing.
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Making inferences|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Using texts as models, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Our caterpillars in the classroom have been in their cocoons for so long. Now it's time for them to hatch. When the butterflies come out, they take part in all of our lessons!
node.esiss_activities: This book has interesting facts about butterflies. Write a list of the facts you have learnt. If you know other facts about butterflies, write these down, too.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: History Walk
node.content: History Walk follows a group of children going on a history walk around their city. Features of the Book • The use of historical and present day photographs to support the text. • Written information presented in a variety of ways. • The factual information about the city and commentary of the walk. • The "ed" word ending to create past tense. • The letter clusters - "str" and "thr." Purpose History Walk can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing and reading a range of different word patterns, including word endings • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • discussing the organizational structure of the text • using diagrams, charts, and other illustrations to support writing.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining main ideas, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: What's the best way to find out about the history of your town? Go on a history walk! Look at the map and follow the kids as they discover interesting facts about people and places.
node.esiss_activities: The children in this book are looking at the history of their town. What interesting places are in your town? Draw a map to show these places on a history walk.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Impossible Bridge
node.content: The Impossible Bridge recounts how a group of children build a variety of bridges to solve problems and challenges. Features of the Book • A range of genres, including diary, recount, report writing, and instructions. • The use of close-up photography to show detail. • The use of specialised vocabulary - "beam," "arch," and "suspension." • The contractions - "wasn't," "can't," "it's," and "isn't." • The use of compound words - "cardboard," "today," "awesome," and "lengthways." • The use of the possessive apostrophe - "Mr. Keith's" and "Engineers'." • An environment that encourages children to use problem-solving strategies. Purpose The Impossible Bridge can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • recognizing the features of different genres • discussing the organizational structure of the text • summarising the ideas from a story • developing the skill of writing in a sequential manner.
node.theme: Problem solving|School and community|Science and technology
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Comparing and contrasting, Determining main ideas, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Bridges come in many shapes and sizes. This class makes models of some kinds of bridges. Then the teacher gives the class a challenge. Can they build a bridge from one piece of paper?
node.esiss_activities: Follow the instructions at the back of the book. Make your own impossible bridge. Write a list of the things that it can hold. Which things are too heavy?
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Riding the Steam Train
node.content: This story follows two children taking their first ride on a steam train and what they learn from their experience. Features of the Book • Written information presented in a variety of ways. • The use of photographs to support the text. • A variety of punctuation cues. • The use of the "ing" word ending to create the present continuous. • The use of compound words - "fireman," "firebox," "smokestack," "platform," and "countryside." • The use of contractions - "it's" and "he's."
