Purpose Riding the Steam Train can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing the organizational structure of the text • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • using diagrams, charts, and other illustrations to support writing.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making connections|Making judgments
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Drawing conclusions, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: It's fun to ride on an old train. Rose and Michael take a trip and find out how steam can make a big train move.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are on the steam train with the children. Write about the things you see at each station. Use the map to help you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Fishing off the Wharf
node.content: A girl and her father go fishing for herring - and later enjoy their catch. Features of the Book • The variety of factual information in the text. • The detail in the photographs. • The summary of information on page 16. • The frequency and variety of verbs. • The technical language. • The graphics that complement the photographs. • The repeated blends - "fl" and "ch." • The contractive apostrophes - "don't," "you'll," "we've," "can't," "It's," "that's," "I'm," and "There's." Purpose Fishing Off the Wharf can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • developing effective strategies for reading unknown words within a story • writing in a similar style to that of the story • writing about the steps in a procedure.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Exploring writer's craft|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring writer's craft, Comparing and contrasting, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: This girl likes to go fishing with her dad. But if you want to fish off the wharf, you have to know all of the tricks. Then comes the best part. Cooking the fish for dinner.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are writing to someone who has never been fishing. What steps does he or she need to follow to catch a fish? Write the steps in order. Use the book to help you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Sam and Kim
node.content: Sam and Kim are green sea turtles who live in a saltwater pond in Hawaii. This book gives general infomation about green sea turtles, how they live, and why they are endangered. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - maps, glossary, index, fact boxes, diagrams, photographs, - Specialised vocabulary - turtles, saltwater, volcano, carapace, plastron, keratin, predators, endangered, illegal, scientists, algae, - Retelling of Sam and Kim's story, - Labelled diagram of turtle parts, - Question-and-answer section
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making inferences|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making inferences, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Sam and Kim are green sea turtles. They were both injured by fishing nets and were taken to Peggy Forgues to be taken care of. What makes Sam and Kim special?
node.esiss_activities: Green sea turtles are endangered. How can people make sure they are kept safe? Make a poster to show people how to protect green sea turtles. Write a heading that will get people's attention. Include things we should do and things we shouldn't do to help green sea turtles. Add a drawing of a green sea turtle.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Divers' Dream
node.content: Divers' Dream tells the story of an old tugboat that is moved from a museum to the sea where it is sunk and used as a place for recreational divers to visit. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - reports, labelled diagrams, maps with a key, glossary, index, photographs, - Sequential text and photographic sequence of events, - Quotation marks to distinguish technical terms, - Newspaper features - headline, article, letters to the editor, - Problems and solutions, - Specialised vocabulary - towed, harbours, museum, scuba diver, marine biologist, engineers, funnel, trailer, welded, - Compound words - tugboat, businessman, newspaper, everyone, portholes, fireworks, slipway Purpose Divers' Dream can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring problem resolution, - following a sequence of events, - forming and supporting opinions, - paraphrasing and summarising information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: The Taimoa is a tugboat that has had an interesting life. It has worked in harbours and been a museum on land. Now it's at the bottom of the sea. How and why was the tugboat sunk?
node.esiss_activities: Design an advertisement to tell divers about the sunken tugboat and where they can find it. Draw a picture to show what the tug boat looks like under the water. Add labels. Write a caption to persuade divers to check out this new diving spot.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: So You Want to Move a Building?
node.content: This informational text contains two stories about moving buildings - Moving the Cabin and Hotel on Wheels. Features of the Book • The contents page. • Two stories in one book. • The labeled diagrams. • The numbered steps for the process. • The use of bold, colored type for effect. • The specialised vocabulary - "telescopic boom," "bogies," "rams," "road wheels," and "pad feet." • The use of ellipses - "Up up up." • The simile "standing like a huge insect." Purpose So You Want to Move a Building? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • understanding the function, feature, and category of new words encountered in the story • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • discussing the organizational structure of the text • using diagrams, charts, and other illustrations to support writing.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: How do you move a big building? Can you lift it up or put it on wheels? You can do both of these things. You might need some railway tracks as well!
node.esiss_activities: Think of a building in your town or city._x000D_ Now think of a place you could move it to._x000D_ Draw a plan to show how you would do this.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: What Keeps Them Warm?
node.content: The text begins with the thought-provoking statement Animals Don't Wear Clothes and goes on to answer how a variety of animals keep warm. Features of the Book • The variation in text size and placement according to difficulty. • Good model of expository text with narrative evolving from a single question. • Extra blocks of text that provide further information. • The use of multiple photographic images on the page. • Key vocabulary appearing in large, bold print. • The repetitive use of "Some animals." • The high-frequency words - "them," "have," "that," "This," and "get." Purpose What Keeps Them Warm? can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following strategies: • reading an increasing range of high-frequency words in context • encouraging rereading of the text to clarify understanding • introducing and discussing the meaning of new words within the story • developing an awareness of the specific structure within a story.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Science and technology
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Animals have many ways to keep warm when the weather turns cold. Foxes have fur and whales have blubber. How do you keep warm in winter?
node.esiss_activities: These animals have different ways of keeping warm. Choose one of the animals. Find out more about it. Write a short report about the animal. Include a picture.
node.esiss_hfw: them, have, that, this, get
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Stories in Stones
node.content: Stories are usually associated with books - but what are stories in stones? The fossils in this book tell stories of a time long, long ago. Features of the Book • The use of technical terms - "fossil," "mosasaur," and "ammonite." • The concept of a vast amount of time passing. • Additional challenges in text in smaller type. • The use of bold type for emphasis. • The explanatory section on page 4. • Text that encourages close attention to the photographs and illustrations. • The use of comparisons - "like a penguin" and "like an octopus." • Multisyllabic words - "mosasaur", "dinosaurs", "elasmosaur", "ammonite" and "octopus." • Changes of tense throughout the text. Purpose Stories in Stones can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing the organizational structure of the text • discussing new vocabulary and extending understanding • writing to the author about their opinion of the story.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Fossils can tell you a lot about the way life was millions of years ago, and the kinds of weird and wonderful creatures that lived then. Fossils are like stories in stones.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the fossils in the book. Long ago they were living animals. Choose one fossil and imagine what it looked like when it was a live animal. Draw a picture of the animal. Use the fossil in the book to help you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Inventors' Diary
node.content: The Inventors' Diary recounts four children's attempts to make a marble track. Features of the Book • The diary form of writing. • The daily stages of recording, using the pattern "On Monday" and so on. • The use of "but," "now," and "so" as indicators of progress in problem solving. • The reproduced computer diary entries. • The children's diagrams. • The vocabulary that communicates success - "looped," "jumped," "whizzed," and "clattered." • The logical steps of hypothesis, experiment, design, and modification. • The use of prepositions - "around," "along," "across," "over," and "into." Purpose The Inventors' Diary can be used in a guided reading setting to introduce and reinforce the following skills and strategies: • discussing the organizational structure of the text • discussing the relationship between cause and effect within the story • recognizing the features of different genres writing about the steps in a procedure.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making inferences|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Making inferences, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Making a marble track is great fun. Improving the design is even better. The children in Mrs Edward's class write a diary about the improvements to their invention.
node.esiss_activities: These children worked hard to make a marble track. Make your own marble track. What could you use? How will you put the pieces together? Try out the track. Measure how far the marble goes.
node.esiss_hfw: use, home, about, long, before, around, along, into, next, school, going
node.wushka_text_type: General Narrative, Diary, Journal or Log
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1470 ACELA1825 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Hunting Dragons
node.content: Blanca goes to stay with her auntie and uncle in the city because her mum is having an operation. The apartment building is very different to Blanca's house and her imagination starts to play tricks on her. Hunting Dragons can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of a narrative; • recognising point of view; • making personal connections with the text. Features of the text: • Narrative fiction • Told from a child's point of view • Theme of exploring a strange new place • Family relationships • Problem and solution are not explicit
node.strategies: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: When Blanca stays with Auntie Serena in the city, she hears strange noises. Is there a dragon in the apartment building?
node.esiss_activities: • Write about your experiences of visiting a new or strange place • List some of the noises that you hear every day and imagine how Blanca might hear them • Retell the story from Auntie Serena's point of view.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1826 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Lost Wheels
node.content: "Lost Wheels" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the main idea of a text • making inferences • identifying plot and character development • using the text as a model for writingFeatures of the text: • narrative • mystery • plot, characters, setting, theme
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making inferences|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences, Sequencing, Making Predictions
node.esiss_blurb: When Sam recovers from an illness, he discovers that his skateboard is missing and that strange things have been happening in his house.
node.esiss_activities: Plan and write your own narrative in the style of 'Lost Wheels' Make a diagram to show the chain of events in the story
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences, Sequencing, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Tail Talk and other poems
node.content: This anthology of poems looks at the ways that animals communicate with each other and with people. Animal Messages Animals don't communicate in the same way as people, but they can still find each other, frighten off predators, and share information about food. They do this in lots of different ways often without making a sound! Features of Tail Talk and Other Poems • Variety of rhymes • Narrative poetry • Descriptive language used to convey how animals communicate - waggle, tail's a flag, rattle Purpose Tail Talk and other poems can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • encouraging students to enjoy reading and listening to poetry • understanding how rhythm and rhyme are used for poetic effect • exploring the author's use of Descriptive language • finding factual information in poems
node.strategies: Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Sequencing, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: How does a dog say hello? How does a bee tell other bees where the nectar is? Animals may not talk like we do, but they all have their own ways of communicating. These poems tell you how.
node.esiss_activities: The poems in this book tell you how animals "talk" to each other. Choose an animal that isn't in the book. Describe the ways it communicates. Write some sounds that describe the actions and sounds it makes. Use these words to write a poem showing how this animal talks.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Ant and the Grasshopper
node.content: This fable is about a hard-working ant who preserves food for the winter and an idle grasshopper who lazes in the sun. The moral of the story is that hard work, working together, and perseverance pay off. Features of The Ant and the Grasshopper • Fable with a moral • Narrative using animal characters • Direct speech • Animal sounds in italics Purpose The Ant and the Grasshopper can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the genre of fables • making predictions • discussing the importance of sequence in a narrative • exploring character development
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Food|History|Science and technology
node.strategies: Making judgments|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Summarising, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Ant is working hard, storing corn for his family to eat over winter. Grasshopper, on the other hand, is lazing in the sun. Who will be happiest when the cold weather arrives?
node.esiss_activities: Ant worked hard to get ready for winter by collecting and storing corn. Make a mind map of the ways that other animals prepare for winter. You could use the Internet or books to help you. What animals prepare for winter like Ant?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1826 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Canoe Diary
node.content: This recount of a canoe journey in the wilderness is presented in the form of a diary or notebook of the author's observations. The author has used several ways of representing facts and impressions about the plant and animal life he encounters along the way. Features of the Book - Information presented in a variety of ways - text, captions, photographs, sketches - The index of animals and plants - The diary format - Design features such as clipped-on photographs, ruled lines, spiral binding - Natural history vocabulary - The use of descriptive language - curious, protective, peaceful, magical - The combination of fact and opinion in the text - Bold type for links to the index Purpose Canoe Diary can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - clarifying the features and uses of the diary format; - making predictions using prior knowledge and clues from the cover of a book; - identifying the author's purpose for writing; - distinguishing between fact and opinion; - using a diary to record information, feelings, and ideas
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Drawing conclusions|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Drawing conclusions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Come on a river journey with photographer Nic Bishop. He'll introduce you to a moose, a loon, a muskrat, and a plant that eats insects.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are on the canoe trip with Nic and Vivienne. Write two diary entries, using the photographs and the text in the book for inspiration. Focus on the two animals that you would most like to see in the wild.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Amazing Soya Bean
node.content: Soya beans are an important food for millions of people. This book explains the history of soya beans, the different foods and objects that can be made from soya beans, and includes instructions for growing soya beans. Features of the Book - Explanation, - Step-by-step illustrations of a procedure, - Specialised vocabulary - sprouting, harvest, bacteria, fertiliser, nitrogen, protein, pests, hulls, pods, biodegradable, diesel, synthetic, - Quotation marks used for emphasis - "the miracle crop", - Descriptive names - Yellow, Jewel, Great Treasure, Heaven's Bird, magic beans Purpose - The Amazing Soya Bean can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - exploring specialised vocabulary, - understanding and using a glossary, - recognising the features of non-fiction text, - distinguishing fact from opinion, - using graphic aids such as diagrams to convey information, - exploring the production, distribution, and consumption of goods. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Summarising|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Summarising, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: People have grown soya beans for thousands of years. Today, they are even grown in space. Why are soya beans so amazing? Why are they called the miracle crop?
node.esiss_activities: On page 17, a flow diagram shows how soya beans are processed to make different kinds of food. Choose another product such as rice or milk. Using the flow diagram in the book as a model, draw a flow diagram that shows how this product is processed.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: All about Seals
node.content: Kim writes in her diary about the excitement of a planned class trip to SeaLand. Each student has been given a task to help organize the visit. The trip is almost ruined when Kim forgets to do her task. All about Seals can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • distinguishing between significant and minor details; • analyzing aspects of the author's writing style; • reading diaries and journals; • using the text as a model for writing.Features of the text: • Diary format • Colloquial language • Recount with dates • Personal record of thoughts and actions
node.strategies: Making predictions |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: In this book Kim forgets to do something very important for the school trip. How do you feel when someone says they will do something but they don't do it?
node.esiss_activities: • select a minor character and write his or her diary entries over the same time period • write the thank-you letter that Conor might have written to SeaLand • read other journals, diaries, and logs.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1675 ACELY1680 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELA1790 ACELA1483 ACELA1826

node.title: Should Mac Leave Town?
node.content: A fictional report in which a student uses different ways to find out what people think about a dog that attacked a cat. Should Mac Leave Town? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • distinguishing between significant and minor detail • exploring newspaper reports; • making inferences; • using the text as a model for writing. Features of the text: • Cause and effect • Handwriting • Photographs, newspaper articles • Conclusion based on research
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect |Making Inferences|Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: In this book Cody and his classmates ask their family and neighbours about a recent dog attack. Sometimes people have different opinions about an issue. Why do you think that is?
node.esiss_activities: • write your personal opinion as to whether Mac should stay or go and support it with reasons • select another topic and use techniques similar to those in the report to find out the opinions of others
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1675 ACELY1680 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELA1790 ACELA1483 ACELA1826

node.title: Animal Messages
node.content: Animals don't communicate in the same way as people but they can still find each other frighten off predators and share information about food. They do this in lots of different ways often without making a sound! Features of Animal Messages • Fact boxes and grids • Labelled diagrams • Captioned photographs and illustrations • Headings and subheadings • Contents page, glossary, index • Specialised vocabulary Purpose Animal Messages can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using the features of non-fiction such as headings, glossaries, captions, and grids • locating information using the contents page and index • summarising and retelling information • using the text as a model for writing
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining importance|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Sequencing, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: What do the bright colours on a wasp tell you? They mean, Stay away, I'm dangerous! Animals can give each other, and us, messages using smell, colour, light, chemicals, and even dance!
node.esiss_activities: Animals communicate in many ways. On a piece of paper, write the headings, Animals and People. Under each heading, write the different ways that animals and people communicate the same messages. Use the book to help you. Perform some of the sounds or actions to your family. Do they know what your animal is saying?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Snow Scare
node.content: A family trip into the mountains becomes dangerous when the weather changes unexpectedly. Features of Snow Scare • Fictional first-person recount • Orientation, problem, climax, resolution • Descriptive language • Focus on the problem-solving process and safety • Direct speech Purpose Snow Scare can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making personal connections with the text • exploring how the use of language can deepen meaning • sequencing the main events • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • Making predictions
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Sequencing, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: An overnight trip into the mountains turns bad for two girls and their father. Luckily, Dad is very well prepared and knows just what to do.
node.esiss_activities: We have many different kinds of weather. In this story, it snows when the family are on the mountain. List the different kinds of weather you know. Write a pro (good thing) and a con (bad thing) about each kind of weather. For example, when it's sunny you can play outside, but it's dangerous to stay in the sun too long.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Water in the Weather
node.content: Water is in the weather in many ways. Snow, rain, and frost all look and feel very different from one another - but they are all water which is a concept this book explores. Features of Water in the Weather • Explanation • Headings and subheadings • Labeled diagrams • Scales for measurement • Information in photographs, diagrams, and fact boxes • Glossary
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Using graphic sources, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Snow, rain, hail, and mist. These are all examples of water in the weather. This book also tells you about different kinds of clouds, and explains the difference between rain and drizzle!
node.esiss_activities: On page 4, the author explains that the water on Earth goes around in a cycle. Draw your own labelled diagram to explain how the water cycle works. Use the book to help you. You will need a sheet of paper and some markers. Show the diagram to your family and explain it to them.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Annie Peck: A Life of Adventure
node.content: Annie Peck was born in 1850 and spent most of her life climbing mountains. Annie faced many challenges in the wilderness as well as in daily life. Features of the text • Biography • Map • Timeline • Photographs with captions • Conclusion Purpose 'Annie Peck - A Life of Adventure' can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the main idea of a text; • describing the sequence and setting of events; • using the text as a model for writing.
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: Annie Peck wanted to climb higher than any women had ever climbed, and she wanted to climb higher than men. Why this was so unusual for a woman in the nineteenth century?
node.esiss_activities: • write a summary of the biography using dates as headings • compare the clothing that Annie wore with that worn by climbers today • write a list of questions you would ask Annie Peck if she were alive today.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELA1827 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Time Capsule
node.content: When an old classroom is demolished, a time capsule is discovered that contains items from 1950. Features of The Time Capsule • First-person narrative • Recount from the past within the story • Key events set in different times Purpose The Time Capsule can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • locating information in a text • responding to text • sequencing the main events
node.theme: Change over time|History|School and community|Science and technology
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using graphic sources, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Workers are building a new classroom at Tyler's school. When the workers discover a time capsule under the old classroom, the children have the chance to find out about the town's past, and leave another message for the children of the future.
node.esiss_activities: You can make your own time capsule, like the children in this story. Collect things that will help someone in the future understand what life is like today. You could find a photograph, a coin, and some newspaper articles. Write a special message. You could hide it, like the children in the story, or put it away and open it later.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Keeping the Past Alive
node.content: Preserving treasures from the past is not easy. Even without use many items will deteriorate over time unless they receive special care. This text explains the processes of deterioration preservation and conservation. Features of Keep the Past Alive • Headings and subheadings • Bullet points • Photographs with captions • Tables • Specialised vocabulary • Contents page, glossary, index Purpose Keeping the Past Alive can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • finding and interpreting information • synthesising information • making personal connections with the text • using the text as a model for writing
node.theme: Change over time|History|School and community|Science and technology
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Determining main ideas, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Workers at a museum explain the many ways they preserve the special treasures on show. You'll also read about the ways that nature preserves objects.
node.esiss_activities: We can preserve different objects by looking after them. Make a list of the things that cause objects to break down and the conditions that preserve them. Use the book to help you. Choose a special treasure. Write a plan that describes how you can preserve it.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Fun and Games
node.content: People have played games for thousands of years. This book explores why we play games and looks at the origin of the games we play today. It includes instructions for playing three games. Features of the Book - Historical information, - Connections between past and present games. - Procedural text, - Lists, - Labelled diagrams, - Specialised vocabulary - competition, equipment, mancala, opponent, jai alai, - Purpose - Fun and Games can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: -identifying and Summarising Determining main ideas, - reading and following procedural texts, - exploring root words, - retrieving and classifying the information in a text, - exploring ways that recreational activities can facilitate global understanding. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Hide-and-seek, tag, chess, soccer, go fish ... People have played games for thousands of years. Find out why people play games and who first played them. Then learn to play the ancient game of mancala.
node.esiss_activities: Try playing a new game from this book. Mancala is easy to make. Follow the instructions on page 12 to make a mancala board. Read the rules of the game. Now teach someone in your family how to play mancala.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Helen Keller: A Life of Adventure
node.content: When she was a child, Helen Keller suffered an illness that left her deaf and blind. This is an account of her life. Features of the Book - Biography, - Theme of overcoming obstacles, - Specialised vocabulary - mute, meningitis, poorhouse, lipread, - Use of adjectives to describe Helen's feelings - frustrated, jealous, lonely, - Different forms of communication, - Invention - the Braille alphabet, Purpose Helen Keller: A Life of Adventure can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - recognising the features of a biography, - using a glossary to clarify vocabulary, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - exploring spelling patterns such as adding ies to nouns ending with y, - using graphic aids such as time lines to convey information, - using a biography to gain an understanding of how people's lives have changed over time, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Helen Keller was blind and deaf for most of her life. This didn't stop her from achieving great things. Read her story of courage and adventure.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the Braille alphabet on page 17. Write the answers to these questions in Braille: • Where was Helen Keller born? • How did she communicate? Ask someone in your family to use page 17 to translate your answers.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Mapping the World
node.content: There are different maps for different purposes. This text describes many kinds of maps and how and when they are used. It also explains how people use a compass and the Global Positioning System (GPS) to find their way. Features of the Book - Procedural text, - Historical information, - Mapping activity, - Maps with legends and scales, - Example of a street index, - Specialised vocabulary - scale, legend, symbol, grid, contour, - Acronym - GPS (Global Positioning System), Purpose - Mapping the World can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying the Determining main idea and supporting detail, - Summarising information, - exploring word families, - writing procedural text, - interpreting maps and using them as a source of information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Since ancient times, maps have taught people about the world. There are maps of towns and cities as well as weather maps and maps of the seabed. There are even maps of the universe. Why do we have different kinds of maps, and how do we use them?
node.esiss_activities: On pages 10 to 11 there are instructions for making a map. Use these to map your backyard. Draw the large objects, such as a shed and the garden. Now hide an object in your backyard. Mark its location on the map. Ask a family member to use the map to find the object in your backyard.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Visualising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Measuring Up
node.content: People use all kinds of tools to measure distance, weight, area, and time. This book explains how these things were measured in the past and the history of the measuring tools and systems that we use today. Features of the Book - Comparison of past and present measuring systems, - Procedural text, - Specialised vocabulary - thermometer, units, standard, area, milestone, paces, imperial Purpose - Measuring Up can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying problems and predicting outcomes, - making inferences and supporting them with evidence from the text, - exploring word families, - developing an argument based on information in the text, - exploring the ways that people measure distance weight area and time. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: We measure food, time, land, distance, and buildings - sometimes, we even measure ourselves. We have all kinds of tools to help us measure these things, but what did people do in the past?
node.esiss_activities: Practise measuring some things in your house. • How many cubits (page 5) is the length of your bed? • How many digits (page 7) is the height of your computer? • How many spans (page 7) is the length of your kitchen bench? Now follow the instructions on pages 24 and 25 to find the area of your foot.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Floss and Mo
node.content: "Floss and Mo" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of an explanation • drawing conclusions from a text • finding information from a text • using the text as a model for writing. Features of the text: • Explanation • Narrative procedure • Cause and effect • Subheadings • Glossary words
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect| Summarising| Making Inferences|Identifying the features of an explanation|Using the text as a model for writing
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Summarising, Making Inferences, Identifying the features of an explanation, Using the text as a model for writing
node.esiss_blurb: Floss and Mo are rabbits that live in a hutch. Parthiv and Janki are responsible for caring for them. This article_x000D_ explains how and why they keep pet rabbits.
node.esiss_activities: use the Internet or library to find out how to take care of fish in a fish tank make a model of, or draw, a hutch for rabbits write a speech about taking care of rabbits and present it to the class
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Summarising, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Smog, Dust, and Toads
node.content: Environmental disasters have caused a lot of damage to our planet and affected the lives of many people. This book looks at three disasters and explains why they happened. Features of the Book - Three examples of environmental disasters, - Historical information and photographs, - Maps and labelled diagrams, - Cause-and-effect relationships Purpose - Smog, Dust, and Toads can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - using strategies to determine the meaning of unfamiliar words, - recognising cause-and-effect relationships, - studying the use of adjectives, - determining the author's message, - writing to support an opinion, - exploring the ways people's actions can have a negative effect on the environment. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Asking and answering questions, Monitoring for meaning, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: What could smog, dust, and toads have in common? There's more than you might think. Each has been part of an environmental disaster that has harmed people and Earth in many different ways.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you live in London in the 1800s. You want to improve the quality of the air. Write a letter to the Prime Minister describing the steps you would take to reduce the smog in the air. Read these steps to someone in your family. Do they think your plan will work?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: They Changed the World
node.content: This book contains biographies of Galileo Galilei, Benjamin Franklin, Marie Curie, and Mario Molina, and explains how their discoveries changed the world. Features of the Book - Biographies, - Historical information and photographs, - Time indicators - Soon after; Over several weeks; After a while, - Information about scientific practice, - Theme of overcoming obstacles, - Specialised vocabulary - telescope, conductor, lightning rod, uranium, radium, Purpose They Changed the World! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - recognising the features of a biography, - Making inferences and supporting them with evidence from the text, - identifying common prefixes, - Summarising information, - identifying the ways in which scientists' work affects our lives, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Monitoring for meaning, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Some scientists invent machines or tools. Others study the way things work. Here are the stories of four scientists whose work has changed the way we live.
node.esiss_activities: These four scientists have done things to help change the world. Choose one scientist. Write a short letter to him or her. Explain why you think the work the scientist did is important. Read your letter to your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Monitoring and Clarifying, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELA1827 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Tomorrow's Energy
node.content: People have used fossil fuels for hundreds of years, but these sources of energy are running out. This book looks at how we have made and used energy in the past and sources of energy for the future. Features of the Book - Flow chart and graph, - Specialised vocabulary - fossil fuels, solar, turbines, generator, carbon dioxide, methane, nuclear, toxic, uranium, - Questions addressed to the reader, - Statistics, Purpose - Tomorrow's Energy can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying and Summarising the Determining main ideas, - determining the author's purpose, - exploring word derivations, - recognising cause-and-effect relationships, - using graphic aids such as charts to convey information, - recognising different kinds of energy and their limitations. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: People have used coal, oil, and gas for hundreds of years. One day, these fossil fuels will be gone. Luckily, scientists have many ideas about energy for the future.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The World of Water
node.content: Water is one of the most important resources on Earth. This book explains where water comes from and how it gets to our homes. It also explores water pollution and the importance of water conservation. Features of the Book - Explanation, - Map, - Flow diagrams, - Alliteration - The World of Water Digging Deeper Follow the Flow, - Specialised vocabulary - reservoir, monsoon, glacier, filter, sewage, nutrients, evaporated, - Comparisons, Purpose - The World of Water can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Making inferences by Drawing conclusions, - exploring synonyms, - interpreting diagrams and using them as a source of information, - writing persuasive text for a specific audience, - understanding that water is an important natural resource. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Water is one of the most precious resources on Earth. People, plants, and animals need to survive. Luckily, water is all around us.
node.esiss_activities: The diagram on page 18 shows how water gets from a reservoir to your home. Draw another diagram to show how water gets from the clouds to the reservoir. Add labels and arrows to show the direction the water is flowing.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: On the Move
node.content: "On the Move" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • summarising information • interpreting information from diagrams and photographs • using text as model for writing. Features of the text: • sequenced explanation of a process • cause and effect • headings and subheadings • diagrams and photographs • procedure
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: The lighthouse at Cape Hatteras is the world's second tallest brick lighthouse. In 1999, it was moved inland to stop it from falling into the ocean.
node.esiss_activities: Write a summary of 'On the Move' and present orally to the class Make a model of the Cape Hatteras lighthouse on the move
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELA1825 ACELT1587 ACELT1589 ACELT1591 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Your Body in Balance
node.content: The human body needs the right balance of food, water, exercise, and sleep. This book explores why each of these things is important to stay healthy. Features of the Book - Figurative language - your body in balance; an amazing machine, - Statistics, - Fact boxes with a question-and-answer format, - Charts and a bar graph, - Specialised vocabulary - cells, digesting, organs, muscles, nerves, blood vessels, nutrients Purpose - Your Body in Balance can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - exploring the author's use of figurative language to increase understanding, - interpreting and using charts as a source of information, - recognising cause-and-effect relationships, - studying the use of verbs, - writing a response to the text, - understanding the body's need for food water exercise and sleep to stay healthy. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Do you often feel hungry or tired? If so, your body may not be getting what it needs. The human body is an amazing machine. To stay healthy, it needs the right balance of food, water, exercise, and sleep.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the glossary on pages 30 and 31. Find five more words in the book. Write the word and then write what it means. Read the meaning of the words to your family. Can they guess what the words are?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Big Race
node.content: Duck Lake Primary School has only eleven students so it's a problem choosing the best runner to represent the school in The Big Race. Trevor is picked because he has the longest legs in the school - but can he run? Features of the Book - Tall tale genre - an unlikely story, - Speech and thought bubbles, - Changes in typeface in the illustrations, - The map of the race, - The humour in the text and illustrations, - Similes - as strong as an ox; legs like an antelope; a body like a beanpole, - The information in the illustrations, - The growing sense of exaggeration, - The use of Descriptive language - a fate worse than death; butterflies in my stomach Purpose - The Big Race can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising the first person point of view, - using graphic sources of information, - predicting probable outcomes, - identifying the use of figurative language such as similes, - comparing texts on similar themes in different genres. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Sequencing, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Duck Lake Primary School is very small. There are only eleven students, and they are not very good at sports. What happens when the skinniest boy in the school is chosen to take part in The Big Race?
node.esiss_activities: In the story, each runner had a map, but some of them still got lost. Draw a map of the race. Show the path that Trevor took and what happened to him at each place. Mark the places where other runners such as Milly McMinn and Ben Banks went.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Sequencing, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Ducks Crossing
node.content: This is the story of a family of ducks who are trying to cross a newly-built highway. The ducks finally succeed with the help of some concerned motorists. Features of the Book - The environmental theme, - Repetition in the dialogue - "Stop the traffic!" shouted the crowd. - The descriptions of the city, the mayor, the chief of police, - Verbs used as an alternative to "said" - shouted, yelled, cried, - Challenging vocabulary - accident, secretary Purpose - Ducks Crossing can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using Prior knowledge to predict meaning and make sense of the text, - making judgments about the actions of characters, - identifying cause and effect in the story line, - determining the author's purpose in writing, - recording the responses of different characters to a problem, - determining similarities and differences in characters. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Mother Duck didn't like Moonlight Lake. She decided to cross the busy highway and take her six fluffy ducklngs with her. Find out how much trouble this causes for Buck Handy, mayor of Sun City.
node.esiss_activities: The little girl's mother calls the chief of police to help the ducks. Who would you call if you found the ducks trying to cross the road? Write a four-step plan to explain how you would make sure the ducks were kept safe.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: I'm So Hungry and other plays
node.content: This collection of plays offers readers the choice of a variety of genres - from whodunnit to fantasy to just plain gross! There are four plays in the book - The Bus Kids, Fishy, The Magic Wand, and I'm So Hungry. Features of the Book - The inclusion of characters, a plot, and a setting in the plays (as in narratives), - Lists of characters, - The conventions of written drama - italics for stage directions, - The description of the "scene", - Two plays by the same author, - The range of illustrative styles Purpose I'm So Hungry and other plays can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - learning the conventions and language of drama, - practising creative interpretation, - rewriting text as narrative, - reading with pace and expression, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Worm pies? Super Special Slime? And who stole the chocolate fish out of the fridge? These four plays are fun to read and act out.
node.esiss_activities: In the play "I'm So Hungry", The Big W serves interesting food. Make a menu for dinner at your house. Give each item on the menu an unusual name, like at The Big W. Ask an adult to help you make something from your menu and be a guest at your restaurant.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Bird in the Basket
node.content: This story from Indonesia is about three poor but hard-working sisters. When the youngest sister discovers a magical bird, it seems that their worries are over. Each morning the bird fills a basket with rice - more than enough to meet their daily needs. However when their mean uncle hears of this wonderful bird he wants to have it for himself. Features of the Book - Traditional tale, - Indonesian setting, - Formal language, - Stylised illustrations, - The message in the story, - Past forms of verbs - grew, flew, woke, ate, burst, - Evocative use of language - Ke-ke-ko kekeko Purpose - The Bird in the Basket can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising the form of traditional tales, - understanding that authors write in different ways for different purposes, - determining the author's underlying message, - comparing traditional tales with other genres, - using text as a model for students' own writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Little One's sisters are glad when she finds a bird to talk to while they are away working in the fields. They never dreamt this would change their life so much.
node.esiss_activities: This story from Indonesia has a sad ending because of the uncle's actions. Imagine that Kekeko could explain why he didn't fill the uncle's basket with rice. What would the bird say? Write a letter from Kekeko describing what the bird would say.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1826 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Strike
node.content: This is a non-fiction text about drumming and the performance group called Strike. The book includes photographs, diagrams, and instructions for making a simple drum. Features of the Book - Non-fiction text features - photographs, labelled diagrams, glossary, index, - Instructions for making a drum, - Interactive text that asks questions of the reader, - Specialised vocabulary - percussion, musical, echoing, strikers, drumstick, mallet, vibrates, instruments, - Variations on root words such as drum, hit, - Onomatopoeia - bang, crashing, echoing, clapping, thudding, hissing, - Repetition of sentence patterns, - Inclusion of a child to engage the reader Purpose - Strike can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - finding information in a non-fiction text, - extending vocabulary by using a glossary, - following instructions, - exploring sound words, - using charts and diagrams to record important details. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Following instructions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Sequencing, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: So you want to be a drummer, but you don't have a drum? Read about a band called Strike. They drum on all kinds of things to get people's toes tapping and hands clapping.
node.esiss_activities: Look for things in your house that would make a good drum. Experiment with different things to drum them with. Which things make a high sound and a low sound? Now you could follow the instructions in the book to make your own drum.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Losing Lillie
node.content: "Losing Lillie" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the literary features of texts • making predictions • making personal connections with the textFeatures of the text: • Narrative • Plot, characters, setting, theme • Dialogue
node.strategies: Making Predictions| Making Connections| Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Predictions, Making Connections, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: When Lillie goes missing at the mall, her big sister, who was supposed to be taking care of her, panics.
node.esiss_activities: rewrite the story as a play and perform it to an audience write a job description for a babysitter of a young child.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Connections, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Sock Gobbler and other stories
node.content: This is a collection of short stories with similar themes around the imaginary characters or situations that children use to describe or explain events in their lives. The Sock Gobbler relates to one boy's attempt to discover what happens to missing socks. Features of the Book - Theme of imaginative invention, - Each story told in one chapter, - Variety of illustrative styles, - Stories told from different points of view, - Creative typography, - Short passages of text on each page, - Descriptive language - slimy, gurgled, draggled, slobbering, sweaty, browny-greeny Purpose - The Sock Gobbler and other stories can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - identifying the imaginative fantasy theme of a story, - exploring an author's use of Descriptive language, - recognising a story's problem, - Comparing and contrasting writing styles, - using text as a model for students' own writing. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Brent crept up to the washing machine and dropped in one of his sister's socks. Then he waited to see what would happen. He didn't have to wait long. Read these stories and decide whether sock gobblers, giants, and Martians are part of the real world.
node.esiss_activities: List the words 'gurgling, gobbling, grimy, draggled, slimy' and 'SCHLOP' as well as other descriptive words the students have noted or found interesting. Beside each word, write what the students think it means. Discuss the root word, where appropriate, and ask them to suggest how these words might be used in a sentence.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Where and Back
node.content: "Where and Back" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the literary features of a text • describing the setting and sequence of events • using the text as a model for writing. Features of "Where and Back": • science fiction narrative • present tense • direct speech to express thoughts and feelings • orientation, problem, resolution, conclusion.
node.strategies: Analysing the literary features of a text|Describing the setting and sequence of events|Making inferences|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Analysing the literary features of a text, Describing the setting and sequence of events, Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Dwayne Dunn kicks Amy's dog and pushes Amy into the pond. Amy goes back in time with Dr Doriddle to try to solve her problem with Dwayne.
node.esiss_activities: Write a science fiction story that solves a problem you have.
node.wushka_comprehension: Analysing the literary features of a text, Describing the setting and sequence of events, Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Night Noises
node.content: While staying with his grandfather, John becomes frightened by an owl tapping against his window. Grandpa shows John the owl and tells him a story about how the owl got his feathers. Features of Night Noises • Myth within a realistic narrative • Information about owls • Interaction between the characters • Style of the illustration for the myth Purpose Night Noises can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making personal connections with the text • discussing the purposes behind myths • exploring dialogue
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: What is that strange noise in the night? And why has Grandad got an unusual mask on the wall. Grandad tells John a story he heard when he was a boy in Puerto Rico, and John is not scared of the noise any more.
node.esiss_activities: Find a quiet place at home and sit down for five minutes. Make a list of the noises you hear. Write a word for each sound, such as "zoom" when a car passes your house. Try out your sounds on someone in your family. How many sounds can they guess?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Shapes from Nature
node.content: This non-fiction text contains stories from around the world that explain phenomena related to the sun and to the birds. Features of Shapes of Nature • Explanations about birds and the Sun that appear in stories and art • Illustrations that support the text • Text boxes to explain specific stories • Tables • Illustrated procedure • Glossary and index Purpose Shapes from Nature can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • gathering information from written and visual texts following the instructions in procedural text • exploring symbolism • understanding the features of a non-fiction text
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Using texts as models, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: People have always painted, carved, or told stories about shapes in nature. They helped to explain events such as lightning and the path of the sun across the sky. At the end of the book, you'll find out how to make your own thunderbird.
node.esiss_activities: Follow the instructions on pages 14 to 15 to make a Thunderbird. Use your model as a puppet and make up an adventure for the Thunderbird.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Dinosaur Dig
node.content: A scientist goes on a dinosaur dig to study the remains of T. Rex. Her two children make an interesting discovery too! Features of Dinosaur Dig • First-person narrative • Direct speech • Riddle with clues • Factual information Purpose Dinosaur Dig can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making predictions • developing a sequence of events • locating and interpreting information
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Synthesising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Visualising, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Two children are lucky enough to go with their mum on a dinosaur dig in the desert. When they find some unusual clues, they follow the trail and discover something that gives them a huge fright.
node.esiss_activities: In this story, the children's mother makes up a riddle about a T. rex. Choose an animal and write some clues to help someone guess what it is. How many clues does it take before they guess the animal?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Animals in Danger
node.content: This non-fiction text describes animals that are endangered the most important reasons why and the measures that are being taken to protect them. Features of Animals in Danger • Report • Contents page, glossary, index • Bullet points • Tables • Footnote • Headings and subheadings • Case studies • Labelled diagrams and illustrations Purpose Animals in Danger can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using questions to focus reading • using graphic features to find information • exploring cause and effect
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Discussing point of view, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Pollution, poachers, and natural disasters all contribute to animals becoming endangered. This book explains what people are doing to help species on the brink of extinction.
node.esiss_activities: Design a poster that tells people how they can help stop animals from becoming extinct. Use the information in this book to help you. Which information is the most important? Show the poster to your class.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Gold Fever
node.content: A town booms when something happens that brings a lot of people and work to the area. A town can go bust when disaster strikes or when resources run out. This book explores the cycle of boom and bust associated with the Klondike gold rush. Features of the Book - Historical information, - Map, - Proper nouns including the names of people, places, and ships, - Numbers expressed in a variety of ways - 1890s, six months, $500,000, three kilometres, eight thousand, 1800, six0 000, Dredge Number 4, - Specialised vocabulary - gold rush, boom, bust, sluice, pan, claim jumper, prospectors, grubstaker, pay dirt, stake a claim, dredge, tailings, Purpose - Gold Fever can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: -interpreting information from historical photographs, - recognizing cause-and-effect relationships, - exploring antonyms, - retrieving and classifying the information in a text, - writing a narrative procedure, - recognizing the effect that resources industry and tourism can have on a community. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Drawing conclusions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: A place booms when something happens that brings many people to an area. In the 1890s, thousands of people travelled to the Klondike Valley to look for gold and a town called Dawson was built. But would the boom last?
node.esiss_activities: The gold miners faced many challenges. Imagine you are a miner at the Klondike. Write a letter home to your family. Use the information in the book to describe the hardships you are facing. Read the letter to your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Iron Road
node.content: In the 1800s, travelling across Canada was difficult and dangerous. To make it easier, a railway was built across Canada. This book describes the construction of the railway and the difficulties of getting it through. Features of the Book - Detailed diagrams, - Historical information and photographs, - Geographic information, - Different perspectives of historical events, - Labelled diagram with zoom effects, - Maths in authentic contexts, - Maps, - Specialised vocabulary - nitroglycerine, trestle, rail, spike, sleeper, immigrants Purpose - The Iron Road can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying problems and solutions in a text, - interpreting text and using it as a source of information, - recognising synonyms and exploring how they are used, - writing a narrative account, - identifying the ways in which transport has changed people's lives. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Drawing conclusions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: In the nineteenth century, travelling across North America could take months. Many people didn't like making the long and difficult journey. In 1875, work began on a railroad across Canada. It would need thousands of workers and would take ten years to build.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are a Chinese worker on the Iron Road. Write a letter home to China explaining the what life is like and the dangers of the work. Read the letter to your family. Would they want to work on the railroad.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Escape!
node.content: Zimm and Tarek, space travellers on the starship Astra, are carrying out an important mission on a dark dusty planet when they realise that they are not alone and are in serious danger. Features of the Book - Science fiction genre, - Prologue, - A plot that is often developed through dialogue, - Different language structures used by the two main characters, - Sci-fi vocabulary - force field, scanner, trillium, freeze-beams, - Invented names - Tarek, Zimm, Andral, Quod, - Illustrations in cartoon style Purpose Escape! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising the distinguishing features of science fiction narrative, - analysing plot structure - problem crisis and resolution, - interpreting specialised vocabulary in context, - building an awareness of the relationship between oral and written language, - using text as a model for students' own writing, - making judgments about characters from their actions, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Drawing conclusions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Starship Astra is running on half power. The crew have stopped near a strange, dark planet to fix the engine. Zimm and Tarek take a shuttle down to explore the planet. But who, or what, is trying to stop them?
node.esiss_activities: Zimm and Tarek had a plan for escaping from the Dark Planet. Fold a piece of paper in half, then in half again. In the squares, draw Zimm and Tarek's escape plan as a cartoon. Add speech bubbles to show what they are saying to each other.
node.wushka_comprehension: Sequencing, Making Connections, Making Inferences, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Rainbows All Around
node.content: Jenny feels left out. Her friends have a new club, and she's not invited. But Jenny has an idea - she makes friendship bracelets for everyone in the class. Now, nobody feels left out. Features of the book - Similes - Autumn leaves floated down like snowflakes, - They sounded like bees buzzing in a hive, - Compound words - snowflakes, backpack, sunlight, teardrop, classmates, bedtime, basketball, rainbow, - Descriptive language, - Instructional language within the narrative, - A sequence of steps with diagrams, - The use of direct speech, Purpose - Rainbows All Around can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating the content of a book to students' own experiences, - identifying a problem and predicting outcomes, - exploring characterisation, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings and relationships, - discussing compound words, - studying similes, - reading and following instructions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Jenny knows what it feels like to be left out. Her friends have a club and she's not asked to join. So when Jenny shows something special at sharing time in school, she makes sure nobody is left out.
node.esiss_activities: Eirca shows Jenny how to make a friendship bracelet. Find some wool or thread to make your own bracelet. Follow the instructions on page 32. Show your bracelet to your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Measuring Time over Time
node.content: "Measuring Time Over Time" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills:• creating an organising structure • explaining the main idea of a text • defining and sequencing the information needed to carry out a procedure. Features of "Measuring Time Over Time": • report • diagrams with captions and labels • procedural text • timeline • cause and effect
node.theme: Change over time|History|Invention|Physical science|Problem solving|Science and technology|Weather
node.strategies: Creating an Organising Structure|Finding Main Ideas|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Creating an Organising Structure
node.esiss_blurb: This report explores how people have measured time through history.
node.esiss_activities: Use the procedural text in this report as a model to write instructions for making a candle clock.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELT1589 ACELY1665 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670 ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Strange Creatures
node.content: Tarek and Zimm are intergalactic explorers from the starship Astra. Fresh from an adventure with the Andrals, they begin to explore a strange planet in the Terron star system. Zimm is excited because this planet looks a lot like Earth. Features of the Book - Science fiction genre, - Sequel to Escape!, - The style of the illustrations, - The prologue and introduction to characters on page 3, - Different language structures used by the two main characters, - Sci-fi vocabulary - shuttle, scanner, freeze-beam, sensors, Terron Purpose - Strange Creatures can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - discussing the features of the science fiction genre, - using text and picture clues as aids to prediction, - considering the consequences of characters' actions, - looking at how the author has defined the main characters. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Zimm and Tarek are excited when they find a strange new planet that looks like Earth. But Tarek is stung by a unusual plant and Zimm doesn't know what to do. Some strange creatures come to the rescue!
node.esiss_activities: Tarek and Zimm are brave explorers, but even they make mistakes. Travelling to new places can be dangerous if you don't know what you'll discover. Write a list of ways you could stay safe if you were a space explorer like Zimm or Tarek.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Cat Talk
node.content: Cat Talk explains how cats communicate with one another and with people. The book describes a range of feline actions and gives the reasons behind them. Features of the Book - Headings and sub-headings, - The use of parentheses to give additional information, - Questions addressed to the reader, - Illustrations that enhance the meaning of the text, - Precise Descriptive language, - The personal nature of the writing, - Subtle humour, - The "cat talk" in quotation marks Purpose - Cat Talk can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising the features of non-fiction especially the organisation of material, - relating what students read to their own experiences and observations, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, - using a text as a springboard to further research, - recognising the author's purpose and point of view. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Cats really do talk to each other and to us, but they don't use words like we do. This book is a dictionary of cat language. It will help you understand how to start answering your cat when it talks to you.
node.esiss_activities: The author describes this book as a dictionary of cat language. Use it as a model to create a dictionary of dog language. Think of three ways that dogs send people messages. Draw a picture to show each message. Can your family guess what the dog pictures are saying?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Saving the Yellow Eye
node.content: The yellow-eyed penguin is the rarest penguin in the world. Scientist John Darby explains how he set out to help recreate its endangered habitat. Features of the Book - The information in the photographs, - The use of graphs, - Fact files on penguins, - The use of photo insets, - Specialised vocabulary - habitat, extinct, endangered, hemisphere, - The clear statement of the problem and solution, - The use of maps Purpose - Saving the Yellow Eye can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring specialised vocabulary, - summarising important ideas, - exploring cause and effect, - using maps and graphs as sources of information, - using electronic media to research a topic. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Drawing conclusions
