node.esiss_blurb: The yellow-eyed penguin is the rarest penguin in the world. Many of the places it lives are being destroyed. Scientist John Darby explains what is being done to keep the yellow eye safe.
node.esiss_activities: Like the yellow-eyed penguin, many kinds of animal face extinction. How can you or your family help protect endangered species? Talk with your family about this. Make a poster to show your solution.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Shapes of Water
node.content: This collection explores the theme of the shapes and patterns in water, leaves, seeds, and fruit, with examples of both expository and procedural texts. Features of the Book - Five articles on a theme, - Contents page, - Procedural text in the activities, - Articles by the same author, - Bold type for keywords, - Simple science concepts, - Specialised vocabulary - vein, droplet, pod, spine Purpose - The Shapes of Water can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - locating and explaining keywords, - distinguishing fact from opinion, - following procedural text, - using text as a model for students' own writing, - locating and researching new sources of information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Spooky fog, crystals on leaves, the beauty of rainbows. This book explores the different shapes of water. It also looks at the shapes of leaves, seeds, and fruits.
node.esiss_activities: Write a list of words that describe how water moves, looks, feels, and sounds. Use the words to write a poem that paints a picture of water. Read the poem to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1484 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Bad Dad List
node.content: Chloe's dad hasn't got a job any more, and he is supposed to be looking after her while her mum works. But Chloe doesn't think he's very good at it, and she gets cross with him that she makes a "Bad Dad list".
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Making predictions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Chloe's dad hasn't got a job any more. He's supposed to be taking care of Chloe while her mum is working. But Chloe doesn't think he's very good at it and starts a list of all his mistakes.
node.esiss_activities: Chloe changes her ideas about her dad after the accident at the lake. She realises that being a parent is a hard job, and there are lots of things to remember. Reread the book and find the places where Dad makes mistakes. For each mistake, write how Chloe could have helped her dad rather than criticising him.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and effect, Making Predictions, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1826 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Beating the Drought
node.content: In an effort to keep his prize pumpkin alive during the water restrictions, Grandpa carries the art of water recycling to an extreme, driving his family crazy in the process. Features of the Book - The humour in the illustrations - The twist in the story on page 30 - The use of inference - "That's it," said Mum, gazing at the television. "Everyone has to share the bathwater." - Compound words - bathwater, wastewater, wheelbarrow, everything, everyone - Contractions - that's, we're, can't, there'll - Verbs - groaned, switched, mumbled - Specialised vocabulary - drought, sprinkler Purpose Beating the Drought can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using prior knowledge to anticipate meaning and to make sense of the text; - recognising the distinguishing features of narratives - plot, characters, setting, and theme; - demonstrating comprehension by identifying answers in the text; - exploring the conventions of direct speech; - recognising the Determining main idea and retelling the events of the story in order.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Forming opinions|Making judgments
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Forming opinions, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Grandpa tells everyone that water is the secret to his prize pumpkins. So what happens when there's a drought? You may be surprised where Grandpa finds the water he needs.
node.esiss_activities: Grandpa was clever at saving water to feed his pumpkin. Imagine you are living with this family. Write a list of rules about which water is to be used to feed the pumpkin so you could eat the soup without feeling sick.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Fact from Opinion, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1826 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Stuck!
node.content: A boy becomes trapped in the lift at his grandmother's apartment building. He stays calm and uses the emergency phone to call a rescue team. Features of Stuck! • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Character development • Fictional first-person recount • Sequence of events • Theme of problem solving under pressure Purpose Stuck! can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring character development • determining the author's purpose • discussing problem solving in a narrative text
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Activating prior knowledge, Using graphic sources, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: A trip to see his grandma ends in disaster for Justin when the lift in her apartment building gets stuck. Even worse, the person in the lift with him is scared of small spaces!
node.esiss_activities: The two boys in this book respond differently when they are stuck in the lift. Compare how the boys behaved. What did they do that was the same? What did they do that was different? Show your ideas on a Venn diagram. Use the diagram to explain your ideas to someone in your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Compare and Contrast, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Cast Your Vote
node.content: John's class is holding an election for class president, but he's not sure that he wants to run. By exercising his civic responsibility, John learns that he has some ideas worth sharing and a desire to change things for the better. Features of the Book - Chapter titles with cultural references and double meanings, - Different points of view, - Specialised vocabulary - platform, Student Council, campaign, candidate, - Figurative language - similes, - Humour, - Dialogue. Purpose Cast Your Vote can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making and revising predictions, - forming opinions based on evidence and personal preferences, - analysing cause and effect, - identifying forms of civic discussion and participation in a democracy. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: John's class is holding elections for class president, and Sofia has nominated herself. John decides to run against her, but he has no policies and he's afraid of public speaking. His chances don't look good.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are running for class president. Write a short speech to tell your classmates how you will improve your school. Make sure you talk about the skills you have that will be good for the job and why people should vote for you. Read the speech to someone in your family. Would they vote for you?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Truth about Sharks
node.content: Sharks are often portrayed as aggressive predators but they usually avoid contact with people. This text explains some facts about sharks and challenges some of the myths that surround them. Features of The Truth about Sharks • Fact boxes • Captioned photographs • Comparisons • Specialised vocabulary - carnivore, marine biologist, plankton, scavenger, tissue, valve • Labelled diagram Purpose The Truth about Sharks can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information • monitoring for meaning while reading identifying facts and opinions
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Sharks are usually viewed as aggressive predators, but they rarely attack people. Find out more about these magnificent creatures, and why they have such a bad name.
node.esiss_activities: Make a shark mobile. You will need some string, paper, or cardboard, markers, scissors, and a hole punch. Draw one kind of shark. Then colour it and cut it out. Write five facts about the shark's behaviour or appearance on small pieces of card. Hang the facts from the shark picture. Hang the mobile so your family can see it.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Living Rain Forest
node.content: Naturalist and photographer Nic Bishop explores the multilayered rain forest. He explains why this fragile environment is home to such a variety of life. Features of the Book - Factual text with precise language, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - A variety of amazing photographs, - Fact boxes, - Captions that provide additional information, - The graph, map, and diagrams, - Writing that contains fact and opinion Purpose - The Living Rain Forest can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - retrieving information from a non-fiction text, - exploring the form and function of captions, - studying the use of adjectives, - studying the origin and use of compound words, - comparing a book with other books by the same author, - using a text as a basis for research. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Exploring text structure|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: From the toads and tapirs of the damp forest floor to the parrots and monkeys of the sunny canopy, rain forests are home to an amazing variety of life. Nic Bishop gives you a guided tour!
node.esiss_activities: Butterflies, frogs, and caterpillars in the rain forest have bright colours to keep predators away. Draw a set of butterfly wings. Design a pattern on the wings to scare off predators.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELT1602 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Coyote Not-so-clever
node.content: This is a collection of four separate fables in which Coyote is the brunt of others' jokes. The underlying message of the tales is that Coyote never learns from his mistakes. The stories are examples of traditional narratives - in this case fables of trickery. Features of the Book - An anthology of separate tales, - The humour in the illustrations, - The language of traditional narratives - It happened long long ago, - The narrative form - problem, complication, resolution, conclusion, - Frequent use of direct speech, - Parallels with other trickster tales such as The Hare and the Tortoise Purpose - Coyote Not-so-clever can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - analysing a fable as an example of a traditional narrative, - distinguishing between the Determining main idea and the supporting detail, - predicting possible outcomes, - Comparing and contrasting fables from different cultures, - discussing cause and effect in the text, - rewriting a text in a different genre. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: That Coyote. He thinks he's so smart, so clever. But he's always getting himself into trouble. And when he gets his fine, new tail, things get even worse.
node.esiss_activities: These Coyote stories explain why he is a trickster and why he is sometimes silly. Choose another animal with a special feature and write a paragraph about how the animal got that feature. You could explain how the giraffe got such a long neck, or why sloths move so slowly.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: High Wire
node.content: A boy is visiting his cousin in Australia and they go on a camping trip. They learn the importance of safety on a ropes course the hard way. Features of High Wire • First-person narrative • Realistic fiction • Descriptive language • Interaction between the characters Purpose High Wire can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • making personal connections with the text • exploring narrative structure and character development • making inferences
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: A camping trip in the Australian bush sounds like a lot of fun, especially when you get to take part in a ropes course. The boy in this book learns that listening to the instructor is an important part of staying safe.
node.esiss_activities: Use the blackline master to write alternative endings for the story • Choose an extreme sport and list the rules and the safety equipment that would be needed • Explain the rules that the narrator broke and why they are important.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Tunnel under the Sea
node.content: How do you get from England to France without using a plane or a boat? Engineers came up with the idea of digging a tunnel under the sea. This title examines the many problems faced in this project and the solution that the engineers provided. Features of Tunnel under the Sea • Photographs and illustrations • Graphs, cross-sectional diagrams, map • Captions and labels • Problem/solution format • Specialised vocabulary • Report structure Purpose Tunnel under the Sea can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using graphic sources of information • retrieving and classifying information from a text • identifying the problems and solutions in a text
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining main ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Summarising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: How do you travel between England and France. You can fly or take the ferry. And now, you can travel by train under the ocean. Read about the construction of the amazing Chunnel!
node.esiss_activities: Digging the Channel Tunnel was hard and dangerous work. Write a job description for a tunnel worker. What challenges and problems would the worker face. Would you want to be a tunnel worker?
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Summarising, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Travelling to Titan
node.content: Kate and Zac are travelling with their family by spacecraft to live on Titan one of the moons of Saturn. When they discover mice in the spaceship, Zac has the answer - he has smuggled his cat Smoky on board. Features of Traveling to Titan • Plot, characters, setting • Descriptions of each scene • Character list • Play script conventions, such as italics for directions • Factual information about space travel and the solar system Purpose Traveling to Titan can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the conventions and language of drama • practicing reading aloud with pace and expression
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Kate and Zac are travelling to Titan, one of Saturn's moons. When they discover that there's a mouse on board, and it's eating their supplies, Zac has a secret weapon that will solve the problem.
node.esiss_activities: The family in this book are travelling to Titan to find out if people could live there if a meteorite hit Earth. Use your knowledge of planets to design a perfect planet for people to live on. What would we need to survive? Be creative. Draw the planet and label its features.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Voyage to the Giants
node.content: In August 1977 the spacecraft Voyager II left Earth to explore the giant planets Jupiter Saturn Uranus and Neptune. Now "Voyager II" has left our solar system, but it is still sending back signals and has travelled farther than any modern spacecraft. Features of the Text • Labelled photographs and diagrams • Fact boxes • Timeline • Contents page, glossary, index • Specialised vocabulary - probe, gravity, slingshot effect • Graphic at the foot of each page spread Purpose Voyage to the Giants can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using prior knowledge to make predictions • questioning the text • locating the Determining main ideas • finding and recording information
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune are the largest planets in our solar system. Voyager II is a spacecraft that travels to these planets and send back information about them. It is still sending back signals and has travelled farther than any modern spacecraft.
node.esiss_activities: Voyager II sent a lot of information back to Earth. Draw a space map that shows where Voyager II travelled. Next to each planet, write an interesting fact that scientists discovered.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Legend of Pheidippides
node.content: A serious young runner is sent to Athens to inform the people about the outcome of a battle between the Greeks and Persians at Marathon. Features of The Legend of Pheidippides • Recount of a legend • Narrative • Setting, plot, characters, sequence of events • Map with enlargement and scale Purpose The Legend of Pheidippides can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • identifying the features of a legend • exploring narrative structure and character development
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Determining main ideas|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: When the Persians arrive to invade Greece near the town of Marathon, the army chose their fastest runner to carry the news to the people of Athens. The marathon race is a reminder of this soldier's amazing run.
node.esiss_activities: Pheidippides travelled a long way to deliver his message. It would have been much easier to use email! Make a mind map of the different ways Pheidippides could have delivered the message if he were running today. Ask a family member if they can add any other form of communication to your mind map.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Marathon Run
node.content: The legend of Pheidippides' run from Athens has led to a number of modern marathons. For serious runners the training is very difficult and the competition intense. This text describes some famous marathons and gives instructions for organizing a fun run. Features of The Marathon Run • Report • Procedural text • Fact boxes • Maps and keys • Tables • Bullet points Purpose A Marathon Run can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using questions to focus reading summarising and synthesising information • using the features of non-fiction texts to locate information
node.strategies: Making connections|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Summarising, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Today, the marathon is a key event in the Olympic Games, and marathons are held in cities all over the world. Find out about the origins of this race and the feats of some of the marathon champions.
node.esiss_activities: Reread pages 12 to 15. Then design your own "mara-fun" in your neighbourhood. Use the five headings from the book help you. Draw a map showing the landmarks and any danger spots. Ask someone in your family to run the course with you!
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Hidden Treasure
node.content: Karen finds a letter in an old book at her grandparent's house. A riddle in the letter takes Karen and her brother Toby on an exciting hunt for treasure from the past. Features of Hidden Treasure • Third-person narrative • Sequence of events • Rhyming riddle with clues • Dialogue Purpose Hidden Treasure can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • reading and interpreting riddles • making and supporting opinions • using Prior knowledge
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When Karen finds an old letter in one of her grandma's books, it starts them on a hunt for hidden treasure. They'll have to solve some of Grandad's riddles if they want to find it.
node.esiss_activities: Make a treasure hunt. What will the treasure be? Make sure the clues aren't too easy, so the player has to guess where the next clue is hidden. Use the book to help you. Ask a family member to do the treasure hunt.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Looking at Letters
node.content: Throughout history people have written letters for a variety of purposes. This text examines different kinds of letters and gives information about the development of the postal service. Features of Looking at Letters • Facsimiles of letters and envelopes • Procedural text • Timeline • Headings and subheadings • Contents page, glossary, index Purpose Looking at Letters can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of letters• reading and interpreting timelines • reading specialised vocabulary
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Discussing point of view|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Discussing point of view, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: These days we use email to communicate, but some people still send letters. How does a letter get from the post box to your mailbox? You can check out the history of letter writing as well.
node.esiss_activities: Write a letter to someone special. What interesting facts can you tell them about letters throughout history? Look at pages 9 to 10 to help you begin the letter and address the envelope correctly. Ask a family member to help you post the letter.
node.wushka_text_type: Email, Letter or Postcard, Procedural Text
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Great Piano Hoist
node.content: Carlos is excited when he inherits his great-aunt's piano, but getting the piano into his apartment is going to be tricky. This story shows how Carlos's problem is solved through the creative use of pulleys and levers. Features of the Book - Problem solving, - Specialised vocabulary - pulley, lever, dolly, - Descriptive language - rummaged, musty, peered, maestro, - Historical references, - Factual information in a fiction text. Purpose - The Great Piano Hoist can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Visualising to make sense of a complex text, - Asking questions to deepen understanding of a subject, - using strategies to make meaning of unknown words, - exploring how pulleys and levers move objects. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Carlos is excited when his mum inherits a piano, but getting it into their apartment is going to be tricky. In fact, piano-moving day holds more challenges than Carlos could ever have imagined.
node.esiss_activities: Raymond used a lever to get the piano onto the dolly wheels. On page 29, there is an explanation of how levers work. Find something you can use as a lever such as a ruler. Try to lift some small objects using your lever. Change the position of the lever to see what happens. Show your family what you have discovered.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Badlands
node.content: A meteorite has collided with Earth, and Krystal and a small group of survivors are living in a subway station. This futuristic story explores themes of trust, community, and survival. Features of the Book - Dramatic illustrations, - Futuristic setting, - Suspense, - Character development. Purpose - Badlands can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing how changes in the environment affect people, - analysing theme, - Making inferences. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: A meteorite has collided with Earth. Krystal and a small group of other survivors are living in a subway station. Their food supply is running low, and tempers are frayed. When Krystal is caught giving away some food, her life goes from bad to worse.
node.esiss_activities: Some children are living in an underground train station after a meteor struck Earth. Imagine you live with Krystal and the other children. Write a diary entry that describes the events of a day, such as how you get food and water and how you stay warm and dry. Read the diary entry to someone in your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Beating the Frost
node.content: The Krissler family lost half their cherry crop in the big frost last year, and now money is tight. It's Jack's eleventh birthday soon, but there doesn't seem to be much to celebrate. This narrative explores Jack's feelings as he comes to terms with what's really important. Features of the Book - Figurative language - similes, metaphor, alliteration, - Descriptive language, - Themes of responsibility, self-questioning, and reflection. Purpose Beating the Frost can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing character development, - analysing descriptive language, - using texts as a model for writing, - exploring how science and technology play a role in local challenges. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Determining cause and effect, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Since they lost half their cherry crop in the big frost last year, money has been tight for the Krissler family ... And it looks like their problems are about to get worse.
node.esiss_activities: The author has used descriptive verbs to explain the things that are happening in the story. Find these verbs in the story and work out what they mean: • yabbering (page 11) • plummet (page 12) • withered (page 23) Now write a synonym for each word.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Double Switch
node.content: Jordan Blake looks like a spy because of his gangly frame, his long thin nose, and his mother's insistence that he wear a long coat and a hat. He unwittingly becomes involved in a spy ring and finds himself in the wrong place at the wrong time. Throughout the story, Jordan is pursued by an unwanted admirer Maggie, whose continual attention proves to be his salvation. Features of the Book - The structure and language of a mystery novel, - Compound words - briefcase, everywhere, homework, bookshelves, handcuffs, sugarplum, footsteps, - The punchy humorous style of prose, - Text supported by illustrations of key actions, - Figures of speech - almost jumped out of his skin; catch your death of cold; ran like lightning, - The use of irony - the man waving at Jordan and Maggie's unwelcome attention Purpose - Double Switch can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using prediction to promote discussion, - identifying the author's intention, - using inference to draw conclusions, - exploring the story's narrative structure, - working with compound words and suffixes, - mapping the story line. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Jordan Blake works hard at not looking like a spy, but his long thin nose and the coat and hat his mother bought him don't help!
node.esiss_activities: People think Jordan has spy equipment in his briefcase, but he really uses it to carry his lunch. Make a spy collection of your own! Look around your home and collect some objects that would be useful if you were a spy. Explain your spy collection to your family. Describe what each object is used for.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Hang in There, Oscar Martin!
node.content: Oscar Martin isn't a very good reporter (or photographer), and the school newspaper is suffering. The situation worsens when a neighbouring school starts publishing its own newspaper. Oscar is given the task of finding a news story to save the Redmond School News. Features of the Book - The pun in the title, - The example of a newspaper article, - Informal speech - Oh yeah?, Boy! - The humour in Oscar's writing, - The irony in the way Oscar saves the day, - Misleading headlines - Children Run as Greengrocer Waves Knife, - Specialised vocabulary - chief editor, headline, reporter, shot, zoom lens, printer, ready Purpose - Hang in there Oscar Martin! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on students' prior experience, - identifying the problem in the story, - distinguishing between the Determining main idea and supporting details, - conducting effective interviews, - distinguishing between fact and opinion. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Oscar Martin is the reporter for the Redmond School News. But he isn't doing very well, and neither is the newspaper. It's time for Oscar's lucky break.
node.esiss_activities: In the story, Oscar write funny news stories about the things he sees during the day. Think about what you have seen and done today and write some news stories. Try to make each story funny. Present your news stories to someone at home.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Ask Einstein!
node.content: Henry is writing to his teacher Miss Wilson to explain why he hasn't done his homework. The letter introduces the family's crazy cat Einstein and goes on to describe a series of disasters that Henry hopes will explain his missing homework. Features of the Book - A personal recount in letter form, - The tall tale genre, - Exaggerated illustrations for effect, - The use of caricature, - Text features - footnote, labels, speech bubbles, text in illustrations, - The use of puns - He seems to be over his attack of the blues, - The joke in calling the cat Einstein Purpose - Ask Einstein! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - expressing and supporting a response to a text, - using illustrations as a source of information, - describing the features of humorous writing, - reading aloud with attention to phrasing and punctuation, - predicting probable actions and outcomes. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Henry hasn't done his homework, and it's all his cat Einstein's fault, or so he says. Could one cat really cause so much trouble? Henry says that Einstein has a brain the size of a peanut, so anything is possible!
node.esiss_activities: Henry writes a letter to his teacher to explain his lost homework. What unbelievable homework excuse can you invent? Write your excuse in a letter and show it to your teacher. Would your teacher believe the excuse if it were real?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Einstein - Champion of the World
node.content: In this second adventure with the crazy cat Einstein, we meet the new neighbours and their pets get a lesson from Dad on how not to barbecue steak and find out how Einstein changes from a scaredy-cat to the hero of the street. Features of the Book - Exaggerated illustrations for effect, - A sequel with known characters, - The use of caricature, - Text in illustrations, - The unusual names of the pets, - Text features - footnote, labels, speech bubbles, - Punctuation and italics to indicate pace and style Purpose Einstein - Champion of the World can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - calling on Prior knowledge to make sense of the text, - describing the features of humorous writing, - exploring the use of speech bubbles, - reading aloud with attention to phrasing and punctuation, - using illustrations as a source of information, - comparing texts by the same author. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Most cats have places where they like to sit and play and climb. They don't like other cats coming into these special spaces. But not Einstein.
node.esiss_activities: Write a story map that shows how the family barbecue ended in disaster. Write each event in a box. Join the boxes with arrows to show the order of events. Give the story map to a family member. Can they follow the storyline?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Compare and Contrast, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Pirate Pie
node.content: In this story, two mean and nasty pirate cousins, Hector Humbug and Horatio Hogswoggle, attack Peg Leg Lil's cooking efforts. However, the confrontation brings them all unexpected business success. Features of the Book - The exaggerated racy language, - Onomatopoeia - whizz, whap, splat!, - Illustrations that support the tone of the text, - Specialised vocabulary - barnacles, jellyfish, bilge, rigging, galley, bosun, - The rhyme in the pirates' dialogue - I'll be a blue-bellied bass!, - The names of the pirates and the ships, - Text in the illustrations, - The slapstick humour and fast-moving plot Purpose - Pirate Pie can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - analysing the structure of fantasy narrative, - describing the mental images evoked by the text, - distinguishing between "real" and "invented", - writing with metaphors and similes, - comparing texts on similar themes in different genres, - using role-play to explore a text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Peg Leg Lil is a true blue pirate. She can sail through a typhoon and dig for treasure all day long. But everyone on her ship knows she can't cook to save herself. At least, until the Blue-water Bake-off is announced.
node.esiss_activities: Peg Leg Lil, Horatio Hogswoggle, and Hector Humbug each have their own personality. Design a pirate flag for each ship, to match each pirate's personality. What kind of pirate personality would you have? Design your own flag to show your pirate personality.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Never Hitch a Ride with a Martian!
node.content: When a short-sighted Martian loses his way and crashes on Earth, three children get a chance to be taken for the ride of their lives. This story describes the excitement of their trip into outer space and what they find, to their amazement, on their return. Features of the Book - An example of narrative fantasy, - The humour in the text and illustrations, - The use of adjectives - tremendous, shaky, bulgy, disgusting, gigantic, - Text in the illustrations, - The Martian's voice, - The concept of the missing time, - Inventive language - gunk, Ziggle Toad, goo, - The invitation from the author that leads the reader into the story Purpose - Never Hitch a Ride with a Martian! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - identifying the importance of setting to the story's development, - using inference to draw conclusions, - studying word meanings systematically, - using text as a model for students' own writing, - exploring the use of Descriptive language. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The Martian needed some help to fix the scanner on his spacecraft. The children give him a hand, and in return, the Martian takes them for the ride of their life. Next time, they won't be so quick to offer help!
node.esiss_activities: The children's problems begin when the alien borrows a toolbox to fix its spacecraft. Imagine you are one of the children. Write an argument against going for a ride with the alien. Use the ideas in the story to help you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: How I Met Archie
node.content: When Alice's mother gets a job as a newspaper reporter, Mrs Lilly comes to take care of Alice after school. But Alice doesn't like the fact that her mum is working, and she makes things very difficult for Mrs Lilly. Things change when Alice meets Archie, Mrs Lilly's pet monkey. Features of the Book - Six short chapters, - The theme of conflict, - Punctuation - ellipses, long dashes, parentheses, - The use of italics for emphasis, - Similes - like a very dead rat; howling like a werewolf, - Illustrations that support the text, - The slang used by Alice - no way, gross take it easy, cool Purpose - How I Met Archie can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating ideas and events in the text to students' experiences, - supporting interpretations with examples from the text, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings, relationships, and actions, - exploring the author's choice of language. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Alice wasn't happy about her mother getting a job. She was determined to make things as difficult as possible for Mrs Lilly, who came to take care of her after school. That is, until she meets Mrs Lilly's pet.
node.esiss_activities: Looking after a monkey is hard work. Think of another unusual pet, such as a tarantula or a snake. Make a PMI (Plus, Minus, Interesting) chart about the animal. Then write a paragraph explaining why you would or wouldn't like to own the animal as a pet.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Bridging the Gap
node.content: Whether they are big or small, all bridges are important - they "bridge the gap" and help us get to where we want to go. Bridge expert Steve Miller explores the topic of bridges with the help of a team of student engineers. Features of the Book - Factual information presented in a narrative style, - Contents page, glossary, and index, - Labelled diagrams, - Specialised vocabulary - span, truss, stringer, - The bridge challenge in each chapter, - The model for a scientific investigation, - The varied layout - photographs, diagrams, fact files, different typefaces, - Additional information in fact files, - Background images to support meaning Purpose - Bridging the Gap can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making links between students' experiences and information in the book, - previewing photographs and text, - discussing the use of labelled diagrams, - using a glossary to clarify vocabulary, - identifying and Summarising Determining main ideas, - following procedural text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Asking and answering questions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Big or small, all bridges are important. They bridge the gap and help us to get where we want to go.
node.esiss_activities: Find a gap at your house such as between the couch and a chair. How will you bridge the gap? Choose a beam, an arch, or a suspension bridge. Using information from the book, create your bridge from any materials you can find.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: There's No Place Like Home
node.content: Imagine that scientists have predicted that a meteorite will hit Earth in 2020. It will cause a lot of damage. If this really were to happen, what could we do? Amateur astronomer David Hill explores the planets and moons of our solar system to discover if there is anywhere else quite like Earth. Features of the Book - Fact files for each planet, - Captioned photographs, - The diagrams above the chapter headings, - Comparison diagrams, - The detail in the photographs, - Specialised vocabulary - diameter, core, atmosphere, orbit, - The example of scientific investigation, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Background images to support the theme Purpose - There's No Place Like Home can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - establishing a purpose for reading, - interpreting scale diagrams, - discussing the mental images evoked by a text, - interpreting and using graphic information from a text, - raising questions from reading a text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: If you took a tour of the planets in our solar system, you'd discover that none are quite like Earth. Mars is the best possibility for a future colony away from Earth. But really, there's no place like home!
node.esiss_activities: Earth is sometimes described as a Goldilocks planet. It has all the things we need to live. Using your knowledge of the fairy tale, Goldilocks and the Three Bears, write an explanation of why Earth is called a Goldilocks planet. Share your writing with your family. Do they agree with your explanation?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Measuring the Weather
node.content: We can't get away from the weather - it happens all around us, every day. Find out how meteorologists measure and forecast the weather. Features of the Book - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Labelled photographs and diagrams, - Background shapes and textures that support the theme, - The procedural text in the students' activities, - The use of graphs, - Specialised vocabulary - hurricane, vapour, climate, meteorologist, - Additional information in fact boxes Purpose - Measuring the Weather can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - assessing and building on students' prior knowledge, - using the contents page, glossary, and index, - identifying the main points in a text, - discussing a text to generate further questions, - using electronic media for further research, - writing and following instructions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When it rains, snows, or blows, meteorologists have the job of measuring the weather. How do you tell how strong the wind is, and how drizzle is different from rain? You'll also find out how to make your own measuring devices.
node.esiss_activities: Follow the instructions on page 25 of the book to make a rain gauge. Ask an adult to cut the bottle for you. Put your rain gauge outside in an open space. The next time it rains, check the gauge to see how much rain has fallen.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: What's Living at Your Place?
node.content: If your house is warm and dry, you could have all kinds of creatures as house guests. In this book, entomologist Bruce Chapman tells us about some of these unwelcome creatures and why they like to come and live with us. Features of the Book - The regular structure of each chapter, - The introduction and poem, - Specialised vocabulary - pupa, abdomen, moult , - Life cycle diagrams, - Labelled diagrams of the creatures, - Photographs with captions, - Detailed freeze-frame photographs, - Background images that support the theme Purpose - What's Living at Your Place? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising and using different parts of a book, - to locate information, - understanding and using a glossary, - interpreting and using graphic information in a text, for example, life cycles, - Summarising and presenting information in appropriate formats, - using text as a model for students' own writing.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Science and technology
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Maybe you don't know it, but you could have house guests. If your house is warm and dry, you might have all kinds of insects and other small creatures living with you. Find out about these unwelcome visitors, and why they like living in your home.
node.esiss_activities: On page 5 of the book, there is a poem called Bug Zoo. Choose a household pest from the book and write a poem about it. Think about how the bug moves, sounds, and what it looks like. Try to make every second and fourth line rhyme, like the poem in the book. Read the poem to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: What's Cooking?
node.content: In this book, students are cooks. There are simple recipes for treats like crispy cookies, mini-pizzas, and chocolate pudding. Following the recipes will help students understand how heat cooks food in different ways. Features of the Book - Recipe book format, - Specialised language - digest, convection, broil, - Abbreviations - tsp., tbsp., - Photographic sequences, - Step-by-step explanations, - Additional information in "hot tips" boxes and bulleted lists, - Labelled diagrams, - The humour in the chapter headings Purpose - What's Cooking? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring language specific to the recipe book, - format, - reading and writing procedural text, - locating information using a contents page and an index, - using text as a model for students' own writing, - Summarising a piece of text using the main points. For most information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: In this book, you can be the cook. Coconut crispies, mini pizzas, and chocolate custard. These are just some of the great recipes you can try.
node.esiss_activities: Choose a recipe from the book and make it. Ask an adult help you. Enjoy your creation!
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Down on the Ice
node.content: This recount is taken from an interview with conservationist Rose Evans who spent three weeks in the Antarctic studying a hut built there by the famous British explorer Robert Scott. The text includes some of the history of Scott's expedition, an account of Rose's preparations for her adventure, and descriptions of life on the ice and of the animals of the region. Features of the Book - The use of historic photographs, - Additional information in captions, - Dramatic photographs, - The use of maps, - Similes - like big green fridges joined by tubes; like diving down to the Titanic, - The use of photographic backgrounds to give atmosphere, - Specialised vocabulary - frostbite, wannagan, wilderness, blizzard, crevasse Purpose - Down on the Ice can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using graphic aids such as maps and graphs to convey information, - exploring specialised vocabulary, - describing the mental images evoked by texts, - raising questions from information gathered, - using electronic media to research a topic. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: One summer, Rose Evans went to Antarctica to spend three weeks studying a famous explorer's hut. It was an experience she'll never forget.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the photographs in the book and make a list of the equipment that Antarctic explorers and workers need to survive in this harsh climate. Imagine you are going to Antarctica, and you can take only five things with you. What would you choose? Draw and label a picture of yourself with these things.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Desert Run
node.content: John Bonallack and his son Dan take part in the "toughest footrace on Earth!" This true account of their gruelling run through the Sahara Desert is in the form of a diary with maps to show their progress day by day. Features of the Book - The diary format, - The safety issues, - Footnotes that provide additional information, - The use of maps, - Distances in parentheses, - The day-by-day progress shown by the maps and the "dog tag", - Lists to summarise information, - Place names - Casablanca, Ouarzazate, Sahara, Tazzarine, Rissani Purpose - The Desert Run can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using a diary to record information feelings and ideas, - using graphic aids such as maps and graphs to convey information, - exploring specialised vocabulary, - using electronic media to research a topic. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: John and his son Dan set out to take part in the toughest running race on Earth. They run for seven days through the Sahara Desert carrying all their food and gear. Nearly 500 runners start the race. How many will finish?
node.esiss_activities: The desert run was a challenge that John Bonallack really wanted to complete. On page 3, John lists the equipment he needed to take on the run. Imagine John is going to do a run through snow and ice. What changes would he need to make to the list? What new items would he need to take? Make a new list and show it to your family. Do they agree with your changes?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Tasmanian Devils
node.content: Rod Morris has written a non-fiction account of an unusual marsupial, the Tasmanian devil. He explains how this fierce little animal with a big mouth is winning the battle of survival against the odds. Features of the Book - The animal life cycle, - The arresting cover, - Specialised vocabulary - marsupial, nocturnal, predator, prey, extinct, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Fact boxes, - Diagram and map, - Close-up photographs Purpose - Tasmanian Devils can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using a glossary and index, - comparing non-fiction genres, - debating different sides of an argument, - researching and presenting information, - writing a scientific report, - creating a glossary. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Find out about the fierce little animal with the big mouth. You'll also meet some of its marsupial neighbours on the island of Tasmania.
node.esiss_activities: On page 30, there is a life cycle of the Tasmanian devil. Choose another Australian marsupial from the book. Find out about its life cycle. Create a diagram that shows the life cycle. Use the information on page 30 to help you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Searching for Sea Lions
node.content: Photographer and writer Kim Westerskov undertook an expedition to remote Campbell Island. The purpose of this visit was to take underwater photographs of rare species of sea lion for a film and a book about a remote South Pacific Island. Features of the Book - Dramatic photographs, - Non-fiction in first-person narrative form, - Descriptive language - a very special underwater dance; seaweed sways and dances, - The author's affinity with the sea lions, - The use of framed and overlapping images in the layout, - The simple fact chart, - Specialised vocabulary - bull pup, flipper, - The factual information in italics, Purpose - Searching for Sea Lions can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring how information is presented in different ways, - discussing technical vocabulary, - discussing an author's use of Descriptive language, - describing the mental images evoked by the text, - forming and revising questions for investigation, - locating and Summarising information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Kim's usual job is taking photographs of the sea and its animals in places like Antarctica. He thinks it's the best job in the world. Join Kim on an underwater search for sea lions.
node.esiss_activities: Elephant seals grow to a massive size. Reread pages 12 and 13. Look at the measurements of an elephant seal. On your floor, measure out how long and wide an elephant seal can grow. How many of your family members can fit into the shape?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Close Call
node.content: When Harry borrows his father's mobile phone for the day, the result is a sequence of events involving Harry, his father, and Harry's pet rat. Despite Harry's mistake, the story shows that there is a strong bond between him and his father and that Harry learns from his actions. Features of the Book - The pun in the title - The use of metaphor in the opening paragraph - The third person point of view - The range of verbs - spooned, dangled, swung, flipped, blurted, lunged, thundered - Alliteration - clatter of cutlery and crockery - Informal language - c'mon, yeah, bye Purpose A Close Call can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring how the author sets the scene and introduces characters; - predicting outcomes of characters' actions; - discussing how characters change as the result of experience; - examining the use of metaphor; - investigating the use of verbs.
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Harry's dad is hoping to win an important contract at work. The only problem is that Harry has his dad's mobile phone, and his dad has Harry's pet rat in his briefcase. Will the deal go ahead?
node.esiss_activities: Harry and Dad both had one of those days where nothing goes right. Harry's rat must have been really confused. Write a paragraph about what happened from the rat's point of view.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Chief Great Raven
node.content: The villagers are upset because Raven Boy shares food with the ravens. When the villagers move to another place in search of food they leave Raven Boy behind as punishment. The birds help Raven Boy to survive and eventually the villagers and birds learn to live together. Features of Chief Great Raven • Traditional tale • Third-person narrative • Orientation, problem, climax, resolution • Character development • Animals that speak Purpose Chief Great Raven can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • forming and supporting opinions • making inferences • sequencing the main events • identifying the features of a traditional tale • summarising and restating information
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Habitats|Life science|Relationships
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Exploring writer's craft, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: They called him Raven Boy because he shared the fish he caught with the ravens that crowded around the fishing boats. The villagers hated the ravens because the birds stole their fish as well. When food becomes scarce, the villagers blame Raven Boy.
node.esiss_activities: In this traditional tale, Raven Bird shares his food with the birds. Would the story have ended differently if he hadn't shared his fish? Imagine you are Raven Boy. Write a letter to the chief explaining why you share your food.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Living with Salties
node.content: This report looks at the conservation of saltwater crocodiles (salties) in northern Australia. It provides historical and factual information and explains the ways in which people and crocodiles have learned to live in the same environment. Features of Living with Salties • Report • Photographs and map • Fact boxes • Specialised vocabulary, salties, prey, habitat • Legend • Comparison chart Purpose Living with Salties can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making inferences • summarising and synthesising information • studying cause and effect • identifying the Determining main ideas in a non-fiction text • finding information in written and visual text
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Summarising|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Summarising, Using graphic sources, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Salties are saltwater crocodiles. They live in areas where people now live as well. How can people and salties get along together?
node.esiss_activities: This book talks about conservation groups finding ways in which crocodiles and people can live together safely. Write a list of five "house rules" for crocodiles and people describing what is allowed and what is not allowed, so that we can live together peacefully.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The First Family on Mars
node.content: A family is hoping to be selected as the first family to live on Mars. When their house at the Mars training base burns down they have to be resourceful in order to live in an unusual and tiny place. Features of The First Family on Mars • First-person narrative • Problem-solving focus • Interaction between the characters • Factual information about space travel Purpose The First Family on Mars can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • exploring dialogue • making inferences • identifying the problems and solutions in a text
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Determining author's purpose, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: It's the year 2020, and Gemma and her family are training to be the first family to live on Mars. When there's a fire in their home at the training base they have to prove they are real pioneers.
node.esiss_activities: Gemma was good at inventing useful things. Imagine you are at the Mars training base. Design an invention that would be useful on Mars. What would you need to build it? Draw a diagram of your invention. Write a short explanation of how it works. Show the invention to your family. Can they help you improve it?
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Space Station
node.content: The International Space Station is a base for astronauts who live and work in space for up to six months. This book examines the challenges of living in this unusual environment. Features of Space Station • Explanation • Captioned photographs • Contents page, glossary, index • Labelled diagrams Purpose Space Station can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • Asking questions to deepen understanding • finding information in photographs • identifying the problems and solutions in a text.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Forming opinions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making connections, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Would you like to live in space for a few months like the crew of the International Space Station? You'll have to learn to brush your teeth without water and sleep standing up!
node.esiss_activities: Fold a piece of paper to make three columns with the headings - Pros, Cons, and Questions. What are the good things about living in space? Write your ideas in the Pros column. What are the bad things about living in space? Write your ideas in the Cons column. What questions do you have for astronauts living in space? Write these in the Questions column. Ask a family member to help you find answers to your questions.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Connections, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: North Pole Walk
node.content: British explorer Dave Mill wanted to be the first person to walk alone and unaided to the North Pole. This text describes the problems Dave faced during his journey and explains how the technology he used enabled his support team to rescue him. Features of North Pole Walk • Report • Photographs • Labelled diagrams and maps • Contents page, glossary, index • Fact boxes • Specialised vocabulary, ice floes, pulk Purpose North Pole Walk can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information from the text and graphic features • identifying the problems and solutions in a text • reading specialised vocabulary and using a glossary • drawing conclusions from the text
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: In March 2002, Dave Mills set out to be the first person to walk alone and unaided across the North Pole. His dream ended when his cooking stoves stopped working. His challenge then was to build a runway on the ice so a rescue plane could land!
node.esiss_activities: Dave felt many emotions on his journey. On a piece of paper, draw six boxes with the headings - worried, proud, confused, happy, amazed, frustrated. Imagine you are Dave. Write an event in each box to show when you felt these emotions during the journey.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Tree Falls Down
node.content: In this play, five animals who live in the forest are worrying about the effects of a fallen tree that is blocking their stream. They all work together to help solve the problem. Features of the Text • Plot, characters, setting • Sequence of events • Play script conventions, such as italics for stage directions • Problem solving Purpose A Tree Falls Down can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the conventions and language of drama • practising reading aloud with pace and expression • exploring character development.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Life science|Problem solving
node.strategies: Making predictions|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: When a tree falls down in the forest, the animals discover they have to work together to avoid an ecological disaster. This play is fun to read or perform with your friends.
node.esiss_activities: Create a paper puppet for each character in this play. Use the pictures of the animals in the book to help you. Using the puppets, act out the play for your family. Use a different voice for each character.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Helpful or Harmful?
node.content: This non-fiction text describes the relationships between various forest plants and animals, explaining why they are helpful or harmful. Features of the Text • Report • Contents page, glossary, index • Fact boxes • Photographs with captions • Glossary words in bold text • Diagram • Specialised vocabulary - deciduous, ecosystem, photosynthesis, producers, consumers, recyclers, parasites, habitats Purpose Helpful or Harmful? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring specialised vocabulary • exploring cause and effect • restating the information in a text.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Life science|Problem solving
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: There are all kinds of relationships between plants and animals. Sometimes the relationship is helpful to both partners. At other times, one partner definitely gets the best deal!
node.esiss_activities: This story has facts about how plants and animals live together. Some harm each other and some help each other. Make a booklet that describes the relationships between these plants and animals. Share the booklet with your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Wood Stork Swamp
node.content: In this story, the narrator recalls his childhood growing up near a swamp and the events that led to the destruction of the wood storks' habitat. Features of Wood Stork Swamp • Realistic fiction in the form of a memoir • Descriptive language • Factual information about the wood storks' habitat • Main events occur over a long period of time Purpose Wood Stork Swamp can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • sequencing the main events • forming and supporting opinions • discussing the importance of sequence in a narrative
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Change over time|Environment|Life science
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: Wood Stork Swamp is special to Sam, so when developers want to build a new hotel and golf course there, Sam is worried. The developers are offering big money for people's land. Will his mother have the heart to leave?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are the boy's mother. What would you have done if the lawyer sent you the letter? Write a short letter to the lawyer explaining why you would or would not sell your land. Show the letter to your family. Do they agree with you?
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion,
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Galapagos Giants
node.content: The Galapagos Islands are home to the giant tortoise, the land iguana, and the marine iguana. These animals aren't found anywhere else in the world, and this book describes why they're endangered. Features of Galapagos Giants • Diagram • Fact boxes • Glossary and index • Map • Photographs and illustrations • Specialised vocabulary, breeding, cold-blooded, endangered, equator, extinct, habitats, pests, predators, rarer, sanctuary, scaly, unique Purpose Galapagos Giants can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • drawing conclusions from the text • studying cause and effect • reading to find specific information summarising and restating information
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Change over time|Environment|Life science
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Meet some of the unusual giants of the Galapagos Islands. Huge tortoises and unusual iguanas live on this protected wildlife sanctuary. Join Rod Morris on a private tour.
node.esiss_activities: The giant tortoise and the land iguana are interesting animals. Think about the features, habitats, and behaviours of the tortoise and the iguana. Which things are unique to the tortoise? Which things are unique to the land iguana? What do the two animals have in common? Record your ideas on a Venn diagram. Explain the diagram to your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Full of Hot Air
node.content: Two families are enjoying a trip in a hot-air balloon but not everyone realises that Soon is scared of heights. His friend does his best to help. Features of Full of Hot Air • First-person recount • Puns related to hot-air ballooning - full of hot air, having a bad air day • Factual information • Similes • Illustrations from unusual perspectives Purpose Full of Hot Air can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring factual information in a fiction text • forming and justifying opinions about characters' actions
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Drawing conclusions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Comparing and contrasting, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: When two families go up on their first hot-air balloon flight, no one realises that one of the children is scared of heights. Find out how his friend helps him.
node.esiss_activities: Look through the book for information about how a hot air balloon works. Draw a diagram of a hot air balloon and label the different parts. Explain the diagram to someone in your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Bird's-eye View
node.content: This book explores the developments that have allowed people to view and photograph Earth from above. Features of Bird's-eye View • Report • Aerial photographs • Illustrations with captions • Compound adjectives - bird's-eye view, hot-air balloon, seventy-five years, search-and-rescue workers, ever-changing world • Historical information Purpose Bird's-Eye View can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • scanning and skimming a text for specific information • comparing and contrasting information • organising ideas and information using a concept map
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Forming opinions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: For much of history, people could only imagine what a bird's-eye view was like. In recent times, technology has allowed us to see Earth from above.
node.esiss_activities: Images from a birds-eye view look different to the way we usually see things. Imagine you are a bird flying over your house. What would you see? Draw a bird's-eye view of your house. Show it to someone in your family. Do they recognise the view?
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Learning to Fly
node.content: Dallas has always wanted to be a trapeze artist, but the effects of a bad dream means he has to change his plans.
