Features of Learning to Fly • First-person narrative • Realistic fiction • Descriptive language • Interaction between the characters Purpose High Wire can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • making personal connections with the text • exploring narrative structure and character development • making inferences
node.theme: Character education|Interviews|Leisure and recreation|Overcoming obstacles|Physical science|Safety|The arts
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Dallas has always wanted to be a trapeze artist like his mother but he changes his mind after a bad dream.
node.esiss_activities: After dreaming that he falls from the trapeze, Dallas doesn't want to be a trapeze artist any more. Divide a piece of paper in half. Imagine you are Dallas. Record your feelings about the trapeze before the dreams. Then record your feelings after the dreams. Write a paragraph explaining how you feel about deciding not to use the trapeze any more.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Flying by Wire
node.content: The Fly by Wire is an unusual and thrilling adventure flight. This text explores how it works and describes the safety features involved. Features of Flying by Wire • Report • Photographs • Labelled diagrams and maps • Contents page, glossary, index • Fact boxes • Specialised vocabulary - ice floes, pulk Purpose North Pole Walk can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information from the text and graphic features • identifying the problems and solutions in a text • reading specialised vocabulary and using a glossary • drawing conclusions from the text.
node.theme: Character education|Interviews|Leisure and recreation|Overcoming obstacles|Physical science|Safety
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Fly by Wire is described as the fastest adventure ride in the world. Come along and find out about the thrills, speed, and safety of this ride.
node.esiss_activities: Neil Harrap got the idea for his flying machine from a dream. If you could create a thrilling ride, what would it be. Draw the ride, then list the features that would keep you safe. Share your ride with your family. Would they like to try it?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1668 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: Maui's Fish
node.content: For thousands of years, people have been telling myths to explain events in the natural world. The people of Hawaii tell this tale of Maui and his big catch to explain the origin of their islands. Features of Maui's Fish • Retelling of a myth • Simple past tense • Third-person narrative • Based on oral tradition • Explains origin of things in the natural world Purpose Maui's Fish can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the features of myths • making inferences
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Sequencing, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Maui had many powers, but he couldn't catch fish. He longed to catch a great fish to show his brothers he was as good as they were.
node.esiss_activities: This myth gives an explanation of how the Hawaiian isalnds were formed. Write your own myth with a different explanation about how Maui was involved in the formation of the islands. Share both myths with your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Island Beginnings
node.content: An island is a unique environment that is home to many kinds of life. This book describes the many ways that islands are formed and introduces a few of their special inhabitants. Features of Island Beginnings • Comparisons • Aerial photographs with zoom effects • Fact boxes • Captioned photographs • Diagrams • Maps • specialised vocabulary - debris, ecosystem, glacier, habitat, ice age, organism, archipelago Purpose Island Beginnings can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • retrieving and classifying information • exploring Cause and effect • Comparing and contrasting information • determining importance
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Sequencing, Making predictions, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Many islands have become isolated from other bodies of land for millions of years. During that time, some extraordinary animal species have adapted to life on the islands.
node.esiss_activities: This book describes how different kinds of islands are formed. The diagrams on page 9 show how volcanic islands form. Use the information on pages 13 to 14 to help you draw similar diagrams, which explain how a coral island forms.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1466 ACELA1469 ACELY1669 ACELY1670

node.title: The Navigators
node.content: When Beeby and Jenna try out the controls on their starship, they are confronted by the most feared sight in the galaxy. They need to think quickly to navigate their way to safety. Features of The Navigators • Science fiction vocabulary and conventions • Futuristic story written in the past tense • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Orientation, problem, climax, resolution Purpose The Navigators can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associatednon-fiction title • exploring the structure of a narrative • examining the language that builds tension in a narrative
node.theme: Adventure|Challenge|Change over time|Problem solving|Science and technology|Space
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Jenna and Beeby are the only people awake on the spaceship Alpha. Jenna makes a decision that leads the ship into all kinds of danger.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine Jenna and Beeby weren't able to escape down the wormhole. How could they stop the crew of Star Scorpion from firing on them or trying to board their ship to steal the platinum. For example, they could send a message to the Star Scorpion saying that there has been a deadly outbreak of space measles. Write the message that the children would send.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Finding Freedom
node.content: This non-fiction text explores the different ways blind or visually impaired people get from one place to another. Features of Finding Freedom • Specialised vocabulary - visually impaired, sound-wave technology, Braille, ultrasound, transmitter, receiver, synthesizer, GPS • Photographs with captions • Fact boxes • Historical information Purpose Finding Freedom can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making connections with the text • identifying and making generalisations • restating information from different points of view
node.theme: Change over time|Problem solving|Science and technology
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Find out how blind and visually impaired people use their hearing and sense of touch to get around.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of a guide dog. Around the outside, write the characteristics a guide dog needs and the skills it must learn so it can work with a visually impaired person.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing, Making Predictions, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Spoiled by Oil
node.content: A girl records her experiences in a diary on holiday with her father who works as a wildlife officer. When an oil spill threatens hundreds of birds, she reluctantly helps out. Through the rescue effort, she gains a new respect for her father's work, the wildlife he protects, and herself. Features of Spoiled by Oill • First-person narrative • Character development • Sequence of events • Diary format and language • Theme of developing empathy and respect • Simple past tense • Linking words relating to time - this morning, finally, tomorrow, this afternoon Purpose Spoiled by Oil can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring character development • confirming and revising predictions
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Character education|Habitats|Life science|Personal change|Relationships
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: When an oil spill threatens hundreds of seabirds, a young girl reluctantly helps out. She changes her mind when she sees the damage to the birds and the shoreline.
node.esiss_activities: The girl in this story experiences an oil spill firsthand. Using the information from her diary and the illustrations, list the different actions the community takes to save the wildlife. Then create a poster that tells people what to do in an oil spill.
node.wushka_text_type: General Narrative, Diary, Journal or Log
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELA1491 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Living with the Nenets
node.content: The Nenets are nomadic reindeer herders who live in western Siberia, Russia. The author of this book spent six months living with the Nenets. Features of Living with the Nenets • Fact files and charts • Specialised vocabulary ethnographer, nomadic, clan, dialect, indigenous, tendon • Journal structure • Maps Purpose Living with the Nenets can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring the features of a journal • questioning the text • synthesising information from text and visual sources • analysing the meanings of complex words
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Following instructions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Making connections, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: Sven Haakanson spent six months living with the Nenets, who are nomadic people from Siberia, Russia. Sven describes their culture and their traditional way of life.
node.esiss_activities: Nenets live in special tents called myas. Think about what you would need in your mya if you were living with the Nenets. Draw a floor plan of the layout of your mya. Show it to your family. Would they change anything about your mya?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Nosey Investigators
node.content: Something in Matt and Arlo's apartment building smells terrible. In this humorous narrative, Matt and Arlo use their problem-solving skills to solve the problem. Features of the Book - Humour, - Double meanings, - Dialogue, - Colloquial language, - Inquiry and problem solving Purpose - The Nosey Investigators can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making, revising, and confirming predictions, - Making inferences, - exploring scientific inquiry through problem solving, - exploring common suffixes. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: There's a bad smell in Matt and Arlo's apartment building, but the boys can't find the source of the problem. So they devise a plan to solve the mystery of the big stink.
node.esiss_activities: Write a letter to Arlo's mother asking her to change the food she gives him for lunch. Describe the food Arlo likes and the food he doesn't like. Write in a convincing way so the letter will persuade his mother to make these changes.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Triangle Fire - Hannah's Diary
node.content: This historical narrative draws on actual events to tell the story of recent immigrants, Hannah and her father. In their efforts to start a new life in the United States, they endure terrible living and working conditions and survive a life-threatening fire. Features of the Book - Historical and factual information in a fiction text, - Cultural and geographical references, - Diary format, - Different text forms. Purpose - The Triangle Fire: Hannah's Diary can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Asking questions to deepen understanding and explore personal response to a text, - analysing the importance of setting in historical fiction, - Comparing and contrasting different accounts about past events. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining cause and effect, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Hannah and her father have moved from Poland to the United States to start a new life. They live and work in terrible conditions so they can save enough money to reunite their family. Just when it seems that things can't get any worse, disaster strikes.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are Hannah's father. Write a diary entry on the day of the fire. What would Hannah's father be doing that day? How would he find out about the fire? What would he do when he heard the news? How would he feel? Read the diary entry to someone in your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Lake Life
node.content: Lakes support plants, animals, bacteria - and even people. This text examines the importance of lakes and explores some of the potential threats to this precious resource. Features of the Book - Comparisons, - Maps with fact boxes, - Diagrams, - Nature photography, - Cause and effect. Purpose - Lake Life can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Visualising to make meaning of text, - Comparing and contrasting, - exploring organisms and their environments, - Making inferences beyond the text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Lakes support all kinds of living things, including people, plants, animals, and even bacteria! However, human activities, such as shipping and fishing, are causing some lakes to become polluted. What can we do to save our lakes and protect our lake life?
node.esiss_activities: The map on pages 4 to 5 shows famous lakes of the world. What is the closest lake to your town or city? Write some facts about this lake. Explain how it formed, what it is used for, and how people make sure it isn't polluted.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1680 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Beware!
node.content: Jake and Kelsey have often picked grapes from a property they pass on their way to school. However, this year Mr Granger the new owner puts up a sign saying "Beware of the Agapanthus". After the children discover what an agapanthus really is, they decide to pick some grapes - only to find that Mr Granger has beaten them to it. Features of the Book - The high-interest narrative, - The clear message in the text, - Unfamiliar vocabulary - agapanthus, schnauzer, - Effective use of language - squeaked, sniffed, scowled, hissed, - Attitudes and values that require critical thinking and response Purpose - Beware! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating ideas and events to students' own experience or views, - identifying the Determining main idea or theme and supporting detail, - identifying the author's intention, - discussing the author's choice of language, - analysing characters' feelings and actions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Jake and Lisa used to sneak grapes from the vine in the Young's driveway. Now the house has a new owner, and he's put a scary sign outside the entrance. But what is an agapanthus, and why should the children beware of it?
node.esiss_activities: Mr Granger makes a sign to scare off Kelsey and Jake. Make a "Beware" sign for your bedroom door. Think of an idea that will keep people out. Use some unusual words to confuse people like Mr Granger did.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Wild Bird and other stories of adventure
node.content: This collection of three stories has the theme of adventure - at sea, in the air, and underground. Each adventure involves children taking part in unusual and exciting activities - living on a yacht, making a tandem parachute jump, and exploring caves to look for treasure. Features of the Book - Three stories by the same author and photographer, - The settings - sea, air, and underground, - The range of typefaces, - Bold text for effect, - Specialised vocabulary - jumpsuit, harness, tandem, - The theme of challenge and adventure, - Design features - frames, overlapping photographs Purpose - Wild Bird and other stories of adventure can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - discussing the genre of adventure, - exploring different texts by the same author, - Comparing and contrasting ideas, - looking at root verbs and their endings, - using the text as a model for students' own writing. For more information, please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Scott does a parachute jump for his birthday, Janelle and Chris go on an underground journey, and Wendy and Rita live on a yacht. What these people have in common is adventure.
node.esiss_activities: The people in these stories have some amazing adventures. Imagine you are entering a competition to win an adventure on Earth or in space. Write a paragraph to describe the adventure, why it is amazing, and why you should be chosen to go on this adventure.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: When I Was Young and Wild Bill's Secret Wish
node.content: These two stories have letters in common. When I Was Young is a tall story told through letters between Karen and her various relatives about going to school in the olden days. The second story relates the frustration of Wild Bill Miggs, who always receives the wrong kind of mail. Features of the Book - The common element of letters, - A range of typefaces, - The page layout in the form of letters, - Punctuation - ellipses, parentheses, exclamation marks - The contents page, - The styles of the illustrations, - The tall tale genre Purpose - When I Was Young and Wild Bill's Secret Wish can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on prior experience to make sense of a text, - discussing the use of exaggeration, - reading aloud with appropriate pace and expression, - recognising that authors organise text in different ways for different purposes, - locating meanings and derivations of words. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Forming opinions|Making judgments|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making judgments, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Was it really hard to get to school in the old days, and why is Wild Bill Miggs jumping up and down on his mail?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are an adult, and you are writing a letter to tell someone how you went to school when you were young. Make up a tall tale. Make it funny and unbelievable. Read the story to your family. Did you make them laugh?
node.wushka_text_type: Tall Tale, Email, Letter or Postcard
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Land of the Dragons
node.content: Writer and photographer Rod Morris combines legend and personal narrative to provide a fascinating factual account of the Komodo dragon - the world's biggest lizard. Features of the Book - The combination of fact and fiction, - The use of personal narrative and legend, - Descriptive language - long-lost; flickering; rulers of the island; forked; hot on his tail; buried treasure, - The use of italics, - The use of a map and inset, - Design features - chapter headings dragon motif to end chapters, - The comparison with dragons from fairy tales Purpose - Land of the Dragons can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - Comparing and contrasting different writing styles and genres, - identifying the author's purpose for writing, - interpreting and using graphic information, - using electronic media for further research. For more information please see our Lesson Plan
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Komodo dragons are the world's biggest lizards and just as fierce as the dragons you read about in story books. There are three thousand of these lizards living on Komodo, and they really are the rulers of the island.
node.esiss_activities: Write a list of the features of a Komodo dragon such as smell, sharp teeth, and the noises they make. Now choose a word that describes each feature. Use these words to write a poem about the dragon.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1490 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Coyote Discovers Fire
node.content: This Native American myth explains how Coyote brings fire to his friends so that they can stay warm during the cold winter months. Coyote Discovers Fire can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: •making inferences and predictions about events and characters; •identifying the features of a myth; •identifying the author's purpose; •reading aloud with expression; •using the text as a model for writing. Features of the text: •Narrative myth •Explanation of a natural event •Setting, problem, climax, resolution, conclusion •Animals with human characteristics •Story with a message
node.strategies: Making Inferences|Making Predictions|Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Making Inferences, Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: This story about Coyote and his friends is a Native American myth. Do you know any other myths? What culture or country are they from?
node.esiss_activities: Read another myth and list some features that are similar to the myth Coyote Discovers Fire
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1478 ACELA1479 ACELA1790 ACELA1483 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELY1675 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Tuba
node.content: Eric has decided to learn the tuba, but he is having difficulty finding a place to practise. The Tuba can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making inferences; • analyzing a character's point of view; • describing the sequence of events; • identifying the theme. Features of the text: • Narrative • Problem solving • Dialogue • Characters, plot, setting
node.strategies: Making Inferences|Recognising Bias and Prejudice| Sequencing| Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Making Inferences|Recognising Bias and Prejudice| Sequencing| Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: In this story Eric struggles to find a suitable place to practise the tuba. What do you do when you have a problem? What do you do if your first solution doesn't work?
node.esiss_activities: • write a list of all the solutions that Eric tried • write a list of other solutions that he could have tried • research to find out more about the tuba.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Sequencing, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1793 ACELA1780 ACELA1828 ACELT1602 ACELT1603 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Alice's Diary
node.content: Alice is a diabetic. In her diary, she explains how she learned to cope with her illness and why she is still just a "regular" kid. Features of the Book - Diary format, - Timetable and charts, - The theme of coping with illness, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Fact boxes, - Diagrams - food pyramid, flow chart Purpose Alice's Diary can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating the text to students' own experiences, - interpreting timetables, charts, and diagrams, - discussing a text to generate further questions, - rewriting a story from a different point of view, - retrieving information and displaying it in a different way. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Forming opinions, Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: When Alice first found out she had diabetes, she said she felt like some kind of freak. But it doesn't really bother her now that she's used to it. She feels like a normal kid after all.
node.esiss_activities: Alice gets sick and discovers that she had diabetes. Make a flyer that describes what diabetes is and the signs that someone has the disease. Include information on the kinds of healthy food that people with diabetes need to eat.
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1491 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Tower
node.content: Ren and Zaila have tried to reach the tower many times. This time they're determined to make it. Many obstacles lie ahead including guards underground tunnels and a Jet Ski patrol. Features of The Tower • Action-packed plot • Dialogue • Sequence of events • Third-person narrative • Futuristic setting and illustrations • Surprise ending Purpose The Tower can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring narrative structure • making inferences • identifying the sequence of events
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Determining author's purpose, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: Ren and Zaila are running out of time. They have to make their way past the guards and the hovercraft to the tower. It looks like the only way through is by using the tunnels under the city. But what danger lurks there?
node.esiss_activities: Ren and Zaila face many obstacles on the route to the tower. Use the information in the story to draw a map of the route. Label the landmarks, and show the path of the tunnel. In a different colour, mark the route that you would have taken to get to the tower.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The People Planet
node.content: A thousand years ago, there were around a quarter of a billion people on Earth. Today, the world's population is around six billion. This text explores what caused this rapid growth in population and considers the implications for the future. Features of the Book - Cause and effect, - Dates and times, - Historical information and photographs, - Comparisons. Purpose - The People Planet can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - using Prior knowledge to support understanding, - analysing cause-and-effect relationships, - Summarising and restating information, - examining human impact on the environment. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: A thousand years ago, there were only a quarter of a billlion people on Earth. Today, there are around six billion. What caused this population explosion, and will it go on forever?
node.esiss_activities: Make a census form like the one on page 7. Use the form to gather information on your family. Ask questions that will give you interesting. answers. Report back to your family on your census.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Universe and other poems
node.content: This anthology of poems focuses on the planets, moons, and other bodies of our solar system and the ways in which people are exploring space. Features of The Universe and Other Poems • Free verse • Rhyming quatrains • Haiku • Descriptive language Purpose The Universe and other poems can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • encouraging students to enjoy poetry • identifying the features of poetry • exploring the author's use of Descriptive language • using different kinds of poems • discussing the factual information in a poem • creating a glossary
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Making inferences|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Discussing point of view, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: These ten poems will tell you a lot about the universe you live in. From the twinkling stars to the changing face of the moon, and how an astronaut feels as the sonic boom cracks, and he speeds into space.
node.esiss_activities: These poems describe our solar system. Brainstorm other words and phrases to describe our universe. Use words that will help create pictures in the reader's head. Now write a poem about space using the words. You could use a poem from the book as a model.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Lighthouse of Alexandria
node.content: The Pharos Lighthouse of Alexandria was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. This report describes it and explains what happened to it. The Lighthouse of Alexandria can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • interpreting information from maps and tables; • identifying the features of a report; • forming and supporting opinions. Features of the text: • Report • Maps •Photographs, illustrations, diagrams • Labels and captions
node.strategies: Making Inferences | Making Connections | Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Inferences, Making connections, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: This book tells us about The Lighthouse of Alexandria and other Wonders of the Ancient World. Do you know any of the Wonders of the Modern World?
node.esiss_activities: • construct a model of the lighthouse and explain it to the class • research and write about another of the ancient wonders • write an explanation of how the lighthouse was destroyed.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Making Connections, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1793 ACELA1780 ACELA1828 ACELT1602 ACELT1603 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Turtle's Pond
node.content: Beaver takes over Turtle's pond, so they have a race to decide who can stay and who has to move. Turtle's Pond can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of a traditional tale; • using prior knowledge;• identifying character traits; • locating information in a text. Features of the text: • Traditional story • Animals that speak • Plot, characters, setting, theme • Conflict and resolution
node.theme: Animals birds and insects |Conflict | Overcoming obstacles|Relationships
node.strategies: Making Predictions | Finding Main Ideas |Making Inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences
node.esiss_blurb: In this story Beaver and Turtle race each other to decide who can stay in the pond. How do you usually settle an argument?
node.esiss_activities: • reread the story in pairs • research the homes of turtles and beavers and compare them on a chart • choose other animals and identify their character traits, then write a story using this tale as a model.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1793 ACELA1780 ACELA1828 ACELT1602 ACELT1603 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Animal Neighbours
node.content: Many different animals find food and shelter in our backyards. This text explores the compost pile, tree, and garage habitats and introduces some of the animals that live in these places. Features of the Text • Contents page, glossary, index • Fact boxes • Photographs and illustrations with captions • Report structure • Specialised vocabulary - adapted, camouflage, decomposing, environment, foliage, habitat, nutrients, pincers, predators, prey. Purpose Animal Neighbours can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • gathering information from written and visual text • summarising and restating information • interpreting science in an authentic context • using the contents page, glossary, and index to deepen understanding.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Comparing and contrasting|Determining author's purpose|Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Activating prior knowledge, Using graphic sources, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Even though you don't know it, you may share your house and garden with all kinds of animal neighbours. This book is a fact file of information about some of these creatures, from rove beetles to white-footed mice.
node.esiss_activities: Choose two animals from the book. Draw the perfect habitat for them. Show what the animals eat and where they shelter. What environment suits them? Show a family member. Can they add anything to your picture?
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Connections, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1494 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Skate Jam
node.content: Kate loves skateboarding and is practising for a skate contest. She overcomes teasing from other skaters and last-minute nerves to perform well at the jam. Features of The Skate Jam • Realistic fiction • First-person narrative • Interaction between the characters • Action and dialogue Purpose The Skate Jam can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • identifying the sequence of events • analysing the theme • drawing conclusions from the from the text • identifying and analysing characters' emotions
node.theme: Challenge|Character education|Invention|Leisure and recreation|Science and technology
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Making predictions, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Kate likes to skateboard, but is she good enough to enter a skate jam? She hasn't got the greatest gear, and the kids at the park are pretty scary. But on the day of the contest, Kate surprises herself.
node.esiss_activities: Write a recount of a time when you worked really hard at something and felt proud of yourself. What challenges did you face?
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Tug Of War
node.content: "Tug of War"can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • forming and supporting opinions • making, confirming, and revising predictions • making inferences • making personal connections with the text. Features of "Tug of War': • narrative • humor • double meaning of the title
node.strategies: Making Predictions|Making Inferences|Questioning
node.esiss_strategy: Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Questioning
node.esiss_blurb: When Dad decides to become a vegetarian, his family becomes tired of their new diet. Things change when Dad helps out at the school fair.
node.esiss_activities: Make a story map showing the main events in the story Research vegetarianism and present their findings to the class _x000D_ Have a class debate about the pros and cons of being a vegetarian Create a vegetarian recipe book
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: City Lights
node.content: Tor is visiting his aunt in the city. He discovers that city life is completely different from his home in the desert. Features of City Lights • Third-person narrative • Dialogue • Interaction between the characters • Sequence of events • Descriptive language Purpose City Lights can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • discussing the importance of sequence in a narrative • making personal connections with the text • exploring dialogue • responding to the text
node.strategies: Determining importance|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Exploring text structure, Determining importance, Making connections,
node.esiss_blurb: When Tom's dad goes to an astronomy conference in the city, Tom goes with him and spends the day with his aunt. He misses being able to see the stars in the clear sky. And he's doesn't like the crowds in the city either.
node.esiss_activities: Compare the desert with a city. Use the text and pictures in the story to help you. You could organise your ideas on a PMI (Plus, Minus, Interesting) chart. Now use the information to describe where you would prefer to live and why. Show your writing to your family. Where would they prefer to live?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Expect the Unexpected
node.content: Writer and adventurer John Bonallack travels to Papua New Guinea to help write and publish books for a local school. One trip is more of an adventure then he expected! Features of Expect the Unexpected • Recount • Captioned photographs • Maps • Fact box • Contents page, glossary, index Purpose Expect the Unexpected can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • finding information in photographs and maps • analysing the theme • summarising and restating information • identifying the problems and solutions in a text • making personal connections with the text
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Forming opinions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Analysing themes, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Travel with adventurer John Bonallack to Papua New Guinea where he meets the locals and finds out how problems are solved in this wild, mountainous country.
node.esiss_activities: John Bonallack had some interesting adventures in Papua New Guinea. One of them was unexpected and scary. John stayed calm by thinking about the journey home. Write a list of tips for people travelling in Papua New Guinea. What do they need to watch out for? How can they keep safe? Ask someone in your family to read your list and add some more ideas.
node.wushka_comprehension: Visualising, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: No Safe Place
node.content: A space shuttle lands on planet Thork, looking for a spacecraft that crash-landed four days earlier. The crew encounters many strange creatures and environments in their search for the missing craft. Features of the Text • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Futuristic setting and illustrations • Interaction between the characters • Direct speech • Science-fiction vocabulary and conventions Purpose No Safe Place can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • analysing the features of science-fiction writing • exploring the narrative structure and character development • developing a sequence of events • summarising and restating information.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Discussing point of view|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Discussing point of view, Comparing and contrasting, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: When Sashi lands the shuttle on the planet Thork, everything seems calm and peaceful. It looks as though searching for the crashed spaceship will be easy. Things change when a vrox attacks them. How have the animals on the planet changed so quickly? And where are their missing friends?
node.esiss_activities: The author used his imagination to create some interesting creatures in the story. Use your imagination to create an imaginary animal. Draw the animal and describe its behaviour. Is it dangerous or harmless? Explain how the animal's features help it survive.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1494 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Racing with the Sun
node.content: A group of engineering students takes up the challenge to build a solar-powered car. Working together they plan design and build the car then take part in a race from Chicago to Los Angeles. Features of Racing with the Sun • Specialised vocabulary - chassis, solar cells, carbon fiber • Fact boxes • Quotes • Timeline • Map • Diagrams and photographs • Footnote Purpose Racing with the Sun can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using a glossary and an index to build understanding • summarising and restating information • reading and interpreting timelines, maps, and diagrams text
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine racing down the highway in a car powered only by the sun, all the way from Los Angeles to Chicago. That's the challenge a team of engineers set themselves. Read about designing, building, and racing The Monsoon.
node.esiss_activities: Draw a picture of the Monsoon solar car and a picture of a regular car. What is the same? What is different? Use a Venn diagram to organise your information. Label the features of each car.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion,
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Stage Fright
node.content: In Stage Fright, a young girl is overcome by nerves when she has to read aloud in class, but she finds a way of coping with her problem. Features of the Text • Third-person narrative • Realistic fiction • Character development • Direct speech • Interaction between characters Purpose Stage Fright can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring narrative structure and character development • identifying the problems and solutions in a narrative • making predictions.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making connections|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Drawing conclusions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Min has trouble with words. Standing up and reading her poem in front of the class gives her the jitters. Her skill at drawing and a special gift from her grandfather help her through her fears.
node.esiss_activities: Min was worried about reading aloud to the class. List the things the characters did that made Min more worried. List the things people did to help her feel better. Write two things you learnt from this story about helping your friends.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Heather's Story
node.content: Heather's life changed forever when she was badly burnt in a fire. The story and photographs recount her recovery and show her amazing attitude towards life. Features of the Text • Biography • Report • Photographs and illustrations • Contents page, glossary, index • Headings • Fact boxes Purpose Heather's Story can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information from the text and graphic features • making personal connections with the text.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Heather was badly burnt as a child, and spent much of her childhood in the hospital. When she left school, she trained as an accountant. Now she is a teacher. Heather shares her amazing journey and explains that how you look isn't as important as who you are.
node.esiss_activities: People often ask Heather questions about her accident. What would you like to ask Heather? Write five questions. Talk about them with your family. What do you think Heather's answers might be?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Summarising, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Lost in the Dark
node.content: When Brent goes on a hiking trip he becomes separated from his group and spends an anxious night lost on the mountain. A search and rescue operation begins and Brent is eventually found tired and hungry but safe. Features of Lost in the Dark • Fact boxes • Timeline and twenty-four-hour clock • Map and key • Personal recount • Newspaper article • Glossary and index Purpose Lost in the Dark can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making personal connections with the text • summarising information from the text and graphic features • exploring the features of a non-fiction text.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Sequencing|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Sequencing, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: When Brent is separated from his friends on a day hike, search and rescue teams spring into action. Find out about the search from two different points of view.
node.esiss_activities: Brent did some clever things when he realised he was lost, such as making a bed from ferns. What else did he do to keep safe? Talk to your family about being lost. Make a plan about what to do if you were lost. Write down your plan, and display it for your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Red Planet
node.content: Scientists think that Mars is the only planet in our solar system on which people might be able to live one day. Spacecraft exploring the planet have sent back valuable information but there are still many problems that need to be solved before people could live there. Features of The Red Planet • Photographs and illustrations • Captions and labels • Fact boxes • Glossary and index • Newspaper articles • Report Purpose • The Red Planet can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • asking questions to deepen understanding • drawing conclusions from the text • gathering information from written and visual text • summarising and restating information
node.strategies: Determining importance|Determining main ideas|Making judgments
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Determining importance, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: People have been fascinated by Mars for thousands of years. The Romans even named the planet after their god of war. Explore the red planet, and discover why scientists think that one day people may be able to live there.
node.esiss_activities: Do you think people could ever live on Mars? Write an argument for your opinion. Be sure to use facts from the text to support your thinking. Show your ideas to your family. Would they join you on a voyage to Mars or would they rather stay on Earth?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Grandma's Gift
node.content: Felipe's grandmother shares a box of family treasures with him which prompts her to tell the story of a small carving she was given by her brother when she was young. Features of Grandma's Gift • Strong relationships between characters • Oral storytelling within a narrative text • Emotive language and descriptions • Theme of the value of sharing memories Purpose Grandma's Gift can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • identifying themes • analysing complex relationships between characters
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Summarising, Forming opinions
node.esiss_blurb: Felipe is disappointed when his grandmother shows him the small carved figure from the shoebox. But after he hears its story, he realises that it has sentimental value.
node.esiss_activities: Felipe's grandma shares a special memory and helps him to understand the past. Find a special object at home - it might have a sad or a happy memory. Draw a picture of the object. Write about what it reminds you of. Share your writing with your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Summarising, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1610 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Balanced Diet
node.content: "A Balanced Diet" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of an explanation • making personal connections with the text • exploring the use of style features in nonfiction • identifying the author's purpose. Features of the text:• explanation • cause and effect • diagrams and illustrations • use of style features - quotation marks, italics, parentheses
node.strategies: Identifying Author's Purpose|Identifying Features of an Explanation|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect, Identifying Author's Purpose, Identifying Features of an Explanation
node.esiss_blurb: This article explains the importance of knowing about the food we eat and how it affects our bodies.
node.esiss_activities: Keep a journal of your food intake for a week Identify the food groups that you need to eat more or less of Using the information in the article explain how this will affect your energy levels or health
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect, Identifying Author's Purpose, Identifying Features of an Explanation
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Man on the Moon
node.content: This title combines a personal memoir with factual information about the 1969 moon landing. Features of Man on the Moon • Memoir • Combination of first-person and third-person narrative • Specialised vocabulary - satellite, sputnik, space industry, command module, lunar module, service module, gravity, communication devices • Fact boxes • General information about growing up in the 1960s Purpose Man on the Moon can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying the features of a memoir • analysing a text to identify the author's point of view For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Read about what it was like to grow up in the 1960s during the time of the first moon landing.
node.esiss_activities: This book describes what life was like in the 1960s. Use the text and photos to list the differences between life then and life now. Think creatively to predict three ways of life that will be different in 50 years' time. Share your predictions with your family. Do they agree with your ideas?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1605 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Go, Buggy!
node.content: Three friends decide to enter the local buggy race. They don't have any tools or mechanical knowledge, but they are able to use problem-solving skills and teamwork to create something original. Features of Go, Buggy! • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Labelled diagram • Specialised vocabulary - axle, terrain, chassis, mag wheels, hacksaw, bull bar, throttle • Narrative procedure • Simile and metaphor • Problem-solving focus Purpose Go, Buggy! can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • comparing and contrasting the actions and motives of characters • revising predictions based on evidence in the text • analysing word meanings
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Exploring writer's craft|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Making inferences, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: When three friends enter a buggy race, they design and build a buggy with a difference. Do they win the race?
node.esiss_activities: The children in the story designed their own buggy. Try designing a buggy. Make a list of the things you would need to build it. Then draw a picture of your buggy. Label its features.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Sailing on Land
node.content: A Blokart is a wind-powered go-cart. This text explores the history, design, and safety features of Blokarts then explains how to sail one. Features of Sailing on Land • Procedural text with photographs and diagrams • Captions • Fact boxes • Math used in an authentic context • specialised vocabulary - axle, chassis, mast, suspension Purpose Sailing on Land can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • paraphrasing information • comparing different text forms • exploring specialised vocabulary • using prior knowledge to support understanding • using the text as a model for writing
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making predictions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Have you heard of a Blokart? Blokarting is a fun sport that combines the speed of go-karting with the feel of sailing.
node.esiss_activities: A blokart is a vehicle that is powered by the wind. Design a vehicle that is powered by the sun, wind, or water. Draw a diagram and label the parts. Write a paragraph to show how your vehicle works.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: I Spy
node.content: Aunt Serena asks Kim to look after her plants while she's away. When the seeds of a rare plant start disappearing, Kim thinks of an innovative way to solve the mystery. Features of I Spy • Mystery genre • First-person narrative • Similes • Procedural text within a narrative Purpose I Spy can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • identifying a problem and its resolution • exploring figurative language
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Exploring text structure, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Kim is taking care of a rare plant for her aunt, but the seeds keep disappearing. She figures out a way to find out who is taking the seeds.
node.esiss_activities: Kim designs a clever invention to solve the mystery of the disappearing seeds. Using the description in the book, draw the invention. Label its parts. Show the invention to someone in your family. Do they understand how it works?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Secrets of the Deep
node.content: In May 2003 a group of scientists set sail to explore the seamounts and ridges between New Zealand and Australia. During the expedition they encountered many problems that required the use of technology and innovative thinking. Features of Secrets of the Deep • Jigsaw-puzzle analogy • Photographs with captions • Maps • Fact boxes • Descriptive language • Specialised vocabulary - sled, SWATH mapping, formalin, seamount Purpose Secrets of the Deep can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • examining the use of descriptive language in non-fiction texts • using prior knowledge to support understanding of complex texts • exploring analogies
node.strategies: Exploring writer's craft|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring writer's craft, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Explore the secrets of the deep sea and find out about the technology scientists use to explore the seafloor and the weird and wonderful creatures that live there.
node.esiss_activities: The scientists described in this book had many problems to solve. List four problems they faced. How did they solve these problems?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Jason and the Golden Fleece
node.content: To avenge the betrayal of his father by his uncle and win back his kingdom, Jason must travel to a distant land to seize the golden fleece. Features of Jason and the Golden Fleece • Recount of a legend • Mythological characters • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Orientation, problem, climax, resolution Purpose Jason and the Golden Fleece can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • analysing the features of hero stories in legends and myths • analysing word meanings
node.strategies: Determining importance|Forming opinions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Forming opinions, Determining importance
node.esiss_blurb: In this retelling of the ancient myth of Jason and the argonauts, Jason faces a series of challenges in his quest to seize the golden fleece.
node.esiss_activities: Summarise the main events in this story. Start with Pelias's instructions to Jason and finish with Jason presenting the fleece to Pelias. Write the events in order to create a timeline of the story.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Forty-Hour Runathon
node.content: Each year the students hold a fundraiser for charity. This title describes their effort to raise $50,000 for World Vision by holding a runathon. Features of Forty-Hour Marathon • Multiple text forms - explanation, procedure, narrative • Timeline and schedule of events • Use of math in an authentic context Purpose Forty-Hour Runathon can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • evaluating information to form an opinion • questioning the text • synthesising information • analysing steps in developing a plan
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Some high school students held a forty-hour runathon to raise money for World Vision. Find out how they planned, promoted, and organised the event.
node.esiss_activities: The students in this story were raising money for World Vision. If you were on the committee, what three ways would you suggest to raise money on the day of the run.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: The Lighthouse People
node.content: Becca's family have lived and worked at the lighthouse all her life. Now there is talk that the lighthouse will be automated and they will have to leave. This realistic narrative examines themes of dealing with change, working together to overcome difficulties, and the importance of family. Features of the Book - Figurative language - similes, - Evocative illustrations, - Emotive language and descriptions, - Comparisons, - Times used as chapter heading. Purpose - The Lighthouse People can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing point of view to form an opinion, - analysing descriptive language, - identifying how science and technology have changed people's lives, - using strategies to make meaning of unknown words. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Using graphic sources, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Becca's family has operated the lighthouse since she was a baby. Now, there's talk that they aren't needed anymore. The night of the big storm may be their last chance to prove that, sometimes, people can be more reliable than technology.
node.esiss_activities: Becca and Tim are sad that they will have to leave their lighthouse home. Think about whether you'd like to live in isolation like the family in the story. Write a list of pros (good things) and cons (bad things) of this lifestyle. Now imagine you are Becca or Tim. Write about whether you would like to keep living at the lighthouse or leave. Explain why.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Hiyomi and the Moon Men
node.content: Hiyomi is curious when she sees the strange-looking men in the forest. They're as tall as the sky, and their faces are paler than the moon. This story describes the Amazon from two different perspectives. Features of the Book • cultural comparisons • descriptive and figurative language • parallel story lines • different text forms - third-person narrative, journal • culturally specific vocabulary - yano, virola Purpose Hiyomi and the Moon Men can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: making inferences; • analysing the writer's craft; • comparing how people from different cultures think about and deal with their environment; • evaluating text structure by comparing text forms and features; • analysing descriptive language.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Following instructions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Following instructions, Sequencing, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Hiyomi was curious when she first sees the strange-looking men in the forest. They wear strange clothing, and their faces are paler than the moon. Who are they - and why have they come?
node.esiss_activities: Only five percent of the world's rainforests are protected. Choose a country from the map on pages 30 and 31. Write a letter to the leader of that country. Clearly state the steps you think he or she should take to protect the rainforests.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: "There She Blows!"
node.content: This text explores the history of whaling, from the traditional practices of the Basque and Inuit people to modern methods. Current conservation efforts to protect whales are also discussed. Features of the Book - Historical information, - Different points of view, - Environmental theme, - Maps. Purpose "There She Blows!" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - exploring fact and opinion - Analysing themes, - examining environmental and global concerns. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Change over time|Different views|Environment|History
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Visualising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Long ago, whaling was a part of everyday life for the Basque and Inuit people. One whale could provide enough food for an entire community. Things changed once whales were hunted for their oil, and today, many species face an uncertain future.
node.esiss_activities: People have different views about the right to hunt whales. What is your opinion about whaling? Write a letter to the Prime Minister explaining your opinion. Be sure to support it with facts.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Visualising, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: From Cane to Crystals
node.content: Millions of tonnes of sugar are produced every year. This text investigates the history of the sugar trade, the reasons why sugar is such a valuable commodity, and the process of sugar production. Features of the Book - Fact boxes, - Explanation, - Different points of view, - Cause-and-effect relationships. Purpose - From Cane to Crystals can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - determining the author's purpose, - analysing text organisation and structure, - evaluating information to form an opinion, - exploring the production, distribution, and consumption of goods. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making inferences|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making inferences, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Millions of tonnes of sugar are produced every year. Why is there such a demand for sugar, and how exactly do sugar cane and sugar beets become those tiny crystals in your sugar bowl?
node.esiss_activities: Draw a flow chart that shows the production of sugar. Begin with the sugar cane growing in the fields and end with sugar in your kitchen. Add labels and captions to describe the process.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: New France
node.content: In 1603, the first settlers from France arrived in North America. Since then, thousands of other French people have made the United States and Canada their home. This text explores the influence of French culture in North America. Features of the Book - Sequenced account of events, - Historical photographs, - Diagrams, maps, and timeline, - Explanation, - Purpose New France can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - exploring ways people create places to reflect their cultural identity, - Drawing conclusions, - analysing point of view, - using strategies to make meaning of unknown words. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Making connections, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: In 1604, the first French settlers arrived in North America. Since that time, thousands of French people have made Canada and the United States their home. They have shared their culture and language in more ways than you might realise.
node.esiss_activities: One of the reasons that settlers went to Arcadia was to trap animals for their fur. There were pros (good things) and cons (bad things) of this way of life. Research one reason why settlers came to your country. Make a chart with the headings, "Pros" and "Cons". Write the good things and the bad things about the settlers' decisions to move to your country.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Harry Houdini - Wonderdog!
node.content: When Joe convinces his father to save a dog from death row at the local pound, everything changes for the family. Joe calls his new pet Harry Houdini after the famous magician and escape artist. Harry is nothing to look at, and his habits leave a lot to be desired, but Joe loves Harry. However, Joe's mum and dad and his sister Lizzie all want to send Harry back to the dog pound. Features of the Book - Different text forms - narrative list, - The humour of the author's descriptions, - Illustrations that complement the text, - The narrator's use of parentheses to give the reader extra information, - Ellipses, italics, and capital letters for effect, - The text in the illustrationPurpose - Harry Houdini - Wonderdog can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on students' prior knowledge, - predicting possible outcomes, - writing persuasive text, - looking at the story's structure, - exploring characters' attitudes and actions. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: I called my dog, Harry Houdini, after the famous escape artist. My dog is an escape artist too, you see. But after he ate my sister's bridesmaid's dress, I'm not sure he'll escape being sent back to the dog pound!
node.esiss_activities: Draw your own picture of Harry. Around the picture, add labels showing Harry's qualities. Colour his crazy qualities one colour and his heroic qualities another colour. Show your picture to your family. Do they agree with Harry's crazy qualities and his heroic qualities?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Dog I Share
node.content: When a new tenant with a dog moves into the ground floor of her apartment block, Becky gets very excited. She can't wait to have a dog to play with as pets are not usually allowed in her building. But the new neighbour isn't very friendly and Becky has to think of a plan. A chance event allows Becky to help Carl the dog's blind owner. Features of the Book - First person point of view, - Additional information in the illustrations, - Speech bubbles that introduce and close the story, - The theme of people with special needs, - Thoughts expressed as pictures, - The humorous style - I almost barked; I waved - not my tail just my arm; I can't let her out of my sight Purpose - The Dog I Share can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using Prior knowledge to make sense of the text, - discussing in detail the issues that arise in a text, - exploring additional information in the illustrations, - determining the author's intention, - analysing characters' motives. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining cause and effect|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Becky lives in an apartment building where no dogs are allowed. When a man moves in to the building, she notices he has a dog. When she discovers the man is blind, Becky has a plan to get a dog after all.
node.esiss_activities: The neighbour's dog is a seeing-eye dog. They are important companions for blind people. Find out what other jobs dogs are trained to do. Make a poster with the heading "Clever Dogs" to show what you find.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Questioning, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Night Lights
node.content: In Night Lights, read about the mysteries of moons, how we might travel to Mars, and the truth about sound in space. Features of the Book - Four non-fiction articles by three different authors, - Varied layout - photographs, fact boxes, and diagrams, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Lists of questions to promote research, - Photographs with captions, - Specialised vocabulary - bacteria, meteorite, gravity Purpose - Night Lights can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using Prior knowledge to help make sense of the text, - identifying the author's intention, - reading aloud with appropriate pace and expression, - investigating questions raised in a text, - researching fables myths and legends, - skim-reading to locate and summarise Determining main ideas
node.strategies: Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: When you take a cruise around the solar system you'll dodge space junk, travel to Mars, explore the mysteries of moons, and find out how noisy it really is in space.
node.esiss_activities: In 1969, Neil Armstrong became the first person to walk on the moon. Imagine you are the first person to set foot on Mars. Reread pages 18 to 25. Write a message to mission control, describing what you see and how you feel.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Mountain Bike Challenge
node.content: This book follows Myra Moller as she trains for and then rides in a 30-kilometre mountain bike race through dense forest and over rocks, streams, and mud, and up and down huge hills. Features of the Book; Labeled diagrams; The cross section of the terrain; Family photographs; Images that suggest motion; Photomontages; Quotes from the subject; The map of the whole course; The author's commentary on the race. Purpose - The Mountain Bike Challenge can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: establishing a purpose for reading a text; discussing the effect of images; analysing the writer's style; interpreting maps and cross sections; presenting information in different formats; recognising and retelling events in sequence. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Monitoring for meaning, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Join Myra Moller as she trains for and rides in a 50-kilometre race through dense forest, over rocks, streams, and mud, and up and down huge hills.
node.esiss_activities: Myra's bike is designed for cross-country racing. Choose three features from the diagram on pages 8 to 9. Write an explanation of why each feature is important for safety. Describe what might happen if the safety feature didn't work.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Creatures of the Reef
node.content: A coral reef is home to thousands of strange and wonderful creatures. This book describes life in the reef and the unusual ways in which these creatures use each other to survive.
