Features of the Book - Non-fiction content, - Specialised vocabulary - plankton, parasite, polyp, algae, - Captions that provide additional information, - Dramatic photographs, - Labelled diagrams, - Fact boxes, - Descriptive language - like an underwater city; like a "fast food outlet"; uses its large front claws to bulldoze sand Purpose - Creatures of the Reef can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using Prior knowledge to make sense of the text, - discussing how photographs assist with comprehension, - reading to become informed, - using text as a model for students' own writing, - locating and summarising information, - creating and using an index. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The coral reef is a vast, rocky hiding place for thousands of fish and other small creatures. This book explains the unusual ways these creatures use each other in order to survive.
node.esiss_activities: Many underwater creatures disguise themselves to hide from predators. Look at the photograph on page 12. Imagine you are a creature living there. Draw how you think you would look if you were invisible to predators. Label each feature of your disguise.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: River Rats
node.content: This action-packed, first-person narrative takes readers on an emotional roller-coaster ride as two girls roll, dip, and drive their way down white-water rapids. Features of the Book - Colloquial language - easy stuff, take a break, hang in there, - The metaphors - river rats, chicken seats, spaghetti rafting, Meat Grinder, - Enlarged text integrated with photographs, - The use of verbs - roll, dip, dive, bounce, race, rock, spin, jam, slide, sweep, whip, - The prologue in italics, - Personification of the raft, the river, and the trees, - The blurred and curved images to create a sense of movement, - Present tense to convey immediate events, - Internal and external dialogue Purpose - River Rats can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring the features of a personal narrative, - discussing the use of figurative language such as metaphors, - identifying the use of personification, - discussing the impact of visual techniques, - exploring the emotions of the characters. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Exploring text structure|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: Come on a rafting trip with Fleur and Laura. Find out how to stay safe on the rapids, and have a fast, furious, fun time!
node.esiss_activities: Rik, the rafting guide, gives clear instructions. He wants everyone to be safe and enjoy the experience. Write a numbered list of Rik's instructions and rules, in the order he gives them. Show them to someone in your family. Do the instructions explain how to raft safely?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Sky's the Limit
node.content: Tony Christiansen talks to Angie Belcher about his remarkable life. His many achievements are all the more remarkable because he lost both of his legs in an accident at an early age. Tony's quiet determination and positive attitude shine through the text and the photographs. Features of the Book - A biography based on an interview, - The inspirational story line, - Photograph-album format, - Labels that provide clues to chapter contents, - The non-linear story line, - Design features - photo corners items such as the hospital tag and medal, - The humour of the main character - life's too short like me! Purpose - The Sky's the Limit can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - identifying the author's intention, - discussing layout and text features, - understanding the emotions of the characters, - creating a time line, - formulating questions for further study, - comparing events in the story with students' own experiences. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Having no legs didn't stop Tony from climbing trees, racing go-carts, or being a surf life-saver. His motto is, it's not what happens to you that counts, it's what you do about it.
node.esiss_activities: Tony Christiansen has achieved some amazing things by having the right attitude and thinking positively. Think of something you can't do but would like to do, such as riding a bicycle or being good at drawing. Write five reasons why you think you can't do this thing. For each reason, write one thing you can do to achieve it.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Sky Rider
node.content: This informational text describes the thrills and excitement of sky surfing with extreme-sports enthusiast, Logan Shipgood. The layout and photographs provide a clear explanation of the process that Logan goes through to learn and perfect his technique. Features of the Book - The combination of fact and opinion, - The layout of photographs to emphasise movement, - Sequences of detailed photographs, - Procedural text, - The use of similes - like a great, green pancake, - Specialised vocabulary in bold type - canopy, pilot chute, altimeter, jumpsuit Purpose Skyrider can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using context clues and prior knowledge to determine meaning, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, - creating a glossary, - exploring the use of figurative language, - writing and following procedural text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: The great thing about sky surfing is that you can see everything. The Earth is spread out below you like a great, green pancake. Ride with Logan Shipgood and find out what surfing the sky waves is really like.
node.esiss_activities: Surfing the sky is an exciting and a dangerous sport. Being prepared is very important. Write a checklist for sky surfers that gives instructions for preparing for a jump and keeping safe during the ride.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Inferences, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Keep Out
node.content: Two boys searching for adventure discover an out-of-bounds building site and begin exploring it on their bikes. The boys encounter some frightening situations, including a trip to the local police station and facing up to angry parents. Features of the Book - Fast-moving action-packed story line, - The theme of breaking the rules and facing consequences, - First person narrative - orientation, problem, climax, resolution, conclusion, - The movement and emotion in the illustrations, - The use of italics for emphasis, - Colloquial language - Brat-boy; You ... are worse than slime; Life's cool Purpose - Keep Out can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating ideas and events in the text to students' own experiences, - Making predictions based on text and illustrations, - exploring the consequences of characters' actions, - discussing the author's intention, - investigating the structure of an adventure narrative, - comparing different authors' treatment of the same concept. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When Bruno and his friend Eddie see a construction site that would be the perfect bike track, they just have to get over the fence, even though the sign says: Keep Out, Our Dog Buries What It Can't Eat.
node.esiss_activities: The boys in this story found out that construction sites are dangerous places. Think about places in your neighbourhood that are dangerous. Make a "Keep Out" poster. Explain each danger and why it's not a safe place to play.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1602 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Curse of Being Pharaoh
node.content: Mrs Herrera the class's regular teacher is away on a course. In her absence the substitute teacher decides that the class programme will be different for a while. The students will "live their learning" as they study the life and times of the Pharaohs. This seems to mean general chaos and little discipline - perfect as far as Matt the class bully is concerned. Features of the Book - The theme of bullying, - First person point of view, - The prologue, - The way the illustrator has interpreted the author's descriptions, - The descriptive writing - He reminded me of a long quivering leaf, - The facts about Egypt and the Pharaohs Purpose - The Curse of Being Pharaoh can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on students' prior knowledge, - making and confirming predictions, - identifying how setting is important to a story's developmentr, - recognising the first person narrative, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: From the beginning of time up until last Monday, Matt was the bully and know-it-all in our class. Karen Kwon was the quiet one at the back of the class who no one remembered to ask to birthday parties. But last Monday, everything changed.
node.esiss_activities: The author of this story describes the characters in an interesting way. She says that the new teacher was" kind of blank looking, with greenish skin". He reminded the narrator of a "long, quivering leaf with a wriggling caterpillar on it". Choose another character from the story. Write a description of him or her using the same kind of interesting language.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Pioneer Days
node.content: Dad volunteers the family to become pioneers for a reality TV show. It doesn't take long for some family members to find the adjustment too difficult. This humorous story explores themes of trust and honesty. Features of the Book - Fiction - First person narrative - Reality TV - Comparisons of past and present ways of life Purpose Pioneer Days can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions; - analysing characters and their relationships; - comparing and evaluating past and present ways of living.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining cause and effect|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Making predictions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: No chocolate, no cell phones, and no electricity. Matthew's dad has entered the family in a reality TV show where they have to be pioneers for two weeks. It turns out to be more difficult than the family imagined.
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Making Predictions, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Rain Forest Rescue
node.content: Lying around in the sticky heat is no fun for Sara, but then she encounters an injured orangutan and its baby. Set in an orangutan rehabilitation centre in Indonesia, this narrative explores environmental issues in a rain forest setting. Features of the Book - Fiction - Exotic setting - Realistic characters - Character change over time - Theme of conservation Purpose Rain Forest Rescue can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making inferences; - visualising; - examining the way people can work together to rebuild damaged ecosystems and save species; - explaining how many species have become extinct because of the actions of people; - understanding the way every ecosystem supports a unique collection of living things.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Exploring text structure, Determining importance, Making connections,
node.esiss_blurb: Sara is staying with her mother at the Orangutan Rehabilitation Centre in Indonesia. She doesn't like orangutans much. Their arms are too long and their eyes are too sad. However, an encounter in the rain forest causes her to think again.
node.esiss_activities: Finding an injured baby orangutan changes the way Sara feels about living at the rehabilitation centre. Divide a piece of paper in half. On one side, write words or phrases that show how Sara felt before she moved to the centre. On the other side, write how she felt at the end of the story. Write a sentence to explain why Sara's feelings changed.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Lighting Up the World
node.content: There are more patents in the name of Thomas Edison than any other inventor. This text examines the life and some of the inventions of this remarkably determined man. Features of the Book - Biography - Quotations - Use of figurative language Purpose Lighting Up the World: A Biography of Thomas Edison can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making inferences; - exploring the use of figurative language; - evaluating information; - identifying the features of a biography; - examining how the work of scientists has benefits for our day-to-day lives; - recognising how people spend their lives working in science; - exploring how people have always had questions about the way the world works.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Making predictions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: You probably know that Thomas Edison invented the first light bulb, but did you know that he also invented the first power plant and microphone? Learn about the amazing achievements of one man and his "invention factory".
node.esiss_activities: Create a timeline for Thomas Edison's life. Start with his birth in 1847. End with his death in 1931. Use the dates in the book to add important events to your timeline.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Making Predictions, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Shooting Hoops
node.content: It may only involve a ball and a basket, but it is one of the most popular games in the world. This text explores the history of basketball and its special relevance to the United States. Features of the Book - Report with explanations - Historical information - Evidence of discrimination in sport Purpose Shooting Hoops can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making connections; - making and checking predictions; - making inferences; - exploring how prejudice has greatly limited the ability of some groups to participate fully in society; - examining the idea that core values, such as fairness and equality, are integral to sports; - recognising that many sports contribute greatly to the country's economy.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Find out how basketball has grown from a high school sport to a multi-billion dollar industry.
node.esiss_activities: Reread the information on page 8 about women in sports. Imagine you lived in the nineteenth century. Write a letter to a basketball coach arguing that women should be allowed to compete in basketball games. Give three reasons why.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Timber!
node.content: Wood is all around us. This book looks at the history of the timber industry and the importance of growing timber in a way that's friendly to the environment if we are to continue using this valuable resource. Features of the Book - Report - Historical information and photographs - Cross-section diagrams Purpose Timber! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - reading for a purpose; - identifying Determining main ideas and summarising information; - exploring specialised vocabulary; - examining how people have used natural resources to meet basic needs; - exploring the way natural resources are used by industries to produce goods for distribution and sale; - investigating how people manage natural resources to ensure continuity of supply.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Discussing point of view|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Making inferences, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: Wood is all around us. Timber is used in many everyday products. Find out about the lumber industry and the importance of growing lumber in a way that's friendly to the environment.
node.esiss_activities: Reread Chapter 2. Imagine you are a lumberjack. Write a letter to your family. Describe the conditions at the camp and the work you are doing. Read the letter to your family. Would they have liked to work in the camp?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Question of Digestion
node.content: Food is vital to how a person feels and functions. This article describes each stage in the process of digestion and includes features on such issues as food allergies and school cafeterias. Key Reading Strategies: • Evaluating Information • Making Connections • Asking Questions. Curriculum Links: • science • mathematics •health.
node.strategies: Making Connections|Questioning |Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Questioning, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_blurb: In this article you will learn about the process of digestion, and how different foods affect digestion and health. The article includes the phrase 'You are what you eat.' Have you heard this expression before? Do you think it's true?
node.esiss_activities: Survey others in the school about the healthy food options they would like to see on a school cafeteria menu. Collect the data on a chart with the heading 'Food Options' in the left column and the heading 'Tally' in the right column. Using the survey results draw up a healthy menu for the school cafeteria to entice others to try the healthier options on offer.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1793 ACELA1780 ACELA1828 ACELT1602 ACELT1603 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: The Solar Sail
node.content: "The Solar Sail" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the features of science fiction writing • making, confirming and revising predictions • discussing the author's style • restating information from the text. Features of the text: • science fiction • orientation, problem, climax, resolution, conclusion • third-person narrative • theme of problem solving and lateral thinking
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Keifer is on board a spaceship carrying titanium plastic from factories on the moon to Earth. With a meteor strike just two hours away, Keifer has to think quickly to save the ship and crew from disaster.
node.esiss_activities: Make a story map to identify the steps in the story Write a letter from Keifer to her father, explaining the events and the thoughts going through her mind Use the text to write a procedural text for fixing the sails.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Finding Main Ideas, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: The Oak Tree Kids
node.content: "The Oak Tree Kids" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: &bullexploring plot development &bullidentifying emotive and persuasive language &bullmaking, confirming and revising predictions &bullusing prior knowledge Features of the text: &bullNarrative &bullDialogue &bullPlot development &bullEnvironmental theme
node.strategies: Making Predictions|Questioning|Recognising Bias and Prejudice|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making Predictions, Questioning, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Terri and some of the local kids protest when they discover that the council is going to cut down the tree that has their tree house in it.
node.esiss_activities: read the dialogue aloud with expression to convey the moods of the characters write a newspaper article explaining the events in the story list ways you can help to protect the environment of your neighbourhood and display your ideas on a poster.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Questioning, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Asli's Story
node.content: The civil war in Somalia forced Asli and her family to flee their home for a very different country. This book explains some of the many changes that Asli faces as she adjusts to life in New Zealand. Features of the Book - Biography based on an interview, - The theme of overcoming difficulties, - Factual information combined with narrative, - Family photos and memorabilia, - Maps and time line, - Specialised vocabulary - mosque, Ramadan, hijab, sorghum, Koran Purpose - Asli's Story can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making and revising predictions, - using graphic sources of information, - discussing the author's intention, - discussing issues raised in the text, - rewriting text from a different point of view, - using the text as a springboard to independent research, - exploring a character's feelings. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Forming opinions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Forming opinions, Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine this. Your peaceful home isn't peaceful any more. You have to leave and travel great distances to find a country where it is safe to live. This is what happened to Asli, a refugee from Somalia.
node.esiss_activities: Asli found it hard to adjust to life in New Zealand. Everything was different. Imagine you are a refugee in Somalia. What things would you find hard? What things would seem strange? Use the information in the book to write a short letter to a friend in New Zealand.
node.wushka_comprehension: Questioning, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Voice of the Glacier and other poems
node.content: These poems explore some of the powerful forces of nature that shape our world. Features of The Voice of the Glacier and other poems • Poetic devices - onomatopoeia, personification, rhyme, rhythm, repetition, alliteration, metaphor, consonance • Poetic forms - free verse, lyric poetry, narrative, different rhyme schemes • Descriptive language Purpose The Voice of the Glacier and other poems can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • identifying factual information in poems • analysing the features of poetry
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Making predictions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: This collection of poems explores how the forces of nature shape our world.
node.esiss_activities: The poems in this collection are about natural forces. Those on pages 4 and 5 are haiku. Write your own haiki about another force described in the collection. There will be three lines in your haiku - the first and third lines will have five syllables. The middle line will have seven syllables. Share your haiku with your family.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Making Predictions, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELA1486 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELT1606 ACELY1690 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703
node.book_illustrator: James Brown/Kirsty Van Rijk/Freya Blackwood/Andrew Burdan

node.title: Breaking News
node.content: The school newspaper at Newtown School is floundering and the editor is trying to encourage her reporters to write new and interesting stories. They all learn their lesson when they ignore a lead to a great news story. Features of Breaking News • Conventions of play scripts • Text format of play scripts • Specialised vocabulary - editor, reporter, angle, contacts, false leads, follow-up story, breaking news • Humour • Two parallel plots Purpose Breaking News can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • analysing characters' points of view • exploring the plot structure of a play • determining the author's purpose
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Asking and answering questions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: In this play, the editor of the school news team is encouraging the editors to find and write interesting news stories. Little do they realise that a breaking story is happening right behind them.
node.esiss_activities: Carol is encouraging her team to write some interesting stories for the school newspaper. Imagine you go to this school and you decide to write about the gorilla. How would you describe the incident for the school paper? Write a news report with a catchy heading and three sentences describing what is happening.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Finding Your Way
node.content: How do you find your way on the ocean? In the desert? In a strange city or forest? Seasoned traveller John Bonallack explains how to navigate successfully in each of these situations. Features of the Book - The use of personal narrative and expository text, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - The background design that supports the theme, - Fact files, - Labelled diagrams, - Specialised vocabulary - GPS, sextant, polestar, hemisphere, navigation Purpose - Finding Your Way can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - comparing narrative with informational writing, - using Prior knowledge to make sense of a text, - exploring the use of procedural text, - understanding the author's intention, - drawing and interpreting maps, - retrieving information and displaying it in a different format. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: When you're in a strange place, you need to know where you're going, and how to get home again. John Bonallack has explored many corners of the world. Here are some of the things he knows about finding your way.
node.esiss_activities: Think of a route you travel often, such as to school each day. Describe the route as a series of directions. Use landmarks to help make the directions clear. Read the directions to a family member. Can he or she work out the destination?
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1491 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: All in a Day's Work
node.content: A photographer for a small-town newspaper talks about her working day. Features of All in a Day's Work • Specialised vocabulary - photo editor, breaking news, press releases, photo library, memory card, card reader, viewfinder • Chronological sequence • First-person narrative in the present tense • Newspaper article Purpose All in a Day's Work can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring and evaluating text organisation • identifying problems and solutions in a text • summarising and restating information
node.theme: Careers|Communication|Different views|Problem solving|School and community|The media
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring writer's craft|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Drawing conclusions, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: For Jenny, a photographer for a local newspaper, no two days at work are ever the same. With so many different stories to cover, will Jenny get the photos she needs?
node.esiss_activities: Jenny covers a number of stories during the day. Choose one of these stories and write a headline and a paragraph for her newspaper. Use the 5W plus 1H questions to organise your information. Read your news story to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1679 ACELY1680

node.title: Flying High
node.content: A young basketball player has to give up his dream of playing professional basketball when he finds out that he has diabetes. His uncle encourages him to "fly high" by making kites instead. Features of Flying High • Fictional first-person recount • Main events occur over a long period of time • Sequence of events • Descriptive language • Factual information about diabetes Purpose Flying High can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the factual information in a fiction text • making personal connections with the text • making predictions • sequencing the main events
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Exploring text structure
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Comparing and contrasting, Distinguishing fact from opinion
node.esiss_blurb: The boy in this story discovers he has diabetes and can no longer play basketball. His uncle tells him there are other things besides basketball and shows him a kite. This simple act sets the author on a life-long journey.
node.esiss_activities: When the narrator found out that he had diabetes, he had to give up playing basketball. But he kept watching basketball and began collecting caps. Think of something you love doing like a sport or a hobby. Write a list of the pros (good things) and the cons (bad things). If you had to give up this hobby, what could you do instead?
node.wushka_comprehension: Compare and Contrast, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1602 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Beating Diabetes
node.content: Until the discovery of insulin in 1922 many people died from diabetes. Since then scientists' knowledge and developments in technology have greatly improved the ways in which the disease is treated and millions of lives have been saved. Features of Beating Diabetes • Captioned photographs and illustrations • Fact boxes • Specialised vocabulary, insulin, syringe, vaccine, blood glucose level • Procedure • Timeline • Labelled diagrams Purpose Beating Diabetes can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • reading to find specific information • reading and interpreting fact boxes and timelines • gathering information from written and visual text • locating the Determining main ideas
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Making predictions|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using graphic sources, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Diabetes is not a new disease. The ancient Egyptians wrote about it thousands of years ago. Read about the history of diabetes and the advances that have been made in its treatment.
node.esiss_activities: Using information from the book, make a flyer to tell people about diabetes. What do people need to know? Decorate the flyer. Show it to your family. Do they have any questions?
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1602 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Volcano Watch
node.content: This text explores the causes and effects of volcanic activity, volcanic hot spots, the ways volcanoes affect people, and the instruments and methods volcanologists use to study volcanic activity. Features of Volcano Watch • Multiple text forms - explanation, interview • Specialised vocabulary - core, mantle, magma, volcanologists, seismometer, tiltmeter, GPS, radiocarbon dating, lahar • Fact boxes • Captioned photographs • Map and legend Purpose Volcano Watch can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • synthesising information from text and visual sources • drawing conclusions based on evidence from the text • asking questions to deepen understanding of a specialised subject • skimming and scanning information
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making judgments|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Volcanoes are like sleeping monsters. Many have been asleep so long that people forget that they are there. Find out about volcanoes and the work volcanologists do.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are reporting on a volcano that has just erupted. Use the book to help you write a news story to describe the event. Write a heading and use the 5W plus 1H (Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How) questions to help you get started. Read the news story to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Japan: Land of Contrasts
node.content: This text reviews the history, resources, culture, and people of Japan by exploring some of its contrasts. Features of the Book - Cultural and generational comparisons, - Chart with complex captions, - Aerial, historical, and action photographs, - Maps requiring interpretation, Purpose Japan: Land of Contrasts can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Comparing and contrasting, - determining importance, - analysing Cause and effect, - exploring how people create places that reflect their culture, For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Japan isn't a large country, but it's home to many millions of people. It has very few natural resources, yet it's one of the wealthiest countries in the world. Japan has a rich history and culture, but the people are always looking towards the furture. Japan is a land of contrasts.
node.esiss_activities: Reread the information on Asimo the robot on page 23. Design your own robot. What things can it do? Draw a picture of it. Label the different parts. Show the robot to your family. Do they think it would a be a good family member?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Move It!
node.content: Shifting things from one place to another can be hard work. This text explores how simple machines such as levers, pulleys, and wheels work and how they help make life easier. Features of the Book - Analogy, - Procedural text, - Explanations of complex concepts, - Technology used to solve problems, - Historical information Purpose - Move It! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - locating and interpreting information, - summarising Determining main ideas, - following instructions, - understanding the position and motion of objects in space. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising, Exploring text structure
node.esiss_blurb: Moving heavy things from one place to another can be hard work. It's a good thing that we have simple machines to help us. Once you've learnt more about levers, pulleys, and wheels, you won't be able to imagine life without them.
node.esiss_activities: Make a three-column chart with the headings, Wheels, Levers, and Pulley. Look around your house for items that have wheels, levers, or pulleys. Record each item in the correct column. Write a paragraph about the items you found and how they make your life easier.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Nelson Mandela: The Fight for Freedom
node.content: Nelson Mandela, the leader of the anti-apartheid movement in South Africa, spent twenty-seven years in prison because of his beliefs. This is the story of his struggle to achieve justice for his people. Features of the Book - Biography, - Character development, - Cultural and historical comparisons, - Quotes, - Historical photographs Purpose - Nelson Mandela - The Fight for Freedom can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Making inferences in order to think critically about a text, - exploring political and cultural influences on laws, people, and beliefs, - interpreting and reorganising information, - using strategies to make meaning of unknown words. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Monitoring for meaning|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Using graphic sources, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: "A democratic and free society ... Is an ideal to live for and to achieve. But if needs be, it is an ideal for which I am prepared to die." Nelson Mandela spent twenty-seven years in prison because of his beliefs. This is the story of his struggle to achieve a better life for his people.
node.esiss_activities: Nelson Mandela lived a difficult but amazing life. Make a timeline of his life from his birth in 1918 to his death in 2013. Along the timeline, show the events that were important in Mandela's life.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1602 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Sounds Like Music
node.content: A large orchestra can have up to a hundred musicians and around twenty different kinds of instruments. Each of these instruments makes its own sound. This book examines how instruments and musicians make music and explores the science of sound. Features of the Book - Comparisons, - Labelled diagram, - Cause and effect, - Use of second-person and third-person voice Purpose - Sounds like Music can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - synthesising information, - sequencing steps in a process, - classifying information, - understanding the science of sound. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Discussing point of view, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: A large orchestra can have up to a hundred musicians and around twenty different kinds of instruments. Each of these instruments makes its own unique sound. Find out how instruments make their sounds - and how musicians make music.
node.esiss_activities: Set yourself a music challenge. Listen to these three kinds of music: • jazz • classical 7bull; pop or rock You can find examples of these pieces of music online. For each kind of music, list the instruments you can hear. Write what kind of instrument it is, for example, percussion, brass, or electric.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Making Connections, Making Predictions, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Lost Sandals
node.content: David is sure that he left his new sandals in the changing room when he changed into his track shoes for long-jump practice. But now they're gone and Rebecca is wearing a pair that looks just like them. David's efforts to get the sandals off Rebecca are complicated and is made more interesting by the rivalry between them in the long jump. Features of the Book - Realistic informal dialogue, - Colloquial language - Fat chance, Miss Know-it-all, - The different perspectives in the illustrations, - The use of simile - Rebecca's legs stretched over the stream like rubber bands, - The facial expressions of the characters, - The twist in the tale, - Verbs - pounded, sailed, snatched, snapped, skidded, glanced, hurtlee Purpose - The Lost Sandals can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using Prior knowledge and experience to make sense of a text, - Making predictions and supporting them with evidence from the text, - identifying everyday synonyms and antonyms, - looking at colloquial language. For more information plese see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: David is sure that Rebecca is wearing his lost sandals. How did she get them, and more importantly, how can he get them back?
node.esiss_activities: David and Rebecca could both jump across the creek. How far can you jump? Create a starting point and measure how far you can leap. Each time, measure the distance and write it down. Now record the distances in a bar graph. Did you get better with each jump?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Knife
node.content: George likes visiting with his grandma because she's always doing interesting things. When he decides that the tiny pocket knife in Grandma's treasure box is just what he needs for a project, he slips it quietly into his pocket. Very soon he wishes he hadn't. Features of the Book - Ten short chapters, - George's internal dialogue and reasoning, - The issues discussed - truth, lies, stealing, - Descriptive language - sharp and slender; a curling pattern; a long string of lies; a metallic gleam, - The use of adverbs - frantically, miserably Purpose - The Knife can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making predictions from text and illustrations, - relating ideas and events in the text to students' own experiences, - analysing characters' feelings and actions, - putting forward an argument, - discussing the author's intention, - comparing texts by the same author, - reviewing the use of adverbs. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: George takes a pocket knife from his grandmother's house. He plans to put it back, but then he discovers a hole in the pocket of his jeans. Where is the knife? How will he tell his grandmother he has lost it?
node.esiss_activities: Grandma describes the treasure box as a "junk box", but each thing has a special story to tell. Think of some objects at home that have special importance. If you're not sure, ask someone in your family to help. Choose one object, and in your own words, write a story about why it's important.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Scary Day
node.content: Sam is not enjoying school much. He stares out of the window while he is trying to write a story but then he has to make some fast, effective decisions on what turns out to be the scariest day of his life. Features of the Book - The style of the illustrations, - The theme of personal growth, - Information about coping in a disaster , - vivid descriptions, - variety of interesting verbs - faded, tugged, wriggled, - The use of simile - like a gunshot; like a giant glass bubble Purpose - The Scary Day can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - reviewing students' Prior knowledge, - looking at how the author describes the setting of the story, - supporting opinions with evidence from the text, - discussing the use of literary devices such as similes, - reviewing the features of procedural text, - discussing how characters change as a result of events in the text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Sam can't find anything interesting to write about. He is so bored. When an earthquake hits, he finds that he has to make some important decisions. Next time he has to write an essay, he has the best topic ever!
node.esiss_activities: Sam is asked to write about the scariest day of his life. He didn't know that the scariest day was about to happen. If you were in Sam's class, what would you write about? Describe the events of that day in your own words.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Lazy Bones Jones
node.content: Laurence Bradley Jones (or Lazy Bones Jones, as his sister calls him) is sick of the rain. It's been falling for days. His sister has spent the time doing puzzles, but L.B. thinks that's boring. So he spoils her fun by hiding the last piece of the puzzle and putting it in place when she leaves the room. If only he'd known what that would lead to. He and his Klutz slip into another world, where L.B. discovers that he does have an imagination. Features of the Book - First person point of view, - Introduction and epilogue in italics, - Text in large bold type for effect, - The unusual structure of the story line, - The use of co-operation to solve a problem, - The way the puzzle connects events in the story, - The hallmarks of the fairy-tale setting - unicorn, ruined, castle, dungeon, girl in the tower, - The idea of the characters meeting again by using another puzzle Purpose - Lazy Bones Jones can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - making links with students' own experience, - discussing the form of narrative fantasy, - reviewing the use of adverbs, - looking at the possessive form of singular nouns, - analysing the story line. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: All his friends call him L.B. but his sister calls him Lazy Bones. When he tries to pay her back by putting the last piece into the jigsaw puzzle, Lazy Bones gets more than he bargained for, including a maiden in a tower and a unicorn!
node.esiss_activities: LB's sister enjoys doing jigsaw puzzles. Look at the shapes of the puzzle pieces. Find a picture that you like. Use theses shapes to turn your picture into a puzzle. Cut out the pieces and challenge someone in the family to put the puzzle back together.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1491 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Saving the Park
node.content: Carl is usually too busy skateboarding to notice what's happening around him until one day he finds out that the council wants to build a new road through the local skateboard park. A meeting in the community hall gives Carl and his friends the opportunity to help save the park for everyone to use Features of the Book - First person point of view, - The energy and movement in the illustrations, - The narrative structure - orientation, problem, development, climax, resolution, conclusion, - The development of the main character, - Colloquial vocabulary - skunk, wise guy, No time, Awesome, - Specialised vocabulary - move, tricky, handplant, boarding, rides the ramp, - The environmental theme Purpose - Saving the Park can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - recognising point of view, - relating ideas in a text to students' own experiences, - exploring the development of characters and the consequences of their actions, - using persuasive language to present an argument, - using research and interview skills. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Carl was usually so busy skateboarding that he didn't have time for anything else. But the meeting about the new road was something he wasn't going to miss. The road was going to go right through the skate park!
node.esiss_activities: Imagine the park in the story is in your neighbourhood. Write a letter to the council explaining why the park needs to be saved. Use persuasive language and back up your opinion with evidence from the story.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Hawaiian Magic
node.content: This non-fiction text explains how plants and animals end up on a newly formed island and the changes they make to survive there. Features of the Book - Labelled diagrams and photographs, - Background textures that support the theme, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Maps, - Alliteration in the sub-headings, - Specialised vocabulary - pollinate, husk, larva Purpose - Hawaiian Magic can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using prior knowledge to make predictions, - exploring the use of adjectives, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, - skim-reading to retrieve information, - displaying information from the text in different ways, - exploring the effectiveness of the design, - discussing and creating labelled diagrams. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Asking and answering questions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: How do plants and animals end up on islands? Why do they change over thousands of years into quite different creatures? Join Rod Morris on the islands of Hawaii, and meet some of the magical plants and animals that live there.
node.esiss_activities: Plants and animals that reach the Hawaiian islands have changed to suit their new environment. Imagine you are an animal from a cold place, and you have washed up on an Hawaiian beach. How would you slowly change to suit the hot, wet environment? Make a list of your features before you arrived and your features after you had changed.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Remembering the Big Quake
node.content: The 1964 Alaskan earthquake killed 115 people and caused damage to the coasts of the United States and Canada. Anne Donaghy, a survivor of the quake, shares her memories of that day with the author, who presents her account as an oral history. Features of the Book - Interview format, - Expository and procedural text, - Historic and family photographs, - Maps, - Fact files, - Specialised vocabulary - aftershocks, debris, tsunami, - Labelled diagrams and photographs, - The use of emotive and Descriptive language Purpose - Remembering the Big Quake can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating events in a story to students' own experiences, - Making predictions from text and photographs, - investigating the language of an interview, - discussing open and closed questions, - planning and conducting an oral history interview, - retrieving and reformatting information, - researching a topic using different sources. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining importance|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Exploring text structure, Determining importance, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: Anne Donaghy was a child living in Alaska when one of the world's biggest earthquakes struck the area. Through this event, the author explains how to record oral history as you share a survivor's memories of this violent natural disaster.
node.esiss_activities: This recount of the earthquake that hit Alaska in 1964 is presented as an oral history. Reread page 8 about planning and carrying out an oral history interview. Choose a family member to interview about an important event. Use the information on page 8 to plan and conduct the interview. Remember to use open questions.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1494 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Bungy 70528
node.content: Adventure photographer Andy Belcher needs some volunteers for a bungy photo shoot, and Samantha, Ben, Rikki, and Gemma agree to give it their best shot. This book describes the safety procedures they undergo and their feelings before, during, and after the jump. Features of the Book - The dramatic cover, - Recount genre, - Design features - wide-angle photographs, layered images, backgrounds of bungy cord Purpose - Bungy 70528 can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - interpreting and analysing characters' actions and feelings, - making links between students' experiences and the information in the text, - supporting interpretations with examples from the text, - discussing how a book contributes to the effectiveness of a book, - researching a topic area for enjoyment. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Samantha and her friends take up the challenge to do a bungy jump for a photo shoot. They are very excited to begin with, but when they are getting ready to jump, they're not so sure.
node.esiss_activities: Bungy jumping is a wild ride on an elastic cord. It takes a lot of courage to stand on the platform and prepare to jump. Write a short poem about how you would be feeling if you were about to jump, and you're waiting for the countdown to begin.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Splatter
node.content: Darryl joins Greg and Sam for the ride of his life - a quad-bike tour that involves a lot of mud! He knows he's in for a tough ride when Greg tells him, Next stop, the water bog! Features of the Book - First person point of view, - Six short chapters, - Colloquial speech - We size it up; This is the ultimate; I am the total mud man!, - Specialised vocabulary - accelerator, hand brake, gears, muffler, handlebar, tank, clutch, exhaust, - The safety procedures, - Rhetorical questions - Hey! Where's Greg taking us!, - Design features - muddy backgrounds, splatters, tyre tracks Purpose - Splatter can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - looking at root words, - supporting predictions with references to the text, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings actions and relationships, - Summarising information in the text, - discussing how design contributes to meaning. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Asking and answering questions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: For Darryl, riding a quad bike is an experience he'll never forget, even if he does end up looking like the total mud man.
node.esiss_activities: The boys in this book go on an amazing adventure on quad bikes. They travel through bush and mud, and help each other out along the way. In twenty words, write a poem describing the boys' journey. Uses your senses to describe what the boys felt, thought, and did. Read the poem to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1500 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Whale Tales
node.content: Naturalist and photographer Kim Westerskov is passionate about whales. In this personal account, he shares his thoughts and feelings as he describes some very special encounters with these animals. Features of the Book - The play on words in the title - tales/tails, - Captions that give extra information, - First person point of view, - The photographic sequence, - Fact summary on the last page, - The special note on page 32, - The combination of fact, feelings, and explanations, - Specialised vocabulary - spy hopping, bowriding, - The use of simile - like I was being hit by a small truck• like a cat plays with a mouse Purpose - Whale Tales can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on students' prior knowledge, - discussing the use of captions, - identifying the author's feelings, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, - using electronic media to research a topic, - locating and recording key ideas, - recording events in sequence. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Whales and dolphins are gentle creatures and fun to be with. Join Kim as he shares some of his exciting encounters with these amazing sea mammals.
node.esiss_activities: Choose a whale from this book. Draw the outline of the whale, using the photographs to help you. Inside the outline, write five facts you've discovered about your whale.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Free Fall
node.content: Kathy Basalaj describes how she came to take part in the 'big one' - one hundred women trying for the world record free-fall jump. This book outlines Kathy's skydiving experiences, the training and preparation for the free-fall jump, and the exhilaration when the judges confirm the new record. Features of the Book - Recount genre, - Fact boxes, - The use of illustrations and photographs, - The information page on clothing and flying positions, - Specialised vocabulary - dirt diving, lead plane, stamp men, free-fall formation, - Colloquial language - Wow! Yeah! OK! Yahoo!, - Sequences of photographs to suggest movement Purpose - Free Fall can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - activating students' prior knowledge, - setting a purpose for reading, - interpreting labelled and captioned diagrams, - discussing the effectiveness of design and the organisation of information, - recalling a sequence of events, - creating a glossary for specialised vocabulary. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Comparing and contrasting, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When one hundred women decide to try for the world free-fall parachute record, Kathy Basalaj is excited to be chosen for the team. Join her as she trains for the big jump. Will they break the record?
node.esiss_activities: The skydivers do a lot of planning and preparation before they attempt the world record. Look at the diagram on page 8. Count the number of people in the formation. Now, design a formation for the team's next jump, with more skydivers taking part. Remember, it has to be symmetrical.
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Compare and Contrast, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1491 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Our Mysterious Aunt
node.content: Lee has travelled to his uncle's place in the country, which is very different from his home in Singapore. He encounters his mysterious Aunt Lynn, who is making strange things in the basement. This story about family connections is told through emails exchanged between Lee and his sister. Features of the Book - Mystery fiction - Email format - Communication between people in different countries - Cultural differences - Factual information Purpose Our Mysterious Aunt can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making and checking predictions; - making inferences; - examining how different groups in society may place different values on cultural practices; - describing the ways in which technology links people and communities; - exploring the use of descriptive language; - understanding how societies around the world are increasingly connected through the use of technology.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Determining cause and effect|Forming opinions|Making judgments
node.esiss_strategy: Determining author's purpose, Determining cause and effect, Forming opinions, Making judgments
node.esiss_blurb: Lee is staying with his Uncle Chen who lives in a creaky house in the middle of nowhere. It's very different from his apartment at home in Singapore. Strangest of all is his Aunt Lynn. What does she do all day in the basement?
node.esiss_activities: Lee's aunt was not very mysterious after all. She had made a statue of the family. Use modelling clay, cardboard, or cloth to make a model of one of the members of your family. Show it to your family. Can they guess who it is?
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Recognising Cause and Effect, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Recyclables
node.content: Jamal decides to save the world when he sees a hermit living a simple lifestyle on TV. He starts a recycling service in his neighbourhood but soon finds out things aren't quite as simple as he thought. This story looks at the value of being organised if you want to make a change. Features of the Book - Realistic fiction - Environmental theme - First person narrative Purpose The Recyclables can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making connections; - exploring figurative language; - examining how our consumer society needs to solve problems to do with the disposal of waste; - exploring how people can make a positive difference in their society.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Asking and answering questions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Analysing themes
node.esiss_blurb: Jamal is going to save the planet. He and his friend Frances come up with the perfect plan: a recycling service. Yet after just one day, things start to go wrong, and it turns out that saving the planet isn't going to be as easy as Jamal thought.
node.esiss_activities: After seeing the hermit on TV, Jamal wants to save the world. But he starts too big when he decides to begin a recycling service, and things don't go to plan. What small everyday changes can you make to save energy, recycle, and use fewer chemicals. Think about your home and your neighbourhood. Write a plan. Show it to your family. Put your plan into action.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Science Fair
node.content: Composting nappies, growing mould, and playing music to beans are Luisa's, Amit's, and Kelly's experiments for the science fair. This humorous narrative explores the scientific method and the value of perseverance when dealing with unexpected setbacks. Features of the Book - Realistic fiction - School and family settings - Scientific information in a fictional story - Application of scientific process Purpose The Science Fair can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making connections; - summarising; - exploring the use of dialogue; - examining how scientists use clear processes to investigate the world; - understanding the way science is used to solve problems that affect people.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Luisa, Amit, and Kelly have decided on their projects for the science fair. Little do they know that science can throw some curve balls and experiments can have a mind of their own.
node.esiss_activities: Kelly and Amit design experiments that take time to show results. Choose one of these experiments and carry it out. The information on pages 30 to 31 will help you. At the end of the experiment, share the findings with your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Money, Money, Money
node.content: Whether bartering goods, exchanging shells, or passing over paper bills, money has always been something that could be touched and handled. This book looks at the history of money and how it is being replaced by plastic cards and online shopping. Features of the Book - Report with explanations - Historical information - Real-life examples - Use of questions in headings Purpose Money, Money, Money can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions; - working out words from context; - examining how people have always traded goods and services, using barter or money; - understanding how the forms of money, and the way it is used, have changed over time.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: They jingle in our pockets and crinkle in our wallets, but coins and bills are being replaced by a new kind of money. It's stored on computers and plastic cards, but will it be better than the money we can see and feel?
node.esiss_activities: Ask a family member if you can look at a five or ten dollar note. Together, look for the fine patterns, the watermark, the security thread, and the serial number. What other security devices could you add to a bank note?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: From Grower to Seller
node.content: "From Grower to Seller" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making inferences; • using the text as a model for writing; • sequencing information; • identifying the organisation and layout of information.Features of the text: • log of events • dates and times • photographs and maps • procedural text • footnote
node.strategies: Making inferences|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making Inferences, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: This article describes the journey that takes place when peony flowers are transported from New Zealand to New York.
node.esiss_activities: Trace the flowers' journey on a world map, showing the times and distances Construct a flow chart showing this process
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: The Rise and Fall of the Incas
node.content: Hundreds of years ago, the Incan Empire was one of the largest in the world. It comprised almost twelve million people who spoke more than twenty different languages. This text explores the reasons for the empire's success and the causes of its demise. Features of the Book - Archeology, - Architectural detail, - Procedural text, - Artist rendering of historical events Purpose - The Rise and Fall of the Incas can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - determining importance, - using analogy to make meaning from unfamiliar words and concepts, - making connections, - examining people's interaction with their environment. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Monitoring for meaning, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Hundreds of years ago, the Incan Empire was one of the largest in the world. It contained almost twelve million people who spoke more than twenty different languages. How did this empire become so strong, and what caused it to crumble only a few centuries later?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Crime Solvers
node.content: Most crime scenes contain clues. This text explores the work of forensic scientists and the tools they use to solve crimes. Features of the Book - Historical references, - Multiple text forms, - Procedural text, - Photographs - crime scene, lab scene, and microscopic images Purpose - Crime Solvers can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - reading for different purposes, - using prior knowledge, - synthesising information, - exploring science as a human endeavour. For more information please see our lesson plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Using graphic sources, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Most crime scenes contain clues. A speck of blood, a single strand of hair, or a fibre from clothing can be enough to convict a suspect. Read about the work of forensic scientists and find out how forensic science was used to solve the case of the missing paintings.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELA1494 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Looking at Light
node.content: This book examines the importance of light, where light comes from, how it travels, and how it provides the source of all colour. Features of the Book - Historical and scientific information, - Specialised vocabulary, - Labelled diagrams and tables, - Scientific theme. Purpose Looking at LIght can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - using a glossary to explore specialised vocabulary, - Synthesising information from text and visual sources, - using text features to locate information, - exploring the properties of light. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Without light, there would be no colour and plants couldn't grow. In fact, there would be no life on Earth at all! How is light created? How fast can it travel? What does light have to do with colour? Find all the answers in this book.
node.esiss_activities: On page 9 of the book, there are instructions on how to make a colour wheel. Follow the instructions to make a colour wheel. Spin your wheel. Do the colours change to white?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Questioning, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Parasites: Nature's Stowaways
node.content: Parasites are uninvited guests that live in or on animals, plants, or people. This text describes different kinds of parasites and their clever survival tactics. Features of the Book - Fact box - Sequence - Labelled diagram - Cause and effect Purpose - Parasites: Nature's Stowaways can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Asking questions, - analysing Text structure, - forming opinions based on evidence from text, - comparing the characteristics of organisms. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Determining author's purpose|Making connections|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Activating prior knowledge, Making predictions, Making inferences, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Parasites are uninvited guests that live in or on animals, plants, or people. What's worse, they give nothing in return! Parasites come in all shapes and sizes, and they have many different ways to survive. Learn about these annoying creatures that some people call "nature's stowaways".
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Recognising Bias and Prejudice
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: All about Arthropods
node.content: Arthropods are creatures that have jointed legs and an exoskeleton. Examples include a scorpion, a grasshopper, and an ant. Arthropods are found just about everywhere - even on the human body! Features of the Book - Nature photography, - Chapter headings that reflect features of reports, - Comparisons, - Unconventional visual supports and charts, - Use of large numbers in both numeric and word form, - Scientific names. Purpose - All about Arthropods can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - interpreting and analysing graphic sources of information, - identifying, analysing, and categorising the characteristics of organisms, - exploring word derivations, - determining importance. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining main ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Determining main ideas, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: What do a scorpion, a barnacle, and an ant have in common? They all have jointed legs and an exoskeleton, and they belong to a group of animals called arthropods. Arthropods are found just about everywhere - there are even some living on you!
node.esiss_activities: Explore your garden and look for arthropods. Make a chart with four columns. In the first column, draw the arthropod. In the second column, write down whether it is an insect, an arachnid, a crustacean, or a myriapod. In the third column, write down how many legs the arthropod has. In the fourth column, write down how it protects itself. Record all the arthropods you find.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Black Gold
node.content: Most of the world's energy comes from crude oil. Finding and selling oil is one of the world's biggest industries. This text examines how oil is formed, processed, and used and why this non-renewable resource is such a precious commodity. Features of the Book - Dates, - numeric and text form, - Historical photographs, - Visual information requiring interpretation, - pie graph, map, diagram, - Comparisons, - Maps Purpose - Black Gold can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing cause and effect, - sequencing, - interpreting graphic information, - exploring types and uses of natural resources. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining author's purpose|Discussing point of view|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Making predictions, Discussing point of view, Determining author's purpose
node.esiss_blurb: Most of the world's energy comes from crude oil. Finding and selling oil is one of the world's biggest inductries. However, the supply of crude oil will soon run out. How will we survive without our precious "black gold"?
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Making Inferences, Making Predictions
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Renaissance
node.content: The Renaissance was a time of discovery. This text examines why so many advances were made in science, art, architecture, and philosophy during this important time. Features of the Book - Art and architecture, - Historical images, - Procedural text, - Science and technology Purpose - The Renaissance can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - Making connections, - recognising cause-and-effect relationships, - sequencing, - classifying information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The Renaissance, which began in Italy during the fourteenth century, was a time when anything seemed possible. Ships sailed off to explore new lands, scientists made many discoveries, and artists created amazing works of art. The world was exploding with new ideas!
node.esiss_activities: During the Renaissance, people discovered, explored, and learnt many new things. Create a timeline of the discoveries in this book. Find the dates and write them in order. Write captions to describe each discovery.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Brave As
node.content: Matt has a lot on his mind - like the speech he has to give in class that day and the strange phone call from Jules the school bully. But as Matt begins to confront and resolve each problem, life doesn't seem so bad after all.
