Features of the Book - The perspectives in the illustrations, - The facial expressions and body language of the characters, - Matt's "built-in picture screen", - The special nature of Matt's speech problem, - The different ideas of bravery, - Thought bubbles, - The humour of the author's style, - Descriptive language - A tide of red flooded his skin; My body and mind were unplugged Purpose - Brave As can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating ideas and events to students' own experiences, - predicting possible outcomes, - clarifying complex concepts, - describing the mental images evoked by the text, - identifying and discussing inflected forms of verbs, - comparing different texts by the same author. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Matt thinks making a speech is the most frightening thing he'll ever have to do. But far worse things are going to happen. It all starts when his mother kisses him goodbye in front of Sarah Bernstein.
node.esiss_activities: Matt is nervous about giving a speech. Imagine you could give him some advice. Write five bullet points that will help him deliver a good speech and not feel nervous.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Interrupting the Big Sleep
node.content: A boy and a rattlesnake are both looking for something - the snake for a place to spend the winter months; the boy for something to relieve the boredom of staying in a remote place with a remote uncle. In the end, it's not just the rattlesnake's sleep that has been interrupted. Features of the Book - The realistic illustrations with unusual perspectives, - Poetic language in short concise sentences, - Two points of view (first person for the snake, third person for the boy), - The way the boy's actions mirror those of the snake, - Specialised vocabulary - cholla, creosote, canyon, saguaro, diamondback, venom, fangs, - Colloquial language - Where're you going? Purpose - Interrupting the Big Sleep can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring specific word meanings, - discussing the author's craft, - making comparisons between characters, - recognising that authors organise text in different ways for different purposes, - describing literary devices such as imagery and symbolism, - using the text as a springboard to further research. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The boy is bored. He's staying with his uncle on a farm where there's nothing but cactuses and space. Or so it seems. When the boy opens the barn door he finds a car like no other, but also something quick and deadly!
node.esiss_activities: In Chapter 5, the snake explains how it ended up in the Cadillac. Write a similar description from the tarantula's point of view, explaining why it was in the car. Try to imagine what the spider would see and feel, and how it would move.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1491 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Caught in a Flash
node.content: This book explains how photographer Nic Bishop came to develop a way of "freezing" the actions of animals and insects - movements that happen too quickly for us to see. The final result is breathtaking. We see crisp images of insects caught in mid flight and a flying squirrel testing its "wings" inside the photographer's house. Features of the Book - The double meaning in the title, - Sequences of images to suggest movement, - The image of the flash on the cover and title page, - The diagram and the accompanying caption, - The variety of text styles - personal narrative, procedural, expository, - The clear example of problem solving, - Design features - using circles and rectangles, - Specialised vocabulary - flashgun, shutter, laser Purpose - Caught in a Flash can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using photographs to aid prediction, - following the problem-solving process, - interpreting labelled diagrams, - reading and writing procedural text, - distinguishing between fact and opinion, - using the text as a springboard to further research. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Problem solving|Science and technology
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever wondered what happens when a raindrop hits a puddle, or how a bird or beetle takes off? These things happen too quickly for us to see them, but Nic Bishop has a special camera that can capture these lightning-fast events.
node.esiss_activities: Nic Bishop uses rapid flash photography to capture the movement of animals. Look at the photograph of the ladybird on the cover of the book. It is three photographs that have been made into one. Draw a combined picture of your own, using an animal such as a bird or a bat.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Soldier
node.content: Book Summary Life is hard for Will and his mother during the Civil War. Then the war comes closer to Will when he discovers a deserter hiding in the barn. This narrative, told through Will's journal entries, looks at what life was like for the people away from the battlefield. Features of the Book - Historical fiction - Preface with factual information - Fictional journal entries Purpose The Soldier can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying an author's purpose and point of view; - evaluating and responding to literature; - exploring how the path to a democratic society has involved situations that have divided communities and families.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising, Activating prior knowledge, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: The Civil War is raging, and Will's dad is away fighting. Life is hard for Will and his mother, and then he discovers something in the barn that brings the war much closer.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are the deserter in the story. Write a diary entry for one of the days that the deserter is hiding in the barn. Use the book to help you. How do you think he feels about being a deserter and being in hiding? What is his view of the war? How do you think he feels about Joe helping him? Read the diary entry to someone in your family. "
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Into the Unknown
node.content: To the early colonists, the western region of North America was an unknown land. In 1803, the president appointed Lewis and Clark to lead a team to explore this area. This book follows Lewis and Clark's amazing expedition. Features of the Book - Historical recount of exploration - Maps and photographs - Labeled diagram - Interactions between explorers and Native Americans - Timeline Purpose Into the Unknown: The Lewis and Clark Expedition can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - visualising; - evaluating information; - examining how early explorers helped to open up new land for settlement, determined new trade routes, and found new natural resources; - analysing how the nation we know today owes much to the endeavours of early explorers.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Synthesising|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Synthesising, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: In 1804, Lewis and Clark set off across the United States through territory that had never been seen by European settlers. Learn about their amazing expedition.
node.esiss_activities: On page 19 of the book, there is a labelled illustration of an explorer's equipment. Imagine you are going on the same expedition today. How would the equipment be different? Draw another labelled illustration to show what you would wear and take.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: A Safe Place
node.content: "A Safe Place" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: &bullusing prior knowledge and the context and glossary to make meaning &bullmaking inferences &bullvisualising to make meaning &bullforming explanations Features of the text: &bullReport &bullSpecialised vocabulary &bullDescription &bullGlossary words
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Changing earth|Environment|Habitats|Life science|Plants
node.strategies: Making Inferences|Making Connections|Visualising|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making Inferences, Making Connections, Visualising, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: This report describes a city wildlife sanctuary and explains why and how it was established.
node.esiss_activities: make a list of the positive things about sanctuaries research native plants or animals from their area if possible, visit a sanctuary collect drawings and photos of introduced and native plants and animals and present them to theclass write a procedural text about creating a city wildlife sanctuary.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Inferences, Making Connections, Visualising, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1479 ACELT1594 ACELT1596 ACELT1599 ACELT1600 ACELY1678 ACELY1679 ACELY1680 ACELA1489 ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692

node.title: Everyday Science
node.content: From cooking an egg to turning on the light, the impact of science can be seen all around us. This text examines how science doesn't always take place in laboratories and test tubes. Features of the Book - Scientific explanations - Examples from daily life - Diagrams and photographs - Causes and effects Purpose Everyday Science can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions; - making, revising, and confirming predictions; - exploring how science can explain many everyday events; - analysing scientific processes and reactions; - recognising that scientific inquiry has led to the invention of many new technologies.
node.theme: Physical science|Problem solving|Science and technology|Scientific investigation
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting|Exploring text structure|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing, Asking and answering questions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Science is all around us, even in the kitchen. This book will tell you about electrons in lemons, bacteria that eat sweat, and waves in the kitchen!
node.esiss_activities: Try doing the lemon battery experiment on page 21. Does it give your tongue a zap? Or you could test the idea on page 8. Add some detergent to the water and oil. Does the oil still float on the surface?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1692 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Leave Only Footprints
node.content: Going on vacation is great, but tourism can have a big impact on the environment. This text looks at eco-tourism, which allows people to have a great vacation and respect the environment at the same time. Features of the Book - Report with explanations - Problems and solutions - Case study - Maps and diagrams - Controversial issues Purpose Leave Only Footprints can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - evaluating information and forming opinions; - identifying the author's point of view; - analysing cause and effect; - understanding how people's leisure activities can have an adverse effect on wildlife and the environment; - examining how individuals have a responsibility to act in ways that don't damage environments or harm species; - analysing how communities and individuals can work together to preserve natural treasures and heritage sites.
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing fact from opinion, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: It's fun to go on holiday, but what about the effect on the environment? This book is a good guide to taking an eco-friendly holiday.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Saving Sector 419
node.content: A dangerous virus has taken hold in Michelle's hometown, and she is asked to help with an evacuation so the town can be decontaminated. This futuristic story shows Michelle's dilemma as she has to choose whether to lie to her neighbours in order to help them. Features of the Book - Fiction - Problem solving - Theme of problems that can be caused by scientific experimentation - Scientific information Purpose Saving Sector 419 can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - evaluating ideas; - making predictions; - analysing a character; - exploring how people have learnt to alter genes to change how a plant or creature looks or behaves; - understanding that each organism carries in its genes a set of instructions for how it looks and behaves; - examining how altering an organism's genes can have unforeseen effects.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Determining cause and effect|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: It's 2.30 a.m., and there's a knock on the door of Michelle's dormitory at the Performing Arts School. Her friends and neighbours are in danger, and she's the only one who can save them. Will she be able to use her acting skills to save her hometown?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine if Professor Patton's parasite had escaped from Michelle's hometown. What long-term effects would it have had? Write a list of pros and cons that show the effects the parasite would have on animals, people, and daily life.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Compare and Contrast
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Miracle Man
node.content: Todd is a talented dirt-bike racer. When Todd realizes that his father won't allow him to ride a more powerful bike, Todd decides to enter a race without telling him and narrowly avoids making a worse mistake. Key Reading Strategies: • Making Connections • Summarizing and note taking. Curriculum Links: • science • technology
node.strategies: Making Connections|Summarising|Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Summarising, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: In this story Todd decides to enter a motercycle race against his father's wishes.
node.esiss_activities: Have a debate with your fellow students over whether Todd should have ridden in the motorcycle race, against his father's wishes.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Eruption of Mount Pelée
node.content: In this fictional recount, which is based on a true story, a survivor of the Mount Pelée eruption describes her experiences in dramatic detail. The Eruption of Mount Pelée can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the writer’s craft • drawing conclusions from the text • questioning the text. Features of the text: • First-person narrative • Sequenced account of events • Historical information • Descriptive writing
node.strategies: Sequencing|Making Inferences|Questioning|Drawing Conclusions
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making Inferences, Questioning, Drawing Conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: The Eruption of Mount Pelée was a natural disaster. Have you experienced or heard about any natural disasters where you live?
node.esiss_activities: Write some interview questions you would like to ask Havivra.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Lost
node.content: Damon and his friends go hiking in the wilderness, but conditions aren't as friendly as they had prepared for. This fictional narrative follows the group of friends as a series of bad decisions cause them to wonder if they will survive their adventure. Features of the Book - Narrative fiction - Plot with setting, characters, problem, and resolution - Told in the third person - Use of an analogy - Causes and effects - Theme of safety and survival - Factual information Purpose Lost can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying the features of narrative structure; - identifying and using an analogy to help follow the plot; - exploring character traits; - discussing procedures for safe outdoor activity and how the body responds to extremes in temperature.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: Damon and his friends set off into the wilderness to have fun, but conditions aren't as good as they had expected. As their plans disappear into the whirling mist, the question of survival comes into focus. How many chances do the friends have before their luck runs out?
node.esiss_activities: The children made a number of decisions as they tried to find their way out of the forest. Create a timeline that maps these decisions and the consequences of each one. Colour the good decisions green and the bad decisions red.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Blackout
node.content: When Lucia moves from a small town to a city, she misses her friends, especially on her birthday. That evening, as she is getting ready to go out with her parents, the power goes off. As her family struggle to cope with the blackout, she finds out that city life isn't at all what she'd imagined. Features of the Book - Realistic fiction - Plot, characters, setting - Theme of change and adjustment - First person narration Purpose Blackout can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making inferences; - evaluating a character's point of view; - comparing and contrasting aspects of a story; - discussing the world's dependence on electricity and the effects of disruptions to power supply.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Lucia can't bear the thought of going to live in a tiny apartment. It will be boring and crowded and she'll miss her friends - especially on her birthday. But city life turns out to be nothing like Lucia expected.
node.esiss_activities: Use the information on page 32 to make a poster that shows how to keep safe during a blackout. for your house. Describe the five main things to do if the power goes off at your place. Write each action as a short, clear sentence so it's easy to remember. Add an icon beside each action. Hang your poster in the kitchen.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Our Own War
node.content: A young girl recounts in her diary the struggles of day-to-day life during the influenza epidemic of 1918. Features of the Book - Fictional diary - Preface with factual information - Historical photographs and newspaper cuttings - Causes and effects Purpose Our Own War: The Diary of Sophie Graham can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - determining the differences between fact and fiction; - making inferences; - exploring the author's purpose; - making connections; - discussing how pandemics spread and affect populations.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Discussing point of view|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Discussing point of view, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: The streets are empty. The school has closed, and no one knows when it will reopen. Will things ever return to normal? With uncertainty and courage, a young girl recounts the struggles of day-to-day life during the influenza epidemic of 1918.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are an influenza virus. Use the information on page 29 to write an explanation of how you work. Why are you so effective at spreading inflenza?
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.wushka_text_type: Historical Fiction, Diary, Journal or Log
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Discovering Vinland
node.content: This book explores how the Vikings became great explorers and traders throughout Europe. Although they struck fear into the hearts of many people, they also brought new ideas and technologies with them. They were also the first Europeans to settle in North America. Features of the Book - Report with recounts and explanations - Historical information - Retelling of a saga - Illustrations, maps, and labelled drawing - Table of contents, glossary, and index Purpose Discovering Vinland can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - activating prior knowledge; - discussing how the actions of early explorers affected different cultures; - identifying the sequence of events; - identifying cause-and-effect structures.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: The Vikings seemed a fearless people. They made dangerous journeys to win honour and adventure. They established their ways of life in new lands. How did the Vikings do it - and why did they believe that unknown lands held such promise?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are on the crew that sets sail with Leif Eriksson. Write a description of what you see when you reach Baffin Island and Labrador. How are these lands different to the icy place you have sailed from?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Break-in
node.content: A break-in occurs at Mei Li's school, and suddenly, she finds herself accused. This mystery story explores Mei Li's fight to prove her innocence, and the real culprit is revealed to everyone's surprise. Features of the Book - Realistic fiction - Mystery to be solved - Surprise ending - Need to infer meaning - Inclusion of factual information Purpose The Break-in can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing the structure of a narrative; - making, revising, and confirming predictions; - summarising information; - analysing a character; - exploring word meanings and derivations; - exploring how new developments in forensic science are used in the fight against crime.
node.theme: Character education|Problem solving|School and community|Scientific investigation
node.strategies: Synthesising|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Mei Li is innocent - so why doesn't anyone believe that she didn't break into the school? She needs solid proof to convince her teachers. Mei Li never backs down from a challenge, but this time she has to call on an expert for help.
node.esiss_activities: Everyone is convinced that Mei Li broke into the school. Imagine you are a lawyer defending Mei Li. Write a short speech that shows why the evidence the police gathered didn't prove that she was guilty.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Fight for a New Nation
node.content: It began as a small group of colonists and developed into a stable, independent nation. This book follows the early history of the United States, from the first calls for independence to the drafting of the Constitution. The Fight for a New Nation can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • making connections; • identifying main ideas; • exploring how the views of groups and individuals help to shape governments; • analysing how governments and power structures develop and change over time. Features of the Book: • Report with explanations • Recount • Historical photographs • Maps
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making Connections|Sequencing|Recognising Bias and Prejudice|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections, Sequencing, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: On April 18, 1775, the first shots of the Revolutionary War were fired in Massachusetts. What led the colonists to stand up to their British rulers and fight for independence?
node.esiss_activities: Find out how a group named in the text influenced the growing nation. These include the patriots in Massachusetts, the Daughters of Liberty, slaves, the Federal Government of 1789, Native Americans, and African Americans.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas|Making Connections|Sequencing|Recognising Bias and Prejudice|Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELA 1829 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Velociraptor
node.content: Jack's uncle is a cryptozoologist who studies creatures that people think don't exist. When Jack and his friends discover that one of Uncle John's valuable specimens has gone missing, they must figure out what has happened to it before a disaster happens. Features of the Book - Narrative fiction with plot, characters, and setting - Problem solving - Use of dialogue and humour - Factual information Purpose Velociraptor can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making, revising, and confirming predictions; - identifying the structure of a narrative; - exploring how scientists study rare and extinct creatures; - developing a character study; - examining the use of dialogue in a narrative; - analysing the use of humour in a narrative.
node.strategies: Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: When Jack and his friends discover that one of Uncle John's valuable specimens has gone missing, they imagine the worst. It's up to them to stop a disaster from happening.
node.esiss_activities: Reread page 29. Choose a cryptid to research such as Bigfoot. Make notes about the cryptid. Use your notes to draw a labelled diagram of the creature showing its features. Show the diagram to your family. Do they think this creature exists?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Super Structures
node.content: Bridges, dams, and pyramids are just some of the structures built by people. This book examines the amazing structures people have built, from the distant past to the present day. Super Structures can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • analysing and synthesising; • summarising; • exploring how people have designed and built large structures since prehistoric times; • examining how people have invented new technologies to help build large structures. Features of the Book: • Report • Historical information • Technical explanations • Problems and solutions
node.strategies: Summarising|Compare and contrast|Finding Main Ideas|Making Connections
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Compare and contrast, Finding Main Ideas, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: This report explores how people have designed and built large structures since prehistoric times. Why do you think people build such huge structures?
node.esiss_activities: Carry out further research into one or more of the structures described in the book. You could research the methods used, the challenges and solutions, the size of the structures, and their age. Alternatively, you could construct scale model of one of the structures.
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELA 1829 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Plate Power
node.content: This explanation describes tectonic plates and how they affect Earth's surface. Plate Power can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • restating information • analysing cause and effect • drawing conclusions based on evidence from the text • summarising information. Features of the text: • Labelled diagrams • Fact boxes • Map • Specialised vocabulary
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect|Making Inferences|Finding Main Ideas|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: In this book we learn about tectonic plates. Do you know which tectonic plate you live on?
node.esiss_activities: Research Mount Everest and add any new information to the fact file on page 5.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELA 1829 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: The Human Machine
node.content: The human body is made up of many parts, just like a machine. This text examines the main parts of the human body and how they work together to keep us fit and healthy. The Human Machine can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • identifying the author's purpose and point of view; • using figurative language to make meaning; • making connections; • exploring how the human body is made up of many parts, each with a specific function; • recognising how some parts of the body work together in systems, for example, the heart and lungs. Features of the Book: • Extended metaphor (body as a machine) • Explanation • Diagrams and captions
node.strategies: Identifying the author's purpose and point of view|Using figurative language to make meaning|Making Connections|Making Inferences|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Identifying the author's purpose, Using figurative language to make meaning, Making connections, Making inferences, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: This book describes how the human body is made up of many parts, each with a specific function, just like a machine. Learn about the the Control Centre, the Nuts and Bolts and the Framework that holds it all together!
node.esiss_activities: Write about a significant event that involved your body. This could include a broken limb, surgery, an injury, working out to increase fitness, or needing glasses or hearing aids. Refer to the text and use any relevant information from the text in your writing.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELA 1829 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Twin Trouble
node.content: Rosa and Maria are mirror-image identical twins. They decide to swap for a day to prove that they can be the same. After some difficult situations, they trick almost everyone. This narrative explores the humorous outcomes of their switch. Twin Trouble can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • making inferences; • making and checking predictions; • recognising how each organism carries in its genes a set of instructions for how it looks and behaves; • understanding that no two people are exactly the same - even identical twins are different in many ways, for example, they have different fingerprints. Features of the Book: • Realistic fiction • School and family settings • Scientific information in a fictional story • Everyday exploration of genetics.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Making inferences|Finding main ideas
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making inferences, Finding main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Identical mirror-image twins Rosa and Maria decide to switch for the day. What could go wrong? Can you make any predictions?
node.esiss_activities: Reread page 32 about Gregor Mendel then research other examples of how genetic information is passed from parents to children. For example, some families may have photographs that show strong resemblances being passed down through generations. You may have pets that that have passed features on to their offspring. Record your findings.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Inferences, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1504 ACELA1505 ACELA1797 ACELT1608 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1698 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Behind the Scenes
node.content: Carla has just starred in a blockbuster movie, but her success has a big downside when her schoolmates start reacting negatively. It all becomes worse when she is asked to be in the school play. Features of Behind the Scenes • Realistic fiction • First-person narrative • Direct speech • Narrative text that includes orientation, problem, climax, and resolution Purpose Behind the Scenes can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making inferences to identify character traits • drawing conclusions
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Summarising, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: Carla gets a hard time from her schoolmates after she stars in a blockbuster movie. It gets worse when she is asked to act in the school play.
node.esiss_activities: Carla is a successful actress, but being in the school play turns her into a nervous wreck. Imagine you are Carla. Write a diary entry for the day before the play explaining why you are so worried. Read your diary entry to a family member. Does he or she understand why Carla is nervous?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Shooting for the Stars
node.content: This report provides a brief history of acting. It explains how to become an actor and it describes some of the skills an actor needs. It also profiles Deanna Lake a young actor who describes her experiences and daily life. Features of Shooting for the Stars • Specialised vocabulary • Factual report with explanations and a personal story • Introduction and ending written in second person • Fact boxes and dialogue boxes • Photographs with captions in the form of direct quotes Purpose Shooting for the Stars can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the features of a report • exploring specialised vocabulary • evaluating information
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Meet fourteen-year-old Deanna Lake. She has been dancing since she was five and is already acting in a TV show. Deanna explains the pleasures and pitfalls of juggling school and working on the show.
node.esiss_activities: Make a mind map about being a child actor. Use the information in the book to help you. Then use your ideas to write a flyer with tips for children who want to be an actor. Include the pros and cons of this career choice.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Snake Country
node.content: When Kim goes off the trail on a class field trip she gets bitten by a snake. Luckily for her Natalie knows about snakes and can identify if the snake that bit her is venomous. Features of Snake Country • Narrative with orientation, problem, climax, and resolution • Setting of class field trip to a national park • Factual, scientific information Purpose Snake Country can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • exploring the relationships between characters • making and confirming or revising predictions • exploring factual information in a fiction text
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Activating prior knowledge
node.esiss_blurb: Natalie, the new girl in class, arrives just in time for a field trip to a national park. Everyone thinks the trip will be boring, but Natalie and her new friend are excited. They love snakes. When Kim gets bitten by a snake, it's just as well Natalie knows what kind of snake is the culprit!
node.esiss_activities: Poisonous snakes use venom to protect themselves against predators and to kill prey. Choose another animal that uses venom in this way. Research the animal and draw a picture of it. Add labels to show the animal's features and where the venom is produced.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Ed's Bull
node.content: "Ed's Bull" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • visualising to make meaning • determining the author's purpose • exploring descriptive language • forming and justifying opinions about characters' actions. Features of the text: • Realistic fiction • Character development • Action verbs • Descriptive language • Problem solving
node.theme: Art and activity|Challenge|Discovery|History|Leisure and recreation|Problem solving|The arts
node.strategies: Distinguishing fact from opinion|Finding Main Ideas|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Ed would rather be watching TV than cleaning the basement, but he soon discovers creative possibilities among the junk and cobwebs.
node.esiss_activities: identify words or phrases that help you form a picture of the basement use descriptive words to write a poem about a dark, scary place or a story about a world without TV make a model from recycled materials and write instructions for it
node.wushka_comprehension: Visualising, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Classified!
node.content: There are millions of different species of plants and animals on Earth. This text explains why systems of classification are developed and reports on the system that we use today. Features of Classified • Report with an explanation • Specialised vocabulary - Latin, naturalists, infection, complex, cells, absorbing, database, philosopher • Photographs and graphics • Fact boxes Purpose Classified! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • interpreting graphic sources of information • analysing features of non-fiction
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Sequencing, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: Throughout history, people have been fascinated by sorting things in to groups. It happens with the music on your MP3 player and with your grandma's teaspoon collection. It also happens in the animal and plant kingdoms. This report will tell you all about taxonomy, the science of classification.
node.esiss_activities: Reread pages 10 to 11 about how taxonomy might work in the transport kingdom. Choose another example, such as the music kingdom and create a classification diagram. Share the diagram with your family. Can they add any other classifications to your diagram?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Digging up the Past
node.content: Jade is looking through her basement searching for something interesting to sell at the school fair. Instead she discovers things from her past as she finds old photographs of her grandfather with some of the fossils he discovered while working as a paleontologist. Features of Digging Up the Past • Narrative text with dialogue • Emotive and Descriptive language • Similes • Realistic fiction Purpose Digging Up the Past can be used for the following purposes: • using visualizing to make meaning • exploring descriptive language
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Sequencing, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: When Jade finds a precious dinosaur tooth among her grandfather's belongings in the attic, it leads her on a treasure hunt, and helps to raise money for the school fair!
node.esiss_activities: Jade finds the fossil of a triceratops horn. Find out more about this dinosaur. Use your information to draw a labelled diagram. Note the dinosaur's special features.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Sequencing, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Fossil Hunters
node.content: For hundreds of years, men and women have hunted for fossils in an effort to find out more about prehistoric life on Earth. This text explains how fossils form and tells us about five fossil hunters in different parts of the world. Features of Fossil Hunters • Factual report • Explanation of the steps in a process • Biographical information • Glossary and index • Photographs, maps, and illustrations with captions • Fact boxes Purpose Fossil Hunters can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • summarising and note taking • drawing conclusions
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas|Forming opinions|Making inferences|Sequencing|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making inferences, Sequencing, Summarising, Synthesising, Determining cause and effect, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Fossils are snapshots of the past. They tell us a lot about the plants and animals that lived millions of years ago. In this report you'll read about how fossils form, and meet three famous fossil detectives.
node.esiss_activities: Fossils are formed over a long time. Reread page 12. Create a flow chart that explains how a fossil can form in a tar pit.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect, Summarising, Sequencing, Making Inferences, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Andromeda and Perseus
node.content: The people of ancient Greece told the story of Andromeda and Perseus to explain the origins of the constellations named after them. This book retells the myth. Features of Andromeda and Perseus • Descriptive language • Retelling of a myth • Explanation of things in the natural world • Third-person narrative • Cultural and historical setting • Pronunciation guide to Greek names • Mythological creatures and humans with supernatural powers Purpose Andromeda and Perseus can be used for the following purposes: • analysing the features of myths • comparing and contrasting characters • making inferences.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Making inferences|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: From the moment she was born, Andromeda was different to other princesses. She didn't want jewels, she wanted adventure. When her mother insults Poseidon, Andromeda gets more adventure than she bargained for.
node.esiss_activities: People made up myths or stories about the constellations. Research the myth of Aquarius the water carrier or Aquila the eagle. Write a paragraph about the myth and draw the constellation.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Mysteries of the Solar System
node.content: This text explores our solar system, identifying some of its mysterious features. It provides an insight into how some of the planets were discovered and identifies some major astronomers. Features of Mysteries of the Solar System • Informational report with explanations • Fact boxes and diagrams • Specialised vocabulary • Organisation into chapters, subheadings, and paragraphs Purpose Mysteries of the Solar System can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing and Synthesising information • identifying Determining main ideas and supporting details • explaining the features of reports.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making connections, Sequencing, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Explore the mysteries of the solar system. Learn about the rings of Saturn and why the mysterious Red Planet is so red. In this book you can also find out what's under the clouds of Venus and ponder the origins of our Moon.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are on a mission to Mars, Venus, or Saturn. Write a report to mission control describing what you see as you orbit the planet. Use the information in the book to help you. Read your report to your family. Can they guess which planet you are orbiting?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Connections, Sequencing, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Mighty Wave
node.content: Antonio Isabella and their father are on holiday when a tsunami strikes. In the chaos and destruction left after the tsunami Antonio and his father try to locate Isabella. Features of The Mighty Wave • Explanation • Interview • Specialised vocabulary - epicenter, plate, seismologist • Eyewitness account • Use of diagrams and photographs • Chronological text organization • Mathematical concepts Purpose The Mighty Wave can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making predictions about plot and character development • using Visualising to make meaning
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making inferences|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Exploring text structure, Making inferences, Synthesising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Isabella and Aaron are on an island holiday with their father. Isabella thinks it's a boring place, and the only good thing will be getting a tan. She hasn't counted on the arrival of a major tsunami!
node.esiss_activities: The people in this book reacted in different ways when they realised there was a tsunami. Make a poster to explain what to do if there is a tsunami warning. Use the information from the story to help you write three or four safety rules.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Tsunami
node.content: Using the tsunami of December 26, 2004, as a starting point, this text explains the various causes of tsunamis. A scientist explains how tsunami warning systems work and an eyewitness gives an account of the 2004 tsunami. Features of Tsunami • Realistic fiction • First-person narrative • Direct speech • Descriptive language • Dramatic story involving a major natural disaster Purpose Tsunami can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring the features of an explanation • Comparing and contrasting information • identifying Determining main ideas and supporting details • Asking questions of the text
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Drawing conclusions, Comparing and contrasting
node.esiss_blurb: The word 'tsunami' comes from Japan, a country that experiences many earthquakes. This report on giant waves includes an eyewitness account and an interview with a scientist.
node.esiss_activities: Find the three causes of tsunamis in the book. Under each heading, write two or three sentences that describe how that event creates the conditions for a tsunami.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: EyePalm
node.content: Samir has the latest digital technology, an EyePalm, to help him find his way around Paris. He runs into trouble however when a young girl leads him to ignore the basic safety rules. Features of Eye Palm • Narrative text with dialogue • Use of metaphor and simile • Descriptive language • Unresolved ending Purpose EyePalm can be used for the following purposes: • making predictions • determining the author's purpose and point of view • making judgments about characters in a story
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Coming first in an essay competition wins Samir a trip to Paris. He also receives a new gadget called an EyePalm. Making friends with a local girl sounds like a great way for Samir to brush up on his French, but revealing the password to his EyePalm is a big mistake!
node.esiss_activities: Samir was tricked into giving away the password for his EyePalm. It's important to keep passwords safe. Write some rules for choosing passwords and keeping them safe. Show them to your family. Can they add any other rules to your list?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Always On
node.content: This report examines modern digital technology, which is becoming more and more a part of people's everyday lives. Features of Always On • Report with explanations • Graphics, illustrations, and photographs • Fact boxes • Subject-specific vocabulary • Questions and answers Purpose Always On can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying Determining main ideas and supporting details • summarising information • comparing and contrasting
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Drawing conclusions, Summarising,
node.esiss_blurb: The Net Generation has grown up always having access to the Internet at home and on the move. Find out how digital devices are used by the Net Generation, and also what the future may hold.
node.esiss_activities: Digital technology is a part of everyday life.Watch your family during an evening. Every time they use digital technology, such as a computer, tablet, or smart phone, write down what they are using and why they are using it. Show them your list. Ask them what they would do if they could no longer use this technology?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Making It Last
node.content: "Making It Last" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • exploring strategies to understand specialised vocabulary • analysing the language of procedural text • asking questions to deepen understanding of a specialised subject • synthesising information from text and visual sources. Features of the text: • explanation with procedural text • text organisers such as numbered lists and bullet points • labelled diagrams • specialised vocabulary
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect|Sequencing|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Recognising cause and effect, Visualising, Questioning
node.esiss_blurb: This text explores how museums conserve objects.
node.esiss_activities: Make a flowchart showing the steps of conserving an old photograph Conduct research about the most valuable pieces in their local museum Write a job description for a museum conservator_x000D_
node.wushka_comprehension: Sequencing, Recognising cause and effect, Visualising, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: When I Forgot
node.content: Amy is often given the responsibility of looking after her forgetful grandad. She doesn't crowd him and she lets him do his own thing. But one day, when she is distracted by a friend's phone call, her grandad goes missing. As Amy searches for him, she meets a number of people who all react differently to her situation. Features of the Book - First person point of view, - The concept of responsibility, - The way the first paragraph sets the scene, - Illustrations that support the text, - Amy's interaction with Sokun - "I know about people going missing" Purpose - When I Forgot can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - relating ideas and events in the text to students' own experiences, - previewing text and predicting outcomes, - describing characters' attitudes, - solving the problem in a text, - comparing and contrasting ideas and events to those in other texts. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Amy always did a good job of looking after her forgetful grandad. She just let him do his own thing, and kept one eye on him to check what he was up to. But one day, she forgot.
node.esiss_activities: Amy is distracted by a phone call and forgets that she is keeping an eye on her grandfather. A lot of things happen before she finds her grandfather again. Draw a timeline to show the events in the story. For each event, write an action or a choice.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Star Thief
node.content: Ricky is crazy about space rocks, so the exhibition of space junk at the museum is a dream come true. However, when the famous Coyote meteorite goes missing from the exhibition, his dream turns into a nightmare as he tries to solve the mystery and find the thief. Features of the Book Technical vocabulary - meteorite, asteroid, gravitational pull, comet, diamond saw • The introduction of clues to assist both the characters and the reader • The atmosphere of suspense • The use of italics for the main character's thoughts Purpose Star Thief can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • relating events in a story to students' own experiences; • discussing issues that arise in a text; • exploring how an author introduces clues in a mystery story; • writing newspaper reports; • rewriting text in different genres; • discussing text to generate further questions; • looking at the consequences of characters' actions.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When a famous meteorite goes missing from the science museum, Ricky thinks he knows who stole it. And he's not letting them get away with it. But how's he going to get it back?
node.esiss_activities: The Mickey Mouse guy had a plan to steal the Coyote meteorite. Imagine looking down on the museum. Draw a floor plan. Add the movements of Ricky, Biff, the thief on the day the meteorite goes missing. Show where the meteorite moves until it leaves the building.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Knightly News
node.content: Scott finds out that being a famous TV cameraman is not all that it's cracked out to be. As he struggles to come to terms with his failures, his mum is fighting a battle of her own. Features of the Book - First person point of view, - The use of humour, - Technical language - network, editing, booth, boom, wide shot, - Realistic informal dialogue, - The use of text boxes to show scripting, - The parallels between mother and son Purpose - Knightly News can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: relating events in a story to students' own experiences, - comparing narrative with report writing, - writing a report in news format, - discussing the characters' feelings, - using similes and metaphors. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making predictions|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Asking and answering questions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Scott's career as a TV cameraman starts off OK - his Knightly News bulletin is a hit with his apartment neighbours. But when he drops a microphone on the mayor's head and then fluffs his assignment to film the ballet, things aren't looking so good.
node.esiss_activities: Scott wants to be a famous cameraman, but he discovers it's hard work. Think of something that has happened to you that you could make into a TV news report. Use the 5W and 1H questions to help you. Present your news report to your family.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELT1800 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Trucker
node.content: Jack is crazy about trucks but not about his mum's boyfriend, Steve. On Jack's birthday, Steve gives him a surprise present - a ride with a trucker in a big rig. When an emergency situation develops, Jack starts to see Steve in a different light. Features of the Book - The theme of stereotyping, - The use of similes - the light around the cab twinkled through the gloom like birthday candles, - First person point of view, - Technical vocabulary - straddle, hydraulic, articulated, - The alternatives to "said" - muttered, called gasped, - The different viewpoints of the characters, - The technical detail in the illustrations, - The use of irony and sarcasm in the conversations Purpose Trucker can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - identifying stereotypes and how stereotypes influence us, - interpreting and analysing characters' feelings and actions, - using text to support opinions, - identifying the different viewpoints of characters, - identifying change of opinion within a story. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Summarising, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Jack's Birthday is going great until Steve, his mum's boyfriend, shows up - "He can't seem to get it through his head that I don't want to have anything to do with him." But when Jack spends the day with a trucker and an emergency situation develops, Jack begins to see Steve in a different light.
node.esiss_activities: Jack loves trucks, and he spends his birthday with a trucker. Big trucks have an important role to play in transporting goods from place to place. Choose three other vehicles that are used to transport goods. Draw each vehicle. List the kinds of goods it might transport.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Outer Space Outlaws
node.content: Millie finds it hard being a younger sister, so when the opportunity to be a hero arrives, she doesn't think about the possible consequences. Her adventures take her to planet Kalguri on the trail of a monster. Her journey gives her the excitement she wants, and she learns some valuable lessons along the way. Features of the Book - Comic strip format, - Text features - labels, speech bubbles, capital letters, enlarged typeface for effect, - Onomatopoeia - Choof! Oof! Schoonk! Sproing!, - Sci-fi vocabulary - vidcast, light year, hyperthrust, - The use of humour, - The use of linking text - And so ...; Meanwhile ...; An hour later, - The speech of Jeeves the help-bot, - The language of the aliens, Purpose - Outer Space Outlaws can be used to introduce and reinforce the following standards-related skills: - drawing on students' Prior knowledge, - exploring the structure of the comic book genre, - expressing and supporting a response to a particular genre, - exploring specialised vocabulary, - viewing situations from different perspectives. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When Millie and her sister Nash get stuck on the spaceship of monster-hunter Saratoga, they're whisked off to Kalguri 5, a non-human planet, on the trail of a monster. Then, Millie is captured by the monster, and it's up to Nash to try and save her.
node.esiss_activities: This story takes place in the future, when robots like Jeeves work for people. Imagine a robot helps out at your house. Think of five jobs you would like the robot to do. Draw your robot. Label the features that would help it do these jobs.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Planet Boring
node.content: Mag gets out of a boring trip to Earth when her friend Gork wins tickets to a concert on planet Frenzzi. The trip to the concert is an adventure, but is it real? Features of the Book - the science fiction genre, - The narrative structure - orientation, problem, climax, resolution, and conclusion, - Invented compound words - thrillsuit, thrilldome, vidwall, Vibeshield, - Colloquial speech - Knock it off! This is for real. Yeah, right! Big thrill. Way cool!, - The use of ellipses for effect and italics and capitals for emphasis, - The use of simile - as white as a slimeworm; like a twisting animal
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Monitoring for meaning|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Mag's friend Gork wins two tickets to the Brainthrasher concert on planet Frenzzi, where nothing is "real". But when they travel there through a fake wormhole, climb steep rockfaces, and raft down a white-water river, Mag begins to wonder where they are and whether this is for real.
node.esiss_activities: Mag calls Earth "Planet Boring" and she doesn't want to go on holiday there with her parents. Write a list of exciting activities you could do with Mag so she enjoys her holiday. List the names of the activities, where they takes place, and the equipment you might need.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Saving The Pink Lady's Slipper
node.content: "Saving the Pink Lady's Slipper" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • analysing the use of multiple text forms to tell a story • determining the author's purpose • forming and justifying opinions about characters' actions. Features of the text: • narrative • persuasive text • newspaper report • environmental and problem-solving themes • factual information in a fictional text
node.strategies: Making connections|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences
node.esiss_blurb: A group of children convince a company to champion an environmental cause.
node.esiss_activities: Research the Pink Lady's Slipper orchid to verify the facts in the story Write some dialogue that shows the characters' personalities Write a letter persuading a company to support a cause they believe in
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Eye in the Sky
node.content: Ali can't resist buying the eyeball in the magic store. It's the perfect birthday present for her brother Damon. In a strange sequence of events, Ali's gift to her brother brings together three very different people. Features of the Book - The parallel plots, - The non-linear story line, - The use of different typefaces for effect, - The way new characters are introduced, - The example of suspense writing, - The use of figurative language - like fossilised ice-cream cones, - The different characters' points of view Purpose - Eye in the Sky can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - investigating the characterisation in a text, - describing the mental images evoked by text, - identifying the feelings of characters and noting how these change, - making judgments about characters from their actions, - understanding the components of plot development, - identifying how setting is important to a story's development. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: An ordinary day in the big city turns to chaos when Ali buys an eyeball for her brother at the magic store.
node.esiss_activities: The fake eyeball in this story creates a lot of trouble. Create a timeline for the eyeball, starting at the magic store. At each point on the timeline, add a label to show how the eyeball changes the story line.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Monitoring and Clarifying, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Our Changing Earth
node.content: The world around us is changing all the time. This book explores glaciers, volcanoes, and earthquakes and explains how these awesome forces create new features on the landscape. Features of the Book Labelled diagrams; Cross sections; Bulleted lists, information grid, and fact files; Stunning photographsl; The section on myths; Specialised vocabulary - magma, geyser, lahar. Purpose - The Changing Earth can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using Prior knowledge to make sense of a text • studying the use of labelled diagrams • comparing different ways of presenting information • retrieving information and displaying it in a different format • reading and creating cross-section diagrams • creating a time line • exploring and writing myths.
node.strategies: Monitoring for meaning|Summarising|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Using texts as models, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: The world around us is changing all the time. Some changes occur slowly. Glaciers take thousands of years to carve valleys out of rock. Other changes can be sudden and violent like earthquakes!
node.esiss_activities: On page 30, there is a list of facts about the largest glacier and the fastest moving glaciers. Use this list as a model to write a fact box about volcanoes - the largest volcanic eruption, the most violent, and so on. Find information from the book and the Internet.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: News Flash!
node.content: This book describes the way a major oil spill in a popular marine reserve and fishing area is reported by three different media - radio, newspaper, and television. Features of the Book - Fact files with highlighted text, - Quotes from the media, - Facsimiles of reports and scripts, - Specialised vocabulary - lead-in, lockout, byline, piece-to-camera, voice-over, - Bulleted lists, - The summary page Purpose - News Flash! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - using a glossary, - studying points of view, - comparing writing styles in different media, - retrieving information and presenting it in a different way, - rewriting stories in a different format. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: When a ship spills oil into the sea, the radio is first to report the accident. TV and the newpaper are close behind. Find out how radio, TV, and newspapers find and report the news.
node.esiss_activities: This book is about the way a news story was reported on the radio, in the newspaper, and on TV. We can also get news on the Internet. Look at a news website with a family member. Then, using a Venn diagram, compare the features of the website with a printed newspaper. Does your family prefer reading a newspaper or reading the news online? Why?
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Compare and Contrast, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1524 ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Hunting the Horned Lizard
node.content: Nic Bishop travels to the Arizona Desert to take part in a field study of the horned lizard. The information the team collects will help biologists to protect this unusual reptile species. Features of the text • The example of a scientific field study • The use of maps and illustrations to convey information • The journal format • The contents page, appendix, glossary, and index • The different ways of presenting information - fact boxes, captions, photographs, and diagrams • the author's style - a mixture of fact and opinion in the text. Purpose - Hunting the Horned Lizard can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using the contents page, index, appendix, and glossary to locate information; • understanding how a field study is carried out • studying the features of a non-fiction text - labelled diagrams, index, and captions • communicating factual information • writing a scientific report • drawing conclusions from the text • creating a glossary. For more information, please see our lesson plan.
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Habitats|Life science|Science and technology
node.strategies: Following instructions|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Using texts as models, Following instructions
node.esiss_blurb: "Suddenly, when he nearly tramped on what he thought was a rock, it sprouted legs and scuttled off under a bush." Nic Bishop goes on a field study in the Arizona desert to hunt horned lizards and finds out more about how they live and survive in the harsh desert environment.
node.esiss_activities: Nic Bishop discovers that the horned lizard is hard to find. Imagine you are a volunteer at the research centre. Write a checklist of tips for finding a horned lizard. For each tip, include a piece of evidence from the book.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Our Star, the Sun
node.content: The heat and light from the sun allow life to flourish on Earth. The sun also provides us with a source of energy. This book looks in detail at the star at the centre of our solar system. Features of the Book - Report with explanations - Technical language - Diagrams, including cutaways - Footnote - References to ancient beliefs Purpose Our Star, the Sun can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - visualising; - evaluating information; - recognising how heat and life from the sun are essential for life on Earth; - understanding that the sun is one of countless starts in the universe.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Using graphic sources, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: The sun has been the centre of our solar system for over four billion years. Without it there would be no life on Earth. Find out how our everyday lives are made possible by this star.
node.esiss_activities: Write a short story called, "The Day the Sun Stopped Shining". Use the information in the book to describe what life on Earth would be like if there was suddenly no sunlight.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Shaping the Earth
node.content: Earth is constantly changing. This text looks at the forces, such as earthquakes, volcanoes, wind, and rain, that continue to shape Earth's surface. Features of the Book - Explanations - Diagrams, including cutaway - Comparative information Purpose Shaping the Earth can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions; - comparing and contrasting information; - recognising that the structure of Earth is always changing; - exploring how water, the weather, and volcanic action cause gradual changes in Earth's crust.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Distinguishing fact from opinion|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Determining cause and effect, Distinguishing fact from opinion, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: From the highest peaks to the deepest canyons, Earth is full of amazing shapes and structures that have formed over millions of years. It's hard to believe, but Earth is constantly changing, shaped by the forces of wind, water, volcanoes, and earthquakes.
node.esiss_activities: Use the illustration on page 7 to draw the super-continent of Pangaea. Label the map with today's continents. Compare your map with a modern map of the world.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Storm Alert!
node.content: Storms can be terrifying. A hurricane can contain more energy than all the electricity generated on Earth at any one time. This book examines the different kinds of storms and asks whether we are prepared for the next one. Features of the Book - Report - Present tense - Tables, charts, maps - Case study - Comparative information - Use of analogy - Quiz Purpose Storm Alert! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - exploring the use of comparative language and analogy; - interpreting graphic sources of information; - exploring the impact of global atmospheric changes on the weather; - recognising that certain events in Earth's atmosphere can lead to extreme weather events; - exploring how the actions of people may lead to changing patterns in the weather.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Making connections, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Hurricanes, blizzards, and tornadoes are all big storms. Once you've learnt something about storms and what they can do, you'll wonder if you're prepared for the next one that comes along.
node.esiss_activities: Look at the Fujita Tornado Scale on page 7. Make your own scale, but instead of using the description for each step, use a picture of that shows the damage from the tornado. You could use the Internet to find photos. Show the pictures to your family. Do they match the descriptions in the book?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: North and South
node.content: The American Civil War lasted just four years, but more lives were lost than anyone could have imagined. This book looks at the great hardships caused by the Civil War and how it helped to create the modern United States. Features of the Book - Report with explanations and recount - Diary extracts - Historical photographs and maps - Timeline Purpose North and South: The American Civil War can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - analysing and synthesising; - asking questions; - interpreting and analysing major historical events to help us to understand how today's society has developed; - recognising how the colonisation and settlement of new lands often led to civil unrest.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Making inferences|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: The American Civil war raged for four long years across the United States. It divided the land and even families, and more American lives were lost than in any war before or since. What caused Americans to fight against their fellow countrymen? This book tells you about one of the most destructive episodes in US history.
node.esiss_activities: Some people helped smuggle slaves to freedom using the "underground railroad" (page 6). Use the information in the book to write a diary entry of a young girl or boy being led to freedom from slavery. Describe what happens, the dangers you face, and your feelings about being free.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Elephant Adventure
node.content: When Juanita travels with her parents to an elephant orphanage in Sri Lanka, she's prepared for a boring, and smelly two weeks. Things take a turn in this realistic narrative when Juanita becomes involved in stopping an animal smuggling operation. Features of the Book - Narrative fiction - Plot with setting, characters, problem, and resolution - Told in the first person - Factual information - Theme of animal protection Purpose Elephant Adventure can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making, revising, and confirming predictions; - asking questions; - identifying the author's purpose and point of view; - considering how the actions of some people put animals in danger of extinction.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: When Juanita travels with her parents to an elephant orphanage in Sri Lanka, she's prepared for a boring - and smelly - two weeks. But when the safety of the orphanage is threatened, Juanita finds herself helping to solve a mystery and prevent a crime.
node.esiss_activities: The diagram on pages 30 and 31 shows the different features of an Asian elephant. Make a labelled diagram of another animal. Name each feature and describe what it is. Show the diagram to your family. Are there any other features you can add?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Night Creatures
node.content: This book looks at how lively the world is on the other side of daytime. The animals that come out after dark are often perfectly adapted to hunting or hiding from predators under the cover of darkness. They have many clever ways of finding their way around. Features of the Book - Scientific report with explanations - Generalisation and examples - Introduction and conclusion - Comparisons between people and other animals - Photographs and diagrams - Table of contents, glossary, and index Purpose Night Creatures can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions and searching for answers in a text; - exploring the features and habitats of nocturnal animals; - working out word meanings; - comparing and contrasting.
node.strategies: Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Is it difficult to find your way in the dark? Perhaps it would help if you had night vision like an owl, large funnel-shaped ears like a fennec fox, or padded feet like a tiger! Then, like the unusual creatures in this book, you would feel perfectly at home under the cover of darkness.
