node.esiss_activities: Creatures come out at night for different reasons. Choose four animals from the book. Make a chart with three columns. Name each creature, explain why it is nocturnal, and describe the special features that allow it to move around at night.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Amelia Earhart
node.content: Amelia Earhart overcame many obstacles: the challenge of long-distance flight as well as the restrictions placed on women. Her achievements inspired people all over the world. This book explores her life and legacy. Features of the Book - Biography - Historical photographs - Connections with world events - Mystery Purpose Amelia Earhart can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - summarising; - analysing and synthesising information; - exploring the contribution and achievements of early women aviators.
node.strategies: Making connections|Making inferences|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Making predictions, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever wanted to take on a challenge? Amelia Earhart was determined to fly an aeroplane. Not only did she learn how, but she made history. Her achievements inspired people all over the world, and her legacy continues to intrigue us today.
node.esiss_activities: Draw four boxes. Label them Amelia Earhart, Anita Snook, Harriet Quimby, and Matilde Moisant. In the boxes, write about their achievements as women aviators. For example, Harriet Quimby was the first woman to fly at night.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Predictions, Making Inferences
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Ancient Greece
node.content: Ancient Greece is where Western civilisation began. This book explores how the ancient Greeks' achievements in science, government, sports, and the arts still shape our lives today.. Features of the Book - Report, - Comparisons with modern life, - Diagrams, maps, and photographs, -Connections with myths and legends, -Table of contents, glossary, and index Purpose - Ancient Greece can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - asking questions, -determining the Determining main idea, - exploring everyday life in ancient Greece and the contributions the early Greek culture has made to our world, - making connections. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Making inferences|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Drawing conclusions, Making inferences, Using graphic sources, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: The ancient Greeks had extraordinary ideas, and their ideas in science, politics, and the arts still shape our lives. In this book, you'll learn how these ancient people lived, and how their stories, inventions, and language are still relevant today.
node.esiss_activities: Reread pages 26 and 27. Find out more about the sports that were played in the ancient Greek games and in the modern Olympic games. You can use the Internet or books to help you. Use a Venn diagram to compare the ancient games with the modern Olympic Games.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Working in the Hot Shop
node.content: "Working in the Hot Shop" can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • scanning and skimming a text for specific information • synthesising information from text and visual sources • using prior knowledge • exploring verbs. Features of the text: • Interview • Procedural text with photographs • Captions • Specialised vocabulary • Question-and-answer format
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making connections|Making inferences|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising, Making inferences, Sequencing, Scanning and Skimming, Synthesising Information
node.esiss_blurb: In this interview, Karen Ellett talks about her job as a glassblower.
node.esiss_activities: Describe the process of glassblowing using labelled diagrams Write questions for an interview with another crafts-person List all the things you know that are made from glass Identify the imperative verbs (take, roll)
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Finding Main Ideas, Summarising, Making Inferences, Sequencing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1490 ACELT1602 ACELT1604 ACELT1605 ACELY1690 ACELY1691 ACELY1692 ACELA1504 ACELA1797 ACELA1512 ACELT1795 ACELT1610 ACELY1701 ACELY1702 ACELY1703

node.title: Switched On
node.content: Electricity has always existed in nature. This book explores how people discovered electricity and how we use it in thousands of different ways. It also explores how electricity may be generated in the future if we are to continue to enjoy using it. Features of the Book - Report with explanations - Photographs and illustrations - Graph, diagrams, chart - Historical information - Causes and effects - Table of contents, glossary, and index Purpose Switched On: The Story of Electricity can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making connections; - asking questions; - using graphic sources of information; - identifying cause-and-effect relationships; - drawing conclusions; - exploring electrical energy: how we generate and use this valuable resource and the implications for the environment.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Determining author's purpose|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Determining author's purpose, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Electricity can be used in thousands of different ways. It's hard to imagine modern life without it. This incredible form of energy has changed the way that we live and the way that our cities run. Find out what electricity is actually made from, and how people have learnt to make use of it to produce heat and light and to power all kinds of machines and appliances.
node.esiss_activities: The first person to write about electricity was a Greek mathematician named Thales. He wrote about static electricity. Look at the experiment on page 5. Try this with someone in your family, using a plastic comb or a balloon. Then try other kinds of materials. Make a list of the materials that do make someone's hair stand on end and those that don't.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Questioning
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The World's Waterways
node.content: Water covers more than two-thirds of Earth's surface. For thousands of years, people have depended on rivers to survive. This text explores the many ways that people use rivers and the importance of rivers as a natural resource. Features of the Book - Report with introduction, generalisation, examples, and conclusion - Historical, geographical, and technical information - Photographs, maps, illustrations, and diagrams - Change over time - Glossary and index Purpose The World's Waterways can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - identifying key ideas; - visualising; - analysing the features of a report; - drawing conclusions; - exploring the construction and uses of the world's major waterways.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Water covers more than two-thirds of Earth's surface! For thousands of years, people have used this vast natural resource in many inventive ways. Find out how rivers and canals have transformed our lives.
node.esiss_activities: What is the nearest waterway to your neighbourhood? It could be a river, a lake, or a canal. Do some research. Find out if the waterway has ever been used to transport goods or people. Write two paragraphs to explain how the waterway has contributed to the growth of your town or city.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Unbelievable and Undone
node.content: Even scientists get hoodwinked by hoaxes. This article describes several infamous hoaxes and how the world reacted before and after the truth was revealed. Hoaxbusting hints are included. Key Reading Strategies: • Drawing Conclusions • Making Connections • Making Predictions. Curriculum Links: • social studies • science.
node.strategies: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion|Making Inferences|Making Predictions|Making Connections
node.esiss_strategy: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Inferences, Making Predictions, Making Connections
node.esiss_blurb: People have been playing practical jokes and inventing hoaxes throughout history. Do you know of any TV shows or movies that involve practical jokes or hoaxes?
node.esiss_activities: What is the difference between a practical joke and a hoax? Use a dictionary to find the definitions, then discuss the differences with a partner.
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion|Making Inferences|Making Predictions|Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1518 ACELT1525 ACELT1613 ACELT1614 ACELT1615 ACELT1616 ACELY1711 ACELY1708 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: School of the Future
node.content: Life at a school of the future is fast paced! To go on a class trip, you'll travel at ten times the speed of sound. This futuristic story traces Shakir's and Maria's class trip to the Amazon rain forest, but they find out that having technology at their fingertips doesn't always make things easy. School of the Future can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • making, revising, and confirming predictions; • making connections to compare and contrast; • discussing how new learning technologies may be used in classrooms of the future. Features of the Book: • Science fiction narrative • Futuristic school setting • Communications by vid-message • Theme of consequences of actions • Causes and effects • Factual information
node.strategies: Making predictions |Compare and contrast |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Compare and contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: What might school be like in the future? What would be the same and what would be different? What technologies do you think might be used in the classroom?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine a technology that schools might use in the future, then investigate the arguments for and against it. Examples could include rapid travel around the world to study different environments, systems that allow schools to track pupils' movements, or brain scanning to measure learning. Work in pairs or small groups with different sides to discuss the arguments. Make notes as you investigate.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Compare and Contrast, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1518 ACELT1613 ACELT1614 ACELT1615 ACELY17108 ACELT1616 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Takeoff!
node.content: People have always dreamed of flying. After countless failures, people discovered the ability of flight. This book explores how people made it happen. Takeoff! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • categorising information; • identifying a sequence of events; • discussing early attempts at flight and how these contributed to the development of powered aircraft; • summarising. Features of the Book: • History of human flight • Report with scientific explanations • Diagrams and photographs • Causes and effects • Comparisons
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect|Compare and Contrast|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Compare and contrast, Sequencing, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: This report discusses early attempts at flight and how these contributed to the development of powered aircraft. Why do you think people have been so obsessed with the idea of flying?
node.esiss_activities: Review the information about some of the less-successful developments in flight, including the mythical wings of Icarus and Daedalus and the ill-fated Hindenburg. Select one example to investigate, find out the background and why it was not successful in the long term.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect|Compare and Contrast|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1518 ACELA1521 ACELA1517 ACELA1518 ACELA1522 ACELA1524 ACELA1525 ACELA1830 ACELT1613 ACELT1614 ACELT1615 ACELT1616 ACELY1708 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Twisting up a Storm
node.content: This informative account of tornadoes presents intriguing facts and true-life experiences. It describes the destruction tornadoes can cause, how they form, how they are tracked, and why people chase them. Features of the text • Dramatic photographs to promote discussion • The use of background images and patterns to enhance the effect of the text • The multiple ways of presenting information - reports, fact files, labelled and scaled diagrams, map • Technical vocabulary - Doppler radar, supercell, meteorologist, air mass • Website information for further research • Procedural text and diagrams • The personal perspectives of the storm chasers. Purpose - Twisting Up a Storm can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • interpreting the information in diagrams • using a contents page, index, and glossary • understanding scientific diagrams • constructing and interpreting flow charts • posing questions for research • using diagrams to sort and present information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Sequencing|Using graphic sources|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using graphic sources, Using texts as models, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: "Dark, heavy clouds hung in the sky above central Texas on May 27 1997. People reported that "something strange was in the air all day." Late that afternoon, a tornado ripped through the small town of Jarrell, Texas, killing twenty-seven people and destroying most of the buildings in the town. Read about the destruction tornadoes can cause, how they form, how they are tracked, and why people chase them.
node.esiss_activities: This book describes what happens before, during, and after a tornado has hit. Use this information to write an eyewitness report from the safety of a storm shelter. Use your five senses to describe your thoughts and feelings about the event.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Sackville Screamers
node.content: Karl has a great new fundraising idea. Unfortunately, most of his ideas aren't successful and some of his classmates laugh at the latest one. His idea is to form a band with himself on bass guitar. There's just one problem - Karl doesn't know how to play bass guitar. Features of The Sackville Screamers • Realistic fiction with orientation, climax, and resolution • Theme of triumph over adversity • Clear sequence of events • Dialogue and action Purpose The Sackville Screamers can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction text • exploring character development • making, confirming, and revising predictions • comparing and contrasting
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Using graphic sources, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: Karl has a great fundraising idea. Entering the Battle of the Bands might win their band some new musical gear. The trouble is, they need to play an original song. Can their new singer Sky come through for them?
node.esiss_activities: Imagine the Sackville Screamers won the battle of the bands and are going to perform at a concert. Create a poster showing the band members and their instruments, and the date, time, and venue. Give the poster an eye-catching heading.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Bea's Resolution
node.content: Bea wants to be able to run like the wind. She thinks that if she keeps her resolution to train hard, she'll make the track team. This realistic narrative follows Bea's progress as she realises that she's no superhero. Features of the Book - Narrative fiction - Told in the first person - Recount or memoir form - Theme of achieving a goal through determination - Character development - Factual information Purpose Bea's Resolution can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - making connections to infer meaning; - identifying the theme of a story; - discussing the importance of a responsible diet and activity plan when taking part in physical exercise; - asking questions to deepen understanding.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Drawing conclusions|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Drawing conclusions, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine being able to run like the wind. Bea thinks that if she keeps her resolution to train hard, she'll keep up with everybody else and maybe even overtake them on the running track. Yet Bea soon realises that she's no superhero.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are Bea and you are training for the trials again. You've learnt from your experience. Make a list of the things that you did wrong the first time around. Then write a short training plan, explaining how you would train more safely.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Making Inferences, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Refugees
node.content: A boy and his older sister are forced to leave their home after soliders burn their house. The children are separated from the rest of their family and have to make the long journey to a refugee camp by themselves. Features of the Book - The relevant contemporary theme, - First person point of view, - The realistic illustrations, - The strong emotions of the characters, - The use of simile - forlorn as a foghorn; like a dog's howl, - The use of metaphor - furnace of anger, - Alternatives to the word "said" - announced, replied, muttered, whispered Purpose - The Refugees can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: identifying with a character in the story, making inferences, drawing conclusions and supporting them with evidence from the text, identifying problems and resolutions within a text, interpreting and analysing characters' emotions and actions, innovating on text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Exploring text structure, Monitoring for meaning, Making connections
node.esiss_blurb: In the dark, charred garage, I interviewed myself: "Who are you?" I am a refugee, a person without a home. I have a mother, a father, and a baby sister - somewhere. And I have a big sister, right here. Once, I had a hero, a soccer star, who ruled my life ...
node.esiss_activities: The children in this story are separated from their family. The boy buries some special things to keep them from being lost or stolen. Write a diary entry for that day. Imagine you are the boy. What three things would you bury? Explain why each thing is important to you.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: In Motion
node.content: Forces hold our world together. They keep airplanes in the sky, allow running shoes to grip the track during a race, and keep Earth orbiting the sun. This book explores the forces that we can't see but are critical to our lives. Features of the Book - Scientific report with explanations - Analogies and examples - Diagrams and photographs - Causes and effects - Table of contents, glossary, and index Purpose In Motion can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - using prior knowledge to make connections; - analysing and synthesising information; - understanding different kinds of motion as well as the effects of friction and gravity on moving objects; - exploring multiple meanings of words.
node.strategies: Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine a world without gravity, momentum, or friction. How would airplanes stay in the sky? How would a runner's shoes grip to a track? Find out why forces are so important and how they hold your world together.
node.esiss_activities: You can explore how friction works by doing a simple experiment. Use a smooth surface like an oven tray. Raise one end so it's about 15 centimetres off the table. Place a wooden block at the highest point. Does the block slide? Now spread some cooking oil onto the tray. Raise the tray again. If the block slides, it's because the oil has helped to overcome the force of friction.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Double Danger
node.content: One night, after waking from a nightmare, Alex surfs the net and finds a mythical monster that imitates people. As Alex attempts to rid himself of the monster, he discovers a lot about his past. Features of the Book - Third person point of view, - The use of adjectives - creepy, chaotic, tattered, - The use of italics, pauses, and short sentences for effect, - Illustrations that enhance the writer's style, - The foreword and afterword, - The use of definitions in the text, - The underlying theme of danger Purpose - Double Danger can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - analysing characters' feelings and actions, - relating ideas and events in a story to students' own experiences, - understanding how text features create an atmosphere, - Making predictions based on new knowledge. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making predictions|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Alex stared at the photograph in the magazine in horror. It was a picture of him, standing beside his prize-winning science project. Only it couldn't be him. He hadn't even finished the project yet!
node.esiss_activities: Alex has to create a family tree that shows his relatives. Draw your family tree. At the bottom of a sheet of paper, write your name and any brothers or sisters. At the top, write your grandparents' names. Ask someone in the family to help you add the names in the middle.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Looking Skyward
node.content: There are many amazing objects in the sky. From the clouds, the sun, the moon, the planets, stars, and satellites, people have been exploring the sky for thousands of years and there are still many discoveries to be made. Looking Skyward can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • making predictions; • asking questions; • discussing the many animals and objects that can be seen above and beyond Earth's surface; • exploring the derivations and meanings of words. Features of the Book: • Scientific explanations • Examples include natural and human-made objects in the sky • Diagrams, photographs, illustrations, and a table • Explanations of "how" and "why" • Specialised vocabulary • Historical and mythological, information • Table of contents, glossary, and index
node.strategies: Making predictions |Questioning |Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Questioning, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_blurb: What do you know about the sky and space? Why is the sky blue? What are constellations?
node.esiss_activities: Using the information on page 28, investigate a constellation and record your findings.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Questioning, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1518 ACELT1613 ACELT1614 ACELT1615 ACELY17108 ACELT1616 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Last Seen
node.content: Once, Earth was home to an even greater variety of living things than it is today. This book looks at some of the amazing animals that have become extinct and those that have been saved from extinction. It also discusses how people can protect plants and animals from dying out.Last Seen can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • evaluating ideas and information; • identifying an author's purpose and point of view; • identifying cause-and-effect relationships; • exploring reasons for the extinction of plants and animals and how people have contributed to this process. Features of the text: • Scientific explanation • Explanations of how and why. • Illustrations, photographs, maps, diagrams, and captions • Cause and effect • Table of contents, glossary, and index
node.theme: Animals birds and insects|Change over time|Changing earth|Environment|Habitats|Life science
node.strategies: Recognising Cause and Effect|Recognising Bias and Prejudice|Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_strategy: Recognising Cause and Effect, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_blurb: What are the main causes of animal and plant extinction and do humans ever benefit from it?
node.esiss_activities: Think about different perspectives that might exist on the causes of extinction. For example, the need to protect people from dangerous animals (see page 14) or the need to clear forests to provide land for food crops in areas of food shortages. Find one issue to research from two points of view, and record your findings.
node.wushka_comprehension: Recognising Cause and Effect, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1524 ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The World of Maps
node.content: Maps help us to locate places and find our way around. Today, many maps are available online. However, maps haven't always been accurate or easy to use. The information they provide is the result of thousands of years of exploration and technology.The World of Maps can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • synthesising prior knowledge with new information; • exploring the development of mapping processes and how we use maps in today's world; • using graphic sources of information. Features of the text: • Report with introduction, generalisation, examples, and conclusion • Historical and modern maps, diagrams, charts, and photographs with captions • History of maps and mapping • Explanation of latitude and longitude and of projections • Glossary and index
node.strategies: Synthesising prior knowledge with new information|Using graphic sources of information|Finding Main Ideas|Making Connections|Making Predictions, Sequencing|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Synthesising prior knowledge with new information, Using graphic sources of information, Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections, Making Predictions, Sequencing, Visualising
node.esiss_blurb: Imagine trying to learn about the world or find your way without maps. Discover what the earliest maps looked like and how they have developed over time.
node.esiss_activities: Use the information in the book to construct a timeline of the development of mapping from ancient times to the present.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Making Connections, Making Predictions, Sequencing, Visualising
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: People Power
node.content: For many people in the world, survival is a struggle. This book looks at the people and organisations that help those in need, whether they are trying to cope after a natural disaster or struggling to overcome poverty. It also explores the small things that all people can do to help.People Power can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: • making connections; • evaluating ideas and information; • identifying an author's purpose and point of view; • discussing how governments, organisations, and individuals can make a difference in crisis situations. Features of the text: Report with explanations: • Examples and photographs from around the world • Theme of people helping people • Strong personal voice
node.strategies: Finding Main Ideas|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_blurb: Without support from aid organisations, many people around the world would not survive poverty and natural disasters. But what is the most effective way to help?
node.esiss_activities: Discuss how governments, organisations and individuals can make a difference in crisis situations. Review the case study on page 23 and use this as a model to write another case study of an individual or organisation that has made a difference to people in need.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Recognising Bias and Prejudice, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1616 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Martial Arts
node.content: This book, in interview format, gives a comprehensive and detailed description of four martial arts. Anjuli, Stevie-Lee, Jack, and Hunter tell the author what it's like to do judo, te kwan do, tai chi, and karate. Features of the Book - The interview format, - Enlarged quotations to draw attention to the text, - The use of photographs to suggest movement, - The use of icons to mark repeated sections in the text, - The contents page, glossary, and index, - Maps with enlargements, - Sections that provide extra information - glossary, "Did You Know?" section, footnote, - Fact files, - Timelines to show the development of martial arts Purpose - Martial Arts can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - exploring the language and structure of interviews, - comparing and contrasting information, - finding information using the contents page, glossary, and index, - distinguishing between facts and opinions, - using a text as a springboard to further research. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Comparing and contrasting|Using graphic sources|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Comparing and contrasting, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever wanted to learn judo or karate? In this book, four young experts describe the rules and origins of their martial arts, and explain why they chose them.
node.esiss_activities: Choose two martial arts from the book. Using a Venn diagram, compare the martials arts. Which things are the same? Which things are different? Which martial art would you like to do? Make a small poster advertising this martial art. Highlight the features that make you want to try it.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Movie Mania
node.content: Jamar gets a video camera for his birthday and drives his family mad making a documentary for his English class. He redeems himself though when he solves the mystery of some missing items. Features of Movie Mania • Narrative text with action and dialogue • Told in the first person • Subplot of missing objects • Subject-specific vocabulary - panning, shooting schedule, video diary documentary, zooming, lens, editing, tripod, wrap up, DVD, launch, footage Purpose Movie Mania can be used for the following purposes: • making connections • exploring the structure of narrative.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Summarising, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: When Jamie gets a video camera for his birthday, he drives his family mad making a documentary for a school project. At the same time, things are going missing in the house.
node.esiss_activities: Jamie makes a documentary about his life when he turns twelve. If you made a documentary about your life now, what things would you focus on - the good and not so good? Make a list of five things. Choose one and write a paragraph explaining how you would film it for your documentary.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Digital Creations
node.content: Making animated movies or writing novels could be time-consuming activities before computers were invented. From painting to writing music, digital technology has changed the way many artworks are created. Features of Digital Creations • Factual report with explanations • Fact boxes • Photographs, digital images, graphics, and captions • Specialised vocabulary - 3-D, abstract, animator, cell, clip, convert, distort, holograph, morph, palette, postproduction, software, stylus, typeface, word processor Purpose Digital Creations can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • asking questions • figuring out words from context • evaluating ideas
node.strategies: Exploring text structure|Exploring writer's craft|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Exploring text structure, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Find out how technology has changed the way we write and make music, illustrate and make art, and make movies.
node.esiss_activities: Using the information in the book, make a list of the features of animated movies and movies that have live actors. Use a Venn diagram to show the differences and similarities between these two kinds of movie.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Oil Feud
node.content: In this futuristic story, the narrator and his mother visit his grandfather. Miguel explores his grandfather's home and finds some ancient machines. He also discovers the reason behind his mother's and grandfather's strained relationship. Features of The Oil Feud • Narrative text that includes orientation, problem, climax, and resolution • Futuristic fiction • Use of vivid descriptive language including personification • First-person narrative Purpose The Oil Feud can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making inferences and predictions • using visualisation to make meaning • exploring figurative language, including personification
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Summarising|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Summarising, Synthesising
node.esiss_blurb: In this futuristic story where fossil fuels like oil and gas are no longer available, Michael goes back in time when he and his mother visit his grandfather in the country.
node.esiss_activities: Imagine you are living in a time when oil has run out. How would life be different? Use the story and your own ideas to write two paragraphs about life after oil. Include at least one positive thing.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Energy of the Future
node.content: Over 80 percent of the world's energy comes from burning fossil fuels. This report discusses some alternative sources of energy that are already being used or are currently being developed. Features of Energy of the Future • Specialised vocabulary - biomass, emission, convert, turbine • Report structure • Glossary and index • Explanations and comparisons • Diagrams • Fact boxes • Statistical data Purpose Energy of the Future can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • using strategies to figure out specialised vocabulary • skimming and scanning for specific information • identifying cause and effect • identifying explanation features within a report
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Exploring writer's craft|Forming opinions
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Activating prior knowledge, Exploring writer's craft
node.esiss_blurb: Most of the energy we use today relies on fossil fuels. However, fossil fuels cause pollution, and will eventually run out. We need to look toward renewable sources of energy, which cause less pollution and won't run out.
node.esiss_activities: There are many forms of renewable energy that we can use instead of fossil fuels like oil and coal. Choose an alternative source of energy such as solar power or bio-fuels. Write a short speech to persuade people to use this renewable energy source. Present your speech to someone in your family. Was your argument persuasive?
node.wushka_comprehension: Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Making Connections
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Project Blog
node.content: Sean and Ravi set up a blog to find out about earthquakes. In the process they make friends with children all over the world. Features of Project Blog • A weblog journal, known as a blog • Third-person narrative with orientation, problem, climax, and resolution • Realistic fiction Purpose Project Blog can be used for the following purposes: • exploring different types of dialogue • forming opinions • comparing and contrasting
node.theme: Character education|Communication|Different views|Earth science|Geography|Natural disasters|School and community
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Determining main ideas|Forming opinions|Making inferences|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Forming opinions, Making inferences, Synthesising, Determining cause and effect, Determining main ideas
node.esiss_blurb: The students in Room 14 have to do projects on natural disasters. When a real natural disaster occurs, Sean and Ravi take the initiative to create a blog, and get a real-time report from the people who are affected.
node.esiss_activities: Sean and Ravi work on a project to find out more about earthquakes, including those that cause tsunamis. Research this topic and describe, step by step, how a tsunami happens. Begin with the undersea earthquake and end with the wave washing onto the land.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Recognising Cause and Effect, Making Inferences, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1616 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Global Village
node.content: People travel for many different reasons. This report looks at why people leave their own countries to start new lives elsewhere and how their lives - and their new communities - are changed by the experience. Features of Global Village • Multiple text forms - a report that includes explanations and personal recounts • Causes and their effects • Historical information • Fact boxes • Vocabulary specific to the theme - famines, circumnavigation, nomadic refugees, descendants Purpose Global Village can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • asking questions • reading for a purpose • synthesising information • evaluating information
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Summarising|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Sequencing, Making connections, Using graphic sources, Summarising
node.esiss_blurb: People leave the countries they were born in for many reasons. When an immigrant arrives from another country they bring skills, knowledge, and ideas with them. They also bring art, culture, and new ways of cooking.
node.esiss_activities: People often bring their traditions with them when they move to a new country. Think about the different cultural groups in your community. Choose a culture and describe one of their traditions using the 5Ws (Who, What, When, Where, Why).
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1616 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Rocket Man
node.content: Robert Goddard has become known as the 'father of the space age' even though his accomplishments were largely unnoticed while he was living. This biography explores his life and outlines his major achievements. Features of The Rocket Man • Biography • Explanations • Diagrams • Sequencing and organization of information • Specialised language Purpose The Rocket Man can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • identifying features of biography • skimming and scanning • synthesising information • note taking
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Exploring text structure|Making inferences
node.esiss_strategy: Making inferences, Exploring text structure, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Robert Goddard was a man on a mission. He dreamt that one day, people would travel into space, and he wanted to be the one to invent rockets that would eventually make this possible. His work paved the way for the moon landings and our exploration of the solar system.
node.esiss_activities: Use the dates in the book to create a timeline of the important events in Robert Goddard's life. Make the first date when he was born in 1882 and the last date 1945 when he died. Choose five key events for the timeline.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Cyber-Team
node.content: The City Council has decided to sell the soccer field to make way for a new housing development. Karina and her cyber friends decide to do something about it. After the mayor doesn't take much notice, they become determined to find a way to keep the field from being sold. Features of Cyber-Team • Realistic fiction • Narrative text that includes orientation, problem, climax, and resolution • Use of dialogue • Use of technology (computer chat room, email, website) • Computer screen layout, emoticons, and dialogue style • Use of aliases and abbreviation - a.k.a. Purpose Cyber-Team can be used for the following purposes: • introducing the ideas and vocabulary in the associated non-fiction title • making connections • exploring dialogue • analysing characters
node.theme: Communication|Environment|Leisure and recreation|Neighbourhood|Overcoming obstacles|Problem solving|Relationships|School and community|Sports
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Making connections|Making predictions|Sequencing|Using graphic sources
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Making connections, Sequencing, Determining main ideas, Using graphic sources
node.esiss_blurb: Karina is furious when the local soccer field is to be sold to real estate developers. She and her friends decide to protest, using email and the Internet to organise themselves and the community. They are overwhelmed with the response.
node.esiss_activities: Jamie designed some posters to advertise the demonstration. Use the information from the text and the children's emails to create your own poster. Include the time, the place, and the reason for the demonstration.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Making Connections, Sequencing, Finding Main Ideas
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Team Up!
node.content: This text examines how teams work and what it means to be a team member. It identifies several kinds of teams and includes a test to determine how much of a team player the reader is. Features of Team Up! • Report with an introduction, examples, a quiz, and a conclusion • Text organisation using subheadings • Specialised vocabulary and glossary Purpose Team Up! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: • making connections • determining the author's purpose • distinguishing between facts and opinions
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring text structure|Making connections|Making inferences|Synthesising
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Exploring text structure, Making inferences, Synthesising, Drawing conclusions
node.esiss_blurb: Have you ever been part of a sports team or a clean-up team? Teams work together to get things done. Read about all kinds of teams, then take the test to see if you are a team player.
node.esiss_activities: Take the quiz on pages 13 and 14. Are you an A, a B, or a C? Test your family members, too. Do they agree with the results?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1613 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Stumpy's Secret
node.content: Joe relates a story about his stash that is missing from his uncle's junkyard. He blames a local character called Stumpy, but as the story unfolds, the real thief is discovered. Features of the Book - First person narrative with direct communication to the reader, - The use of flashbacks to inform the reader, - The use of similes - shine out of that guy's face like a beacon; standing around in their pyjamas like a bunch of escaped hospital patients, - The alliteration in the title and chapter headings - Stumpy's Secret, Gigantic Guilt, Sticks and Stones, - The use of descriptive language - echoed around the yard like Christmas gone mad; It was like those finger bones jumped down my throat and stuck there; my heart tried to burst out through my ribs.
node.strategies: Drawing conclusions|Exploring writer's craft|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Drawing conclusions, Exploring writer's craft, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Joe's stash of broken appliances might seem like junk to some people, but to Joe, it's treasure - his only source of cash. So when someone steals it in the night, he's angry. And he thinks he know who's to blame.
node.esiss_activities: Someone has taken Joe's stash. It seems like junk, but it's special to him. Joe designs a trap to catch the thief. Design and draw a different trap that would show Joe that someone is taking his things. Use different items to those that Joe used. Show your design to your family. Do they think the trap would work?
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Connections, Making Inferences, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1615 ACELT1617 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713 ACELY1801

node.title: Ugly Mug
node.content: Marisa h+B430as moved from a small town to Avonlea, where she doesn't know anyone. During her holidays, she finds an injured cat. To begin with, Marisa sees a problem, but it becomes a way to meet new friends and it makes her a hero. Features of the Book - The use of italics and bold capitals for emphasis, - The use of parentheses and ellipses, - First person point of view, - The different viewpoints of the characters.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Making connections|Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Activating prior knowledge, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: When Marisa finds an old tomcat lying injured in the gutter, it's just another problem she doesn't need. But Ugly Mug turns out to be one of the best things that's happened to her since she moved to Avonlea.
node.esiss_activities: Marissa does a good deed when she helps an injured cat. She takes Ugly Mug to the animal shelter. Clint explains that the shelter needs donations to help it stay open. Write a flyer to explain why people need to donate money to your local animal shelter. Clearly explain why the donations are needed.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Ketchup Deal
node.content: This story is set in the future after a disaster has destroyed much of life on Earth. There are no adults, and children live in tribes. An urban tribe is short of food, and a searcher is sent on a mission to find supplies. A rural tribe captures him and a conflict occurs. Features of the Book - Science fiction genre, - The descriptions of the setting, - The first person point of view, - The structure of the story line - orientation, problem, climax, resolution, and conclusion, - The use of dashes, exclamation marks, italics, and capital letters for emphasis, - Technical vocabulary - inverter, alternator, barter, wind turbine Purpose - The Ketchup Deal can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - predicting events in a plot, - discussing the use of Descriptive language to set a scene, - Comparing and contrasting different settings and characters, - analysing a problem and its resolution in a narrative structure, - Comparing and contrasting ideas and descriptions in a text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Monitoring for meaning|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Making connections, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: Since the Disaster, the Superskaters have lived in supermarkets, munching their way through expired stock and collecting useful junk to keep their electrical appliances working. But now the last supermarket's almost used up ...
node.esiss_activities: Campbell doesn't understand where food comes from. He thinks it just appears at the supermarket. Choose three foods that you have bought from the supermarket like tomato sauce, jam, and bread. Look at the ingredients. For each kind of food, write down the ingredients and where they might have come from.
node.wushka_comprehension: Summarising, Making Connections, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELT1617 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Wreck Trek
node.content: Wreck Treck takes a fascinating look at the underwater wrecks of ships and planes and explains how they change over time. It also follows some wreck hunters and explores the different ways they locate and retrieve treasure. Features of the Book - The cover and layout of chapter headings, linking to the theme of the book,- Specialised vocabulary - sonar, strobe lights, stern, scuttle, - The different ways of presenting information - glossary, index, map, and website addresses, - Varied format - photographs, diagrams, and information boxes, - Examples of procedural text and diagrams, - Historical information Purpose - Wreck Trek can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - predicting content and probable outcomes, - using photographs to help with the overall comprehension of text, - using layout and chapter headings to navigate text, - reading to become informed, - using a variety of sources, including electronic media, to research a topic, - developing a time line to summarise and record key events. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Analysing themes|Discussing point of view|Making connections
node.esiss_strategy: Analysing themes, Making connections, Discussing point of view
node.esiss_blurb: How do people find wrecks that have been at the bottom of the ocean for years, decades, even centuries? What lost treasures do they find inside? Come on a voyage of discovery to some fascinating wrecks, from the Spanish galleon Atocha to the most famous shipwreck of all, the Titanic.
node.esiss_activities: The Mary Rose has been preserved in a special museum. You can find out more by visiting www.maryrose.org. From your research, write five facinating facts about the Mary Rose, from the past and from the present day.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1524 ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Riddles of the Universe
node.content: On 23 July 1999, Commander Eileen Collins took the space shuttle Columbia into orbit. Her mission: to launch the chandra X-ray Observatory, which will help scientists find answers to some of the riddles of the universe. Features of the Book - Fact files, - Technical language - dark matter, payload, sonic boom, supernova, - Dramatic NASA photographs, - Labelled diagrams, - Captioned photographs, - Website references for further research, - Procedural text, - The format of the countdown sequence Purpose - Riddles of the Universe can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - drawing on students' background knowledge, - exploring technical vocabulary, - locating relevant information to support claims, - writing questions that encourage further research, - presenting information in different formats, - rewriting text from a different point of view. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining main ideas|Following instructions|Monitoring for meaning|Sequencing
node.esiss_strategy: Determining main ideas, Sequencing, Following instructions, Monitoring for meaning
node.esiss_blurb: What happens when a star dies? What are black holes? What is dark matter? On 23 July 1999, Commander Eileen Collins took the space shuttle Columbia into orbit. Her mission: to launch the Chandra X-ray Observatory, which will help scientists find answers to some of these riddles of the universe.
node.esiss_activities: This books shows the crew of a NASA space shuttle carrying out experiments and tasks while in space. The space shuttle program ended in 2011. Reread pages 14 to 19. Use the Internet to find out about a day in the life of astronauts on the International Space Station today. Write a short report about how they eat, sleep, wash, housekeep, and do experiments.
node.wushka_comprehension: Finding Main Ideas, Monitoring and Clarifying
node.wushka_text_type: Explanation, Report, Diary, Journal or Log
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1618 ACELT1800 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Endurance
node.content: Sir Ernest Shackleton and his crew of twenty-seven men sailed from England on the Endurance in August 1914. Their goal was to cross the Antarctic via the South Pole. The expedition was stranded when pack ice destroyed ther ship. This true story tells their remarkable tale of survival. Features of the Book - Technical vocabulary - ice floe, ballast, tiller, sextant, caulk, - The use of historical photographs, - The use of both descriptive narrative and diary excerpts, - The theme of survival against all odds, - The Various ways information is presented - epilogue, timeline, glossary, index, captioned photographs, maps, and fact files, - The features of the layout that set the scene - engravings, photograph corners, banner headings, watery background images Purpose - Endurance can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - locating and Summarising Determining main ideas, - using a contents page, glossary, and index effectively, - using evidence in a text to support interpretations and points of view. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Making connections|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Making connections, Asking and answering questions
node.esiss_blurb: Sir Ernest Shackleton and twenty-seven men set out to cross Antarctica. But the sea froze around their ship, crushing it. After months camped on the ice, six of them brave 1300 kilometres of wild, freezing ocean in a lifeboat to seek help. This is the story of one of the most amazing feats of survival in human history.
node.esiss_activities: Frank Worsley's diary gives a detailed picture about life on the Endurance. Imagine you are on the ice with Shackleton's expedition. Choose two days from the timeline in the book. Write journal entries for these days, explaining how you feel and what is happening.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: Recycling Rubbish
node.content: Can a plastic bottle become part of a shirt? Can a pile of garbage provide power for hundreds of homes? This book answers these questions and gives information on turning rubbish into something useful. Features of the Book - The range of visual features - photographs, charts, and diagrams, - Bullet points to highlight key points, - Flow charts and diagrams to clarify processes in the text, - Alliteration in chapter headings - Stinky Smelly Stuff; Worm Work, - The captions with additional information, - Technical vocabulary - acid rain, toxic, insulation, polyethylene, terephthalate Purpose - Recycling Rubbish can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - reading an informational text effectively, - studying how the layout of a book adds to the effectiveness of the text, - locating and recording key ideas, - interpreting flow charts and diagrams. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Discussing point of view|Making inferences|Visualising
node.esiss_strategy: Visualising, Discussing point of view, Making inferences
node.esiss_blurb: Can a plastic bottle become part of a shirt? Can a whole pile of rubbish provide power for hundreds of homes? Can rubber on the road be made into rubber in the road? And what can be done with astronauts' dirty underwear? Find out how to turn rubbish - stuff that used to be useful - into something useful again.
node.esiss_activities: People have invented ways to reuse and recycle materials instead of sending them to landfills. Plastic bottles are a big problem. Find a plastic bottle and think of three items you could make from the bottle. Choose one way and make your recycled item.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1524 ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: The Big Picture
node.content: It takes many months and hundreds of people to make a television drama series. This book joins the cast and the crew of The Tribe to find out what life is like on a film set and how the series is transformed from the written script to the screen. Features of the Book - The features of a non-fiction text - contents page, glossary, and index, - , The use of bold and italicised text, - Large, labelled photographs, - The extra information in the fact files and captions, - Daily schedule to show the sequences involved, - Quotes from production crew and actors, - Procedural text, - The varied format - patterned backgrounds, facsimiles of documents, film frames as borders. Purpose - The Big Picture can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - formulating questions to help locate relevant information, - Summarising information using key phrases, - using an index to navigate text, - reading and writing procedural text, - creating effective flow diagrams, - exploring different ways of presenting information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Making connections|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Making connections, Sequencing, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: Ever wondered what goes on behind the scenes of a TV show? What's it like for the actors? And what about all the people who you don't see on the screen - what do they do? Join the cast and crew from The Tribe and find out how a TV drama series is made.
node.esiss_activities: In this book, there are photographs of the set for The Tribe. Imagine you are making a new TV series, for example, A school on Mars. Draw a set for the show. Write and draw descriptions of two main characters. Show your work to your family. Would they want to watch your show?
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: It'll Be All Right on the Night!
node.content: When seven students took up the challenge of producing a play, they thought it would be a breeze. But it wasn't that easy. This book explains the problems they faced and how they solved them. It also includes the script of the play that the students performed. Features of the Book - The procedural text, - The two sections of the book - report and play script, - The layout features of the play script - italics, parentheses, bold, capitals , - The visual text features - photographs, headings, borders , - The use of charts and diagrams to present complex information, - The use of speech bubbles and footnotes Purpose - It'll Be All Right on the Night! can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - studying the use of text features such as speech bubbles and footnotes, - understanding the features and format of a play script, - studying the uses and features of charts, - reading aloud with appropriate pace and expression, - understanding the features of a play, - planning and producing a play. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Activating prior knowledge|Asking and answering questions|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Activating prior knowledge, Asking and answering questions, Using texts as models
node.esiss_blurb: When seven students took up the challenge of producing a play, they thought it would be a breeze. But it wasn't that easy - to begin with, there were ten people in the play and what on earth does a producer do?
node.esiss_activities: It's always hard to remember your lines. The best actors still have to learn their lines. Choose a piece of text or write a short speech, and practice until you are confident you can read it clearly in front of others, with no prompting.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELT1617 ACELY1816 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: How I Met Einstein
node.content: Author Alan Trussell-Cullen explains the different steps in the publishing process for his book, Ask Einstein! The reader follows as his original idea is transformed into the finished product. Features of the Book - An explanation told from different points of view, - The chapter headings that show the steps in the publishing process, - The features of a non-fiction text - index, glossary, photographs, diagrams, - Fact boxes to provide extra information, - The summary in the form of a flow diagram, - The use of cartoon drawings and speech bubbles, - Different typefaces to show change of narrator, - Technical language - typeface, manuscript, stylus, roughs Purpose - How I Met Einstein can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - discussing texts written from different points of view, - exploring the effectiveness of the layout of a book, - understanding how a book is written and published, - studying variations in vocabulary, - reading and writing procedural text. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Following instructions|Sequencing|Using texts as models
node.esiss_strategy: Using texts as models, Following instructions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: "Einstein is a character. He just kind of popped into my imagination one day. But the idea for a character like Einstein really did start with a real cat. This is how it happened." Follow author Alan Trussell-Cullen as he takes you on a journey that begins with an idea and ends with a published book.
node.esiss_activities: The story writing and publishing process is interesting. Many people work together to bring ideas to life. Create a flow diagram of the process. This can be a valuable tool when you are working in the classroom.
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: What Can You Do with an Elephant House?
node.content: Two students get an opportunity to make a difference in their community. They learn about writing proposals and planning and carrying out a project at the City Zoo. Features of the Book An example of the problem-solving process, - The use of historic photographs to set the scene, - The example of a chronological report, - The transactional writing, - The use of brainstorming to set priorities, - Thought bubbles, sketch map, and lists, - The use of different typefaces for effect Purpose - What Can You Do with an Elephant House? can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills: - writing a proposal, - using chapter headings as clues to content, - retrieving information from a text, - discussing the pros and cons of a course of action, - using diagrams to present information. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Asking and answering questions|Sequencing|Summarising
node.esiss_strategy: Summarising, Asking and answering questions, Sequencing
node.esiss_blurb: Two Year 6 students come up with a novel way of reusing the old Elephant House at the zoo. Along the way, they learn a range of skills, from writing a proposal to finding the best paint deal.
node.esiss_activities: Miriam and Aleisha wrote a proposal for their project to use the elephant house. Use the information on page 4 to write a proposal to use a vacant building in your town or city. Use the girls' two proposals (on pages 24 and 25) to help you. Show the proposal to your family. Do they think your proposal would be accepted?
node.wushka_text_type: Procedural Text, Recount, Persuasive Text writing
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713

node.title: China - A New Nation
node.content: China is home to more than one billion people. As one of the fastest growing nations in the world, it faces many challenges as it transforms from a rural country to an urban one. Features of the Book - Report with an introduction, statement of topic, discussion, and conclusion, - Historical information, fact file, and a timeline, - Diagrams, maps, and illustrations , -Table of contents, glossary, and index, - Causes and effects, - Connections with North America. Purpose - China - A Changing Nation can be used to introduce and reinforce the following skills and understandings: - comparing and contrasting ideas and information, - exploring the history and growth of China and the effects of rapid industrialisation, - making inferences to identify an author's point of view. For more information please see our Lesson Plan.
node.strategies: Determining cause and effect|Forming opinions|Making predictions
node.esiss_strategy: Making predictions, Forming opinions, Determining cause and effect
node.esiss_blurb: The story of China is one of struggle, triumph, and incredible progress. Today, China is travelling into the future faster than any other country in the world.
node.wushka_comprehension: Making Predictions, Distinguishing Between Fact and Opinion, Recognising Cause and Effect
node.esiss_curriculum: ACELA1830 ACELY1711 ACELY1712 ACELY1713
